plants.blurb: Wildlife powerhouse: fragrant white flower racemes, dark fruit, golden fall color.
plants.water: Moderate; drought-tolerant once set
plants.spread: Running — rhizomes to 35 ft, forms thickets
plants.seasons: White racemes spring; red-black fruit late summer; gold–orange fall
plants.wildlife: Top Lepidoptera host; bee magnet; birds eat fruit
plants.toxic: Leaves, bark & seeds contain cyanide compounds — toxic to people/pets/livestock; ripe fruit flesh edible, seeds not

edible.summary: The ripe fruit flesh is edible — almost always cooked into jelly, syrup, juice, or wine — but the leaves, twigs, bark, and the seed pit inside each cherry are poisonous and must not be eaten.[1]
edible.parts: Only the ripe fruit flesh (mesocarp) is edible. Chokecherries are a longstanding food across the Front Range and Great Plains — used for preserves, jelly, syrup, juice, and wine, and historically pounded whole and dried into pemmican by Native peoples. The leaves, twigs, bark, and especially the stone (seed pit) inside each cherry are not food.[1]
edible.preparation: Eat the fruit fully ripe and cook it: remove the pits (do not crush or chew the stones) and simmer the flesh — cooking and drying drive off most of the cyanogenic compounds, and the trace that leaches into jelly or wine is too low to harm people. The flesh is mouth-puckeringly astringent ("choke"-cherry) raw, so it is essentially always cooked and sweetened.[1]
edible.caution: The leaves, bark, twigs, and seed stone contain cyanogenic glycosides (chiefly prunasin) that release hydrogen cyanide when the tissue is chewed, crushed, frost-damaged, or wilted. The fruit flesh itself is not toxic, but the pit it surrounds is — so do not crush or chew the pits, leaves, or twigs, and keep children from doing so. Wilted or frost-damaged foliage is markedly more dangerous and is a well-documented livestock-poisoning hazard to cattle and sheep; never feed wilted prunings to animals.[1][3]

plants.spread: Running — rhizomes form dense thickets
plants.seasons: Maroon-gold catkins early spring; grey to red-brown bark; short-lived
plants.wildlife: Browse for deer/elk/rabbits; birds eat seeds; key insect host

edible.summary: Mountain alder is not a food plant — no part is eaten, though it isn't notably poisonous to people.[2]
edible.caution: Alder is grown and valued for nitrogen-fixation, streambank stabilization, and wildlife browse, not for human food; the bark and cones were traditionally used for dyes and tanning rather than eaten. There is no documented edible part, so it should simply not be treated as forage for people. Do not confuse young alder catkins or cones with the edible parts of unrelated plants.[2]

plants.water: Moderate, regular — deep weekly soak in summer
plants.spread: Clumping — single tree; needs a pollinizer apple nearby
plants.seasons: Pink-white blossom mid–late spring; large red-blushed fruit Sept–Oct
plants.wildlife: Blossoms feed bees; dropped fruit feeds birds & mammals
plants.toxic: Fruit flesh edible; seeds contain cyanogenic amygdalin (mildly toxic if chewed in quantity)
plants.origin: Central Asia and Afghanistan; cultivar bred in North America
plants.habitat: Wild fruit-tree forests and mountain slopes of Central Asia

edible.card: Crisp, sweet fruit — fresh, baked, or pressed
edible.summary: The fruit is one of the best fresh-eating apples there is — explosively crisp and honey-sweet — and is also baked, sauced, dried, or pressed to cider; only the seeds carry a trace toxin.[3][5]
edible.parts: The fruit (flesh and skin) is the edible part, eaten fresh or cooked.[4] 'Honeycrisp' is prized above all as a fresh dessert apple for its loud crunch and balanced sweet-tart, honeyed flavor, and it also bakes, sauces, dries, and presses to juice and cider well; it holds its crispness in cold storage for months.[3]
edible.preparation: Eat fresh out of hand, or core and slice for pies, crisps, sauce, drying, and juice/cider.[3][4] Cut out and discard the seedy core — the flesh needs no special preparation to be safe.[5]
edible.caution: The flesh and skin are entirely safe; the only concern is the seeds, which (like all apples and stone fruit) contain cyanogenic amygdalin. Amygdalin releases hydrogen cyanide only when seeds are crushed or chewed — whole seeds swallowed by accident pass through harmlessly, and a person would have to deliberately chew on the order of dozens to hundreds of seeds to risk poisoning — so simply core the apple and don't eat or grind the pips, and keep children and pets from chewing them.[5]

edible.card: Crisp, spicy-tart keeper fruit — fresh, baked, or cider
edible.summary: The fruit is a top-class eating and cooking apple — spicy-tart off the tree, mellowing sweeter in storage — eaten fresh, baked, sauced, or pressed to cider; only the seeds carry a trace toxin.[5][6]
edible.parts: The fruit (flesh and skin) is the edible part, fresh or cooked.[4] 'GoldRush' is a complex, spicy-tart dessert and culinary apple with high sugar and acid; it is celebrated as a keeper whose flavor deepens and sweetens through months of cold storage, and it bakes, sauces, dries, and makes excellent cider.[5]
edible.preparation: Eat fresh (the tartness mellows after some weeks in storage), or core and slice for pies, sauce, drying, and juice/cider.[5] Cut out and discard the seedy core — the flesh needs no special treatment to be safe.[6]
edible.caution: The flesh and skin are entirely safe; the only concern is the seeds, which contain cyanogenic amygdalin like all apple pips. Amygdalin releases hydrogen cyanide only when seeds are crushed or chewed — whole seeds swallowed by accident pass through harmlessly, and it would take deliberately chewing on the order of dozens to hundreds of seeds to risk poisoning — so just core the apple, don't eat or grind the pips, and keep children and pets from chewing them.[6]

edible.card: Tart cherries — pies, jam, juice, fresh
edible.summary: The ripe cherry flesh is edible — 'Evans' is sweet enough to eat fresh, and excellent for pies, jam, and juice — but the pit inside each cherry, plus the leaves and twigs, are cyanogenic and must not be eaten.[4][3]
edible.parts: Only the ripe fruit flesh is food. 'Evans'/'Bali' tart cherries are dark red and milder than most sour cherries, used for pies, preserves, jam, and juice and palatable fresh off the tree.[4] The stone (pit) inside each cherry, the leaves, and the twigs are not food and should never be eaten.[3]
edible.preparation: Eat the cherries ripe and pit them before use — remove the stones and do not crush or chew them.[3] The flesh works fresh, but is most often cooked and sweetened into pie, jam, syrup, or juice; cooking the pitted flesh is the standard, safe preparation.[4]
edible.caution: The pits (stones), leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic compounds that release hydrogen cyanide when the tissue is chewed, crushed, or wilted — the pits are specifically described as poisonous.[3] Never swallow, crush, or chew the pits; pit the fruit before cooking and keep children and pets from gnawing seeds or stems. Wilted or frost-damaged foliage is the most dangerous form and is a documented poisoning hazard to livestock (cattle, horses) and to cats and dogs — do not let animals browse the tree or eat fallen prunings.[3]

edible.card: Tart pie cherries — pies, jam, juice
edible.summary: The ripe cherry flesh is edible — Montmorency is the classic pie/jam/juice cherry, usually cooked and sweetened — but the pit inside each cherry, plus the leaves and twigs, are cyanogenic and must not be eaten.[1][3]
edible.parts: Only the ripe fruit flesh is food. Montmorency cherries are bright red and mouth-puckeringly tart, the standard cherry for pies, preserves, jam, juice, and drying.[1] The stone (pit) inside each cherry, the leaves, and the twigs are not food and should never be eaten.[3]
edible.preparation: Pit the cherries before use — remove the stones and do not crush or chew them — then cook and sweeten the flesh, which is far too tart to enjoy raw.[1][3] Pitted, cooked, sweetened fruit (pie filling, jam, syrup, juice) is the standard and safe preparation.[1]
edible.caution: The pits (stones), leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic compounds that release hydrogen cyanide when chewed, crushed, or wilted — the pits are specifically poisonous.[3] Never swallow, crush, or chew the pits; always pit the fruit before cooking, and keep children and pets from gnawing seeds or stems. Wilted or frost-damaged foliage is the most dangerous form and is a documented poisoning hazard to livestock (cattle, horses) and to cats and dogs — don't let animals browse the tree or eat fallen prunings.[3]

plants.blurb: Self-fertile European prune plum: sweet blue fruit superb fresh, dried, or canned.
plants.water: Moderate, regular
plants.spread: Clumping — self-fertile (one tree fruits)
plants.seasons: White blossom spring; deep-blue prune plums late Aug–Sept
plants.wildlife: Bee magnet in bloom; fruit for wildlife
plants.toxic: Fruit flesh edible; pits, leaves & twigs cyanogenic — toxic to people/pets/livestock
plants.origin: SE Europe to W Asia (Turkey, Caucasus region)
plants.habitat: Forest margins and scrubby hillsides of SE Europe and W Asia

edible.card: Sweet blue plums — fresh, dried, canned
edible.summary: The ripe blue plum flesh is sweet and excellent eaten fresh, dried into prunes, or canned — but the pits, leaves, and twigs are cyanogenic and must never be eaten.[5][3]
edible.parts: Only the ripe fruit flesh is food. 'Stanley' is a classic freestone prune plum: deep blue-purple drupes whose flesh is sweet and meaty, eaten fresh or dried into prunes.[5][3] The single seed (pit) inside each plum, along with the leaves and twigs, is not food.[4][5]
edible.preparation: Eat the fruit fully ripe and fresh, or process it — its freestone flesh slips cleanly off the pit, making it ideal for drying into prunes, canning, jam, and baking.[1][3] Discard the pits; do not crack, crush, or chew the stones to get at the kernel inside.[4]
edible.caution: Like all Prunus, the seed pit (kernel), leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic glycosides that release hydrogen cyanide when the tissue is chewed, crushed, or wilted; the fruit flesh is safe but the stone it surrounds is not — never crack or eat the pits, and keep children from doing so.[4][5] Wilted or frost-damaged foliage is markedly more toxic and is a documented poisoning hazard to dogs, cats, horses, and livestock, so never let animals browse the leaves or fallen prunings.[4][5]

edible.summary: The ripe reddish-bronze plum flesh is intensely sweet ('bubblegum plum') and excellent fresh or in preserves — but the pits, leaves, and twigs are cyanogenic and must never be eaten.[1][6]
edible.parts: Only the ripe fruit flesh is food. Toka's reddish-bronze plums have apricot-colored flesh that is famously sweet and spicy — the reason it is nicknamed the 'bubblegum plum.'[1] The seed (pit) inside each plum, along with the leaves and twigs, is not food.[5][6]
edible.preparation: Eat the plums fully ripe and fresh, or cook them into jam, sauce, and preserves; the sweet, aromatic flesh is at its best straight off the tree.[1][2] Discard the pits — do not crack, crush, or chew the stones to reach the kernel inside.[5]
edible.caution: Like all Prunus, the seed pit (kernel), leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic glycosides that release hydrogen cyanide when chewed, crushed, or wilted; the fruit flesh is safe but the stone it surrounds is not — never crack or eat the pits, and keep children from doing so.[5][6] Wilted or frost-damaged foliage is markedly more toxic and is a documented poisoning hazard to dogs, cats, horses, and livestock, so never let animals browse the leaves or fallen prunings.[5][6]

edible.summary: The ripe peach flesh is delicious eaten fresh or cooked, but the stone (pit), seeds, leaves, and twigs are cyanogenic and must not be eaten.[2][3]
edible.parts: Only the ripe fruit flesh is food — 'Reliance' is a freestone, so the sweet yellow, red-blushed flesh lifts cleanly off the central stone for fresh eating, canning, freezing, pies, and preserves.[1][2] The hard stone (pit) it surrounds, the seed inside that stone, and the tree's leaves and twigs are not food.[2]
edible.preparation: Eat the flesh ripe, fresh or cooked; discard the stone rather than cracking it for the kernel.[1][2] Tree-ripe fruit is best, but firm-ripe peaches finish on the counter over a few days.[1]
edible.caution: The stone, the kernel/seed inside it, and the leaves and twigs contain cyanogenic glycoside (amygdalin), which releases hydrogen cyanide when the tissue is crushed or chewed — so do not crack and eat the pit/kernel or chew leaves and twigs, and keep children and pets from doing so.[2][3] The fruit flesh itself is not toxic; the danger is the seed and foliage, and it is greatest when those tissues are ground up or chewed.[2]

edible.summary: The pear fruit flesh is straightforwardly edible — 'Summercrisp' is grown to be eaten crisp and fresh; only the seeds carry a mild cyanogenic compound.[1][2]
edible.parts: The fruit flesh is the food. 'Summercrisp' is picked and eaten while still firm and crisp (not ripened soft like a dessert pear), sweet and good fresh out of hand; the flesh can also be cooked or canned.[1][2] The core and seeds are discarded.[3]
edible.preparation: Eat it fresh and crisp — that is the whole point of this cultivar — picking in mid-to-late August when firm and lightly blushed; do not let it ripen soft before eating, which turns it mealy.[1][2] Core it and discard the seeds.[3]
edible.caution: The fruit flesh is safe to eat. Like apple and other pome-fruit seeds, pear seeds contain a cyanogenic compound (amygdalin) that can release small amounts of hydrogen cyanide if the seeds are crushed and chewed — a hazard only if many seeds are deliberately chewed, not from eating the fruit normally.[3] Discard the core and seeds and don't chew them; the flesh itself poses no such risk.[3]

plants.gaps: Photos are species-representative Pyrus communis — no open-licensed photograph of the 'Summercrisp' cultivar exists on Commons/iNaturalist/GBIF/Openverse/Flickr as of 2026-06; re-source and swap in a cultivar shot if one ever appears.

plants.blurb: Colorado's state tree: a stiff, steel-blue evergreen that holds a near-perfect pyramid for decades — bone-cold-hardy, drought-tough once rooted, and unmistakable on the Front Range skyline.
plants.size: ~30–60 ft tall × 10–20 ft wide (slow- to medium-growing; far taller in the wild)
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tough once established, but deep-water in dry spells — drought stress invites cytospora canker
plants.spread: Single trunk, dense pyramidal crown; non-suckering, slow-growing
plants.seasons: Year-round steel-blue to blue-green four-sided needles on a tidy pyramid; pendant papery chestnut-brown cones; snow-holding evergreen winter structure
plants.wildlife: Dense cover for deer, elk, small mammals and birds; many birds eat the seeds and red squirrels cache the cones
plants.deer: Browsed only infrequently — among the least-preferred conifers for deer and elk
plants.toxic: Foliage and bark are not toxic; the sharp, stiff needles can prick

edible.summary: The soft new spring growth — "spruce tips" — is a recognized edible across the spruce genus, including Colorado blue spruce: tart, citrusy, and very high in vitamin C.[6]
edible.parts: Only the tender, light-green new needle growth at the branch ends in spring, while it is still soft; the mature stiff needles, bark, and cones are not eaten. All spruce (Picea) produce edible tips so long as the tree has not been sprayed.[6]
edible.preparation: Pinch off the soft tips and use them fresh, dried, or frozen — steeped (not boiled) for tea to keep the vitamin C, or made into syrup, jelly, salt, or sugar, or chopped finely into sauces, soups, and baked goods.[6]
edible.caution: Eat only after positive identification — true spruce (Picea) have sharp, four-sided needles set singly all around the twig; do NOT confuse evergreens, and never eat yew (Taxus), a common landscape conifer whose foliage and seeds are deadly poisonous. Harvest sparingly (a small share of one tree, never the leading terminal shoot) and only from unsprayed trees away from roads. As with any new wild food, try a little first; some sources advise caution during pregnancy.[6]

Summer — a blue spruce standing out in an open meadow · © James Bailey (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Winter — snow capping a steel-blue branch · © Chris Sichko (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: One of the largest trees on the Front Range — a fast, broad-crowned native cottonwood of streambanks and bottomlands; triangular fluttering leaves, brilliant clear-yellow fall, and massive winter architecture. Females release the famous early-summer cotton.
plants.size: ~60–100 ft tall × 35–60 ft wide (a true shade-giant)
plants.water: High — moist bottomland; not for dry zones, thirsty + brittle
plants.spread: Single broad-crowned trunk; suckers and seeds into stands near water
plants.seasons: Yellow-green catkins on bare twigs early spring; cottony seed in early summer; brilliant clear-yellow fall; deeply furrowed gray bark and bold bare architecture in winter
plants.wildlife: Keystone riparian tree — cavity-nest and roost sites, a larval host for mourning cloak, viceroy and tiger swallowtail, and cover/browse for deer, beaver and birds
plants.deer: Seedlings and young trees browsed by deer, rabbits and beaver — not deer-proof
plants.toxic: No serious toxicity; brittle wood drops limbs and the greedy roots heave pavement, lift sewer lines and crowd foundations

edible.card: Inner bark & spring catkins (survival food, cooked)
edible.summary: A documented Indigenous and survival food in its parts: the spring inner bark/cambium and the young catkins were eaten — but it is a salicin-bearing tree, so people sensitive to aspirin should avoid it, and it is a famine food, not a salad green.[1][3]
edible.parts: The sweet inner bark (cambium layer) was scraped and eaten in spring — the Teton Dakota and other Plains peoples used cottonwood inner bark as food — and the immature male catkins were eaten or, with the sticky sap, chewed like gum.[1][3] These are lean survival/famine foods, not a regular vegetable.[3]
edible.preparation: Harvest the inner bark only in spring when the sap is running and it peels cleanly; eat it raw in strips or dry and grind it as a flour extender, and cook the young catkins before eating.[3] Take bark sparingly and never girdle a tree — stripping the cambium all the way around kills it.[1]
edible.caution: Cottonwood bark, buds and leaves contain salicin (a salicylate, the aspirin precursor), so anyone allergic or sensitive to aspirin, pregnant, or on blood-thinners should NOT eat it.[1][3] This is a survival food eaten in small amounts, not a culinary green — the leaves and resinous buds are bitter and not eaten as food. Be certain of identification (the deltoid leaves and flattened petioles), since this is foraged from large wild trees.[3]

shots.cap: Summer — a broad-crowned cottonwood in full leaf, one of the largest trees on the Front Range · © asanc145 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — massive trunk and deeply furrowed gray bark · © Jennifer Ackerfield (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — close-up of the thick, deeply ridged gray bark · © millepon (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — triangular (deltoid), coarsely toothed leaves on long flattened petioles that make them flutter · © rodneyv56 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — deltoid leaves and coarse twigs in the canopy · © srknott (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Spring — dangling red-green catkins open on bare twigs before the leaves (dioecious) · © Sandy Wolkenberg (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Early summer — seed capsules split to release the famous white cottony seed · © Sandy Wolkenberg (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Fall — the whole tree turning brilliant clear-yellow · © Suzanne Dingwell (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — bold bare architecture against a snowy bottomland · © evie94278 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.origin: Eastern and central North America — Quebec and Maine west to southeastern Manitoba and the eastern Great Plains (eastern Nebraska/Kansas, the Dakotas), south to North Carolina, Tennessee, and eastern Texas
plants.habitat: Rich, moist deciduous forests, mesic mid-slopes, and bottomlands on deep, fertile, well-drained loams and silt loams
plants.blurb: The big native American linden — a 60–80 ft shade tree with a broad, dense crown and huge, broadly heart-shaped leaves up to 8 in across, coarsely toothed with the lopsided base that tells it apart from the small, fine-toothed European littleleaf linden also in this guide. In midsummer the fragrant pale-yellow flowers hang from a distinctive strap-like pale bract and draw bees in humming numbers, yielding the prized 'basswood honey'; hard pea-sized nutlets then dangle from that same bract into winter. Clear-yellow fall and smooth gray bark aging to ridged. It is NOT a Front-Range native — on the Boulder Front Range it's a planted ornamental that wants deep, moist, fertile soil and resents our hot, dry, alkaline-clay sites.
plants.size: 60–80 ft tall × 30–50 ft wide at maturity (occasionally to 100 ft); broadly oval to rounded crown; medium growth rate
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade; at least 6 hours direct sun for the best form and flowering
plants.water: Moderate to high — NOT xeric; wants deep, moist, fertile soil and regular deep summer watering, especially in heat and wind; leaf margins scorch when it dries out
plants.spread: Suckers freely from the base and roots, often forming a multi-stemmed clump or a ring of sprouts; casts dense shade under the broad crown
plants.seasons: Fragrant pale-yellow flowers in early-to-midsummer draw bees for days; hard nutlets hang from the persistent strap bract through fall into winter; clear pale-yellow fall color; smooth gray bark aging to long ridged furrows, with a broad bare-branched silhouette in winter
plants.wildlife: A premier bee tree — the midsummer nectar and pollen are a major resource for honeybees and native bees alike (the source of prized 'basswood honey'), and the flowers hum with insects in full bloom; foliage is a larval host for many moths and the eastern tiger swallowtail; nutlets and buds feed birds and small mammals
plants.deer: Foliage and young sprouts are browsed by deer and rabbits; larger established trees are largely beyond reach, but protect young trunks from buck rub and rodent girdling
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to people, dogs, cats, and horses; the flowers and young leaves are edible (see Edible parts)

edible.summary: The tender young spring leaves are a documented mild salad green, and the fragrant flowers are dried for linden-blossom tea (like the European tilleul). Use young leaves and freshly-dried flowers only, in moderation — old or improperly stored flowers and the hard mature leaves/nutlets are not for eating.[1][3][4]
edible.parts: The young, soft leaves gathered in spring as the buds unfurl are mild and slightly mucilaginous and are eaten raw in salads or sandwiches; once the leaf hardens it is no longer a salad green.[1][4] The fragrant pale-yellow flowers (collected with their bract) are dried and brewed as linden-blossom tea — the same traditional tea as the European lindens — with a sweet, honey-like fragrance, and the spring sap can be tapped for a sweet drink or syrup. The flowers and immature fruit have historically been ground as a chocolate-like paste, though it spoils readily and is little used today.[1][3][4]
edible.preparation: For tea: harvest freshly opened flower clusters with their bract on a dry morning, dry them in a single layer at room temperature out of direct light for one to two weeks, then steep the dried flowers in hot (not boiling) water for 5–10 minutes; use within a year of drying. For salad: pick only the freshest, most tender spring leaves before the leaf hardens — they are pleasant raw and need no cooking. Do not eat the hard mature leaves, the nutlets, or the bark.[1][4]
edible.caution: Use only fresh, properly dried flowers — old or poorly stored linden flowers have been reported in some sources to cause headache or mild ill effects when brewed very strong, and several authorities caution that over-frequent or excessive use of the tea is best avoided, so keep it occasional and use well-cured, recently dried stock. Eat only the soft young leaves; the mature leaves and the hard nutlets are not culinary. Don't confuse the linden/basswood (Tilia) with the unrelated 'lime' citrus or with any Toxicodendron. The plant is non-toxic to people, dogs, cats, and horses.[1][3][4]

shots.cap: Summer — pale-yellow fragrant flowers on the strap-like pale bract, over a large coarsely-toothed leaf · © Zack Harris (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Summer — the big, broadly heart-shaped, coarsely-serrate leaves with an oblique base (the tell vs the small fine-toothed littleleaf linden), with nutlets dangling from the bract · © Jim Walker (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Late summer — hard pea-like nutlets hanging from the diagnostic strap-shaped pale bract · © dtwilliams (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — the tall, broadly oval shade-tree habit in rich woods · © blankell (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — looking up the straight trunk into the dense umbrella of shade · © Erik Danielsen (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Bark — smooth gray on young trunks, aging to long flat-topped furrowed ridges · © Daniel Sembroski (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — the canopy turning clear pale-yellow · © Keith Bradley (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — a tan persistent winged nutlet-bract hanging from a bare twig over snow · © Nick LeBlanc (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — the bare broad-crowned silhouette in a snowy landscape · © simmar01 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: The most Front-Range-hardy evergreen rhododendron — small leathery leaves that bronze deep purple in winter, smothered in lavender-pink flowers in early-mid spring. One of the very few rhodies that survives here, but ONLY with serious acid-soil work.
plants.sun: Part shade — 2–6 hours direct sun; afternoon shade and wind shelter on the Front Range
plants.water: Moderate — even, steady moisture; never bone-dry and never waterlogged
plants.spread: Clumping — a dense rounded evergreen mound; no suckering or self-sowing
plants.seasons: Lavender-pink flower clusters early-mid spring; small leathery evergreen leaves all year; foliage flushes mahogany-purple through winter cold
plants.wildlife: Early-spring nectar for queen bumblebees and other bees emerging on warm days; deer tend to leave the toxic foliage alone
plants.deer: Usually deer-resistant — the grayanotoxin foliage deters browsing
plants.toxic: Toxic to people, dogs, cats, horses and livestock — all parts contain grayanotoxins; even the flower nectar yields poisonous 'mad honey'
plants.origin: Garden hybrid (R. carolinianum × R. dauricum); parent species from the SE-US Appalachian mountains & NE Asia
plants.habitat: Wild parents grow in acidic, humus-rich montane woodland — rocky Appalachian slopes and cool NE-Asian uplands

edible.summary: Do not eat — every part of any rhododendron or azalea is poisonous, and even honey made from the nectar is toxic.[1][3]
edible.caution: All parts of Rhododendron 'PJM' — leaves, flowers, stems, and nectar/pollen — contain grayanotoxins, which disrupt nerve, skeletal-muscle and cardiac-muscle function. Ingestion causes drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, weakness, loss of coordination, dangerously low heart rate and blood pressure, and in serious cases collapse, coma, or death; it is toxic to people, dogs, cats, horses, and grazing livestock (sheep and goats will browse it). Honey made by bees from rhododendron/azalea nectar — the classic 'mad honey' — carries the toxin and has poisoned people. There is no preparation that makes any part safe to eat. Plant where pets, children, and livestock cannot browse it, and call poison control or a veterinarian (ASPCA Animal Poison Control, 888-426-4435) if ingestion is suspected.[1][3]

shots.cap: Spring — the whole rounded shrub smothered in lavender-pink bloom · © SB Johnny (CC-BY-SA) / Wikimedia Commons
Late winter — evergreen mound holding its leathery leaves through the cold · © Nucatum amygdalarum (CC-BY-SA) / Wikimedia Commons
Spring — a truss of ruffled lavender-pink flowers face-on · © David J. Stang (CC-BY-SA) / Wikimedia Commons
Spring — single flower in crisp detail, long curved stamens · © David J. Stang (CC-BY-SA) / Wikimedia Commons
Spring — magenta-pink bloom over the dark mahogany-purple winter-flushed foliage · © David J. Stang (CC-BY-SA) / Wikimedia Commons
Small leathery evergreen leaves flushing red-bronze in the cold · © Nucatum amygdalarum (CC-BY-SA) / Wikimedia Commons
Spring — flower clusters set against the purple-tinged evergreen leaves · © David J. Stang (CC-BY-SA) / Wikimedia Commons

plants.blurb: The hardiest azaleas there are — a U. of Minnesota deciduous series bred to −45°F bud-hardiness, smothered in fragrant pink/yellow/orange flower trusses in late spring before the leaves, then good red-bronze fall color. Acid-soil-demanding: a true companion plant for an amended ericaceous bed, never bare Front Range clay.
plants.size: 3–6 ft × 3–6 ft (cultivar-dependent; dwarf 'Orchid Lights' ~2–3 ft, 'Mandarin'/'Spicy Lights' ~5–6 ft)
plants.sun: Part shade to full sun; afternoon shade best on hot Front Range sites to prevent leaf scorch
plants.water: Moderate; consistent moisture — shallow-rooted and NOT drought-tolerant, never let it dry out
plants.spread: Clumping, well-behaved mounded shrub; does not run or sucker aggressively
plants.seasons: Fragrant pink/yellow/orange flower trusses late April–May before or with the leaves; green summer foliage; red-to-burgundy fall color; bare twigs with fat flower buds in winter
plants.wildlife: Late-spring nectar and pollen for bumblebees, mason bees and other early pollinators; the showy trusses also draw hummingbirds — but see the toxicity note on the nectar
plants.deer: Not reliably deer resistant; deer browse azaleas, though the toxic foliage offers some deterrence
plants.toxic: Toxic to people, dogs, cats, horses and livestock — all parts (leaves, flowers, nectar, twigs) contain grayanotoxins; honey made from azalea nectar is the toxic 'mad honey'
plants.origin: Garden hybrid series (U. of Minnesota), bred from cold-hardy deciduous azalea species incl. R. prinophyllum (roseshell azalea)
plants.habitat: Wild parent azaleas grow on acidic, moist, humus-rich woodland edges, streambanks and bog margins in eastern North America

edible.summary: Do not eat — all azaleas and rhododendrons are poisonous to people and pets. Every part contains grayanotoxins, including the flower nectar, so even honey made from azalea blossoms ('mad honey') is toxic.[3][5]
edible.caution: All parts of Rhododendron — leaves, flowers, twigs, sap, seeds and especially the nectar — contain grayanotoxins, which interfere with nerve and heart-muscle (sodium-channel) function. In people, ingestion causes salivation, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, weakness, low blood pressure, slowed or irregular heartbeat and, in serious cases, collapse — the classic 'mad honey' poisoning from honey bees forage on azalea/rhododendron nectar. ASPCA lists azalea as toxic to dogs, cats and horses: even a few leaves can cause vomiting (not in horses), diarrhea, weakness and, in large amounts, cardiac failure; it is also a hazard to grazing livestock. There is no preparation that makes any part safe to eat. Keep clippings away from children and pets, never compost them where animals graze, and seek medical or veterinary help on ingestion. No part of this plant is food.[3][5]

plants.gaps: The named 'Northern Lights' / 'Lights'-series cultivar photos (the yellow 'Northern Hi-Lights' and orange 'Northern Lights' flower clusters) come from Wikimedia Commons; the habit-in-bloom, pink flower close-up, foliage, autumn foliage and bare-winter shots are honestly-captioned species-representative photos of the hardy deciduous parent azalea Rhododendron prinophyllum (roseshell azalea) — no open-licensed cultivar photo of those shot types exists on Commons/iNaturalist/GBIF/Openverse as of 2026-06. No vivid CC fall-color shot of either the cultivar or the parent was found; the autumn shot is honest late-season green-to-bronze woodland foliage (MBG notes the cultivar's leaves turn red-burgundy). Swap in a cultivar shot if one ever appears.

Spring — 'Northern Hi-Lights' flowers face-on with their yellow blotch and new leaves · © Elsa Blaine (CC-BY) / Wikimedia Commons

Winter — bare twigs strung with fat flower buds over snow (species-representative R. prinophyllum) · © Matt Nusstein (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: Not a true yucca but a tough Chihuahuan-Desert succulent perennial — an evergreen clump of arching, grassy blue-green sword leaves that throws tall, airy spikes of coral-red tubular flowers all summer. Bone-dry tough, heat-proof, and one of the best hummingbird plants for a hot, lean Front Range bed.
plants.size: Foliage clump 2–3 ft tall × 4–6 ft wide; flower spikes rise to 4–6 ft
plants.water: Very low; xeric once established — no supplemental water once rooted, drought- and heat-proof
plants.spread: Slow-growing solitary clump; clumps up gradually and does not run or self-sow aggressively here
plants.seasons: Evergreen grassy blue-green sword leaves year-round; coral-red to pink tubular flowers on tall branching spikes June through frost; green three-lobed seed capsules ripen brown by fall; spent stalks and rosettes give winter structure
plants.wildlife: A premier hummingbird plant — the long coral tubes are built for hummingbird beaks; also draws butterflies and native bees; deer browse the flower stalks and soft foliage
plants.deer: Not deer-resistant — deer browse the unarmed soft foliage and flower spikes
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic — leaves and fruit contain saponins, mildly irritating to people; ASPCA lists it as toxic to dogs and cats (saponins; stomach upset, vomiting)
plants.origin: Chihuahuan Desert of central/west Texas south to Coahuila and northeastern Mexico
plants.habitat: Hot rocky desert slopes, prairies, and mesquite groves on sharply drained sandy, caliche, or limestone soils

edible.summary: Not a recommended food. The nectar-rich flowers are reported edible and some foragers cook the petals, but the use is poorly documented; the leaves and fruit contain mildly toxic saponins, and the plant is listed as toxic to pets.[2][4][5]
edible.caution: The **leaves and fruit/seed capsules are mildly toxic** to people — they contain saponins (gastrointestinal irritation) — and the plant is **listed by the ASPCA as toxic to dogs and cats** (saponins; vomiting and stomach upset). Only the **flowers** are reported as edible (eaten as a cooked or salad flower in the desert Southwest), but this use is thinly documented and there is no well-established food tradition, so this guide does **not** treat it as a food plant. Do not eat the leaves, fruit, or seeds. Keep pets from grazing the foliage.[2][4][5]

shots.cap: Summer — whole clump with coral-red flower spikes rising above blue-green grassy foliage · © fleatx (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — tall branching flower stalk rising well above the evergreen foliage clump · © sa_joe (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — coral-red to pink tubular flowers along a branching spike, built for hummingbirds · © Rich Kostecke (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — a slender flower spike emerging from the grassy rosette in rocky desert terrain · © Eric Knight (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Summer — a green three-lobed seed capsule forming among the coral flowers · © Diana-Terry Hibbitts (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — the arching, grassy blue-green sword leaves of the basal clump · © Patricia Poore (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — a flowering clump on dry rocky ground, showing its lean xeric habitat · © Curren Frasch (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — a clump in flower in open dry desert grassland, its native setting · © Rich Kostecke (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: Colorado's tallest, most tree-like native cactus — an upright, shrubby candelabra of stout cylindrical, knobby-tuberculate green joints armored with barbed spines and glochids, erupting in vivid magenta-purple bowls in early summer, then hung with yellow knobby fruits that cling through the winter on a bare, sculptural spiny skeleton.
plants.size: 3–5 ft × 3–5 ft (occasionally taller in the wild)
plants.water: Very low; xeric, no supplemental water once established
plants.spread: Slow-growing upright clump; broken joints root where they fall, so it can colonize a dry slope over years — non-invasive in a tended garden
plants.seasons: Cylindrical green-jointed candelabra year-round; magenta-purple flowers Jun–Jul; knobby yellow fruits ripen late summer and persist on the bare spiny stems through fall and winter; joints purple-tinge and shrivel slightly in hard cold, plumping back in spring
plants.wildlife: Flowers draw native bees (a Xerces-listed special-value-to-native-bees plant), bumblebees and butterflies; the spiny clump gives nesting cover and the persistent fruit feeds birds and small mammals through winter
plants.deer: Highly deer- and rabbit-resistant — the dense barbed spines and glochids deter all browsers
plants.toxic: Not chemically toxic, but heavily armed: stiff barbed spines plus tiny detachable glochids at every areole lodge painfully in skin, eyes, lips and mouth and are nearly impossible to see or remove — handle only with thick leather gloves and long tongs

edible.card: Flower buds, despined & cooked (cholla buds — high in calcium)
edible.summary: Edible only with careful preparation: the unopened flower buds ("cholla buds") are a traditional, calcium-rich Indigenous food once every spine and glochid is removed — the spines and glochids are a serious hazard if any remain, and a few unrelated look-alike risks apply.[5][6]
edible.parts: The unopened flower buds are the prized edible part — harvested just before the flowers open, despined, and cooked. Cholla buds (from this and related Cylindropuntia) are an important traditional food of Southwestern Indigenous peoples, notably the Tohono O'odham, and are exceptionally high in calcium (cooked buds run on the order of thousands of milligrams of calcium per 100 g) — long valued for nursing mothers and as a guard against bone loss. The knobby yellow fruit is also edible — eaten raw, cooked, or dried, and added unripe to soups for an okra-like thickening — though on this species it is comparatively dry and lean; the seeds can be parched and ground into a meal or thickener.[4][5][6]
edible.preparation: **Buds:** with long tongs, pick the plump unopened buds in late spring/early summer; the universal first step is to remove the spines and glochids — singe them off over an open flame and/or roll the buds in coarse sand or scrub them in a wire basket, then boil or steam until tender. De-glochided buds are eaten cooked — added to stews, beans, eggs, or salads — and traditionally dried for winter storage. **Fruit:** harvest ripe yellow fruit with tongs, burn or scrub off any glochids the same way, then peel and eat raw, cook into a sauce, or dry. Never handle buds or fruit bare-handed, and do not eat anything from this plant until the spines and glochids are completely gone.[5][6]
edible.caution: The barbed spines and — worse — the tiny detachable glochids at every areole are the real danger: they dislodge on the lightest touch, lodge deep and nearly invisibly in skin, lips, tongue, and throat, and cause persistent stinging pain; glochids in the eye need medical attention. Even on harvested buds and fruit, residual glochids can remain, so the singe/scrub step is mandatory and must be thorough before anything reaches the mouth. The plant flesh itself is not chemically toxic to people or pets, but raw, unprocessed material should never be eaten. Harvest only confidently-identified Cylindropuntia (not a spurge or other spiny desert plant), and keep children well away from raw buds, fruit, and dropped joints.[5][6]

Summer — tall tree-like cholla on a dry foothills slope, showing scale · © symayers (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — magenta-purple bowl flower face-on with a boss of yellow stamens, on a knobby joint · © no rights reserved (CC0) / iNaturalist
Summer — a cluster of magenta-purple blooms open at once · © FrontRangeWildflowers (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — flowers and buds against the cylindrical, tuberculate, barbed-spined green joints · © utah_klaus (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Fall — the plant hung with persistent yellow knobby fruits · © rowz (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.origin: Pacific Northwest — BC to N California & the Rockies' western edge
plants.habitat: Open conifer forest, woodland edges, rocky slopes
plants.blurb: An upright evergreen shrub (3–6 ft) with glossy, spiny, holly-like pinnate leaves that bronze in winter, fragrant bright-yellow flower clusters in early spring, then powdery blue edible berries — the taller, glossier counterpart to the low CO-native creeping Oregon grape, and Oregon's state flower.
plants.sun: Full sun to part/full shade; best with part shade here
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established (shelter from drying winter sun/wind)
plants.spread: Forms an upright clump that widens slowly by suckers into a thicket; far taller and more upright than the low creeping Oregon grape (Berberis repens), which mats along the ground by stolons
plants.seasons: Bright yellow flower clusters Apr; powdery blue-black berries ripen late summer–early fall; glossy evergreen leaves flush purplish-bronze/burgundy in winter cold
plants.wildlife: Berries eaten by birds (robins, waxwings, thrushes) and small mammals; early yellow flowers a valued nectar/pollen source for native bees; dense spiny evergreen stems give bird cover
plants.deer: Deer-resistant (spiny holly-like foliage and bitter berberine compounds)
plants.toxic: Ripe blue berries tart/edible (used for jelly); bark and roots contain berberine, which is bitter and toxic in quantity — caution with children

edible.summary: The ripe powdery-blue berries are edible — a well-documented Pacific Northwest food, usually cooked into jelly, syrup, or wine — but they are very tart and seedy raw, and the bark/roots contain berberine and are not food.[1][2][4]
edible.parts: Only the ripe blue-black berries are eaten. They are a documented food of Pacific Northwest peoples and make excellent jelly, syrup, and wine; high in pectin but tart and seedy. The bright-yellow inner bark and roots are loaded with berberine and were used as a traditional 'barberry' medicine and dye — not as food.[2][4][1]
edible.preparation: Pick the berries fully ripe in late summer to fall; they are intensely sour and seedy raw, so they are almost always cooked and sweetened into jelly, syrup, juice, or wine. Use the berries only — never the bark or roots. Wear gloves when harvesting around the spiny holly-like leaves.[1][4]
edible.caution: The berries are edible, but the bark and roots contain berberine, a bitter alkaloid that can cause gastrointestinal upset in quantity and is not recommended in pregnancy — these are a folk medicine, not food, so eat only the ripe berries and keep children from chewing the stems or roots. The spiny leaf margins are a physical hazard. Be certain of identification before eating, and never eat berries from a plant you cannot positively identify.[1][2][4]

shots.cap: Spring — bright yellow flower racemes face-on over glossy holly-like leaves · © Rodamu (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Spring — an upright shrub flushed with yellow flower clusters in full bloom · © Jo Roberts (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
The upright evergreen shrub — taller and more upright than the low creeping Oregon grape · © Callan (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Glossy, spiny, pinnately compound holly-like leaves (several leaflets per leaf) · © machaut2 (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Fall — powdery blue-black grape-like berry clusters · © (CC0) / iNaturalist · © no rights reserved (CC0) / iNaturalist
Fall — blue berries amid foliage reddening in cool weather · © Eric van den Berghe (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — evergreen leaves flushing rich purplish-bronze in the cold · © Howard Bruner (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — snow-dusted bronze evergreen foliage holding structure through the cold · © ecoexplore_wnc (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: Compact woody xeric sage with small aromatic leaves and months of tubular flowers hummingbirds and bees mob.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established, sharp drainage essential
plants.spread: Clumping woody mound — stays put
plants.seasons: Mint-green aromatic foliage; tubular red-to-pink flowers summer into fall; bare/twiggy in winter
plants.wildlife: Hummingbird & bee magnet
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to people, dogs & cats
plants.origin: Chihuahuan Desert — SW/central Texas to northern Mexico (San Luis Potosí)
plants.habitat: Rocky limestone slopes & desert scrub, ~5,000–9,000 ft elevation

edible.summary: The aromatic leaves are used fresh or dried as a mild seasoning and tea, and the small flowers are edible — but use this ornamental sparingly and only when you've confirmed the plant is true Salvia greggii.[2]
edible.parts: The small aromatic leaves can be used fresh or dried to flavor seasonings and teas, and the tubular flowers are edible — a sweet-nectared garnish often pulled straight off the plant.[2]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves sparingly as a mild aromatic flavoring, fresh or dried, rather than as a bulk culinary herb; rinse flowers and use as an edible garnish. This is a fragrant ornamental sage, not a heavy-use kitchen herb like culinary sage (*Salvia officinalis*).[2]
edible.caution: Salvia greggii is grown as an ornamental, and its food use — aromatic leaves for tea/seasoning and edible flowers — is only lightly documented, so use it modestly and never in medicinal quantity. Be certain of the plant's identity before eating any part: many ornamental and red-flowered salvias look similar, and only true *S. greggii* is documented this way. It is listed as non-toxic to dogs, cats and people, so it poses no poisoning hazard, but treat it as a minor flavoring/garnish, not a staple green.[2]

Summer — autumn sage growing in its native rocky desert-scrub habitat, showing overall size and form · © Carlos G Velazco-Macias (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Summer — the small, rounded, aromatic mint-green leaves and slender flowering stems · © lotusmcelfish (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — whorls of red flowers held above the foliage on the woody stems · © Missy McAllister-Kerr (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.origin: East Asia — wild parent species native to China, Korea, and Japan; modern garden plants are Hemerocallis hybrids
plants.habitat: Moist meadows, grassy slopes, forest margins, and streamsides in temperate East Asia
plants.blurb: The workhorse perennial of the summer border: a clump-forming plant that throws up branched scapes of big funnel-shaped flowers over a long season, each bloom lasting just a single day before the next opens. The garden daylilies are Hemerocallis hybrids selected in every color — the one photographed here a deep maroon. Tough, adaptable, and near-indestructible in alkaline clay, they ask almost nothing; their one fault is that deer happily nibble the buds and flowers.
plants.sun: Full sun (6+ hours) for best bloom; tolerates part shade (2–6 hours)
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established — a deep weekly soak in summer keeps bloom heaviest
plants.spread: Clump-forming; clumps expand slowly outward and are divided to multiply or rejuvenate
plants.seasons: Strap-shaped leaves flush bright green in spring; branched scapes of funnel flowers from early summer into late summer (each bloom lasts one day, fresh buds open daily); foliage yellows and dies back to the ground after frost — no winter presence
plants.wildlife: Flowers draw butterflies, hummingbirds, and bees for nectar; not a notable larval host
plants.deer: Not deer-resistant — frequently browsed; deer relish the buds and flowers
plants.toxic: Toxic to cats (all parts; causes kidney failure — keep away from cats); non-toxic to dogs and horses. Flowers, buds, and tubers are eaten by people in Asian cooking, but cause stomach upset in some and should be eaten only in moderation

edible.card: Flower buds & petals, and tubers, cooked — Asian cuisine
edible.summary: Daylily flowers, unopened buds, and the small tuberous roots are a documented food in Asian (especially Chinese) cooking — but eat only in moderation, only the true daylily, and never give any part to a cat.[1][3][4]
edible.parts: The **flowers and unopened buds** of Hemerocallis are the best-known edible part: the buds (picked young, about 1.5–2 in. long, before they turn bitter) are stir-fried or added to soups, and the petals can be eaten fresh in salads or dried — dried daylily buds are the 'golden needles' (gum jum) of Chinese cooking. The young tuberous **roots** are also eaten cooked, with a mild, nutty, water-chestnut-like flavor. Sources note the flavor of the cooked flowers as something like asparagus or zucchini.[1][3][4]
edible.preparation: Cook the buds and tubers — sauté, stir-fry, or add to soups; petals can be used raw as a garnish or dried for later use. Pick buds young, before they become bitter. Introduce daylily to your diet in small amounts the first time, as some people experience digestive upset (it can act as a mild diuretic or laxative). Use only flowers from plants you know have not been treated with pesticides.[3][4]
edible.caution: Two serious cautions. First, **daylily (Hemerocallis) is highly toxic to cats** — all parts, including the flowers, leaves, and even water from a vase, can cause acute kidney (renal) failure and death in cats; there is no safe amount for a cat. Never let a cat ingest any part, and keep cut flowers away from them. (It is non-toxic to dogs and horses.) Second, **do not confuse the daylily with the true lilies (Lilium spp.)** — they are unrelated plants despite the shared name; true lily flowers and bulbs are NOT the traditional edible and Lilium is likewise deadly to cats. For people, even genuine daylily should be eaten only in moderation and cooked, as raw or excessive amounts cause stomach upset, nausea, or diarrhea in some individuals.[1][3][4]

plants.origin: Temperate Eurasia — mountain woodlands from Europe east to Mongolia
plants.habitat: Woodland edges, clearings, and subalpine meadows on humus-rich, often limy soils
plants.blurb: The aristocrat of the shade border and the parent of the martagon hybrids: a tall, slow-building lily that throws a single straight stem hung with up to thirty small nodding flowers, each with petals curled fully back into the unmistakable 'Turk's cap' — rosy-pink to white, freckled inside with dark maroon, and lightly fragrant. The lance-shaped leaves sit in tidy whorls up the stem. Unlike the gaudy Asiatic and Oriental lilies, the martagon is a woodlander: it wants part shade, moist humus-rich soil, and patience — it sulks the first year or two and resents being moved — but once settled it is long-lived, all but pest-free, and slowly forms a colony. Its tolerance of slightly alkaline, clayey ground makes it one of the better true lilies for the Front Range. (Like all true lilies it is deadly to cats.)
plants.sun: Part shade to full sun; afternoon shade is ideal in our intense high-altitude sun (more shade gives taller stems)
plants.water: Moderate — keep the soil evenly moist through the growing season; not a xeric or drought-tolerant plant, but needs sharp winter drainage
plants.spread: Slow-clumping from a bulb; settled plants form small colonies over years and resent being dug or moved
plants.seasons: Whorled leafy shoots push up in spring; a single stem of nodding Turk's-cap flowers opens mid-to-late summer; foliage yellows and the stem is cut back in fall as the plant dies down to a dormant bulb — no winter presence above ground
plants.wildlife: The fragrant, nectar-rich nodding flowers are worked by long-tongued bees, hawkmoths, and butterflies (notably swallowtails), and draw hummingbirds
plants.deer: Not deer-resistant — deer browse the buds and flowers, and rabbits and voles dig and eat the shoots and bulbs
plants.toxic: All parts are highly toxic to cats — ingesting even a small amount (or grooming off pollen, or drinking vase water) causes acute kidney failure and death; there is no safe amount for a cat. Non-toxic to dogs and horses. The ornamental bulbs are not a food and should not be eaten by people

edible.summary: The martagon lily is an ornamental, not a food, and like all true lilies (Lilium) it is deadly to cats. Although the bulbs of some Asian lilies are eaten cooked, ornamental martagon bulbs should not be eaten — and no part should ever reach a cat.[2][4]
edible.caution: Two things to know. First, **all true lilies (Lilium species), including the martagon, are extremely toxic to cats**: every part — flowers, leaves, pollen, and even the water a cut stem has stood in — can cause acute kidney failure and death, and there is no safe amount for a cat. Keep the plant and any cut stems well away from cats, and seek emergency veterinary care immediately if a cat ingests any part. (It is non-toxic to dogs and horses.) Second, while the bulbs of a few lilies are cooked and eaten in parts of Asia, the martagon is grown strictly as an ornamental and its bulbs are not a recognized food here; treat the whole plant as not-to-be-eaten, and never confuse ornamental lily bulbs with edible bulbs.[2][4]

Summer — a flowering plant in a subalpine meadow, showing its habit in context · © matthias1978 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — the whole plant in bloom above an alpine lake, its full stature · © dsteward (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.origin: Mexico, Central America & northern South America
plants.habitat: Rocky banks, cliffs, walls, and open slopes in upland Mexico and Central America
plants.blurb: A dainty, ferociously long-blooming little daisy that froths over walls, steps, and the front of a border in a haze of tiny flowers from early summer to frost. Each bloom opens pure white with a gold eye, then ages through pink to purplish, so a single wiry, narrow-leaved mound carries all three shades at once for a charming two-tone effect. Native to upland Mexico and Central America, it is the classic self-seeding 'wall daisy' that tucks itself into paving cracks and stone crevices. It is borderline-hardy on the Front Range — only reliably hardy to about zone 6 — so here it behaves as a frost-tender perennial that mostly carries on by self-sowing. A cheerful, drought-tolerant filler; just site it away from natural areas and deadhead or pull strays, since it self-seeds prolifically and is a known invasive in mild, wet-winter climates.
plants.sun: Full sun for the heaviest, longest bloom; tolerates light/part shade
plants.water: Low — drought-tolerant once established, though it mounds up fuller with occasional summer water; needs sharp drainage, not wet ground
plants.spread: Spreading, carpet-forming mat from wiry stems and woody rhizomes; self-sows freely into cracks and gravel (can run where winters are mild)
plants.seasons: A low, semi-evergreen mound of grey-green leaves; smothered in white-to-pink daisies from early summer until hard frost; killed back or knocked down by cold here, returning from the crown or from self-sown seedlings in spring — little winter presence in our climate
plants.wildlife: The long succession of small open daisies is excellent for pollinators — butterflies, bees, and hoverflies work the flowers all season
plants.deer: Generally not browsed — fleabanes are little troubled by deer and rabbits
plants.toxic: Not known to be toxic to people, dogs, cats, or horses; it is an ornamental daisy, not a food plant

edible.summary: Mexican fleabane is an ornamental daisy grown for its flowers, not a food plant. It is not known to be toxic, but it has no established edible use and should not be eaten.[1][2]
edible.caution: There is no documented culinary use of Erigeron karvinskianus, and it is not a recognized edible plant — treat it as ornamental only and do not eat any part. It is not reported as poisonous to people or to dogs, cats, or horses, so an accidental nibble is not a known hazard, but as with any daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant, people with sensitivities to that family could react. Enjoy it in the garden rather than the kitchen.[1][2]

Summer — an older flower aged to pink, showing how the white rays flush pink then purplish with age · © Saryu Mae 前 朝琉 (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Summer — a froth of daisies in white and aged pink together, the plant's signature two-tone effect · © zebedeugalinha (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Summer — a flowering mat over the wiry, narrow grey-green leaves · © rainerburkard (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Summer — a long-blooming mound spilling along a path, showing its scale and habit · © zebedeugalinha (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Summer — a clump cascading from a rock ledge, the classic wall-and-crevice habit · © Jinyan Yang (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Spring — the low rosette of narrow, few-toothed grey-green leaves · © zebedeugalinha (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: A tidy clump of narrow grey-green leaves throws wiry, near-leafless stems topped with lavender-blue cornflower-like flowers — fringed, toothed ray tips around a darker eye — over a long summer. The papery, silvery, strawy bracts give the buds an everlasting look prized for drying. Xeric, lean-soil-loving, and short-lived but easy to renew.
plants.water: Low; xeric and drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; not aggressive, but self-sows where happy
plants.seasons: Low tuft of grassy grey-green basal leaves spring through fall; lavender-blue flowers on wiry stems July–Sep amid papery silvery buds; dies back to the ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Butterfly and bee plant; the nectar-rich blue flowers draw butterflies and pollinators all summer
plants.origin: Mediterranean — southwestern Europe and northwestern Africa
plants.habitat: Dry meadows, garrigue, and rocky slopes on lean, well-drained calcareous ground

edible.summary: Cupid's dart is an ornamental aster relative grown for its flowers and everlasting buds — there is no documented edible use, so don't eat it; it is not notably poisonous, but it is not a food plant either.[1][2]
edible.caution: Catananche caerulea is grown strictly as an ornamental and dried-flower plant. Authoritative horticultural sources record no culinary use and no established food record for any part — flowers, foliage, or roots — and likewise flag no notable toxicity. With no positive edible record, treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat it. (It belongs to the daisy/aster family; people with composite-family sensitivities should also avoid handling it freely.)[1][2]

plants.blurb: Also called perennial statice — a tough, drought- and salt-proof steppe perennial that throws a huge airy 'baby's-breath' cloud of tiny papery lavender-blue flowers in midsummer over a basal rosette of broad leathery leaves; one of the best fresh-or-dried everlasting cut flowers for the dry Front Range.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established, tolerates drought and dry soil
plants.spread: Clumping; a single non-running rosette from a deep taproot, may self-seed
plants.seasons: Basal rosette of dark spoon-shaped leaves spring through fall; airy lavender-blue flower cloud July–Aug; the branched flower stems dry to a buff 'everlasting' skeleton that persists into winter
plants.wildlife: Bees, butterflies and other pollinators work the tiny flowers; the airy bloom is a favorite of small native bees
plants.toxic: No toxicity reported; grown as an ornamental and cut/dried flower, not a food plant
plants.origin: Steppes of SE & central Europe to the Caucasus (Black Sea region)
plants.habitat: Dry saline steppe and rocky calcareous slopes

edible.summary: Sea lavender is grown as an ornamental and cut/dried flower — there is no documented edible part, so don't eat it; it is not notably poisonous, just not a food.[1][2]
edible.caution: Limonium platyphyllum is cultivated strictly as an ornamental and an everlasting cut flower. Authoritative horticultural sources record no culinary use and no toxicity rating — it is neither a recognized edible nor a known poison.[1][2] With no positive food record from a reliable source, treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots. (Note the look-alike trap: the dried flower clouds sold as florist 'statice' come from this genus and its relatives and are decorative material, not food.)

plants.origin: Europe, the Mediterranean, and western Asia
plants.habitat: Dry rocky hills, heath, and lean grassland in full sun
plants.blurb: The classic stepable groundcover: a low, woody-based aromatic mat just a few inches tall, clad in tiny fragrant blue-green leaves and smothered in pale pink-to-lavender flowers that bees mob in early-to-midsummer. It's tailor-made for the Front Range — bone-dry tough once established, happy in lean alkaline ground, and able to take light foot traffic, so it's the go-to plant to tuck between paving stones, soften a path edge, or carpet a hot, gravelly slope where little else will grow. A milder relative of kitchen thyme, its leaves and flowers are edible and aromatic too.
plants.size: 2–3 in tall foliage mat (flowering stems to ~4 in); spreads 12–18+ in wide
plants.sun: Full sun (6+ hours); tolerates light part shade
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — needs sharp drainage, rots in wet soil
plants.spread: Spreads by rooting prostrate woody stems into a dense weed-smothering mat; not aggressively invasive
plants.seasons: Low blue-green semi-evergreen mat much of the year; sheeted in pink-lavender bloom early-to-midsummer; foliage persists or bronzes through Front Range winters under snow
plants.wildlife: A superb pollinator plant — bees (honeybees and natives) and butterflies cover the nectar-rich summer flowers
plants.deer: Deer and rabbit resistant — both avoid the aromatic foliage
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to people and pets; the leaves and flowers are edible culinary herbs (a milder wild thyme)

edible.summary: A milder culinary thyme: the aromatic leaves and edible flowers season food and steep into tea, though the flavor is gentler and less prized than common cooking thyme.[1][2][3]
edible.parts: The tiny aromatic leaves and the small pink-lavender flowers are both edible.[3] Creeping (wild) thyme is a true culinary thyme — used as a seasoning herb and steeped into an herbal tea — but its scent and flavor are milder and more variable than common cooking thyme (Thymus vulgaris), which is the standard kitchen thyme, so it is used less often in the kitchen.[1][2] The edible lavender flowers make a pretty garnish and a fine tea.[3]
edible.preparation: Snip 2–3 in. of leafy stem tips and use the leaves fresh or dried; strip the tiny leaves from the woody stems before adding to food.[3][4] The flowers can be scattered fresh over salads or steeped along with the leaves for tea.[3] Flavor is strongest in summer, but the foliage can be harvested into late fall on the Front Range.[3]
edible.caution: Harvest only from plants you know are unsprayed (no pesticides or herbicides), and be sure of your identification — this is a low creeping Thymus, not a look-alike. As a culinary herb it is safe in ordinary food amounts; medicinal-strength preparations or thyme essential oil are concentrated and are a separate matter (avoid in pregnancy and for very young children). No part of the plant is toxic.[1][2][3]

plants.blurb: Old-fashioned dooryard shrub prized for plumes of intensely fragrant spring bloom.
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Suckering — forms a clonal thicket over decades
plants.seasons: Fragrant lilac/white panicles mid–late spring; green summer; little fall color; bare woody frame in winter
plants.wildlife: Butterflies, bees & hummingbirds at the fragrant blooms
plants.origin: Southeastern Europe — the Balkan Peninsula
plants.habitat: Rocky, scrubby hillsides and ravines on limestone slopes

edible.summary: Lilac flowers are edible and used as a fragrant garnish or flavoring, but the rest of the plant is not eaten.[3]
edible.parts: Only the flowers are used as food. The small lilac florets are edible and can be scattered over salads and desserts as a fragrant garnish, or steeped to flavor syrups, sugars, soft cheeses, ice cream, and pastries. Flavor varies by cultivar from nearly flavorless to a green, slightly bitter note to a true perfumed-lilac taste.[3]
edible.preparation: Pick freshly opened flowers, separate the tiny florets from the bitter green stems, and use them in small amounts — the flavor is perfumey and a little goes a long way. They are best fresh as a garnish, candied, or infused into sugar or syrup; over-using them leaves a soapy or bitter aftertaste. As with any edible flower, eat only blooms you know are unsprayed.[3]
edible.caution: Lilac is grown as an ornamental, not a food crop: only the flowers are eaten, and the leaves, bark, and woody parts are not used as food. The flowers can be bitter or astringent, so use sparingly. Don't confuse common lilac (Syringa vulgaris) with unrelated plants that share the name 'lilac' (e.g. Persian lilac / Melia azedarach, which is toxic). Eat only flowers from plants you are certain are Syringa and that have not been treated with pesticides.[3]

Late spring — fragrant panicles & heart-shaped leaves · © Jared Shorma (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Fall — characteristic heart-shaped (ovate-cordate) dark-green leaves, late season · © Nick Butcher (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

plants.commons: Common Lilac Syringa vulgaris 'Marechal Foch' Flowers.JPG

plants.blurb: The classic winter shrub — fiery red (or yellow 'Flaviramea') stems against snow.
plants.water: Moderate–high; streamside shrub — give regular summer water, not for dry zones
plants.spread: Running — suckers form colonies
plants.seasons: White flowers May–Jun; white berries; brilliant red/yellow winter stems
plants.wildlife: Pollinators; bird berries; Spring Azure host

edible.summary: The small white berries are intensely bitter and generally regarded as not worth eating; the inner bark was a traditional smoking mixture, not a food — treat this shrub as essentially inedible.[2][4]
edible.parts: The white-to-bluish berries were eaten by some Interior tribes — mostly mashed and mixed with sweeter fruits like saskatoon or chokecherry to make the bitterness tolerable — but on their own they are bitter and seedy, and they are not a recommended modern food. The dried inner bark and leaves were used in traditional smoking blends ('kinnikinnick'-style mixtures), which is a non-food use, not something to eat.[4]
edible.preparation: If eaten at all (not recommended), the berries were never used fresh on their own but pounded together with abundant sweeter berries and often dried for winter; the strong bitterness makes them unpalatable otherwise. The bark used in smoking mixes is dried and smoked, never ingested. For practical purposes treat the plant as ornamental/wildlife food rather than human food.[4]
edible.caution: The berries are bitter and astringent and are best regarded as inedible/wildlife food for people; do not eat them in quantity. More importantly, do not confuse red-twig dogwood's white berries with edible blue/black fruits of other shrubs — white berries in general are a warning sign and several white-berried plants are toxic. The inner bark is a traditional smoking material, not food. When in doubt, leave the berries for the birds, who eat them readily.[2][4]

plants.blurb: Fragrant pink wild rose with red hips that hang on all winter.
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Running — rhizomatous, forms thickets
plants.seasons: Pink fragrant flowers May–Jul; red hips & canes persist through winter
plants.wildlife: Xerces native/bumble bee value; hips feed birds & mammals
plants.deer: Young spring leaves browsed — only somewhat resistant

edible.card: Hips & petals (clean the hips' seed hairs)
edible.summary: The bright orange-red hips and the petals are edible and a well-documented native food, but the irritant seed hairs inside the hips must be removed before eating.[2][3]
edible.parts: Two parts are eaten: the fleshy outer rind of the hips (the ripe fruit) and the fragrant petals. Wood's-rose hips are a traditional Plains and Intermountain food — eaten fresh, dried, or cooked into jelly, syrup, and tea — and are rich in vitamin C; the petals make a fragrant garnish, tea, or flavoring. The seeds and the hairs surrounding them inside the hip are not eaten.[2][3]
edible.preparation: Harvest hips after the first frost softens and sweetens them. To use the rind safely, slice the hip open and scrape out the seeds and the bristly internal hairs, then cook the remaining flesh into jelly, syrup, or tea — or dry it. Straining cooked hip pulp through fine cloth is the reliable way to leave the irritant hairs behind. Petals can be used fresh. Avoid eating whole raw hips with the seed mass intact.[2][3]
edible.caution: The fine hairs surrounding the seeds inside each hip are a mechanical irritant — they can irritate the mouth, throat, and digestive tract (the same property that makes them a folk 'itching powder'), so always remove the seeds and strain out the hairs before eating. Rose hips and petals themselves are not poisonous, but be certain of identification: eat only hips you can confirm are a true rose (Rosa), and never eat material from plants treated with pesticides. The thorny canes are a physical hazard, not a toxic one.[2][3]

plants.blurb: Silvery aromatic mound crowned with violet flowers and lasting mauve bracts.
plants.water: Low; sharp drainage (esp. winter)
plants.spread: Clumping mound — stays put
plants.seasons: Silvery semi-evergreen foliage; violet-blue blooms w/ mauve bracts Jun–Nov; winter structure
plants.wildlife: Pollinator magnet
plants.origin: Southwestern US — California, Nevada, and Arizona mountains
plants.habitat: Dry rocky slopes and chaparral, 5,000–10,000 ft elevation

edible.summary: Mojave sage is a fragrant ornamental desert sage, not a culinary herb — it has no documented food use and should not be eaten or confused with cooking sage.[1][3]
edible.caution: Despite the name and aroma, Mojave sage (*Salvia pachyphylla*) is grown strictly as an ornamental and is NOT the culinary sage of the kitchen (that is *Salvia officinalis*). No authority documents it as a food plant; its only recorded traditional uses are medicinal and ceremonial. The foliage is rich in bitter aromatic terpenoid compounds (carnosol, carnosic acid and related diterpenes) and, like other ornamental salvias, can cause digestive upset if eaten in quantity. Treat it as inedible: enjoy the fragrance, but do not cook with or consume any part, and don't let its sage-like scent lead you to substitute it for the culinary herb.[1][3]

plants.blurb: An airy haze of lavender-blue over silver stems — tough, xeric and long-blooming.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Woody-based clump; can self-sow & creep by rhizomes — pick a compact cultivar & deadhead to limit it
plants.seasons: Silvery aromatic stems; lavender-blue haze midsummer–fall; silver winter structure (cut back hard in late winter)
plants.wildlife: Pollinator magnet — bees, butterflies, hummingbirds
plants.origin: Central to Southwest Asia — Afghanistan, Pakistan, Tibet, W. Himalayas
plants.habitat: Dry hills, steppes, and gravelly slopes in semi-arid montane zones

edible.summary: Russian sage is an aromatic ornamental, not the culinary sage — it is not a recognized food plant and is best treated as inedible.[2]
edible.caution: Despite its common name and sage-like scent, Russian sage (*Salvia yangii*, formerly *Perovskia atriplicifolia*) is NOT culinary sage (*Salvia officinalis*) and is not a documented food plant. Its only recorded uses are ornamental and traditional-medicinal (in parts of Asia it has been used to treat fevers and other ailments), not culinary. The strongly aromatic foliage is rich in camphor and other volatile terpenes and can irritate skin on handling and cause gastrointestinal upset if eaten in quantity. Treat all parts as inedible; do not substitute it for cooking sage, and keep children from nibbling the leaves or flowers.[2]

plants.blurb: Prairie bunchgrass turning mahogany-copper with fluffy winter seed heads.
plants.water: Low; very drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Clumping bunchgrass — stays put
plants.seasons: Blue-green summer; mahogany/copper fall; upright through winter
plants.wildlife: Skipper larval host; songbird seed

edible.summary: Little bluestem is a prairie forage grass, not a human food — it is grazed by livestock and wildlife but has no edible use for people.[2][3]
edible.caution: Little bluestem (*Schizachyrium scoparium*) is one of the most important native warm-season forage and rangeland grasses, valuable as cattle and wildlife forage and as cover and seed for birds — but it is not a human food plant and has no recognized edible parts. The tough, fibrous blades and seed are not eaten by people. It is not poisonous, but treat it as inedible: its role is ornamental, erosion control, and grazing forage, not the kitchen.[2][3]

plants.blurb: A native daisy — lavender-blue rays around a gold eye, blooming for months.
plants.water: Low–moderate; dry to moist
plants.spread: Clumping; spreads slowly by rhizomes into colonies
plants.seasons: Lavender-blue daisies Jun–Oct; very long bloom
plants.wildlife: Exceptional pollinator plant — bees, butterflies & more; sulphur butterfly nectar

edible.summary: Aspen fleabane is not a food plant — there is no established culinary use, though fleabanes have minor traditional medicinal use that is not eating.[3][4]
edible.caution: Fleabanes (Erigeron) are not a recognized edible green, and this species has no documented food use. Native American records list Erigeron speciosus only for minor non-food medicinal purposes (a poultice, a wash), which is not the same as eating it — do not treat traditional medicinal mention as a green light to consume it. It is not reported as seriously poisonous, but as with any aster-family plant, people sensitive to ragweed/daisy-family pollen can have allergic reactions, and the fine bristly seed 'fluff' is best not ingested. When in doubt, don't eat it.[3][4]

plants.blurb: Colorado's state flower — sky-blue-and-white spurred blooms nodding over ferny foliage.
plants.water: Moderate; moist, well-drained
plants.spread: Clumping & short-lived; reseeds gently to persist
plants.seasons: Blue & white long-spurred flowers late spring–early summer; foliage fades by midsummer
plants.wildlife: Hummingbird & hawk-moth nectar; bees
plants.toxic: Mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are poisonous if eaten in quantity; the flowers were historically nibbled for their sweet nectar in tiny amounts but are not a safe food

edible.card: Flowers in tiny amounts (seeds & roots toxic)
edible.summary: Columbine flowers were historically eaten in tiny amounts as a sweet, nectar-flavored garnish, but the mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are genuinely poisonous — this is not a casual edible.[2]
edible.parts: Only the flowers have any food history: several Indigenous North American peoples sucked nectar from columbine blooms or ate small numbers of flowers with spring greens, and the petals were once eaten in small quantities as a sweet, colorful salad garnish. The sweetness comes from the nectar in the spurs. The seeds and roots are not food.[2]
edible.preparation: If used at all, only a few fresh flowers as a garnish — never the seeds, roots, or leaves, and never in quantity. There is no safe way to prepare the seeds or roots for eating. Given the documented poisonings, the conservative approach on the Front Range is to treat columbine as ornamental and not eat it.[2]
edible.caution: The mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides (dhurrin and triglochinin) that are dangerous to the heart if consumed: documented cases include convulsions, respiratory distress, and heart failure after eating fresh foliage, and a child who ate a dozen flowers developed limb weakness, cyanosis, drowsiness, and pinpoint pupils. Keep seeds, roots, and foliage away from children and pets, and do not rely on the old 'flowers are safe' assumption — escalate to caution.[2][3]

plants.blurb: A tidy chartreuse dome that blazes red-orange in autumn.
plants.water: Low; sharp drainage
plants.spread: Clumping mound; can self-seed
plants.seasons: Sulfur-yellow bracts spring; green summer; red/orange/purple fall
plants.wildlife: Early-season interest; minor pollinator value
plants.toxic: Milky sap irritates skin/eyes — wear gloves; caution with kids/pets
plants.origin: Central and SE Europe to NW Turkey
plants.habitat: Rocky open hillsides, dry woodland edges, and scrub on limestone

edible.summary: Cushion spurge is poisonous — all parts are toxic if eaten and its milky sap is a skin and eye irritant. No part is edible.[3][4]
edible.caution: Like all Euphorbia, cushion spurge bleeds a toxic milky-white latex (the irritant compounds are diterpene esters) whenever a part is cut or broken. All parts — flowers, fruit, leaves, stems, and sap — are toxic if ingested, causing burning of the lips, mouth, and throat and, in worse cases, vomiting and diarrhea. Skin contact with the sap can cause redness, swelling, and blistering; eye contact is especially dangerous, causing redness, blurred vision, and potentially lasting corneal damage. Keep it away from children and pets and never put any part in the mouth.[3][4]
