plants.blurb: Tender tubers that explode into bold, geometric blooms from midsummer to frost.
plants.water: Moderate, consistent — not xeric
plants.spread: Clumping tuber — no self-sowing
plants.seasons: Prolific color midsummer–first frost. TENDER: not winter-hardy in Boulder — dig & store tubers, replant after last frost (~mid-May)
plants.wildlife: Open/single types feed bees; double 'dinnerplate' types offer little pollen
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic to pets if eaten
plants.origin: Mountains of Mexico and Central America (parent Dahlia spp.)
plants.habitat: Moist mountain meadows and woodland edges, 5,000–12,000 ft elevation

edible.summary: Dahlia tubers were historically a food (eaten by the Aztecs) and the petals are an edible garnish — but flavor and quality are highly variable, the plant is toxic to pets, and the sap can irritate skin, so it is a caution-level edible rather than a reliable vegetable.[3]
edible.parts: Two parts have a food history: the tubers, which the Aztecs cultivated and ate and which can be cooked (texture compared to water chestnut or jicama, flavor ranging from sweet to bitter and inconsistent between cultivars), and the petals, which are non-toxic to people and used fresh as a colorful garnish on salads and desserts. The leaves and stems are not eaten and are toxic to pets.[3]
edible.preparation: Eat tubers cooked, and only tubers you have grown yourself organically — retail/ornamental tubers are routinely treated with chemicals not intended for consumption. Expect variable, sometimes bitter flavor; sample a small amount of a given cultivar first. Use petals raw as a garnish, picking clean, unsprayed blooms. Wear gloves when digging or dividing and wash skin afterward.[3]
edible.caution: Dahlia is listed by the ASPCA as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses — eating the tubers, stems, or leaves causes mild gastrointestinal upset and mild dermatitis in animals — so keep tubers and trimmings away from pets. For people, the plant contains polyacetylene compounds that can cause skin irritation, and phototoxic dermatitis (a sun-triggered skin reaction) is possible after handling the sap, so wear gloves. Anyone with daisy-family (Asteraceae) allergies may also react. Treat retail tubers as ornamental only — never eat tubers that may have been chemically treated, and don't assume every cultivar tastes good.[3]

plants.blurb: Lavish, fragrant late-spring blooms on a clump that can outlive the gardener.
plants.water: Moderate; deep weekly soak in summer, not xeric
plants.spread: Clumping — long-lived & slow; resents being moved
plants.seasons: Big fragrant flowers late spring–early summer; lush green summer; bronze fall; dies to ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Bees (the ants on buds are harmless)
plants.toxic: Toxic to dogs, cats & horses if eaten (toxic principle paeonol); roots and other parts not for casual human consumption
plants.origin: Northeastern Asia — China, Mongolia, Siberia, and Korea
plants.habitat: Open meadows, scrub, and forest margins in temperate grassland and steppe

edible.summary: The peony plant — especially the roots — is toxic to pets and is not a casual human food; the petals have a narrow history of use as a tea/garnish in some cultures, but treat this plant as ornamental.[4]
edible.parts: Only the flower petals have any culinary tradition: in parts of East Asia and historically in Europe, peony petals were occasionally floated in tea, candied, or used as a garnish. The roots, foliage, stems, and seeds are not food, and the root in particular is the part associated with toxicity.[4]
edible.preparation: If petals are used at all, only a few from a known cultivar, well washed, as a garnish or steeped briefly — never the roots, seeds, or foliage. Because authoritative food guidance is thin and the plant is toxic to animals, the conservative choice on the Front Range is to grow peony purely for its flowers and not eat it.[4]
edible.caution: Peony (Paeonia) is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses; the toxic principle is paeonol (concentrated in the bark/roots), and ingestion causes vomiting, diarrhea, and depression. The roots and other plant parts should not be eaten by people without expert guidance, and the plant should be kept away from pets. When in doubt, do not eat it.[4]

plants.blurb: A low Rocky Mountain native forming spreading yellow-green mats.
plants.water: Low; xeric
plants.spread: Mat-forming — rhizomatous, spreads
plants.seasons: Yellow-green flowerheads; soft mounded foliage
plants.wildlife: Pollinator-friendly blooms
plants.toxic: Milky sap irritates skin/eyes — wear gloves; caution with kids/pets

edible.summary: Horned spurge is poisonous — like all Euphorbia its milky latex is a skin and eye irritant and the plant is toxic if eaten. No part is edible.[2][3]
edible.caution: Like all spurges, horned spurge bleeds a milky-white latex (the irritant compounds are diterpene esters) whenever a stem or leaf is broken. The sap is a skin and eye irritant — contact can cause mild to severe contact dermatitis, and eye contact can cause temporary to permanent corneal damage. Ingestion blisters the lips and mouth and can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Wear gloves (and eye protection when cutting) and keep the plant and its cut stems away from children and pets. Never put any part in the mouth.[2][3]

plants.blurb: Enormous crepe-paper blooms with inky centers — a showstopper, then it naps.
plants.water: Low–moderate; sharp drainage
plants.spread: Clumping; summer-dormant after bloom — pair with fillers
plants.seasons: Huge scarlet/pink/white flowers late spring–early summer; foliage dies back midsummer, returns in fall
plants.wildlife: Bees love the pollen
plants.toxic: Toxic — all parts contain isoquinoline alkaloids (including thebaine); the milky sap and unripe seedpods are poisonous if eaten. Not the breadseed poppy; not a food plant
plants.origin: Southwestern Asia — Turkey, Iran, and the Caucasus mountains
plants.habitat: Rocky hillsides, disturbed ground, and montane meadows

edible.summary: Oriental poppy is poisonous and is not a food plant — all parts, including the milky sap and seeds, contain alkaloids; it is not the culinary breadseed poppy.[3]
edible.caution: All parts of Papaver orientale are reported poisonous, including the bark, flowers, fruits, leaves, roots, seeds, and stems, and the milky juice (latex) is also toxic; the plant contains isoquinoline alkaloids (oriental poppy is notably rich in thebaine). Do not confuse it with the breadseed/culinary poppy (Papaver somniferum) whose ripe seeds are eaten — Oriental poppy seeds are not a culinary seed and should not be eaten. Keep the plant and its sap away from children and pets, and never ingest any part.[3]

plants.blurb: Upright wands of violet-blue in early summer — a tidy, deadhead-to-rebloom border salvia & pollinator favorite; not to be confused with noxious Mediterranean sage (Salvia aethiopis, a CO List A weed).
plants.water: Low–moderate; good drainage
plants.spread: Clumping; stays tidy — shear after the first flush to rebloom
plants.seasons: Violet-blue spikes late spring–summer, reblooming if deadheaded; aromatic gray-green foliage
plants.wildlife: Bees & butterflies throng the spikes
plants.origin: Europe to west-central Asia
plants.habitat: Dry meadows, steppes, and open rocky slopes

edible.summary: Ornamental meadow sage has no established food use and is not the culinary herb — do not cook with it; the kitchen "sage" is the unrelated Salvia officinalis.[2]
edible.caution: This is Salvia nemorosa, a strictly ornamental garden salvia — it is NOT the culinary cooking herb (that is common/garden sage, Salvia officinalis) and it should not be substituted for it in the kitchen. NC State's toolbox records no edible use and no toxicity rating for S. nemorosa; the only documented human use is external, traditional Turkish folk medicine where the crushed leaves were applied to stop bleeding. Treat it as a non-food ornamental: there is no documented safe culinary preparation, so do not eat the leaves, flowers, or any part. As with all aromatic salvias, handle is fine but don't confuse the showy garden plant with the herb of the same common name.[2]

plants.blurb: The classic fire-engine-red bee balm (eastern-US native) — a hummingbird magnet, though thirstier and more mildew-prone than Colorado's wild bergamot.
plants.water: Moderate; likes consistently moist soil
plants.spread: Runs by shallow rhizomes — can colonize; divide to keep in bounds
plants.seasons: Scarlet 'mop-head' blooms midsummer; seed heads & stems hold into winter
plants.wildlife: Ruby-throated hummingbirds, bumblebees & swallowtails
plants.origin: Eastern North America — Appalachians south to Georgia, north to New York
plants.habitat: Moist streambanks, rich woodland edges, and wet meadows

edible.summary: The fragrant leaves and scarlet flowers are edible — the classic 'Oswego tea' and a citrusy mint-family seasoning and garnish.[2][3]
edible.parts: The leaves and the red tubular flowers are the edible parts. Scarlet bee balm is the original 'Oswego tea,' brewed by the Oswego people and adopted by colonists; the leaves carry an oregano-to-citrus mint aroma and the flowers are sweet and showy. Native American records document Monarda didyma used as a beverage and food seasoning. Roots and woody stems are not eaten.[2][3]
edible.preparation: Steep fresh or dried leaves (and flowers) in hot water for tea; chop young leaves sparingly into salads, fruit dishes, or as an herb; and scatter the petals as an edible garnish. Harvest leaves around bloom for peak flavor. The taste is strong — use a light hand.[2][3]
edible.caution: Safe in normal culinary amounts. Like all the aromatic Monardas it is rich in essential oils (thymol/carvacrol-type compounds), so avoid concentrated medicinal doses, especially in pregnancy, and mint-family-sensitive people may react. Confirm identity by the square stem, opposite aromatic leaves, and red two-lipped whorled flowerheads.[2][3]

plants.blurb: The classic, well-behaved white daisy — the safe garden stand-in for noxious oxeye — with big golden-eyed blooms all summer.
plants.water: Moderate; well-drained (esp. in winter)
plants.spread: Clumping fibrous roots — stays put; pick sterile 'Becky' to skip reseeding
plants.seasons: White-and-gold daisies early–late summer with deadheading; tidy clump
plants.wildlife: Bees & butterflies; long-lasting cut flower
plants.origin: Garden hybrid; parent species from Europe (Leucanthemum spp.)
plants.habitat: Meadows, roadsides, and grasslands (parent species from W. & C. Europe)

edible.summary: Shasta daisy is an ornamental with no recognized food use — there is no documented safe edible part, so don't eat it.[1]
edible.caution: Leucanthemum × superbum is grown purely as a garden ornamental and cut flower; authoritative sources record no edible use, no documented safe culinary preparation, and no significant toxicity rating either. With no positive food record it should be treated as a non-food plant — do not eat the flowers, leaves, or any part. Note that it is the deliberate garden stand-in for the look-alike noxious oxeye daisy (Leucanthemum vulgare); the two are easily confused, so don't rely on look-alike foraging guidance. As with many Asteraceae, the foliage and pollen can occasionally irritate sensitive skin, so wear gloves if you handle large quantities.[1]

plants.blurb: Colorado's most common native lupine — silvery leaves, blue-violet spires.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Clumps; colonizes by seed
plants.seasons: Blue-violet spikes Jun–Aug; dies fully to ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Xerces bee value; butterfly host; nitrogen-fixer
plants.toxic: All parts (esp. seeds) toxic if eaten — caution with kids/pets/livestock

edible.summary: Silvery lupine is poisonous — all parts, especially the seeds and pods, contain toxic alkaloids; no part is a safe food.[3][4]
edible.caution: Like all lupines, silvery lupine contains quinolizidine alkaloids (and some species also anagyrine-type alkaloids) concentrated in the seeds and pods but present throughout the foliage. Ingestion can cause nausea, vomiting, trembling, and in quantity nervous-system depression; lupines are a well-documented livestock-poisoning plant and certain species/alkaloids are teratogenic ('crooked calf disease' in cattle that graze them during pregnancy). Do NOT eat any part — the pea-like green pods are especially tempting and dangerous to children. This is not the sweet edible lupin of cuisine, which is a different species processed by special leaching; treat all wild lupine as poison. Keep seeds, pods, and foliage away from kids, pets, and livestock.[3][4]

plants.blurb: Colorado-native bee balm — whimsical lavender 'jester's-hat' heads that hum with pollinators all summer.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Spreads slowly by rhizomes into clumps; reseeds
plants.seasons: Lavender-pink heads Jul–Sep; dried seed heads stand into winter
plants.wildlife: Pollinator magnet — native & bumble bees, butterflies, clearwing moths, hummingbirds; Monarch nectar

edible.summary: The aromatic leaves and flowers are a well-documented edible — used fresh or dried as a thyme-scented seasoning and as a mint-family tea — with a long Native American food and beverage history.[4][5]
edible.parts: The leaves and the showy lavender flowers are the edible parts. Both are sharply aromatic (the active compound is thymol, the oregano/thyme-scented oil), so they are used as a culinary herb and to brew an herbal tea — the plant is also called 'bee balm' or 'Oswego tea.' Native American records document wild bergamot used as a food flavoring, a cooked-food seasoning, and a beverage by many tribes; the roots and woody stems are not used as food.[4][5]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves and flowers fresh or dried. Snip young leaves into salads, soups, beans, or meat dishes as a thyme/oregano substitute, scatter the flowers as a garnish, and steep leaves or flowers (fresh or dried) in hot water for a fragrant tea. The flavor is strong, so use sparingly; harvest leaves just before or at bloom for the most oil.[4][5]
edible.caution: Safe in ordinary culinary amounts, but the high thymol content makes it a concentrated essential-oil herb: avoid medicinal/large quantities in pregnancy, and people sensitive to mint-family plants may react. Be sure of your identification — true Monarda has square stems, opposite aromatic leaves, and the distinctive tubular two-lipped 'jester's-hat' flower whorls; don't confuse non-flowering plants with unscented look-alikes.[4][5]

plants.blurb: Silky orange cups over blue-green lace — a cheerful, drought-proof self-sower (western US native).
plants.water: Low; xeric
plants.spread: Reseeds freely; easy to pull
plants.seasons: Orange (also cream/pink) cups spring–summer, opening with sun; fades in heat
plants.wildlife: Bees & other pollinators
plants.origin: Western US — California, SW Oregon to Baja California
plants.habitat: Open grassy slopes and disturbed sandy or rocky ground

edible.summary: Not a food plant — the whole plant contains opium-poppy-relative alkaloids and is used only as a mild herbal sedative tea or extract, best avoided in pregnancy and before driving.[3]
edible.parts: There is no part eaten as ordinary food. The aerial parts (and sometimes the whole plant) are dried and steeped as a traditional calming/sleep tea or taken as a tincture or capsule for anxiety and insomnia; this is herbal-medicine use, not culinary use. The bright flowers are sometimes added to bouquets but are not a salad green or vegetable.[3]
edible.preparation: If used at all, it is used as a measured herbal preparation — a tea (roughly 2–3.5 g of dried herb) or a standardized extract — not eaten in quantity. Treat it as a mild medicinal herb, not a food: keep amounts small, and consult a healthcare provider first because it can interact with sedatives and other medications.[3]
edible.caution: California poppy contains benzylisoquinoline alkaloids (protopine, allocryptopine, reticuline, californidine and relatives) in the same family as the opium poppy's; these are mildly sedative and can cause drowsiness and nausea, so do not drive or operate machinery after use, and they may produce a positive opiate result on a drug screen. Because it contains opiate-related alkaloids, it should be avoided during pregnancy and breastfeeding, and kept away from children and pets. It is not a casual edible — do not treat the foliage or flowers as a vegetable or eat large amounts.[3]

plants.blurb: Airy, daisy-like blooms on ferny stems — an effortless cottage-garden annual.
plants.water: Low–moderate; lean soil = more bloom
plants.spread: Self-sows freely; not invasive
plants.seasons: Pink/white/magenta blooms midsummer–frost; dies at first hard frost
plants.wildlife: Bees, butterflies & hoverflies; finches eat the seed
plants.origin: Mexico and southern United States
plants.habitat: Disturbed ground, roadsides, and dry open slopes

edible.summary: Garden cosmos is not a food plant and not an established edible — it is non-toxic to people and pets, but its petals are not a recognized culinary ingredient and the rest of the plant has no food use.[3]
edible.caution: Cosmos bipinnatus is listed as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses and is not poisonous to people, so an accidental nibble is not a safety emergency. But it is not a documented food: while the petals of a different species (Cosmos sulphureus) are sometimes used as a colorful garnish, C. bipinnatus is not an accepted edible, so it should not be eaten as a vegetable or salad green. As with any plant in the daisy (Asteraceae) family, people with ragweed/aster allergies may react to the pollen or foliage. When in doubt, treat it as ornamental only.[3]

plants.blurb: Colorado-native prairie annual — airy wands of pink-purple 'spider' flowers, alive with bees all summer, that reseed into drifting colonies. Still widely sold as Cleome serrulata.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Self-sows freely into colonies; not invasive
plants.seasons: Pink-purple spider-flower heads Jun–Sep; slender dangling seed pods follow; dies at first frost
plants.wildlife: Pollinator powerhouse — native & honey bees, butterflies, wasps, broad-tailed hummingbirds; seeds feed birds
plants.toxic: None of concern — foliage was historically cooked as a potherb ('Navajo spinach')

edible.summary: The young plants, leaves, and seeds are edible and were a well-documented Native American food, but they need thorough boiling to be palatable.[3][4]
edible.parts: The young, tender shoots and leaves are eaten as a cooked green (a potherb rich in vitamin A and calcium), and the seeds are edible — traditionally ground into meal for mush, gruel, or bread. This is an extensively documented food plant of Southwestern and Plains tribes: the Hopi boiled the young plants and ate them like spinach, while the Acoma, Isleta, and Jemez cooked the greens or ground the seeds.[3][4]
edible.preparation: Cook before eating. The greens are strong-smelling and bitter raw, so they are boiled — often with one or more changes of water — to make them palatable; the cooked greens can be eaten fresh or pressed into cakes and dried for storage. Seeds are cooked and ground into meal. It is not a raw salad green.[3][4]
edible.caution: No part is reported as poisonous to people, but the raw foliage is acrid and strong-smelling (livestock avoid it), which is why traditional preparation always cooks and often re-waters it. As with any wild green, be certain of your identification — the accepted name is now Cleomella serrulata (syn. Cleome serrulata / Peritoma serrulata); don't confuse it with ornamental spider-flower (Cleome / Tarenaya) or other look-alikes.[3]

plants.blurb: Jewel-toned 'snapping' flower spikes kids love — and bumblebees pry open; the real garden snapdragon, not the noxious look-alike 'wild snapdragon' (yellow toadflax) that sneaks into seed mixes.
plants.water: Moderate; ~1 in/week, water at the base
plants.spread: Clumping; reseeds; cool-season (often overwinters in mild spots)
plants.seasons: Spikes in nearly every color late spring–fall; best in cool weather
plants.wildlife: Bumblebee favorite; good cut flower
plants.origin: Mediterranean — SW Europe (Spain) and NW Africa (Morocco)
plants.habitat: Rocky cliffs, walls, and dry stony ground

edible.summary: Snapdragon flowers are edible and non-toxic — but bitter, so they are used as a colorful garnish rather than eaten in quantity.[2][3]
edible.parts: Only the flowers are used. They are listed as an edible flower, employed to add color to salads, desserts, and plates as a garnish. The leaves, stems, and seed capsules are not eaten. There is no significant culinary tradition of eating snapdragon beyond the ornamental garnish use.[2]
edible.preparation: Use the petals fresh, raw, as a garnish — pick clean, unsprayed blooms and scatter them over a dish for color. Because the flavor is distinctly bitter, they are not eaten by the handful or cooked into food; a few flowers for decoration is the normal use.[2]
edible.caution: Snapdragon is non-toxic to people and, per the ASPCA, non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, so it poses no poisoning hazard. The only practical cautions are ordinary ones: the flowers are bitter, so don't expect them to taste good, and eating a large quantity of any plant can cause mild stomach upset — discourage children from grazing on them. Use only flowers you know are free of pesticides or floral preservatives.[2][3]

plants.blurb: Upright annual frosted with white-margined leaves and bracts late season.
plants.water: Low–moderate; deep soak every 7–10 days, err on the dry side
plants.spread: Self-seeds freely
plants.seasons: Green early; white-edged variegated foliage & bracts late summer–fall
plants.wildlife: Bees visit the bracted blooms
plants.toxic: Milky sap irritates skin/eyes — wear gloves; caution with kids/pets

edible.summary: Snow-on-the-mountain is poisonous — like all euphorbias it bleeds a toxic milky latex that severely irritates skin and eyes; no part is edible.[3][4]
edible.caution: Every part exudes a milky-white latex (the irritant compounds are diterpene-ester toxins) whenever a stem, leaf, or bract is cut or broken. The sap causes painful skin redness, swelling, and blistering, and eye contact is especially dangerous — it can cause intense pain, swelling, and temporary or even lasting damage to vision. Ingestion irritates the mouth and gut, causing burning, vomiting, and diarrhea. Always wear gloves (and eye protection when cutting) and wash skin and tools afterward; keep children and pets from chewing any part. Honey from bees working the bracts can rarely carry trace irritants. Never put any part in the mouth.[3][4]

plants.blurb: Stiff-caned roses trained upright on a wall or trellis, many reblooming all summer.
plants.water: Moderate, regular
plants.spread: Needs tying to a support — not self-clinging
plants.seasons: Repeat flushes late spring–fall (by cultivar); hips & bare canes in winter
plants.wildlife: Single-flowered types feed bees; hips feed birds
plants.origin: Garden hybrids of Asian and European wild roses
plants.habitat: Derived from wild roses of woodland edges, hedgerows, and rocky ground

edible.summary: The rose hips and petals are edible and a noted source of vitamin C — but on ornamental garden roses, eat them only if you know the plant has not been treated with systemic pesticides not labeled for food, and clean the irritating hairs from inside the hips.[4][5]
edible.parts: Two parts are eaten: the **hips** (the red-orange fruit that ripens after the flowers fade) and the fresh **flower petals**. Rose hips are one of the best natural plant sources of vitamin C and are made into jelly, jam, syrup, and tea; the petals are eaten raw in salads or candied and are used to flavor and scent foods. These are well-documented foods across rose species.[1][4]
edible.preparation: For hips, harvest when fully colored (sweetest after the first frost), split them, and scrape out the seeds and the fine hairs surrounding them before cooking into jelly, syrup, or tea — the seed hairs are an irritant. Petals can be used fresh; trim off the bitter white heel at the petal base. Both are usually cooked or steeped rather than eaten in quantity raw.[1][4]
edible.caution: The fruit flesh and petals are not poisonous, but two cautions apply, especially on ornamental cultivars. First, the fine bristly hairs and seeds inside the hip are mechanically irritating to the mouth and digestive tract — remove them before eating (they are the classic ingredient in 'itching powder'). Second, and most important for garden roses: ornamental climbing-rose cultivars are frequently treated with systemic insecticides and fungicides that are NOT labeled for food crops, and those chemicals move into the hips and petals — never eat hips or petals from a plant that has been treated with any product not cleared for edibles. Be sure of identification (true Rosa); there is no toxic look-alike, but avoid roadside plants exposed to spray drift.[4][5]

plants.blurb: Classic large-flowered climber — sheets of velvety violet-purple on a sunny trellis.
plants.sun: Full sun–part shade — top in sun, roots shaded
plants.water: Moderate, consistent; keep roots cool & mulched
plants.spread: Twines by leaf-stalks; clumping crown — not invasive/running
plants.seasons: Showy blooms (spring or summer by pruning group); some give fluffy silver seedheads into fall/winter
plants.wildlife: Bee nectar
plants.toxic: Irritant/toxic if eaten; sap can irritate skin — caution with kids/pets. AVOID Clematis orientalis (yellow orange-peel clematis) — a CO List B noxious weed (all subspecies & cultivars)
plants.origin: Garden hybrid — Chinese (C. lanuginosa) × S.-European (C. viticella) parents
plants.habitat: Derived from woodland margins and rocky slopes in China and S. Europe

edible.summary: Garden clematis is poisonous — all parts are toxic if eaten and the sap irritates skin. No part is edible.[3][4]
edible.caution: Like other Clematis, 'Jackmanii' contains protoanemonin, an irritant compound released when the plant tissue is bruised or chewed. All parts are toxic — the leaves and sap are listed as the poison parts, and ingestion can cause intense burning of the mouth, severe mouth pain and ulcers, salivation, vomiting, and diarrhea; in larger amounts, dizziness, confusion, fainting, and convulsions are possible (severe poisoning is rare because the acrid, bitter taste discourages eating much). The same compound makes the fresh sap a skin and mucous-membrane irritant — handling cut stems can cause redness and a burning sensation, so wear gloves when pruning. It is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses as well as people; keep children and pets from chewing it. Note that drying largely destroys protoanemonin, but never rely on that — treat the whole plant as not for consumption.[3][4]

plants.blurb: Vigorous, flexible-caned roses that 'wander' over fences & arbors in one big flush — plant named cultivars, not invasive wild multiflora rose.
plants.water: Moderate; deep weekly soak, more drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Long arching canes scramble & self-layer — give it room
plants.seasons: One lavish early-summer flush (most types); some hips & arching canes in winter
plants.wildlife: Clustered single blooms draw bees; hips feed birds
plants.origin: Garden hybrids of Asian and European wild roses
plants.habitat: Derived from wild roses of woodland edges, hedgerows, and rocky ground

edible.summary: The rose hips and petals are edible and rich in vitamin C — but on ornamental garden ramblers, eat them only if the plant has not been treated with non-food-labeled pesticides, and clean the irritating seed hairs from inside the hips.[4][5]
edible.parts: The **hips** (the red-orange fruit set after bloom) and the fresh **flower petals** are the edible parts. Rose hips are among the best natural plant sources of vitamin C and are made into jelly, jam, syrup, and tea; petals are eaten raw in salads, candied, or used to flavor and scent foods. These uses are documented across rose species.[1][4]
edible.preparation: Harvest hips when fully colored (sweeter after frost), split them, and scrape out the seeds and the fine hairs around them before cooking into jelly, syrup, or tea — the seed hairs are an irritant. Use petals fresh, trimming off the bitter white base. Both are normally cooked or steeped rather than eaten raw in quantity.[1][4]
edible.caution: The flesh and petals are not poisonous, but two cautions apply on ornamental ramblers. First, the bristly hairs and seeds inside the hip mechanically irritate the mouth and gut (the classic 'itching powder') and must be removed before eating. Second and most important: ornamental rambling-rose cultivars are often treated with systemic insecticides and fungicides NOT labeled for food crops, and those residues move into the hips and petals — never eat hips or petals from a plant treated with any product not cleared for edibles. Confirm the plant is a true Rosa; there is no poisonous look-alike, but avoid roadside plants exposed to spray drift.[4][5]

plants.blurb: Intensely clove-scented, fringed pink flowers hover above a silver-blue evergreen mat all spring — a drought-tough, drainage-loving rock-garden gem that positively thrives in the Front Range's lean alkaline soil.
plants.water: Low–medium; tolerates brief dry spells once established; never let roots sit wet
plants.spread: Slowly spreading mat; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Bright pink fringed blooms May–Jun; occasional rebloom all summer with deadheading; evergreen blue-gray foliage persists through winter
plants.wildlife: Attracts butterflies and bees; deer- and rabbit-resistant
plants.toxic: Saponins in foliage and stems may cause mild GI upset if ingested; contact dermatitis possible with sensitive skin
plants.origin: Western and central Europe, from Britain to the Balkans
plants.habitat: Sunny limestone cliffs, rocky grassland, and well-drained chalk slopes

edible.summary: The fringed petals are edible — a classic clove-scented edible flower for salads, desserts, and crystallizing — but trim off the bitter white petal base first, and do not eat the leaves or stems.[4]
edible.parts: Only the flower petals are eaten. Dianthus petals (the whole Dianthus genus — pinks and carnations) are a long-established edible flower, prized for a sweet, spicy, clove-like flavor. The bitter, narrow white heel at the base of each petal is not edible and is removed before use. The leaves and stems are not food.[4]
edible.preparation: Pull the petals from the calyx, snip away the bitter white base of each petal, then use the colored portion fresh in salads, scattered over desserts, or crystallized with sugar to decorate cakes. Use only flowers grown without pesticides, and be certain of the identification before eating any flower.[4]
edible.caution: Eat the petals only — the leaves and stems contain triterpenoid saponins and can cause mild gastrointestinal upset (including vomiting) if eaten and mild contact dermatitis on sensitive skin. Dianthus (carnations/pinks) is listed as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses by the ASPCA (mild GI signs and dermatitis), so keep pets from grazing it. Always remove the bitter white petal base, and use only pesticide-free, correctly identified flowers.[2][4][5]

plants.blurb: Sky-blue, tissue-paper flowers open at sunrise and drop by noon — a long-season, drought-tough Colorado native that reseeds into airy, naturalistic drifts.
plants.water: Very low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Self-sows readily; not invasive — volunteers are welcome and easy to thin
plants.seasons: Delicate sky-blue blooms May–Jul, with fresh flowers opening each morning; buff seed capsules ripen Jul–Aug; fine blue-green stems remain upright into fall
plants.wildlife: Nectar for native bees and butterflies; seeds taken by birds and small mammals in fall and winter; larval host for some blues (Lycaenidae)
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant due to tough, fibrous stems

edible.summary: The ripe seeds are edible and nutritious like commercial flax — but only after cooking/roasting, because raw seeds and the foliage contain cyanogenic glycosides that release cyanide; eat in moderation.[4][5]
edible.parts: The small ripe seeds are the food part — like cultivated flax (Linum usitatissimum) they are rich in oil and were a documented Native American food, parched and ground into meal or added to other foods. The blue flowers, fine leaves, and stems are not eaten as food.[4][5]
edible.preparation: Always cook the seeds — roast, parch, or boil them; traditional use parched the seeds and ground them into a meal. Heat drives off the volatile cyanide from the cyanogenic glycosides and makes the seed safe and palatable. Eat in moderation rather than in large raw quantities, exactly as is advised for store flaxseed.[4][5]
edible.caution: Raw seeds, immature seed capsules, and the foliage contain cyanogenic glycosides (linamarin-type) that release hydrogen cyanide (HCN) when the tissue is chewed, crushed, or wetted — the same chemistry that makes wilted prunus leaves a livestock hazard. Cooking destroys most of it, so never eat the raw seeds in quantity and don't browse the foliage. Choose the native Linum lewisii and be sure of your ID; this is not a salad green.[4][5]

plants.blurb: Stately, sweetly fragrant domed panicles in pink, white, or lavender crown 3-foot stems all summer — a classic butterfly magnet, though powdery-mildew-resistant cultivars are essential in Boulder's semi-arid climate.
plants.water: Medium; consistent moisture, not drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Clumping; spreads slowly by crown; self-seeds but not aggressively (deadhead to limit)
plants.seasons: Large fragrant flower panicles Jul–Sep in pink, white, lavender, or red; foliage dies back to the ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Major butterfly and sphinx moth magnet; hummingbirds visit; bees work the flowers
plants.deer: Moderately deer-resistant; not a top choice but usually passed over
plants.origin: Eastern and central United States
plants.habitat: Moist open woods, woodland edges, and stream banks

edible.summary: Garden phlox is an ornamental with no recognized food use; though its flowers are sometimes shown as a decorative garnish, there is no real edible record, so don't rely on eating it.[2]
edible.caution: Phlox paniculata is grown purely as an ornamental. Authoritative sources list no toxicity rating and no established edible use; the only documented human use is traditional/medicinal (a leaf extract historically used as a laxative and to treat boils), not food. The flowers occasionally appear on casual lists of edible garnish flowers, but with no authoritative confirmation and no documented safe preparation, treat the plant as a non-food ornamental and do not eat the leaves, stems, or roots. As always, be certain of identification — don't confuse garden phlox with the unrelated, toxic-when-handled plants sometimes mistaken for it.[2]

plants.blurb: Jupiter's beard — dense rose-red flower clusters bloom relentlessly all summer above glaucous blue-gray foliage, thriving in the lean alkaline soil and rock walls where most plants struggle.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows prolifically — deadhead to control (manageable in CO's dry climate)
plants.seasons: Dense rose-red (or pink or white) flower clusters May through first frost; blue-gray foliage; dies back in winter
plants.wildlife: Hummingbirds, butterflies, and long-tongued bees work the flowers heavily
plants.origin: Mediterranean Europe — southern France, Iberia, and the western Mediterranean
plants.habitat: Rocky slopes, old walls, and sun-baked limestone cliffs

edible.summary: The young leaves and the roots of red valerian are edible (leaves raw or boiled, roots in soups), but they are bitter, of minor culinary value, and must not be confused with the unrelated medicinal valerian.[2]
edible.parts: The leaves and the roots are the edible parts. The young leaves can be eaten raw in salads or lightly boiled as a cooked green, and the roots have been used to flavor soups. This is a minor, historical pot-herb use rather than a staple food — the leaves are noticeably bitter, so they are usually used sparingly and young.[2]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves young, when they are least bitter: a few raw in a salad, or briefly boiled to soften the bitterness, like a mild cooked green. The roots are simmered into soups for flavor rather than eaten as a vegetable. Because the flavor is bitter and the food value low, treat it as an occasional garnish or seasoning, not a bulk green.[2]
edible.caution: Do not confuse red valerian (Centranthus ruber, "Jupiter's beard") with medicinal/true valerian (Valeriana officinalis) — they are different plants in the same family, and the sedative herb used for valerian preparations is V. officinalis, NOT this ornamental; don't use Centranthus as a substitute. No part of Centranthus ruber is reported as poisonous, and authoritative sources record no toxicity rating, but as with any wild or garden green, be certain of your identification before eating and start with small amounts, since the leaves are bitter and the documented food use is limited.[2]

plants.blurb: Clouds of tiny forget-me-not-blue flowers drift above heart-shaped, often silver-splashed leaves each spring — the shade garden's most reliable early-season bloomer, provided it gets consistent moisture and protection from Boulder's afternoon sun.
plants.water: Medium; consistent moisture required — not drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Slowly spreading by rhizomes; non-invasive ground cover
plants.seasons: Sprays of tiny blue flowers Apr–May; large ornamental heart-shaped leaves (often silver-marked) provide foliage interest spring through fall; dies back in winter
plants.wildlife: Small bees and pollinators visit the spring flowers; deer- and rabbit-resistant
plants.origin: Caucasus region — western Caucasus and adjacent northeastern Turkey
plants.habitat: Moist mountain forests and shaded woodland margins

edible.summary: Brunnera is an ornamental with no established food use — it is not eaten, and as a member of the borage family it is best treated as not for consumption.[2][3]
edible.caution: No part of Brunnera macrophylla is a recognized food, and it should not be eaten. It belongs to the borage family (Boraginaceae), in which nearly all genera produce hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids — compounds documented across the family that can damage the liver with repeated exposure and are why borage-family plants are not safe for regular consumption. Specific alkaloid data for Brunnera itself is limited, which is all the more reason to be conservative: do not use it as food or herbal tea. It is not, however, reported as acutely poisonous on contact, and deer and rabbits leave it alone.[2][3]

plants.blurb: A non-stop bloomer — lavender-blue pincushion flowers float on wiry stems from late spring to frost, drawing butterflies and bees all season long to this compact, easy-care Front Range perennial.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; slowly expands but not aggressive
plants.seasons: Mound of ferny gray-green foliage spring through fall; lavender-blue flowers May–Oct; dies back to ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Outstanding butterfly and bee plant; a top nectar source for monarchs, swallowtails, and native bees
plants.origin: Mediterranean Europe, W. Asia, and Africa (widespread Old World)
plants.habitat: Dry grasslands, scrub, and rocky ground on calcareous soils

edible.summary: Pincushion flower is an ornamental with no established food use — not notably poisonous, but there is no documented edible part, so don't eat it.[2]
edible.caution: Scabiosa columbaria is grown strictly as an ornamental and pollinator plant. Authoritative sources record no toxicity rating and no established culinary use — it is not a recognized edible herb and has no documented safe edible preparation. With no positive food record, treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots. (Historically the genus was used externally as a folk remedy for skin complaints such as scabies and itching — the source of the name "scabious" — but that is topical, not food use.)[2]

plants.blurb: Showy Russell-hybrid spires in every color from white to deep violet rise above bold palmate foliage in late spring — stunning where conditions suit them, though Front Range gardeners should plan for their preferred cool, slightly acidic conditions.
plants.water: Moderate; prefers consistent moisture
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows if not deadheaded — deadhead to prevent spread near wildlands
plants.seasons: Bold palmate foliage from early spring; tall flower spires May–July; second flush possible if deadheaded promptly; foliage can look tatty by late summer in heat
plants.wildlife: Bumblebees, honeybees, and long-tongued bees work the flowers; larval host plant for some native skippers
plants.toxic: ALL PARTS TOXIC — seeds and foliage contain quinolizidine alkaloids harmful to people, livestock, and pets if ingested; keep away from children and animals
plants.origin: Western North America — Pacific coast from British Columbia to California (parent L. polyphyllus)
plants.habitat: Moist meadows, stream banks, and open forest edges in Pacific mountain ranges

edible.summary: Garden (Russell) lupine is poisonous — its seeds and foliage contain toxic alkaloids; no part is a safe food.[3][4]
edible.caution: Russell hybrid lupines (Lupinus polyphyllus) contain quinolizidine alkaloids throughout the plant, most concentrated in the seeds and green pods. Eating them can cause nausea, vomiting, and nervous-system effects; lupines are a recognized livestock poison and some lupine alkaloids are teratogenic. These are NOT the sweet edible 'lupini' beans of cuisine (a specially bred, thoroughly leached species) — never eat the seeds, pods, or leaves of an ornamental garden lupine. The pea-like pods are an attractive nuisance to children; keep all parts away from kids, pets, and grazing animals.[3][4]

plants.blurb: A bold native riparian shrub — pairs of yellow tubular flowers give way to glossy black twin berries cradled in showy deep-red bracts, a hummingbird and songbird magnet from flower to fruit.
plants.water: Moderate to high; needs consistent moisture — ideal near seeps, streams, or irrigated beds
plants.spread: Slowly spreading by suckers; not invasive — stays in a clump-to-colony over time
plants.seasons: Paired yellow flowers May–Jul; shiny black twin berries in dark-red bracts Jul–Sep; yellow fall foliage; bare architectural stems in winter
plants.wildlife: Hummingbirds are the primary pollinator; bumble bees visit flowers; songbirds (thrushes, grosbeaks, robins) consume ripe berries; larval host for Gillette's checkerspot butterfly
plants.deer: Moderately deer-resistant; not preferred browse, though new growth may be sampled
plants.toxic: Berries mildly toxic to humans if eaten in quantity; not known to harm birds or wildlife

edible.summary: The shiny black twin berries are best regarded with caution — their edibility is genuinely disputed: some tribes ate them sparingly, others considered them poisonous or used them as an emetic, so treat them as inedible unless you have expert local guidance.[2][3]
edible.parts: The only part ever used as food is the paired black berry, and even that is contested. A few Northwest and Interior peoples ate the berries occasionally, but the fruit is bitter, and several tribes (e.g. Makah, Okanagan-Colville) considered the berries poisonous, while others used a berry infusion as a deliberate cathartic and emetic to purge the body. The leaves and other parts are not food. Given this conflicting record, twinberry is not a fruit to forage casually.[3]
edible.preparation: There is no safe, generally recommended preparation that makes these berries a reliable food — the traditional record is too divided, and the most consistent reported effect is emetic (vomiting/purging). The conservative course is to leave the berries for the songbirds, who eat them readily, rather than attempting to eat them yourself.[2][3]
edible.caution: The black berries are widely regarded as inedible-to-mildly-toxic for people: documented tribal uses include treating them as poisonous and using a berry infusion specifically as an emetic and cathartic, so eating them in any quantity can cause nausea, vomiting, and digestive upset. Do not eat the berries; keep children from sampling them. They are not known to harm birds or wildlife, which consume them freely — wildlife tolerance does not imply human safety.[2][3]

plants.blurb: One of the most powerfully fragrant native shrubs on the Front Range — billowy clusters of pure-white four-petaled flowers with citrus-sweet scent blanket the arching branches in early summer.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; slowly suckering with age — well-behaved, not invasive
plants.seasons: Profuse white fragrant flowers Jun–Jul; lush green foliage summer; good yellow fall color; arching vase-shaped stems provide winter structure
plants.wildlife: White flowers attract native bees, bumble bees, and butterflies; dense branching offers nesting cover for songbirds

edible.summary: Mock orange is an ornamental shrub, not a food plant — no part is eaten, though it is not notably poisonous.[2]
edible.caution: Mock orange is grown purely for its fragrant flowers and garden value; it has no documented edible parts, and despite the citrus-like scent the flowers and fruit are not a substitute for orange or any edible citrus. The dry woody seed capsules and the foliage are not food. There is no significant toxicity reported, but there is also no reason to eat any part of it — treat it as strictly ornamental. (Do not confuse it with unrelated 'mock orange' / Pittosporum or with true citrus.)[2]

plants.blurb: A large, dramatic shrub famed for billowing clouds of feathery pink-to-purple seed plumes in summer and some of the most vivid orange-to-scarlet fall color of any Front Range shrub — especially striking in purple-leaf cultivars like 'Royal Purple'.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established — avoid consistently wet soils
plants.spread: Clumping, multi-stemmed; does not sucker or self-sow aggressively
plants.seasons: 'Smoke' plumes of fluffy hairs Jun–Sep; vivid orange-red-scarlet fall color Sep–Oct; bold multi-stem framework in winter
plants.wildlife: Flowers attract native bees and small pollinators; provides cover for birds; fall berries sparingly eaten by birds
plants.deer: Deer-resistant; not preferred browse, though young plants may be sampled
plants.toxic: Resinous sap may cause contact dermatitis, especially for people sensitive to poison ivy (same family, Anacardiaceae); wear gloves when pruning
plants.origin: Southern Europe to central China — a vast Eurasian range
plants.habitat: Rocky hillsides, scrub, and dry open woodland on calcareous soils

edible.summary: Smoke tree is an ornamental shrub with no edible parts, and its resinous sap can cause skin irritation — do not eat any part of it.[2]
edible.caution: Smoke tree (Cotinus coggygria) belongs to the cashew/sumac family (Anacardiaceae), the same family as poison ivy. It has no documented food use, and contact with its resinous sap can cause contact dermatitis — redness, rash, and irritation — especially in people sensitive to poison ivy; wear gloves when pruning. No part (leaves, the feathery 'smoke' plumes, or the small dry fruits) should be eaten. Keep it away from anyone who might sample it, and treat it strictly as ornamental.[2]

plants.blurb: A bulletproof native fruiting shrub — clouds of white spring stars give way to sweet, blueberry-like blue-purple pomes that bake into the best pies on the High Plains, on a tough thicket-former that shrugs off cold, drought, and alkaline clay.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade; fruits best in full sun
plants.water: Low to moderate once established; drought-tolerant but sweetest fruit with occasional deep summer water
plants.spread: Slowly thicket-forming by rhizomes and root suckers — colonizes over time but easily kept in bounds
plants.seasons: White flower clusters Apr–May; blue-purple edible pomes ripen Jun–Jul; orange-red fall foliage; bare grey multi-stem framework in winter
plants.wildlife: Spring flowers feed native bees and butterflies (larval host for elfins and orange-tips); fruit is a songbird and small-mammal favorite
plants.deer: Readily browsed by deer and other big game, especially the twigs in winter — protect young plants in deer country
plants.toxic: Ripe fruit is edible and prized; like all rosaceous stone/pome fruit the seeds, leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic glycosides and are toxic in quantity to people and livestock

edible.summary: The ripe blue-purple pomes are a choice wild edible — sweet, blueberry-like with a hint of almond — eaten fresh or baked; the seeds, leaves, and twigs are not food.[1][3]
edible.parts: The edible part is the ripe pome — the soft, sweet, blue-purple to nearly black fruit that ripens June–July. It's often compared to blueberry but with a slight apple flavor, and is high in iron, potassium, and manganese. Indigenous peoples ate the fruit fresh, dried it like raisins, and pounded it into pemmican; it's classic pie, jelly, syrup, and muffin fruit. The leaves, twigs, bark, and seeds are not eaten as food.[1][2][3]
edible.preparation: Eat the ripe fruit fresh off the bush, or cook it into pies, muffins, jams, syrups, and sauces; it also dries well for winter storage. Ordinary use of the soft ripe flesh is safe, but don't deliberately crush, grind, or eat quantities of the hard seeds, and don't brew 'tea' from the leaves or twigs.[1][3]
edible.caution: Like all rosaceous stone- and pome-fruit (Prunus, Amelanchier, etc.), the seeds, leaves, and especially young twigs contain cyanogenic glycosides that release hydrogen cyanide (HCN); the glycosides are most concentrated in young twigs and least in older leaves. The ripe FLESH is the edible part. Do not brew the leaves/twigs or eat quantities of crushed seed, and never let livestock graze it as sole forage — a diet consisting solely of serviceberry can be fatal to ruminants, and CSU notes the plant is toxic to livestock. Wilted foliage is the highest-risk material. Keep children from chewing twigs or leaves.[2][3]

plants.blurb: A tough, thornless native currant that wreaths itself in clove-scented golden trumpets each spring, then ripens sweet amber-to-black berries — a hummingbird magnet that thrives in alkaline clay from streambank to dry slope.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade; best flowering and fruiting in full sun
plants.water: Low to moderate; very adaptable from streamside moisture to drought once established
plants.spread: Suckers slowly from rhizomes into a loose, multi-stemmed clump or thicket — easily contained
plants.seasons: Clove-scented golden flowers Apr–Jun; berries ripen amber-red to black through summer; yellow-to-red fall foliage; bare arching stems in winter
plants.wildlife: Tubular spring flowers are a top early nectar source for hummingbirds, native bees, and butterflies; songbirds, small mammals, and bears eat the ripe berries
plants.deer: Browsed by deer and rabbits, especially young growth, but tough and resprouts readily — not a preferred browse

edible.summary: The ripe berries — coloring amber-red to black — are a genuine wild edible, eaten fresh or cooked into jam, jelly, syrup, and pies; sweet, mild, and very unlike a European black currant.[1][2]
edible.parts: The edible part is the ripe berry, which ripens unevenly through summer from amber-red and orange to a final bluish-black. The flavor is mild, sweet, and pleasant. Golden currant was an important food of Plains peoples, who ate the fruit fresh or dried it and mixed it into pemmican with dried buffalo or venison. The leaves and stems are not used as food.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Eat fully ripe berries fresh, or cook them into jam, jelly, syrup, sauce, and pies; they also dry well for winter use. Pick when berries are fully colored and soft — underripe currants are tart and astringent. No special detoxification is required for the ripe fruit.[1][2]
edible.caution: Eat only fully ripe berries; underripe fruit is sharply tart and astringent. No significant toxicity is reported for the fruit of golden currant, but as with any wild edible, identify the plant with certainty (note the thornless stems, three-lobed maple-like leaves, and clove-scented golden tubular flowers) and don't confuse it with unrelated shrubs. The fruit is the food; the leaves and stems are not eaten.[1][2]

plants.origin: Northern China, Korea, and the Himalayas
plants.habitat: Mountain slopes and forest margins of cold, semi-arid interior Asia
plants.blurb: An iron-hardy Asian bush cherry that froths pink-budded white over its downy branches in earliest spring, then loads them with sweet-tart scarlet cherries — tough to -40°F, drought-tolerant, and right at home in alkaline Front Range clay.
plants.sun: Full sun for heaviest fruit; tolerates part shade
plants.water: Low; very drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Dense, twiggy, multi-stemmed shrub; not running, but self-seeds modestly without being invasive
plants.seasons: Pink-budded white flowers in earliest spring; scarlet cherries ripen Jun–Jul; downy green summer foliage; bare twiggy framework in winter
plants.wildlife: Very early flowers feed emerging bees; birds eagerly strip the ripe cherries; dense form gives nesting and shelter cover
plants.deer: Browsed by deer and rabbits, especially young plants; protect when establishing
plants.toxic: Ripe fruit is edible and sweet-tart; the pits, leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic glycosides and are toxic to people, dogs, cats, and horses — eat the flesh only

edible.card: Ripe cherry flesh (fresh, jam, jelly, pie)
edible.summary: The ripe red cherries are edible and sweet-tart — good fresh and in jam, jelly, and pies — but the pits, leaves, and twigs are cyanogenic and must not be eaten.[1][2][5]
edible.parts: The edible part is the ripe cherry flesh — bright red, about ½ in across, ranging from sweet to tart, ripening June–July. Use it fresh or cooked in jam, jelly, preserves, pies, and juice. Each fruit holds a single large pit (stone), which is NOT food: like other Prunus stones it is cyanogenic. The leaves, twigs, and bark are likewise not edible.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Eat the ripe flesh fresh, or cook it into jams, jellies, preserves, pies, and juice. Pit the fruit and discard the stones rather than crushing or grinding them; never crack the pits to eat the kernels. Do not brew tea from the leaves or twigs. Ordinary cooking of the pitted flesh is safe.[1][2]
edible.caution: Like all Prunus (cherries, plums, almonds), the pits/seeds, leaves, and twigs contain cyanogenic glycosides that release hydrogen cyanide (HCN); the toxin is especially concentrated in wilting leaves and in the seed kernels. The ripe FLESH is the edible part — discard every pit and do not crack or eat the kernels. ASPCA lists cherry (Prunus) stems, leaves, and seeds as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, causing difficulty breathing and shock; keep pruned foliage and pits away from pets and livestock, and keep children from swallowing or crushing the pits.[1][5]

plants.blurb: CSU's go-to red currant — pendant strigs of glossy, translucent ruby berries that glow on the bush and cook into jewel-bright jelly.
plants.water: Moderate; needs steady summer moisture for good fruit size
plants.spread: Clumping multi-cane shrub; does not run or self-sow
plants.seasons: Greenish-yellow flower racemes Apr–May; translucent red berry strigs ripen in July; green summer foliage; bare cane frame in winter
plants.wildlife: Bees work the spring flowers; songbirds eat the berries if left unnetted
plants.origin: Western Europe
plants.habitat: Damp woods and stream margins on fertile soils

edible.summary: The translucent red berries are edible and excellent — tart fresh, superb cooked into jelly, syrup, and sauce; no part of the plant is poisonous.[1][2]
edible.parts: The fruit is the part eaten: small, glossy, translucent red berries borne in pendant clusters (strigs), ripening in July. They are pleasantly tart fresh and famously good cooked, being naturally high in pectin so they set into bright jelly, jam, syrup, cordial, and sauce with ease. The leaves, stems, and roots are not used as food.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Eat fully ripe berries fresh out of hand or strip them from the strigs for cooking. They are most often cooked with sugar into jelly, jam, syrup, and sauce, or added to summer puddings and baked goods; their high pectin also helps set lower-pectin fruits. Wash before use and pick out stems and any leaves.[1][2]
edible.caution: Red currant has no toxic parts — the berries, leaves, and stems are not poisonous, so it is safe to grow around children and pets. The only real cautions are ordinary ones: the raw fruit is quite tart, so most people prefer it cooked and sweetened, and as with any garden fruit, eat only berries from plants you have not sprayed with pesticides. Note that the bush is an alternate host for white pine blister rust, a tree disease — that is a concern for nearby white pines, not for eating the fruit.[3][1]

plants.blurb: A compact bush of intensely aromatic, blackcurrant-scented foliage and matte-black berries that cook into a deep, musky, incomparable jam and cordial.
plants.water: Moderate to high; intolerant of drought, wants steady moisture
plants.spread: Compact clumping bush; does not run or self-sow
plants.seasons: Pendant flower racemes in April; matte black berry clusters ripen June–July; aromatic lobed summer foliage; bare cane frame in winter
plants.wildlife: Bees visit the spring flowers; birds strip the ripe fruit if unnetted
plants.deer: Aromatic foliage is not a favored browse; not reliably deer-proof
plants.origin: Central & northern Europe and northern Asia
plants.habitat: Damp, fertile woods and stream sides on moist ground

edible.summary: The aromatic black berries are edible and prized — sharp and musky fresh, superb cooked into jam, cordial, syrup, and pie; the leaves are also used for tea, and no part is poisonous.[1][2]
edible.parts: The fruit is the part eaten: matte-black berries in pendant clusters, ripening June–July, with an intense, musky, distinctive flavor. They are very high in vitamin C and pectin. Fresh they are sharp and aromatic; cooked they make exceptional jam, jelly, cordial, syrup, sauce, and pie filling. The young leaves are sometimes brewed as a herbal tea. No part of the plant is poisonous.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Most of the crop is cooked: simmered with sugar into jam, jelly, syrup, and cordial, baked into pies and puddings, or steeped for tea (leaves). The fresh berries are edible but tart and strongly flavored, so they are usually sweetened. Wash and de-stem before use, and eat only fruit from bushes you have not sprayed.[1][2]
edible.caution: Black currant has no toxic parts — berries and leaves are both safe — so the cautions are ordinary ones: the raw fruit is intensely sharp and best cooked and sweetened, and you should eat only unsprayed fruit. The bush's notable hazard is botanical, not dietary: black currant is the strongest alternate host of any Ribes for white pine blister rust, a disease lethal to five-needle white pines, so it should be sited away from such pines and (where required) grown only as a resistant cultivar like 'Ben Sarek' — a tree-health concern, not a food-safety one.[2][4]

plants.blurb: A Finnish-bred dessert gooseberry: spiny arching canes hung with large dark-red berries that have tart skin over surprisingly sweet flesh, good enough to eat off the bush.
plants.water: Moderate; steady summer moisture for plump fruit
plants.spread: Clumping spiny shrub; does not run or self-sow
plants.seasons: Greenish-yellow bell flowers in April; large dark-red berries ripen in July; lobed green summer foliage; bare thorny cane frame in winter
plants.wildlife: Bees work the early flowers; the thorny canes give birds cover
plants.deer: Spiny stems deter heavy browsing; not a deer favorite
plants.origin: Europe, northwest Africa & southwest Asia
plants.habitat: Rocky forests, copses and hedgerows

edible.summary: The large dark-red berries are edible and, in 'Hinnomäki Red', sweet enough to eat fresh off the bush — tart skin over sweet flesh — and equally good in pies and jam; no part is poisonous.[1][2]
edible.parts: The fruit is the part eaten: large, dark-red, faintly striped berries with tart skin and sweet flesh, ripening in July. Picked dead-ripe they are a true dessert gooseberry for eating out of hand; picked a little firmer and tarter they are classic for pies, crumbles, jam, jelly, and sauce. The leaves and stems (which are thorny) are not used as food.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Eat fully ripe red berries fresh, or cook firmer ones with sugar into pies, crumbles, jam, jelly, fool, and sauce. Top-and-tail the berries (remove the blossom and stalk ends) before cooking, wash them, and watch for the cane thorns while picking. Eat only fruit from bushes you have not sprayed.[1][2]
edible.caution: Gooseberry has no toxic parts — the ripe berries are not poisonous — so the cautions are practical ones: the canes are sharply thorny, so wear gloves when picking and pruning; very underripe berries are extremely tart and best cooked rather than eaten raw; and eat only unsprayed fruit. As with the currants, the bush is an alternate host for white pine blister rust, which threatens nearby white pines, not people eating the fruit.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A fall-bearing red raspberry that fruits on first-year canes — mow the whole patch to the ground each spring and still harvest a heavy crop of sweet berries from late summer to frost.
plants.sun: Full sun (6+ hrs); tolerates light afternoon shade
plants.water: Moderate — 1–3 in of water a week, more as fruit ripens; not drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Running — suckers from the roots into a widening thicket; contain with edging or mowing
plants.seasons: White flowers early summer; ripe red berries late summer into fall (the primocane crop); yellow fall foliage; bare prickly canes in winter
plants.wildlife: Bees work the flowers; songbirds and small mammals take the fruit; larval host for several butterflies and moths
plants.deer: Not deer-resistant — deer browse the tender canes and fruit; protect young plantings
plants.origin: Cultivated red raspberry — Eurasian Rubus idaeus crossed with the North American native subspecies
plants.habitat: Wild raspberries grow in clearings, woodland edges, burns, and rocky slopes across the cool northern hemisphere

edible.summary: The ripe red berries are a classic dessert fruit eaten fresh or cooked, and the leaves make a traditional tea — but the fruit is the part to grow this for.[1][3]
edible.parts: The ripe red aggregate berries are the food crop — sweet, fragrant, and eaten fresh out of hand or cooked into pies, preserves, jams, syrups, and wine. The dried leaves are also brewed as a mild herbal tea (the classic 'raspberry leaf tea'). No other part is used as food.[3]
edible.preparation: Pick berries fully ripe (they detach with a gentle roll) and eat them fresh or cook them into jelly, jam, pie, or syrup; they freeze well. For leaf tea, use only fully fresh or completely dried leaves — never partially wilted ones (see caution).[1][3]
edible.caution: The fruit is safe for everyone. The leaves are a different matter: fully dried (or fully fresh) raspberry leaves are fine for tea, but partially wilted or partly dried leaves can release small amounts of cyanide and cause nausea and stomach upset — so only brew leaves that are crisp-fresh or completely dry, never anything in between. The canes carry sharp prickles; wear gloves when pruning. As always, eat only fruit you have positively identified and that has not been sprayed.[3][5]

plants.blurb: A thornless black-currant-by-gooseberry hybrid — a vigorous, trouble-free bush hung with big tart-sweet near-black berries that ripen to currant-and-gooseberry flavor.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade (best fruit in full sun; afternoon shade helps in heat)
plants.water: Moderate — evenly moist soil; taper off after harvest to harden for winter
plants.spread: Clumping — a broad multi-stemmed bush; does not run or sucker invasively
plants.seasons: Maroon-pink and cream flowers April–May; reddish-black berries ripen midsummer; green summer foliage; bare arching stems in winter
plants.wildlife: Bees and early pollinators work the spring flowers; birds will strip the ripe fruit if it isn't netted
plants.deer: Browsed occasionally; not a preferred deer food but not reliably deer-proof
plants.origin: A garden hybrid bred in Germany in the 1970s from European black currant and gooseberry parents
plants.habitat: A cultivated hybrid with no wild habitat; its parent Ribes grow in cool, moist temperate woodlands and thickets of Europe and Asia

edible.summary: The large reddish-black-to-black berries are fully edible — eaten fresh when ripe or, more often, cooked into preserves, pies, and juice.[1][2]
edible.parts: The berries are the edible part. Ripe (fully colored, soft) jostaberries are tart-sweet with a black-currant-and-gooseberry flavor and can be eaten fresh out of hand; under-ripe fruit is quite tart and is better cooked. They make excellent jams, jellies, chutneys, pies, crumbles, juice, and liqueur. No other part of the plant is eaten.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Pick the berries when fully ripe (reddish-black to black and slightly soft) for fresh eating; pick a touch earlier and more tart for jelly, where the higher pectin and acid help it set. Top-and-tail like gooseberries if eating in quantity, then eat fresh, cook into preserves and baked goods, or press for juice; they freeze well.[1][2]
edible.caution: Jostaberry is a straightforwardly edible fruit bush with no known toxic parts. The only real cautions are practical: eat the berries fully ripe for the best (less sharply tart) flavor, and wash fruit from any plant that may have been sprayed. As with any fruit, positively identify the plant before eating.[1][2]

plants.blurb: The Front Range's true blueberry alternative — an edible honeysuckle that shrugs off alkaline clay and −40°F cold and ripens elongated blue, blueberry-meets-raspberry berries before anything else in the garden.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade (afternoon shade helps in the hottest sites)
plants.water: Moderate — keep evenly moist; not drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Clumping — a rounded, arching multi-stemmed bush; not a vine and not invasive
plants.seasons: Pale yellow flowers very early spring; elongated blue berries ripen in early summer (first fruit of the year); green summer foliage; bare twiggy frame in winter
plants.wildlife: Early bumble bees and other bees pollinate the flowers; birds will take the ripe fruit if it isn't netted
plants.deer: Generally left alone; not a preferred deer browse, though young growth may be sampled
plants.origin: Cultivated forms bred from circumboreal wild stock — Russian, Japanese, and Canadian germplasm
plants.habitat: Moist boreal forests, peaty soils, and stream edges across the cool northern hemisphere — Asia, Europe, and North America

edible.summary: The elongated blue berries are fully edible and excellent — a sweet-tart fruit eaten fresh or cooked, described as a cross between blueberry, raspberry, and black currant.[1][2]
edible.parts: The blue, oblong berries are the edible part — there is no toxic part on this honeysuckle. Ripe fruit is sweet-tart with a flavor likened to a blend of blueberry, raspberry, and black currant, and is unusually high in vitamin C (higher than blueberry or raspberry). Eat them fresh or use them in pies, jams, jellies, juices, sauces, ice cream, and wine.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Pick the berries fully ripe — deep blue outside, the flesh no longer green inside, and slightly soft — for the sweetest flavor; under-ripe fruit is sharply tart. Eat fresh out of hand, or cook into jam, jelly, pie, syrup, or sauce; they freeze very well, like blueberries. Handle gently, as the soft fruit bruises.[1][2]
edible.caution: These blue honeyberries are fully edible and safe — but do not confuse this species with the invasive bush honeysuckles (Lonicera maackii, L. morrowii, L. tatarica and the like), whose paired RED or orange berries are toxic and should never be eaten. The tell is the fruit: edible haskap is an elongated BLUE berry on a low, arching, non-twining shrub. Eat only blue-berried Lonicera caerulea you have positively identified, and only fruit that hasn't been sprayed.[1]

plants.blurb: An ultra-hardy berry shrub that pairs spring corymbs of pink-anthered white flowers with brilliant red fall foliage and glossy black antioxidant-packed fruit.
plants.water: Moderate; adaptable but not truly drought-tolerant — wants supplemental water on the dry Front Range
plants.spread: Suckers freely into a multi-stem clonal thicket over time
plants.seasons: Pink-anthered white flower clusters May–Jun; glossy dark-green summer foliage; blackish-purple berries Aug–Sep; brilliant wine-red fall color; bare twiggy frame in winter
plants.wildlife: Spring flowers feed bees & pollinators; ripe berries draw birds (cedar waxwings, robins) into fall
plants.origin: Eastern North America (NE US & Canada — not a Colorado native)
plants.habitat: Bogs, swamp edges, moist woods and dry uplands — wide moisture range on acid to neutral soils

edible.summary: The glossy black berries are edible and famously high in antioxidants — but far too astringent to enjoy raw, so they are cooked, juiced, or frozen first.[1][3]
edible.parts: The ripe blackish-purple berries (technically small pomes) are the edible part.[1] They carry more antioxidants — anthocyanins and flavonoids — than almost any other temperate fruit, roughly three times that of blueberries, which is the whole point of growing them.[3] The leaves, twigs, and bark are not used as food.
edible.preparation: Pick the berries fully black, ideally after a frost or freeze, which softens the mouth-puckering astringency.[4] They are too astringent to eat fresh in quantity, so they are processed: pressed for juice, cooked into jam, jelly, syrup, or wine, dried, baked into goods, or blended (often with sweeter fruit like apple) to balance the tartness.[1][3] Sweetening and cooking turn the harsh raw berry into a richly flavored, deep-purple product.
edible.caution: Aronia (Aronia melanocarpa) is entirely non-toxic — its 'choke' reputation is pure astringency from tannins and polyphenols, not poison.[3] Do not confuse it with chokecherry (Prunus virginiana), an unrelated plant whose leaves, twigs, and seed pits ARE cyanide-bearing.[1] As with other pome fruit in the rose family (apple, pear), the seeds contain only trace cyanogenic compounds and are harmless in normal eating; the flesh is safe. The practical caution is simply that the raw berries are intensely astringent and best cooked or frozen before eating.[3][4]

plants.blurb: A vigorous, suckering silver-leaved shrub armed with vicious thorns and studded in fall with dense clusters of intensely tart, vitamin-C-rich orange berries.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Suckers aggressively into dense clonal thickets and fixes nitrogen — same family as the noxious Russian olive, so confine it: root barrier or regular sucker removal, and keep it away from waterways/ditches
plants.seasons: Inconspicuous greenish flowers before the leaves in spring; silvery summer foliage on thorny stems; dense orange berries cling from fall into winter; bare spiny frame mid-winter
plants.wildlife: Wind-pollinated (little for bees); berries feed birds where they hang into winter; nitrogen-fixing roots build soil
plants.deer: Largely avoided — the sharp spines deter browsing
plants.origin: Cool-temperate Eurasia (Europe to Asia)
plants.habitat: Coastal dunes, river gravels and alpine slopes — sunny, exposed, sandy/poor, sometimes saline ground

edible.summary: The orange berries are edible, safe, and exceptionally high in vitamin C — but intensely acidic, so they are juiced or cooked rather than eaten raw; the real hazard is the shrub's vicious thorns at harvest.[1]
edible.parts: The orange berries are the edible part — among the most vitamin-C-rich fruits in the temperate world, and loaded with other vitamins and antioxidants.[1] They ripen September–October and persist into winter. No part of the plant is poisonous; the leaves are sometimes brewed as tea, but the berry is the food crop.[1]
edible.preparation: The berries are very tart and acidic — too sharp for most people to eat by the handful — so they are pressed into juice, cooked into jellies, jams, sauces, and pie fillings, or used as a tangy flavoring and ice-cream topping; some find fully ripe fruit pleasantly tangy-sweet.[1] A practical harvest trick is to cut fruiting branches and freeze them, then shake the brittle frozen berries free, since hand-picking among the spines is brutal.[1]
edible.caution: The berries themselves are safe and nutritious — the cautions are practical, not toxicological. First, the fruit is extremely acidic: eaten raw in quantity it is mouth-searingly tart and best sweetened or diluted. Second, and more important, the shrub is armed with long, very sharp woody spines, so harvesting is a genuine injury hazard — wear thick gloves and eye protection, and consider the cut-and-freeze method to keep hands out of the thorns.[1] No part of sea buckthorn is poisonous.[1]

plants.blurb: A silver-gray mat of woolly foliage that erupts into a froth of small white star flowers in late spring — stunning in lean, rocky soil where its spreading is kept in check.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Spreads by runners and self-seeds; well-behaved in lean, dry, gravelly soil — aggressive in rich or moist ground
plants.seasons: Silver-gray woolly mat year-round; smothered in white star flowers May–Jun; foliage persists through winter
plants.wildlife: Flowers attract small native bees and hoverflies
plants.origin: Mountains of Italy and Sicily (C/S Europe)
plants.habitat: Rocky alpine slopes, screes, and mountain meadows

edible.summary: Snow-in-summer is an ornamental groundcover with no established edible use — don't eat it.[1][2]
edible.caution: Cerastium tomentosum is grown for its silver foliage and white flowers, not for food, and no authoritative source documents an edible use for it. It is not listed as notably poisonous either — neither the NC State Plant Toolbox nor the Missouri Botanical Garden lists any toxicity for the plant — so treat it as inedible: not a known poison, but not a tested food. It is a woolly-leaved mouse-ear chickweed relative; as with any non-food ornamental, keep children and pets from grazing on it as a general precaution.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A Plant Select staple from South Africa — succulent evergreen mat that blazes with dazzling magenta-pink daisy-like flowers all summer long, thriving where it's blazing hot, bone-dry, and sharply drained.
plants.water: Low; very drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Spreads steadily by trailing stems to fill sunny, dry banks and rock-garden pockets; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Evergreen succulent mat year-round; brilliant magenta-pink flowers Jun–Sep (and often into Oct); foliage remains green-gray in winter
plants.wildlife: Flowers attract native bees, bumble bees, and butterflies
plants.origin: South Africa (Drakensberg highlands and high Karoo)
plants.habitat: High-elevation rocky slopes and arid scrubland

edible.summary: Hardy ice plant is grown as an ornamental, not a food — there is no established edible use for Delosperma cooperi, so don't eat it.[2][3]
edible.caution: Delosperma cooperi has no recognized food use and should not be eaten. While some other members of the ice-plant family (Aizoaceae) are eaten elsewhere, that does not transfer to this South African ornamental groundcover — no authoritative source documents it as a human food. It is not listed as notably poisonous either: the ASPCA classifies ice plant as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, and the NC State Plant Toolbox lists no toxicity for it. Treat it as inedible (not a known poison, but not a food); keep curious children and pets from grazing on it as a general precaution, since it is simply not a tested edible.[2][3]

plants.blurb: The quintessential fragrant subshrub — English lavender fills a sunny, well-drained border with silvery mounds and tall purple spikes beloved by bees and butterflies, and often blooms twice in Colorado's dry summers.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; does not spread or self-sow aggressively
plants.seasons: Evergreen silver-gray mound year-round; purple flower spikes Jun–Jul, often reblooming late summer/Sep; fragrant stems persist into winter
plants.wildlife: Exceptional pollinator plant — honey bees, native bees, butterflies, and sphinx moths flock to it
plants.deer: Strongly fragrant; reliably deer- and rabbit-resistant
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic to dogs and cats if ingested in quantity
plants.origin: W. Mediterranean — S. France, Spain, and N. Italy
plants.habitat: Sunny rocky hillsides and dry limestone scrub, low to mid elevations

edible.summary: The flowers and flower buds are edible and widely used as a culinary herb — but in small amounts, and the strongly aromatic leaves and stems can cause nausea if eaten in quantity.[3]
edible.parts: The dried (or fresh) flowers and flower buds are the part used in the kitchen — infused into teas, baked into shortbread and other sweets, blended into herbes de Provence, and steeped into vinegars, jellies, and syrups. English lavender (*L. angustifolia*) is the type grown for culinary use because it is lower in camphor than the lavandins; use a light hand, as the flavor is intense and turns soapy or bitter when overdone.[3]
edible.preparation: Use the flowers sparingly — a pinch where you'd use any strong herb. Harvest spikes in the morning when about half the florets have opened, then dry them for the longest-lasting flavor. Strip the tiny florets from the stems before adding to food; steep for teas. Lavender pairs with both sweet (baking, honey) and savory (lamb, herbes de Provence) dishes but is easy to overuse.[3]
edible.caution: Although the flowers are a recognized culinary herb, lavender carries a low-severity toxicity rating: the flowers, leaves, and stems contain the essential-oil compounds linalool and linalyl acetate, and eating the foliage — or too much of the flower — can cause nausea, vomiting, and loss of appetite. It is also toxic to dogs, cats, and horses if ingested, so keep pets from grazing it. Lavender essential oil is far more concentrated than the herb and should never be taken internally. Use the dried flowers in modest culinary amounts only.[3][4]
