plants.blurb: A well-behaved twining climber with clusters of long coral-red tubes that hummingbirds work all summer, followed by bright red berries that birds devour — vigorous but never weedy.
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Twining vine; does not sucker or self-sow aggressively
plants.seasons: Coral-red tubular flower clusters late spring through summer; small red berries ripen late summer into fall; semi-evergreen (holds some leaves in mild winters)
plants.wildlife: Outstanding hummingbird plant; berries attract robins, bluebirds, and other thrushes; flowers visited by bumblebees
plants.toxic: Berries are inedible and may cause stomach upset if eaten in quantity; specific toxicity data for this species is limited — treat as not for consumption and keep away from children and pets
plants.origin: Eastern and southern United States
plants.habitat: Woodland edges, thickets, and forest margins

edible.summary: Trumpet honeysuckle is grown for hummingbirds, not for eating — the bright red berries are not a human food and may cause stomach upset, though the flower nectar is harmless.[1][4]
edible.parts: There is no recommended edible part. The only thing people occasionally take is a drop of nectar sipped from the base of a fresh flower, a harmless childhood pastime; the leaves, stems, and especially the berries are not food. The berries exist to feed birds (robins, bluebirds, thrushes), not people.[1][4]
edible.preparation: None — do not prepare or eat any part. Leave the berries for the birds; enjoy the flowers ornamentally and, at most, taste a single drop of flower nectar.[1]
edible.caution: The berries of climbing/ornamental honeysuckles (Lonicera) contain saponins and are regarded as mildly toxic — eating them, particularly in quantity, can cause nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. No human deaths are recorded from this group, but children are most at risk because the showy red fruit is tempting; keep them from eating it. Specific toxicity data for L. sempervirens itself is limited, which is exactly why it should be treated conservatively as not for consumption. Do not confuse the harmless single-drop nectar habit with eating the berries — the berries should never be eaten.[1][4]

plants.blurb: A Canadian-bred cold-hardy twining vine that puts on a blazing long display of scarlet tubular flowers from early summer into fall — one of the best hummingbird plants for northern gardens.
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Twining vine; non-invasive; does not sucker or self-sow
plants.seasons: Scarlet tubular flower clusters early summer through early fall; occasional small orange-red berries; semi-deciduous (holds leaves late into fall)
plants.wildlife: Exceptional hummingbird magnet; berries attract birds; flowers visited by bumblebees
plants.toxic: Berries mildly toxic if ingested in quantity; keep away from small children and pets
plants.origin: Garden hybrid of eastern-US and Great-Lakes native honeysuckles
plants.habitat: Derived from woodland-edge and thicket parents of eastern North America

edible.summary: 'Dropmore Scarlet' is a hummingbird ornamental, not a food plant — its berries are mildly toxic and should not be eaten, though the flower nectar is harmless.[1][5]
edible.parts: There is no recommended edible part. Children sometimes sip a single drop of nectar from a fresh flower (harmless), but the leaves, stems, and the small orange-red berries are not food. The berries are there for the birds.[1][5]
edible.preparation: None — do not prepare or eat any part of the vine. Leave the berries for wildlife and enjoy the flowers ornamentally.[1]
edible.caution: Climbing/ornamental honeysuckle (Lonicera) berries contain saponins and are considered mildly toxic — eating them, especially in quantity, can cause nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal cramps. No human fatalities are recorded for this group, but the bright fruit tempts children, who are most at risk; keep them from eating it. 'Dropmore Scarlet' is a Lonicera × brownii hybrid (a cross of L. sempervirens × L. hirsuta), and like its relatives should be treated conservatively as not for consumption. Sipping a drop of flower nectar is not the same as eating the berries — never eat the berries.[1][5]

plants.blurb: A cool-season cottage-garden classic with tall airy spires of blue, purple, pink, or white — best fall-sown in Colorado for a spectacular May–June show before summer heat shuts it down.
plants.water: Low–moderate; prefers consistent moisture while actively growing
plants.spread: Self-sows freely; taproot resents root disturbance — direct sowing strongly preferred
plants.seasons: Blooms May through June (sometimes into July at higher elevations or in cool springs); rosette foliage in fall if fall-sown; plants yellow and collapse once summer heat arrives
plants.wildlife: Attracts early-season bumblebees and butterflies; hummingbirds occasionally probe the spurs
plants.deer: Generally avoided; toxic alkaloids deter browsing
plants.toxic: ALL PARTS TOXIC — foliage, flowers, and seeds contain toxic alkaloids (delphinine and related compounds) harmful to people, livestock, and pets if ingested; wear gloves when handling and keep away from children
plants.origin: Mediterranean — southern Europe and North Africa
plants.habitat: Disturbed ground, rocky slopes, and dry coastal cliffs

edible.summary: Larkspur is poisonous — every part contains toxic diterpenoid alkaloids and no part is edible; the seeds are the most concentrated and most dangerous.[1][3]
edible.caution: All parts of larkspur (Consolida ajacis) — leaves, flowers, stems, and especially the seeds and young growth — contain toxic diterpenoid (norditerpenoid) alkaloids, chiefly methyllycaconitine along with delphinine, ajacine, and ajaconine. These are potent neuromuscular poisons: they block acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction (a curare-like action), and human ingestion can cause burning of the mouth, nausea and vomiting, numbness and tingling, muscle weakness, slowed heart rate, and in severe cases respiratory paralysis and death. Larkspur (Delphinium/Consolida) is also one of the most important poisonous plants to cattle on western rangelands, causing significant livestock losses. Do not eat any part, wear gloves when handling, and keep the seeds, dried spikes, and prunings away from children, pets, and livestock — dried plant material remains toxic.[1][3]

plants.blurb: The apple-scented tea chamomile — a froth of little white-and-gold daisies over ferny foliage that pulls in beneficial insects and earns its old name, 'the plant's physician.'
plants.water: Low–moderate; well-drained
plants.spread: Self-sows freely into drifts; not invasive (a different plant from noxious scentless chamomile)
plants.seasons: Ferny foliage; white-and-gold daisies early summer into fall; dies at first frost
plants.wildlife: Draws bees, hoverflies, lacewings and other beneficial insects; pollinator-friendly
plants.toxic: Generally safe (flowers are used for tea); can trigger an allergic skin or oral reaction in people sensitive to ragweed-family plants
plants.origin: Europe and western Asia
plants.habitat: Disturbed ground, roadsides, and waste places

edible.summary: German chamomile is the classic edible/medicinal chamomile — the dried flowerheads make the familiar apple-scented tea — with one caveat: people allergic to ragweed and other daisy-family plants can react to it.[1]
edible.parts: The flowerheads are the part used — dried and steeped for chamomile tea, and also used as a garnish and in culinary and craft preparations. This is the true tea chamomile (Matricaria chamomilla). The foliage is aromatic but it is the flowers that are harvested; there is no use of the roots.[1]
edible.preparation: Pick fully open flowerheads on a dry morning, dry them in a single layer out of direct sun, and store airtight; steep the dried (or fresh) flowers in hot water for a calming tea. Used in normal beverage amounts it is well tolerated.[1]
edible.caution: German chamomile is in the daisy family (Asteraceae) and is related to ragweed: people sensitive to ragweed, chrysanthemums, or other Asteraceae can get contact dermatitis, oral swelling, or — rarely — severe allergic (anaphylactic) reactions, so anyone with those allergies should be cautious. The flowers and leaves contain the sesquiterpene lactone desacetylmatricarin (a low-severity contact allergen). Critically, make sure you have the true sweetly apple-scented German chamomile and not a daisy-like look-alike: Colorado's noxious 'scentless chamomile' (Tripleurospermum perforatum) and weedy mayweed chamomile (Anthemis cotula) resemble it but are not the tea herb.[1]

plants.blurb: A woody-based evergreen mat smothered in crisp white flower clusters each spring, then holding its glossy dark foliage through the whole Front Range winter.
plants.water: Low to moderate; very drought-tolerant once established in sharp-draining soil
plants.spread: Slowly spreading mat; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Dense white flower clusters Apr–May; glossy dark evergreen foliage year-round; trim after bloom for compactness
plants.wildlife: Early-season nectar source for small bees and butterflies; not a primary wildlife plant
plants.deer: Moderately deer-resistant; not a top choice for deer
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic — all parts contain glucosinolates; keep away from pets and children who might ingest
plants.origin: S. Europe — Iberian Peninsula east to Turkey and NW Africa
plants.habitat: Sunny rocky slopes, walls, and dry Mediterranean scrubland

edible.summary: Candytuft is a bitter mustard-family ornamental with no recognized food use — it is best treated as inedible and kept from pets and children.[1]
edible.caution: Candytuft (*Iberis sempervirens*) is a member of the mustard/cabbage family (Brassicaceae) grown only as an ornamental groundcover; no authority lists it as a food plant. Like its relatives, all parts contain glucosinolates (the pungent mustard-oil compounds, e.g. iberin) that make the foliage and seed bitter and acrid; *Iberis* seed has a long history as a bitter medicinal but not as food. Eating the plant can cause gastrointestinal upset (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) in people and pets, so treat all parts as inedible and keep curious children and animals from grazing it.[1]

plants.blurb: A sprawling Great Plains xeric perennial (native to NE/KS/MO/TX, not CO) with enormous, silky lemon-yellow cups that open at dusk and glow into the next morning, followed by showy four-winged reddish seedpods — all on a nearly ground-hugging plant built for hot, dry, well-drained sites.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant; dry preferred
plants.spread: Slowly spreading mound; clumping, non-invasive
plants.seasons: Narrow willow-like foliage spring through fall; large (3–4 in) lemon-yellow flowers May–Aug, opening in evening; decorative winged reddish seed capsules persist into fall; dies back fully in winter
plants.wildlife: Nectar source for hawk moths (sphingid pollinators) and hummingbirds; special value to native bees; seed capsules attract seed-eating birds
plants.origin: South-central US Great Plains (Nebraska to Texas, east to Tennessee)
plants.habitat: Limestone glades, bluffs, rocky prairies, and hillsides

edible.summary: Evening primroses (genus Oenothera) have edible roots, young shoots, and seeds, but Missouri evening primrose specifically is not well-documented as a food — treat it with caution and be sure of your identification before eating any part.[1][4]
edible.parts: In the wider Oenothera genus the edible parts are the taproot (boiled like a parsnip), the young shoots and leaves (cooked as a green), and the oil-rich seeds; the seeds of the genus are the famous source of evening primrose oil (rich in gamma-linolenic acid). These uses are best documented for Oenothera biennis; for this Great Plains species, O. macrocarpa, specific food-use records are sparse, so any edible use is by analogy to the genus rather than a documented tradition for this plant.[4]
edible.preparation: Following genus practice, roots are dug from first-year or young plants and boiled (often with a change of water) before eating; young spring shoots and leaves are cooked, not eaten raw, as the mature foliage turns tough and bitter. Because the food use here is genus-level rather than species-specific, prepare conservatively and in small amounts.[4]
edible.caution: No part of Oenothera macrocarpa is reported as poisonous, but it is not an established, well-documented food plant the way O. biennis is, so do not rely on it as forage — identify it with certainty and eat only small, cooked amounts of correctly-identified parts. Evening primrose seed oil can interact with some medications and is not recommended in pregnancy; this is a known caution for the genus's seed oil, not a green-thumb garnish. As always with a wild or ornamental plant, when in doubt, don't.[1][4]

plants.blurb: Upright candlestick spikes of starry flowers (lead form: crisp white 'Icicle', though blue and pink cultivars abound) rise above a neat, clumping base in summer — a tidy, heat-tolerant pollinator favorite that rebloms freely with deadheading.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; expands slowly by short rhizomes; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Rosette of dark green leaves from spring; dense flower spikes Jun–Aug with deadheading; foliage remains tidy through fall; dies to ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Strong pollinator draw — bumblebees, honeybees, small native bees, and butterflies work the spikes; a good front-of-border bee plant
plants.origin: Europe to northern and central Asia (widespread)
plants.habitat: Dry grasslands, rocky slopes, and open woodland margins

edible.summary: Spike speedwell is a low-toxicity ornamental with no established food use — not poisonous, but not a food plant.[1][2]
edible.caution: Veronica spicata has no recognized culinary use and should not be eaten. The Veronica (speedwell) genus is broadly non-toxic and not flagged as poisonous, and a few relatives (e.g. V. officinalis) have a folk herbal-tea history — but this border ornamental and its named cultivars ('Icicle', 'Royal Candles') are not documented food or medicinal plants, so be conservative and do not consume it. As with any non-food ornamental, eating plant material in quantity may cause mild stomach upset.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A heat-loving, drought-tolerant trailing mat that carpets itself with vivid purple flower clusters from late spring until hard frost, drawing butterflies all season and rooting wherever its stems touch soil.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Trailing stems root where they touch; spreads steadily but non-invasively
plants.seasons: Deep purple flower clusters late May through hard frost; semi-evergreen rosette in mild winters; dies back fully in harsh Front Range winters
plants.wildlife: Butterfly magnet — especially skippers, swallowtails, and cabbage whites; also visited by bees and hummingbirds
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant; the aromatic foliage is unattractive to browsers
plants.origin: South-central and southeastern United States — Illinois to Texas and the Carolinas
plants.habitat: Rocky prairies, glades, fields, and disturbed open ground

edible.summary: Rose verbena ('Homestead Purple') is an ornamental with no established food use — it is not eaten, though it is not flagged as notably poisonous.[1][2]
edible.caution: Glandularia canadensis (rose verbena, formerly Verbena canadensis) has no recognized culinary use and should not be eaten — it is not a food plant, and it is the garden trailing verbena, NOT the medicinal European vervain (Verbena officinalis) it is sometimes confused with, so don't substitute it in herbal preparations. It is not flagged as poisonous in the horticultural authorities, but, as with any non-food ornamental, ingesting plant material in quantity can cause mild stomach upset. No part is documented as edible.[1][2]

shots.cap: Summer — trailing mat of rose verbena spreading wide, smothered in rose-purple flower clusters · © Jan Johnson (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — dense rose-purple flower cluster · © bethconklin (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Summer — rose verbena blooming in a spreading drift, showing massed flower clusters in their natural context · © Matt Tomlinson (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — deeply lobed, toothed leaves · © Kristi Zoebelein (CC0) / iNaturalist
Summer — a rounded flower cluster face-on · © Erik Attaway (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — vivid rose-purple bloom cluster · © Dan Spaulding (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: A cheerful, easy-going golden daisy (semi-double in 'Early Sunrise') that blooms prolifically from early summer through fall when deadheaded — short-lived but drought-tolerant, pollinator-friendly, and quick to flower from seed in its first year.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows to persist; short-lived perennial (2–3 yrs)
plants.seasons: Green lance-like foliage from spring; bright golden semi-double daisies Jun–Sep with peak in early summer and again in late summer with deadheading; dies back in winter
plants.wildlife: Excellent pollinator plant — bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects flock to the open-centered flowers; seeds attract goldfinches
plants.origin: South-central and SE United States (NE to TX, east to TN; US native, not CO-native)
plants.habitat: Open sandy woods, roadsides, and rocky fields

edible.summary: This coreopsis is an ornamental — non-toxic to pets and people but with no established food use, so don't eat it.[1]
edible.caution: Coreopsis grandiflora 'Early Sunrise' is grown purely as an ornamental and pollinator plant. NC State's toolbox lists it as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, and it carries no poison rating — but "non-toxic" is not the same as edible: it has no established culinary use and no documented safe edible preparation, so it should be treated as a non-food plant. (A few other Coreopsis species have a documented history as a traditional dye or as a brewed tea, but that use is not established for C. grandiflora, so don't extrapolate it.) Don't eat the flowers, foliage, or seeds.[1]

Summer — the whole clump in a bed, golden semi-double daisies and buds above thread-fine foliage · © savitskaya (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

The basal rosette of spoon-shaped to lance leaves, foliage up close before bloom · © wwwa (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Summer — flowers on slender stems rising over grassy ground · © scottgillingwater (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — evergreen basal foliage through fresh snow · © Sharleen (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: Bold red-and-gold daisies that blaze from early summer through frost — a Front Range native that asks for nothing but sun and sharp drainage and repays you in months of non-stop pollinator traffic.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows lightly and may spread by short rhizomes — not aggressive
plants.seasons: Vivid red-and-yellow or orange-and-yellow flowers Jun–Sep (often into Oct); fluffy, globe-shaped seedheads persist into winter
plants.wildlife: Special value to native bees; also attracts butterflies and hummingbirds; goldfinches eat the seeds
plants.toxic: Hairy foliage may cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals; not systemically toxic

edible.summary: Blanket flower is not a food plant — there is no culinary use; it has traditional medicinal use only, which is not eating, and its hairy foliage can irritate skin.[2][3]
edible.caution: Gaillardia aristata is grown as an ornamental and pollinator plant, not as food — there is no established edible use of its flowers, leaves, seeds, or roots. Native American records document it only for non-food medicinal purposes (e.g., as eyewashes and remedies); a traditional medicinal mention is not a license to eat it. The hairy, mildly aromatic foliage can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive people, so wear gloves if you're prone to skin reactions. It is not reported as systemically poisonous, but treat it as inedible.[2][3]

shots.cap: Summer — multiple flowering plants on wiry stems in natural meadow setting, showing mounding growth habit · © Jo Roberts (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Face-on red-and-gold blanket flower with deep scarlet disc · © mads0012 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Clump of blanket flowers blazing in a dry meadow, showing mounding form · © Bob Walker (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Hairy lance-shaped leaves of blanket flower at the base · © Curtis Drake (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

Summer — a colony of red-and-gold blanket flowers drifting across a Front Range meadow · © omerpearlman (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — snow-dusted dried globular seedhead · © Chris Fluit (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: Wiry, branching stems carry wine-red pincushion flowers from midsummer through early fall — a pollinator magnet that weaves airy drifts through a border without ever dominating it.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Self-sows modestly; clump-forming, non-running
plants.seasons: Burgundy-red pincushion flowers Jul–Sep (sometimes into Oct with deadheading); dies to the ground in winter; basal rosette persists into late fall
plants.wildlife: Excellent pollinator plant — bees, small native bees, and butterflies visit constantly; long bloom season extends nectar availability into fall
plants.origin: Balkans and SE Europe (Macedonia and surrounding region)
plants.habitat: Rocky meadows, open grassy slopes, and scrubby hillsides

edible.summary: Knautia is an ornamental pincushion-flower relative with no established food use — not notably poisonous, but there is no documented edible part, so don't eat it.[2]
edible.caution: Knautia macedonica is grown strictly as an ornamental and pollinator plant. Authoritative sources record no toxicity rating and no established culinary or food use, and there is no documented safe edible preparation. With no positive food record, treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots. It belongs to the same family as scabious/teasel and is similarly a non-food garden plant.[2]

shots.cap: Summer — whole plant in a border: wiry branching stems, pinnate leaves, and wine-red pincushion heads · © maddyv05 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — multiple wine-red pincushion heads on wiry stems in a garden border · © evergreensdad (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: A Southwest US native (AZ/NM) celebrated for its fine, needle-like evergreen foliage and long wands of tubular flowers — the 'Mersea Yellow' form trades the usual scarlet for soft butter-yellow — beloved by hummingbirds and built for sharp-draining, xeric gardens.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping, non-spreading; slowly widens
plants.seasons: Evergreen needle foliage year-round; soft-yellow tubular flowers Jun–Aug; dormant stems hold winter structure
plants.wildlife: Hummingbird magnet; also visited by native bees seeking tubular flowers
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant; not a preferred browse
plants.origin: Southwestern US — Arizona and New Mexico; also N. Mexico (Sierra Madre)
plants.habitat: Dry, rocky open slopes and canyon walls in pine-oak woodlands

edible.summary: Pineleaf penstemon is an ornamental wildflower, not a food plant — penstemons (beardtongues) have no documented edible use, so it should not be eaten.[1]
edible.caution: Penstemons, including pineleaf penstemon (*Penstemon pinifolius*), are grown purely as ornamental and pollinator plants; authoritative references list no edible or culinary use for the genus. Its value is as a hummingbird and native-bee nectar source, not as food. While penstemons are not noted as dangerously poisonous, no part has a recognized food use and ingestion can cause stomach upset, so treat all parts as inedible and keep them out of the kitchen.[1]

plants.blurb: A tough, silvery-leaved garden hybrid with flat-topped canary-gold flower heads that bloom relentlessly from early summer into fall — xeric, pollinator-friendly, deer-resistant, and at home in Front Range sun and alkaline soil.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Slowly spreading clump; divide every 2–3 years to keep vigor
plants.seasons: Silver-grey ferny foliage from spring through fall; flat golden-yellow corymbs Jun–Sep; foliage semi-evergreen in mild Front Range winters; seedheads add minor winter texture
plants.wildlife: Excellent pollinator plant — attracts native bees, beneficial wasps, hoverflies, and butterflies; larval host for some moth species
plants.deer: Deer-resistant; strong aromatic foliage deters browsing; also rabbit-resistant
plants.toxic: Mild — aromatic foliage can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals (wear gloves); toxic to cats, dogs, and horses if ingested in quantity
plants.origin: Garden hybrid; parent species (A. clypeolata, A. taygetea) from SE Europe and W Asia
plants.habitat: Rocky, dry slopes and open grassland in the eastern Mediterranean and Aegean

edible.summary: Yarrow leaves and flowers are a documented bitter culinary/tea herb used in small amounts, but they contain thujone and other compounds — use sparingly, avoid in pregnancy, and keep it away from pets, to whom it is toxic.[1][3]
edible.parts: The leaves and flowers are the edible parts. They are bitter and are used in small quantities — fresh young leaves in salads, and leaves or flowers brewed as a tea or used to flavor liquors and bitters. This is a flavoring/herbal use, not a bulk vegetable; the bitterness limits the amount used.[1]
edible.preparation: Use leaves and flowers in moderation — a few young leaves raw in a salad, or dried leaves/flowers steeped as a tea or infused into bitters and liqueurs. The strong, bitter flavor means a little goes a long way; cooking or steeping mellows it somewhat. Note that 'Moonshine' is a hybrid garden ornamental selected for flower color rather than for culinary use, so treat any tasting cautiously and in small amounts.[1]
edible.caution: Yarrow contains thujone and sesquiterpene lactones; it should be consumed only in small amounts and is best avoided during pregnancy (it has a traditional reputation for affecting the uterus) and by anyone allergic to other Asteraceae (ragweed, chamomile, daisies). Handling the foliage can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive people, so wear gloves. Critically, yarrow is toxic to cats, dogs, and horses — the ASPCA lists it as toxic (principles include achillein, monoterpenes, and the sesquiterpene lactone achillin), with vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, dermatitis, and depression among the signs — so do not let pets graze it. Be sure of your identification: the finely-dissected ferny foliage distinguishes yarrow from poison-hemlock and other look-alikes with similarly lacy leaves.[1][3]

shots.cap: Soft-yellow flat flowerheads · © Shuvaev (CC BY 4.0) / Wikimedia Commons
Silvery ferny clump topped with gold · © Shuvaev (CC BY 4.0) / Wikimedia Commons
Silver-grey ferny foliage with early white buds — the distinctive 'Moonshine' leaf texture · © David J. Stang (CC-BY-SA 4.0) / Wikimedia Commons
Summer — a whole silvery-ferny clump topped with flat golden corymbs, the plant's mounded garden form · © Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz (CC-BY-SA 4.0) / Wikimedia Commons
Spring — the silver-grey ferny foliage rosette before the flat gold heads dominate · © Krzysztof Ziarnek, Kenraiz (CC-BY-SA 4.0) / Wikimedia Commons
Fall — flat seed heads drying to tan over the ferny foliage (representative yarrow, Achillea filipendulina) · © Angel M (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — a clump of tan seed heads standing for winter structure (representative yarrow, Achillea filipendulina) · © Pedro Antonio Lázaro Molinero (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: A tough native evergreen mat-former — glossy dark-green leaves, small pink urn-shaped flowers in spring, bright-red berries in fall and winter — that hugs the ground in sun to part shade on well-drained, lean soil; slow to establish but virtually indestructible once settled.
plants.size: 4–8 in. × 2–6 ft (trailing stems spread wide over time)
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Slowly spreading mat via trailing stems (not runners or suckers); non-invasive
plants.seasons: White-to-pink urn flowers Apr–May; green berries ripen to bright red by Sep, persisting through winter; glossy leaves turn bronze-red in cold
plants.wildlife: Red berries eaten by bears, grouse, robins, and other birds; flowers are an early nectar source for native bees; excellent wildlife cover
plants.toxic: Not toxic; berries technically edible but mealy and astringent — more wildlife food than human food

edible.summary: The mealy red berries are edible (bland raw, better cooked), but the leaves — though a documented traditional tea and smoking herb — contain arbutin/hydroquinone and are not for casual or repeated ingestion.[1][4]
edible.parts: The **berries** (small red drupes) are the edible part. They are dry, mealy, and nearly tasteless raw — a recognized emergency/wildlife food more than a treat — but Indigenous peoples fried or dried them and added them to pemmican, and they make passable jelly, jam, or sauce when cooked. The **leaves** are documented as a traditional beverage tea and a tobacco substitute (the name 'kinnikinnick' refers to this smoking use), and historically as a medicinal urinary-tract astringent — but the leaves are a caution, not a casual food (see below).[1]
edible.preparation: Berries are best cooked — fried, dried, or simmered into jelly, jam, or sauce — to improve their bland, mealy texture; they can be eaten raw in a pinch but are unrewarding. The leaves were traditionally dried and steeped as a tea or smoked; do not treat leaf tea as an everyday beverage given its arbutin content.[1]
edible.caution: The berries are not toxic. The concern is the **leaves**, which are rich in arbutin, a phenolic glycoside that the body converts to hydroquinone — the basis of their historical use against urinary infections, but also a compound that can be harmful in excessive or prolonged doses (potential liver/kidney effects), and that is not recommended in pregnancy or while nursing. The leaves are also high in tannins, which can cause stomach upset. Treat leaf tea as a short-term traditional preparation, not a routine drink, and avoid it if pregnant. Be sure of identification (the glossy-leaved native mat, Arctostaphylos uva-ursi); the berries are safe but unexciting, so this is a wildlife plant first.[1][4]

shots.cap: Spring — small pink urn-shaped flowers nestled among glossy evergreen leaves · © Jared Shorma (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Summer — glossy rounded leaves of the trailing mat close-up, showing the distinctive leaf shape and texture · © Christian Nunes (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — dense evergreen mat of glossy oval leaves, showing the low groundcover form · © drbeucler (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — a maturing red berry among glossy rounded leaves and reddish autumn stems · © ouellet_k (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — a glossy red berry ripening on the evergreen mat · © D Solis (CC-BY-NC) / GBIF
Winter — glossy leaves turning yellow-bronze in the cold season, showing kinnikinnick's characteristic evergreen winter coloration · © Nikolai Braedt (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: A low, stoloniferous native evergreen with holly-like leaves that flush bronze-purple in winter, cheerful yellow flowers in spring, and clusters of tart blue berries in fall — one of the most versatile and tough ground-covering shrubs for Front Range shade or sun.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Spreads by stolons (trailing stems that root along the ground) into a colony; slow but steady — contains easily at a bed edge
plants.seasons: Yellow flower clusters Apr–May; dusty blue-purple berries ripen Sep–Oct; leaves bronze-purple in winter cold, dark green in summer
plants.wildlife: Berries eaten by birds (robins, thrushes, waxwings) and small mammals; flowers attract early native bees; dense stems provide bird cover
plants.toxic: Berries tart/edible (used for jelly); bark and roots contain berberine which is toxic in large quantities — caution with children

edible.summary: The tart blue berries are edible — a well-documented native food, usually cooked into jelly or syrup — but the bark and roots contain berberine and are not food.[1][3]
edible.parts: Only the ripe blue-purple berries are eaten. They are a traditional Western and Plains food (eaten by the Blackfoot, Cheyenne, Flathead, Kutenai, and others) and make excellent jelly, syrup, and wine; they are tart and seedy but high in pectin. The yellow inner bark and roots are bright with berberine and were used medicinally (a traditional 'barberry' remedy), not as food.[3][1]
edible.preparation: Pick the berries fully ripe in fall; they are intensely tart and seedy raw, so they are almost always cooked and sweetened into jelly, syrup, or wine, or strained for juice. Use the berries only — do not eat the bark or roots. Wear gloves when harvesting around the spiny holly-like leaves.[1][3]
edible.caution: The berries are edible, but the bark and roots contain berberine, an alkaloid that is bitter and can be toxic in large quantities (gastrointestinal upset; not for use in pregnancy) — these parts are a folk medicine, not food, so eat only the berries and keep children from chewing the roots or stems. The spiny leaf margins are a physical hazard. Be sure of identification: confirm true creeping Oregon grape (Berberis/Mahonia repens) before eating, and never eat berries from a plant you cannot positively identify.[1][3]

shots.cap: Creeping Oregon grape — bright yellow flower clusters above spiny compound leaves · © ashleygottlieb (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Creeping Oregon grape — low-creeping mat of holly-like compound leaves along a rock face · © Stephen Hornbeck (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Fall — clusters of dusty blue berries ripening alongside spiny-margined compound leaves · © Bob Walker (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

Summer — glossy holly-like pinnately compound leaves with spiny-toothed leaflets · © Nicholas Sly (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — dusty-blue berry clusters amid leaves reddening to scarlet · © maggieeee (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: The famous long-blooming hardy cranesbill — large violet-blue saucer flowers with white eyes appear from early summer and keep going right up to frost with almost no deadheading needed.
plants.water: Moderate; tolerates brief dry spells once established
plants.spread: Mounding and sprawling; sterile (no self-sowing); non-invasive
plants.seasons: Violet-blue flowers with white eyes Jun–Oct; attractive lobed foliage; often shows fall color (red-orange) before dying back; no winter interest
plants.wildlife: Good pollinator plant — bees and small native bees visit the open saucer flowers throughout its very long season
plants.deer: Moderately deer-resistant; aromatic foliage is generally avoided
plants.origin: Garden hybrid — parent species G. wallichianum and G. himalayense from the Himalayas
plants.habitat: Himalayan mountain meadows, woodland edges, and rocky subalpine slopes

edible.summary: 'Rozanne' is an ornamental cranesbill with no documented food use — not notably poisonous, but not a recognized edible.[1][4]
edible.caution: Geranium 'Rozanne' is a true cranesbill (genus Geranium) — note it is NOT the houseplant 'geranium' (Pelargonium), which is the one ASPCA lists as toxic (vomiting, depression, dermatitis) to dogs and cats. No authority documents this hybrid (or cranesbills generally) as a food: PFAF lists no edible use for the genus, so although cranesbill flowers occasionally turn up as a salad garnish, this sterile garden cultivar is not a substantiated edible and should not be relied on as one. Be conservative and treat it as inedible.[1][3][4]

Summer — a sprawling mound of violet-blue saucers, summer to frost · © Burkhard Mücke (CC BY-SA 4.0) / Wikimedia Commons

plants.blurb: One of the very first wildflowers of spring — silky lavender cups push up through the snow on fuzzy stems, then transform into shimmering plumed seedheads that persist for weeks.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clump-forming; non-spreading; taprooted and slow to establish — do not disturb
plants.seasons: Silky lavender to pale purple cup flowers Mar–May (before or with new leaves); feathery silver-plume seedheads Apr–Jun; attractive ferny foliage through summer; foliage dies back by midsummer in heat
plants.wildlife: Early-spring nectar and pollen for native bees, especially queen bumblebees; silky seedheads used by birds for nest lining
plants.deer: Deer-resistant; toxic foliage is avoided by most browsers
plants.toxic: ALL PARTS TOXIC — contains protoanemonin; causes digestive distress and mouth irritation if ingested; fresh sap causes skin inflammation and blistering. Handle with gloves. Keep away from children, pets, and livestock.

edible.summary: Pasque flower is poisonous — the fresh plant and its sap are irritant and blistering, and it should never be eaten.[2]
edible.caution: All parts of fresh pasque flower (Pulsatilla) are toxic: the plant contains the irritant compound protoanemonin (released from ranunculin when the tissue is damaged), and NC State rates the poison severity HIGH. Fresh sap causes inflammation and blistering on skin contact (contact dermatitis), and ingestion causes severe pain in the mouth, vomiting, and diarrhea; it is also toxic to livestock. Drying reduces toxicity (the volatile protoanemonin breaks down), but never rely on this — wear gloves when handling, weeding, or dividing, and keep the plant away from children, pets, and grazing animals. Do not eat any part.[2]

shots.cap: Spring — face-on open lavender cups with golden stamens, fuzzy silky petals characteristic of pasque flower · © mwilliams98 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Spring — whole clump of pasque flowers in bloom on open prairie, showing low fuzzy-stemmed growth form · © Andrew Pauly (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Early spring — fuzzy silky buds pushing up on hairy stems, characteristic of pasque flower before the cup opens · © Aleksei Baushev (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Spring — close-up of the silky silver plumed seedhead, equally ornamental as the blooms · © Ryan Sorrells (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Early spring — a low ground-hugging clump of open lavender-purple cups with golden stamens, showing the compact prairie-mound habit in bloom · © Vitalii Kolomiichuk (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — the post-bloom basal rosette of finely dissected, ferny, silky-hairy palmate leaves · © Elena Pushay (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — dried feathery seedhead plumes amid late-winter snow · © Сергей (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: Tall, wiry-stemmed perennial that lights up the late-summer and fall garden with luminous pink or white poppy-like flowers — one of very few perennials that blooms well into October on the Front Range.
plants.sun: Part shade (morning sun / afternoon shade preferred)
plants.water: Moderate; consistent moisture, not drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Spreads by rhizomes and stolons; can colonize once established — allow room
plants.seasons: Bold, dark-green palmate foliage from late spring; luminous pink or white flowers on branching wiry stems Aug–Oct; dies back to ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Attracts bumblebees and late-season pollinators; flowers persist into fall when little else is blooming
plants.deer: Deer tend to avoid it; toxic alkaloids in foliage are likely deterrent
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic if ingested — all Anemone parts contain protoanemonin (an irritant); causes mouth/GI irritation in people and pets. Wear gloves when handling sap.
plants.origin: Garden hybrid; parent species from China and the Himalaya
plants.habitat: Woodland margins, scrub, and streamside thickets (parent species habitat)

edible.summary: Japanese anemone is poisonous — like all Anemone it contains an irritant compound and should not be eaten.[1][3]
edible.caution: Japanese anemone is toxic if ingested: like all Anemone species it contains protoanemonin, an irritant released when the fresh tissue is bruised or chewed, and NC State lists contact dermatitis as a hazard of handling it. The fresh sap can inflame and blister skin, and eating the plant causes irritation of the mouth, vomiting, and diarrhea. Wear gloves when dividing or cutting it, and keep the plant away from children and pets. Do not eat any part.[1][3]

plants.blurb: A low spring-blooming tuber that carpets the ground with starry blue, white, or pink daisy-like flowers just 4–6 inches tall — plant soaked tubers in fall and let them naturalize under shrubs or in dappled shade.
plants.sun: Part shade to full sun (tolerates full sun in spring before trees leaf out)
plants.water: Low to moderate; dry summer dormancy preferred
plants.spread: Slowly naturalizes by tuber offsets and self-seeding; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Starry 1–2 in flowers (blue, white, or pink) April–May; foliage dies back to ground by June; fully dormant all summer through fall
plants.wildlife: Early nectar source for queen bumblebees and solitary bees emerging in late winter–early spring
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant; bitter alkaloids in foliage are deterrent
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic — all Anemone parts contain protoanemonin, an irritant that causes mouth and GI irritation in people and pets if ingested; sap can cause contact dermatitis. Wear gloves when handling tubers and foliage.
plants.origin: SE Europe and SW Asia (Turkey, Lebanon, and Syria)
plants.habitat: Rocky woodland edges, open scrub, and hillside meadows

edible.summary: Grecian windflower is poisonous — like all Anemone it contains an irritant compound and should not be eaten.[1]
edible.caution: All parts of Anemone blanda are poisonous (bark, flowers, fruits, leaves, roots, seeds, and stems). The plant contains protoanemonin, an irritant released when the fresh tissue is bruised or chewed; NC State rates poison severity LOW but it is still toxic. Contact with the fresh sap causes inflammation, blistering, and contact dermatitis, and eating a large amount causes irritation of the mouth, vomiting, and diarrhea. Wear gloves when handling tubers and foliage, and keep the plant away from children and pets. Do not eat any part.[1]

plants.blurb: A tidy steel-blue fountain of fine, arching foliage — semi-evergreen through Front Range winters — with tall airy oat-like seed sprays rising above the clump in early summer.
plants.water: Low; very xeric once established
plants.spread: Slowly expanding clump; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Semi-evergreen blue-gray foliage year-round; oat-like tan seed sprays Jun–Jul; foliage bleaches some in winter but holds form
plants.wildlife: Seed heads attract finches and sparrows in fall and winter; clump provides modest cover
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant; coarse texture and sharp leaf edges are unappealing
plants.origin: SW Europe — southwestern Alps of France and Italy
plants.habitat: Rocky limestone slopes, gravelly screes, and dry montane meadows

edible.summary: Blue oat grass is purely an ornamental grass — it has no edible use and should not be eaten.[1]
edible.caution: Blue oat grass (*Helictotrichon sempervirens*) is grown strictly as an ornamental clumping grass; authoritative references list no edible or culinary use, and despite the 'oat' in its name it is not the cereal oat (*Avena sativa*) and produces no food grain for people. It is not reported as poisonous, but the fibrous foliage and seed are not food — treat all parts as inedible.[1]

plants.blurb: A tough native short-lived perennial or biennial that covers itself in flat-topped clusters of fragrant orange-to-yellow flowers in spring — one of the earliest Front Range natives to bloom and a magnet for the season's first butterflies.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping (single taproot); self-sows freely to form colonies — not invasive
plants.seasons: Bright orange or yellow flower clusters Apr–Jun; narrow silique seed pods elongate and dry through summer; basal rosette evergreen through mild winters
plants.wildlife: Early nectar source for native bees, butterflies (especially checkerspot and orangetip larvae use Brassicaceae), and hummingbirds; seeds eaten by finches
plants.toxic: Despite being in the mustard family, Erysimum accumulates cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) and is bitter and toxic — do NOT treat it as an edible brassica

edible.summary: Despite being a mustard, western wallflower is not an edible brassica — Erysimum accumulates cardiac glycosides and is bitter and toxic; do not eat the leaves, seeds, or flowers as a green.[3]
edible.parts: There is no reliable edible part. Erysimum is unusual among the Brassicaceae (mustard family) in producing cardiac glycosides as a chemical defense, so although it looks superficially like other edible mustards, it should not be treated as a salad green or potherb. Any documented human use is medicinal/folk, not culinary, and is not a basis for eating it.[3]
edible.preparation: Do not prepare or eat it. The cardiac glycosides are not removed by ordinary cooking, and there is no established safe culinary preparation. Grow western wallflower as an ornamental and pollinator plant only.[3]
edible.caution: Erysimum (wallflower) accumulates cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) — a class of heart-active toxins rare in the mustard family — which deter herbivores by blocking ion channels in muscle cells; Erysimum species are documented as toxic to some vertebrate herbivores. The seeds in particular concentrate these compounds. Do not confuse this plant with edible mustards or wild brassicas: it is bitter and poisonous, and eating it risks cardiac toxicity. Keep seeds and foliage away from children and pets.[3]

plants.blurb: A Plant Select standout — a flat, reliably evergreen mat of tiny dark green leaves that vanishes under a blaze of cobalt-blue flowers every spring, tough between pavers, on dry slopes, and in any sunny or partly sunny spot where nothing else will grow.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Spreads by creeping stems to form a dense mat; non-invasive; takes light foot traffic
plants.seasons: Reliably evergreen dark green mat year-round; brilliant true-blue flowers Apr–Jun; green mat persists through summer and fall; may show purplish tints in intense sun
plants.wildlife: Early-spring bee forage — flowers are among the first blue blooms of the season when native bees are emerging
plants.origin: NE Turkey to the Caucasus (W. Asia)
plants.habitat: Rocky hillsides and dry stony ground in the temperate zone

edible.summary: Turkish speedwell is a low-toxicity ornamental groundcover with no established food use — not poisonous, but not eaten.[1][2]
edible.caution: Veronica liwanensis has no recognized culinary use and should not be treated as a food. The Veronica (speedwell) genus is broadly non-toxic and not flagged as poisonous, and a few other speedwells (e.g. V. officinalis) have a folk herbal-tea history — but this Plant Select ornamental selection is not a documented edible or herbal plant, so be conservative and do not consume it; eating plant material in quantity can cause mild stomach upset in any case. Don't confuse it with the coarse-leaved weed Veronica persica.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Tough, mounding hardy geranium with vivid magenta-to-crimson saucer flowers in early summer, deeply cut leaves that glow red in fall, and a carefree constitution that handles dry shade to full sun on the Front Range.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; slowly expands; may self-sow lightly
plants.seasons: Magenta-crimson saucer flowers May–Jul; sporadic rebloom through summer; deeply cut leaves turn crimson-red in fall; dies to ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Bees and pollinators visit the open saucer flowers; deer and rabbit resistant
plants.deer: Resistant — generally left alone by deer and rabbits
plants.origin: Europe to the Caucasus and temperate Asia
plants.habitat: Dry grassland, rocky limestone slopes, and open woodland margins

edible.summary: Bloody cranesbill is an ornamental with no documented food use — not notably poisonous, but not a recognized edible.[1][3]
edible.caution: Geranium sanguineum is a true cranesbill (genus Geranium) — note it is NOT the houseplant 'geranium' (Pelargonium), which is the one ASPCA lists as toxic (vomiting, depression, dermatitis) to dogs and cats, so don't conflate the two. No authority documents the bloody cranesbill itself as a food: PFAF lists no edible use for cranesbills, and while the flowers occasionally appear as a salad garnish, this is not a substantiated food plant and it should not be relied on as one. The root is high in tannins and has only a folk-medicinal (astringent) history, not a culinary one. Be conservative: treat it as inedible.[1][2][3]

plants.blurb: In late summer the wiry stems of this tough prairie native vanish under a blizzard of tiny white daisies — one of the finest late-season native-bee plants on the Front Range.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Spreads slowly by rhizomes and self-sows; can form loose colonies over time
plants.seasons: Clouds of small white daisy flowers (yellow centers turning reddish) Aug–Oct; persistent feathery seedheads well into winter
plants.wildlife: Exceptional native-bee plant — specialist Andrena bees, butterflies, skippers, beneficial wasps; seedheads feed sparrows and finches

edible.summary: White heath aster is not a food plant — it has documented Native American ceremonial and medicinal uses but no edible use, and livestock avoid it.[3]
edible.caution: There is no recorded use of white heath aster (Symphyotrichum ericoides) as food; its documented Native American uses were medicinal and ceremonial — burned as an herbal steam in sweat baths and used to revive an unconscious person — not culinary, and grazing animals decline to eat it. No part is reported as a serious poison to people or pets, but because it is not an established edible it should not be eaten. As with any daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant, those with ragweed/aster allergies may react to the pollen, and a wild plant should never be eaten without certain identification.[3]

plants.blurb: A low, compact native aster that smothers itself in small white daisies from late summer into fall — one of the toughest, most xeric-adapted asters on the High Plains and foothills of Colorado.
plants.water: Very low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Spreads slowly by rhizomes; var. falcatum forms multi-stemmed clumps, not aggressive runners
plants.seasons: White-rayed, yellow-centered daisy flowers Jul–Oct (later than most prairie flowers); fine-textured grayish-green foliage all season
plants.wildlife: Late-season nectar for native bees, butterflies, and hover flies; seeds taken by sparrows and finches
plants.deer: Deer-resistant; tough, hairy foliage is not palatable

edible.summary: White prairie aster is not a food plant — there is no established edible use; like the other native asters it is grown ornamentally and for wildlife, not for eating.[3]
edible.caution: White prairie aster (Symphyotrichum falcatum) has no recorded use as human food. Native asters in this group are not known to seriously poison people or pets — the ASPCA lists asters as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses — so an accidental nibble is not an emergency, but the plant is not an accepted edible and should not be eaten. As with any daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant, people with ragweed/aster allergies can react to the pollen or foliage, and no wild plant should be eaten without certain identification.[3]

plants.blurb: A tall, willow-leaved native aster that erupts in sprays of white-to-pale-lilac daisies in fall — a vigorous colonizer for moist, sunny spots that needs room to roam.
plants.water: Moderate; prefers moist soils but tolerates short dry spells
plants.spread: Spreads vigorously by rhizomes — can form dense colonies; site and divide accordingly
plants.seasons: Narrow lance-shaped leaves all season; sprays of 0.5–1 in. white to pale-lilac daisies (yellow centers) Sep–Oct; feathery seedheads persist into fall
plants.wildlife: Important late-season nectar source for bumblebees, wasps, and hover flies; seedheads eaten by sparrows, finches, and grouse
plants.deer: Moderately deer-resistant; tall, somewhat coarse foliage is not preferred

edible.summary: Panicled aster is not a food plant — it has documented Native American medicinal uses (wound dressings, salves, treating nosebleeds) but no edible use.[2][3]
edible.caution: Panicled aster (Symphyotrichum lanceolatum) has no recorded use as human food; its documented Indigenous uses were medicinal — the dried, powdered plant as a salve on abrasions, a decoction to dress wounds, and smoke from the crushed blossoms inhaled for nosebleeds — not culinary. Native asters are not known to seriously poison people or pets (the ASPCA lists asters as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses), so an accidental nibble is not an emergency, but the plant is not an accepted edible and should not be eaten. As with any daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant, people with ragweed/aster allergies may react to the pollen or foliage.[2][3]

plants.blurb: Fringed, almost spidery blue cornflowers on silver-felted stems in late spring — beautiful and vigorous, but it spreads by stolons and seeds so manage it with deadheading and dividing.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Spreads by stolons and self-sows; can be aggressive — divide and deadhead to contain
plants.seasons: Blue-violet fringed cornflowers May–Jul (reblooms with deadheading); silver-woolly foliage; dies back in winter
plants.wildlife: Good pollinator plant — bees, bumblebees, and butterflies, especially during the late-spring gap before many other perennials peak
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant due to woolly foliage
plants.toxic: No serious toxicity reported, but Centaurea montana is regarded as inedible (medicinal use only) — not the same as the edible annual cornflower
plants.origin: Mountains of central and southern Europe
plants.habitat: Mountain meadows, open rocky slopes, and forest edges

edible.summary: Mountain bluet (Centaurea montana) is regarded as inedible — unlike its edible relative the annual cornflower (C. cyanus), it is not a food plant, though it has folk-medicinal use.[2]
edible.caution: Do not assume mountain bluet's flowers are edible just because the annual cornflower (Centaurea cyanus) is used as an edible garnish — they are different species. Centaurea montana is described as inedible (used as a medicinal plant in Central Europe, not as a food). No part is documented as a safe edible, and no specific toxic compound or severe poisoning is reported; treat it as ornamental only and do not eat it. (This is the harmless garden perennial, not one of the CO-listed noxious knapweeds.)[2]

plants.blurb: Architectural 'bear's breeches' — huge glossy leaves and dramatic hooded white-and-purple flower spikes. Tender here (~zone 7); needs a sheltered spot and a hard mulch.
plants.sun: Part shade–full sun (afternoon shade in hot sites)
plants.water: Moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping — spreads by deep roots; hard to remove once sited
plants.seasons: Bold glossy foliage spring–fall; tall white-&-purple flower spikes early–mid summer
plants.wildlife: Bees & bumblebees work the hooded flowers
plants.toxic: Not considered toxic; sap and leaf-spines can irritate skin
plants.origin: Western and central Mediterranean — southern Europe and northwestern Africa
plants.habitat: Rocky, scrubby hillsides, open woodland, and dry stream banks

edible.summary: Bear's breeches is an ornamental foliage plant with no edible use — it is grown for its leaves and flower spikes, not for eating.[1][2]
edible.caution: Acanthus mollis is not considered notably poisonous, but it has no food use and should not be eaten. Handle it with care for a different reason: the leaf margins and flower bracts are spiny, and the sap can irritate sensitive skin — wear gloves when cutting or dividing it.[1][2]

plants.blurb: The famous all-summer wallflower: a grey-leaved mound smothered in fragrant mauve racemes that feed early bees. Short-lived and borderline-hardy (~zone 6b).
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant with sharp drainage
plants.spread: Clumping — sterile, non-self-sowing woody mound
plants.seasons: Grey-green near-evergreen mound; mauve-purple racemes much of the year (peak spring)
plants.wildlife: Top early-season bee & butterfly nectar plant
plants.toxic: Not considered notably toxic; not for eating
plants.origin: Mediterranean Europe — hybrid with S. European Erysimum parentage
plants.habitat: Sunny rocky walls, cliffs, and dry scrub in Mediterranean regions

edible.summary: An ornamental wallflower grown for its nonstop fragrant bloom — it is not a food plant and should not be eaten.[1][3]
edible.caution: Like other Erysimum, 'Bowles's Mauve' is not a food plant: the foliage and seeds of wallflowers contain cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) that act on the heart, so no part of the plant should be eaten. (This cultivar is sterile and rarely sets seed, but the toxic chemistry is in the plant regardless.) Grow it for the bees, not the table.[1][3]

plants.blurb: The classic fragrant cottage-garden wallflower — jewel-toned spring spires of clove-scented bloom. Grown as a biennial on the Front Range.
plants.water: Low–moderate; sharp drainage
plants.spread: Clumping — biennial; may self-sow
plants.seasons: Low evergreen rosette; intensely fragrant gold/orange/red/mahogany flowers mid–late spring
plants.wildlife: Fragrant flowers draw bees & early butterflies
plants.toxic: Foliage & seeds contain cardiac glycosides — not for eating
plants.origin: Southern Europe — Mediterranean coasts and rocky hillsides
plants.habitat: Rocky coastal cliffs, old walls, and dry Mediterranean scrub

edible.summary: A fragrant ornamental wallflower grown for spring color and scent — not a food plant; do not eat any part.[1][3]
edible.caution: English wallflower is not edible: its foliage and seeds contain cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) — heart-active compounds the plant makes as a chemical defense — so no part should be eaten despite the sweet floral fragrance. Grow it for the bees and the scent, not for consumption.[3]

plants.blurb: A long-blooming perennial wallflower whose flowers open apricot and age to mauve-pink — months of warm color. Short-lived; needs sharp drainage.
plants.water: Low–moderate; sharp drainage
plants.spread: Clumping — bushy short-lived perennial

plants.wildlife: Long bloom = extended bee & butterfly nectar
plants.toxic: Foliage & seeds contain cardiac glycosides — not for eating
plants.origin: Southern Europe and the Mediterranean — garden hybrid of Erysimum cheiri parentage

edible.summary: An ornamental wallflower grown for its long season of color-shifting bloom — not a food plant; do not eat any part.[1][3]
edible.caution: Like other Erysimum, 'Apricot Twist' is not edible: wallflower foliage and seeds contain cardiac glycosides (cardenolides), heart-active defensive compounds, so no part should be eaten. Grow it for the pollinators, not the table.[1][3]

plants.blurb: Easy peppery-edible annual — round lily-pad leaves and spurred jewel-toned flowers that trail or climb, blooming nonstop till frost.
plants.sun: Full sun (part shade in extreme heat)
plants.water: Moderate; lean soil yields more flowers
plants.spread: Trailing/climbing annual; self-sows lightly
plants.seasons: Round lily-pad leaves & spurred orange/red/yellow flowers, summer to frost
plants.wildlife: Bees & hummingbirds; classic aphid trap-crop
plants.toxic: Edible — peppery leaves, flowers & green seed pods are all edible
plants.origin: South America — Andes of Peru and Colombia
plants.habitat: Moist disturbed slopes and stream margins in Andean foothills

edible.summary: A genuinely edible flower: the leaves, flowers, flower buds, and immature green seed pods are all edible raw, with a peppery, watercress-like bite.[1][2]
edible.parts: The young leaves, the open flowers, the flower buds, and the immature (green, still-soft) seed pods are all edible. Younger leaves are the most tender and flavorful; older leaves turn bitter, and the flowers are milder than the leaves.[1][2][3]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves and flowers fresh — toss them into green and potato salads, egg and seafood dishes, and herb vinegars, or use the bright flowers as an edible garnish (they don't dry well, so use fresh).[1][2] The flavor is peppery and cress-like, from the same mustard-oil (glucosinolate) compounds as garden cress.[3] Pickle the immature green seed pods in vinegar as a caper substitute — the classic "poor man's capers."[2][1]
edible.caution: Eat only flowers and foliage you know are nasturtium and that were grown without pesticides or other chemicals; rinse thoroughly before eating.[1] Introduce edible flowers slowly into your diet and eat in moderation — the peppery glucosinolate compounds are pungent and can be irritating in quantity, and anyone with kidney issues should be cautious with the mustard-oil content.[1][4] Note that nasturtium is listed as toxic to cats, dogs, and horses, so keep pets from grazing on it even though it is fine for people.[3]

plants.blurb: The classic bedding 'geranium' — bold flower balls over scallop-edged zoned leaves all summer. Frost-tender; overwinter indoors or from cuttings.
plants.sun: Full sun (part shade in extreme heat)
plants.water: Moderate; let the topsoil dry between waterings
plants.spread: Mounding tender subshrub
plants.seasons: Rounded zoned leaves; rounded clusters of red/pink/white/coral flowers all summer
plants.wildlife: Some bee interest; mainly ornamental
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic to cats, dogs & horses (ASPCA); sap may irritate skin
plants.origin: South Africa (Cape region) — garden hybrid of P. zonale and P. inquinans
plants.habitat: Rocky hillsides and scrubby coastal slopes of the South African Cape

edible.summary: Not a food plant — zonal geranium is grown purely as an ornamental and is mildly toxic to pets; do not eat it.[1][2]
edible.caution: The ASPCA lists geraniums (Pelargonium spp.) as toxic to cats, dogs, and horses; the toxic principles are the essential-oil compounds geraniol and linalool, and ingestion can cause vomiting, loss of appetite, depression, and skin (contact) dermatitis. It is not part of the human diet, and handling the foliage can irritate sensitive skin. Keep plants away from grazing pets.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Grown for fragrance — brush the deeply cut leaves for rose, lemon, or mint scent (lovely in baking). Frost-tender; an easy windowsill keeper.
plants.water: Moderate; well-drained
plants.spread: Bushy tender subshrub

plants.wildlife: Aromatic foliage; small flowers draw some bees

plants.origin: South Africa — Cape Provinces and Northern Provinces

edible.card: Fragrant leaves flavor sugar, jelly & baking
edible.summary: The fragrant leaves of the culinary scented types (rose, lemon) are used as a flavoring — to infuse sugar, jellies, baked goods, beverages, and teas — not eaten as a leafy green.[1][3]
edible.parts: Only the aromatic leaves of the named culinary scented geraniums (rose geranium, Pelargonium graveolens, and lemon-scented kin) are used in the kitchen, and used as a flavoring rather than as bulk food. Rose geranium is recognized as safe as a food flavoring (FEMA GRAS).[1][3]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves to impart fragrance: layer them in a jar of sugar to scent it, line a cake or pound-cake pan with leaves before baking, infuse them into jellies, syrups, punches, vinegars, and teas, then lift the leaf out before serving.[1][3] The leaf flavors the dish and is typically removed rather than eaten whole.[1]
edible.caution: Use the leaf as a flavoring, not as a bulk food — infuse and remove it, don't eat it by the handful, and use only the named culinary scented types (rose/lemon) grown without pesticides.[1][3] The more pungent scented cultivars are meant for potpourri and perfume, not eating.[1] Important for pet owners: the ASPCA lists Pelargonium (geranium) as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses — the toxic principles geraniol and linalool can cause vomiting, loss of appetite, depression, and dermatitis — so keep pets from chewing the plant even though the leaves are used in human food.[4]

plants.blurb: The earliest, most reliable crocus — slender lilac cups that naturalize into spring drifts and feed the very first bees.
plants.water: Low; water in growth, dry summer dormancy
plants.spread: Naturalizing; spreads by corm offsets and self-seeding into colonies over time
plants.seasons: Slender lilac-to-purple cups Feb–Mar, often pushing through snow; grassy foliage Mar–May; fully dormant May–Sep; corms visible as dried tufts at soil surface in winter
plants.wildlife: Premier early-spring pollen and nectar source for queen bumblebees and mining bees; flowers open only in sun, closing at night and in cloud to protect pollen
plants.deer: Deer-resistant; avoided by deer and rabbits; less susceptible to squirrel foraging than other crocus, though rodent protection is prudent
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic — corms contain colchicine-like alkaloids; ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea in humans and pets. CRITICAL LOOK-ALIKE: Autumn crocus (Colchicum autumnale) is DEADLY, containing colchicine — it looks nearly identical but is highly toxic to humans, pets, and livestock. Spring Crocus has 3 stamens; Colchicum has 6. Autumn crocus blooms in fall with NO foliage present. Never harvest or eat unmarked corms.
plants.origin: Balkans — Bulgaria, Serbia, Hungary, Romania
plants.habitat: Open deciduous woodland, scrubby hillsides, and shaded banks

edible.summary: Snow crocus is not eaten — the corms contain alkaloids and are mildly toxic to humans and pets. The CRITICAL hazard is confusion with the deadly autumn crocus (Colchicum), which looks nearly identical.[1][3]
edible.caution: The corms of Crocus tommasinianus contain colchicine-like alkaloids that cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea if ingested; severity is generally low for a small accidental exposure, but the plant is not a food crop and should never be eaten. The far greater danger is mistaking it for Colchicum autumnale (autumn crocus / meadow saffron), which is HIGHLY TOXIC and potentially fatal to humans, pets, and livestock: Colchicum contains colchicine, an alkaloid that causes severe bloody vomiting and diarrhea, multi-organ damage, bone marrow suppression, and respiratory failure. Every part of Colchicum is dangerous — especially the bulb, which is nearly indistinguishable from a Crocus corm without a label. How to tell them apart: true spring Crocus has 3 stamens and flowers in late winter/spring WITH foliage present; Colchicum has 6 stamens, flowers in autumn WITH NO foliage (leaves emerge separately in spring). Never harvest unlabeled corms from the ground. Keep all Crocus/Colchicum corms and foliage away from children, dogs, cats, and livestock. If ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) immediately.[1][3]

plants.blurb: Nodding white bells that push up through late-winter snow — among the first nectar and pollen of the year.
plants.water: Low–moderate; moist in late winter, drier when dormant
plants.spread: Clump-forming; spreads slowly by bulb offsets and seed to build compact colonies; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Nodding white and green bells Jan–Mar, often through snow; straplike blue-green foliage Mar–May; fully dormant May–Sep; no above-ground presence in summer or fall
plants.wildlife: Vital early-season pollen and nectar for queen bumblebees and solitary bees on the few warm late-winter days when they forage; one of the only flowers open in Feb–Mar on the Front Range
plants.deer: Deer-resistant; rarely browsed by deer, rabbits, voles, or other rodents — likely due to alkaloid content
plants.toxic: ALL PARTS TOXIC — contains phenanthridine alkaloids including galantamine and lycorine, concentrated especially in the bulbs. Ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Keep away from children, pets, and livestock. Do not eat any part.
plants.origin: Europe — Spain east to Ukraine and western Turkey
plants.habitat: Damp deciduous woodland, shaded meadows, and stream banks

edible.summary: Snowdrops are poisonous — all parts contain phenanthridine alkaloids (galantamine, lycorine) that cause vomiting and diarrhea; the bulbs are especially dangerous. Do not eat any part.[1]
edible.caution: All parts of Galanthus nivalis are toxic: the plant contains phenanthridine alkaloids including lycorine (which causes nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea) and galantamine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor used in purified pharmaceutical form for Alzheimer's treatment, but dangerous in raw plant form). The bulbs contain the highest concentration of alkaloids and are the greatest risk; they can be mistaken for edible bulbs (e.g. wild garlic, Allium ursinum) — never harvest unlabeled bulbs. NC State Extension rates the toxicity as low in severity for small exposures, but ingestion of multiple bulbs can cause significant gastrointestinal illness. The plant is also resistant to deer, rabbits, and rodents, which reflects its chemical deterrents. Keep all parts — especially bulbs — away from children, dogs, cats, and livestock. If ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) immediately.[1]

plants.blurb: Bright yellow buttercup cups on green ruffs in late winter — one of the earliest pollen sources, lovely beside snowdrops.
plants.water: Low–moderate; moist late winter, dry summer dormancy fine
plants.spread: Slowly naturalizing; spreads by tuber offsets and prolific self-seeding to form loose colonies; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Bright yellow cupped flowers on a green leafy ruff Jan–Mar, often through snow; deeply divided basal leaves Mar–May; fully dormant May–Sep; no above-ground presence in summer
plants.wildlife: One of the most important early pollen sources for queen bumblebees and solitary bees in late winter; flowers on warm late-winter days when few other pollen sources exist
plants.deer: Deer-resistant; rarely browsed — toxicity deters most wildlife grazers
plants.toxic: ALL PARTS HIGHLY TOXIC — contains cardiac glycosides of the bufadienolide group; potentially fatal if eaten. Do not eat any part. Keep away from children, pets, and livestock.

edible.summary: Winter aconite is highly toxic — all parts contain cardiac glycosides of the bufadienolide group; potentially fatal if eaten. Do not eat any part.[1]
edible.caution: All parts of Eranthis hyemalis are highly toxic, with the tubers being especially dangerous. The plant contains cardiac glycosides of the bufadienolide group — the same class of compounds found in bufotoxin (toad venom). NC State Extension rates the toxicity HIGH and notes it 'may be fatal if eaten.' Symptoms of ingestion include stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, disturbed vision, shortness of breath, irregular pulse, bradycardia, coma, and potential cardiac arrest or sudden death. The small tubers can be mistaken for edible tubers by foragers. Because the plant flowers so early (often through snow), curious children may be attracted to it before adults are aware it is in the garden. Keep all parts of the plant strictly away from children, dogs, cats, horses, and livestock. Wear gloves when dividing or replanting. If ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) immediately — this is a medical emergency.[1]

plants.blurb: Jewel-blue dwarf bulb iris that flowers at snowmelt, weeks before anything else — tiny but brilliant.
plants.water: Low; dry summer baking preferred
plants.spread: Clumping; multiplies slowly by bulblets; does not spread aggressively
plants.seasons: Jewel-toned violet-blue to purple flowers in early March (sometimes late Feb in a warm spell); grassy strap-like leaves emerge with and after blooms, elongating to 12 in before yellowing; fully dormant and invisible by midsummer; bulbs sit bone-dry underground through Colorado's hot summer — this is what they need
plants.wildlife: Very early bee forage — one of the first pollen and nectar sources of the year for queen bumblebees and solitary mining bees emerging on warm late-winter days
plants.deer: Deer- and rabbit-resistant; toxic foliage and bulbs are generally avoided
plants.toxic: Toxic to people, cats, dogs, and horses — contains pentacyclic terpenoids (zeorin, missourin, missouriensin), irisin, iridin, and irisine; highest concentration in the bulbs (rhizomes); causes nausea, vomiting, drooling, diarrhea, and skin irritation on contact. Do not eat any part.
plants.origin: Caucasus, N. & W. Iran, E. Turkey, and N. Iraq
plants.habitat: Rocky mountain slopes with hot, dry summers and cold winters

edible.summary: Dwarf iris is toxic to people, cats, dogs, and horses — do not eat any part.[1][2]
edible.caution: All parts of *Iris reticulata* and iris species generally are toxic. The primary toxic compounds are pentacyclic terpenoids — specifically zeorin, missourin, and missouriensin — along with the glycosides irisin, iridin, and irisine. Highest concentrations occur in the bulbs (rhizomes). Ingestion in people causes nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea; in dogs and cats it also causes salivation, drooling, and lethargy; horses are similarly affected. Contact with the sap causes skin and eye irritation. NC State Extension rates the toxicity severity as low (meaning toxic only in significant quantities), but the ASPCA lists iris as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses regardless of amount — treat any ingestion as a concern and contact ASPCA Poison Control (888) 426-4435. Wear gloves when handling bulbs.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Leathery evergreen perennial whose white cups open in the dead of winter — one of the very few true midwinter flowers for early bees.
plants.water: Moderate; consistent moisture in part-shade, not drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Slow-growing clump; does not spread or self-sow aggressively; takes 2–3 years to establish
plants.seasons: Evergreen leathery dark-green leaves year-round; white (aging to pale pink) cup flowers Dec–Mar in a sheltered Front Range spot or mild winter — one of very few plants blooming in true winter; flowers fade and seedheads form by spring
plants.wildlife: Early bee nectar and pollen on warm winter days (Dec–Mar) — one of the rare genuine winter forage sources; deer- and rabbit-resistant
plants.deer: Strongly deer- and rabbit-resistant; all parts are toxic and browsing animals avoid it
plants.toxic: ALL PARTS TOXIC to people, cats, dogs, and horses — contains cardiac glycosides (bufadienolides), saponins, and protoanemonin; sap is a skin and eye irritant. Ingestion causes drooling, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and depression. Wear gloves when handling; keep away from children, pets, and livestock.
plants.origin: European Alps and N. Apennines (Austria, Switzerland, Italy, Slovenia)
plants.habitat: Mountain woodland and scrub on limestone, in part to full shade

edible.summary: Christmas rose is seriously toxic — all parts are poisonous to people, dogs, cats, and horses. Do not eat any part, and wear gloves when handling.[1][2]
edible.caution: All parts of *Helleborus niger* — leaves, roots, flowers, fruits, stems, bark, seeds, and sap — are toxic. The ASPCA identifies the toxic compounds as bufadienolides (steroidal cardiac glycosides), additional glycosides, veratrin (a neurotoxic alkaloid mixture), and protoanemonin (an irritant from the ranunculin precursor). NC State Extension rates the toxicity as medium severity (toxic only in larger quantities) but confirms all plant parts are poisonous. In pets, ingestion causes drooling, abdominal pain, diarrhea, colic, and depression; cardiac glycosides at higher doses can affect heart rhythm in all species. The sap is a skin and eye irritant causing contact dermatitis — wear gloves and eye protection when cutting stems or removing leaves. Keep away from children and pets at all times. If ingestion is suspected, call ASPCA Poison Control at (888) 426-4435.[1][2]
