plants.blurb: Low evergreen mat that sheets itself in tiny pink bells from late winter — a genuine cold-season bloomer (needs acid soil, so grow it in a pot here).
plants.water: Moderate; even moisture, never bone-dry or waterlogged
plants.spread: Slowly spreading mat; rooting stems spread 1–2 ft over several years; not invasive
plants.seasons: Dense evergreen needle-like foliage year-round (deep green, or bronze to gold in some cultivars); tiny urn-shaped pink to crimson flowers Jan–Mar — a genuine winter bloomer; spent flowers cling attractively until sheared
plants.wildlife: Excellent late-winter bee forage — honey bees and early-emerging solitary bees work the flowers on warm days, making it one of the most valuable cold-season pollinator plants available
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant; the tough, needle-like foliage is largely avoided
plants.toxic: Not considered toxic to people or pets; no significant toxicity concerns reported. Not a food plant.
plants.origin: Mountains of central and southern Europe (Alps of Austria, Italy, and Switzerland)
plants.habitat: Alpine and subalpine coniferous woodland margins and rocky moors on acidic to neutral soils

edible.summary: Winter heath is not a food plant and has no known edible use. It is not considered notably toxic.
edible.caution: No significant toxicity has been established for *Erica carnea* in the scientific or veterinary literature, and the ASPCA does not list it as a toxic plant. It is simply not a food plant and should not be eaten. If a child or pet eats a significant quantity of the foliage or flowers, contact your local poison control center (1-800-222-1222) or veterinarian as a precaution.

shots.cap: Winter — a dense spreading mat smothered in pink urns · © adtll (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — the sprawling ground-hugging mat in full bloom · © bianca_t (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — pale pink urn-shaped flowers with dark anthers · © maxing (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — a spray of urn flowers with needle foliage in whorls · © bernardwieser (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Twig detail — tiny needle leaves in whorls with pink buds · © lastovka (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Late winter — a near clump showing the spreading habit, pink urns over green needle foliage · © hartmutschnuchel (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Late winter — the evergreen mat threading over alpine rock in its natural setting · © William Stephens (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: Aromatic Mediterranean evergreen herb; needle foliage and tiny blue winter flowers bees work — pot it and bring it in over winter.
plants.size: 2–4 ft × 2–4 ft in a large container; wild plants reach 4–5 ft
plants.water: Low; let it dry between waterings — pots dry fast, check often
plants.spread: Clumping; upright or mounding depending on cultivar, not spreading
plants.seasons: Evergreen silver-green needle foliage year-round; pale blue flowers from late winter through spring indoors, and again in spring after it goes back out
plants.wildlife: Bees and butterflies visit the flowers; a valuable late-winter nectar source when blooming indoors
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses
plants.origin: Mediterranean basin — S. Europe, N. Africa, and the Middle East
plants.habitat: Dry rocky coastal scrub and garrigue on thin limestone soils

edible.summary: One of the most widely used culinary herbs: the fresh or dried leaves flavour roasted meat, bread, soups, and vegetables; the flowers are milder and can be used as a garnish.[1][3]
edible.parts: The fresh or dried needle-like leaves are the primary culinary part — harvest young stem tips for the best flavour. The small pale-blue flowers are also edible and carry a milder rosemary flavour; they can be sprinkled over salads, pasta, or savoury dishes as a garnish.[3][2]
edible.preparation: Strip leaves from the stem or use whole sprigs for roasting and slow cooking; remove whole sprigs before serving. Chop finely for bread doughs, marinades, and herb butter. Use the flowers fresh only — they do not dry well and are best used immediately as a garnish or infused briefly into syrups or honey.[3]
edible.caution: Rosemary is safe in normal culinary quantities and is non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses.[4] However, concentrated rosemary essential oil — which is far more potent than the herb — should never be taken internally, and medicinal doses of rosemary extract have been associated with adverse effects including seizures at very high levels. People who are pregnant are traditionally advised to avoid rosemary in quantities beyond normal food seasoning. For kitchen use with fresh or dried herb at ordinary culinary amounts, no caution is needed. Harvest only from plants you know have not been treated with systemic pesticides.[3]

plants.blurb: Low honey-scented carpet of tiny white-to-purple flowers from spring to frost — a magnet for bees and hoverflies.
plants.water: Low–moderate; water in heat, otherwise tough
plants.spread: Mat-forming annual; self-sows readily
plants.seasons: Dense mounds of tiny four-petaled flowers spring through frost; white varieties most fragrant; blooms heaviest in cool weather, pauses in peak summer heat
plants.wildlife: Exceptional beneficial-insect plant — attracts honey bees, native bees, hoverflies, parasitic wasps, and predatory beetles; a classic companion plant for drawing beneficial insects into the garden
plants.deer: Generally ignored by deer; low, mat-forming habit makes it an unappealing target
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses
plants.origin: Mediterranean basin and Macaronesia (Canary Islands, Azores)
plants.habitat: Coastal sands, sea cliffs, and rocky shores

edible.summary: Sweet alyssum is non-toxic (confirmed by NC State Extension), and as a member of the mustard family (Brassicaceae) its young leaves and flowers have occasionally been noted as edible with a peppery bite — but no university extension edible-flower guide reviewed (CSU, UMN) lists it as a culinary plant, and coverage is too thin to recommend it as food.[1][2]
edible.caution: Sweet alyssum is classified as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses by NC State Extension, and no known toxicity to humans has been reported. However, no major university extension edible-flower resource — including CSU Extension's Colorado edible-flowers guide and the University of Minnesota's edible-flowers guide — lists it as a culinary plant. The plant is a Brassicaceae member and its peppery character is documented, but authoritative confirmation of safe culinary use is absent from the sources consulted. Do not consume sweet alyssum as a food plant until better-sourced edibility guidance is available. Grow it for its exceptional pollinator value; enjoy its honey-almond fragrance without eating it.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Cheerful cool-season faces that bloom through frost — easy potted colour bees visit on warm days.
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist, cool-season
plants.spread: Mounding; does not spread or self-sow aggressively
plants.seasons: Bold 1–2 in five-petaled flowers in spring and fall; blooms best below 75°F; fades in summer heat; may rebloom in September from fall-planted stock
plants.wildlife: Bees visit the flowers on warm days in early spring and fall; an important cool-season pollen and nectar source when little else is blooming
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses at normal garden exposure
plants.origin: Garden hybrid — wild ancestors from European mountain meadows (V. tricolor, V. lutea, V. altaica)
plants.habitat: Cultivated; wild ancestors grow in European and Asian mountain meadows and open rocky slopes

edible.summary: Pansy flowers are edible and widely used as a culinary garnish — eaten raw, candied, used on cakes, or floated in drinks; the flavour is mild and grassy.[1][3]
edible.parts: The flowers are the edible part. They can be eaten whole — petals and all — or separated into individual petals. The flavour is mild and slightly grassy; some darker-coloured varieties have a mildly stronger taste. The leaves and stems are not typically used as food.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Use the fresh flowers whole or in separated petals as a garnish for salads, desserts, cakes, and drinks. They can be candied by brushing with beaten egg white and dusting with superfine sugar, then allowing to dry — the candied flowers keep for several days and are a classic decoration for cakes and cupcakes. Harvest in the morning, rinse gently, and use the same day; pansies do not keep well once picked. A note from NC State Extension: dark-coloured flowers may temporarily colour the tongue.[1][3]
edible.caution: Eat only flowers you know are unsprayed — plants purchased from garden centres or nurseries are frequently treated with systemic pesticides that make all parts of the plant unsafe to eat, even after washing. Grow your own from seed or from organic/edible-flower-certified transplants if you intend to eat them. Introduce edible flowers new to your diet in small quantities to check for any personal sensitivity. Pansy flowers are generally non-toxic; no significant toxicity to humans is documented at typical garnish amounts, and the plant is not listed as toxic to dogs, cats, or horses.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Ozark native shrub that unfurls spidery yellow-orange ribbons on bare branches in late winter — fragrant, and the rare woody winter bloomer.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade; flowers best in full sun
plants.water: Moderate; consistent moisture, not for dry zones
plants.spread: Suckering — slowly spreads from the base; prune root suckers to control
plants.seasons: Fragrant yellow-to-orange spidery flowers Jan–Mar on bare stems; green foliage summer; yellow-orange fall color; bare architectural stems in winter
plants.wildlife: Rare late-winter pollen and nectar for queen bumblebees and mining bees active on warm days; songbirds eat the small fruits
plants.toxic: Not known to be toxic; the topical witch-hazel astringent (bark/leaf extract) is for external use only — not for ingestion
plants.origin: South-central US (Ozark Plateau: MO, AR, OK) — N. American native, not native to CO
plants.habitat: Rocky streambanks, gravel bars & moist riparian woodland

edible.summary: Not a food plant. Witch hazel bark and leaf are the source of the familiar topical astringent (witch-hazel water), used externally for skin, bruises, and hemorrhoids — not for ingestion.[1]
edible.caution: The bark and leaves contain tannins and the mild phenolic hamamelistannin, which are irritants when swallowed. Commercial witch-hazel astringent is a steam distillate intended for external use only; ingesting it — or chewing the bark — causes nausea, vomiting, and gastrointestinal upset. No part of Hamamelis vernalis is a recognized food. The small hard nutlets (seeds) are similarly inedible. Keep products out of reach of children. There is no edible use to confuse with a look-alike; the shaggy winter-blooming habit is distinctive.[1]

plants.blurb: Clouds of tiny yellow flowers on bare branches in earliest spring, then edible red fruit — a tough, early-feeding dogwood.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade; best bloom in full sun
plants.water: Moderate; adaptable, drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Multi-stem clump; may produce occasional suckers — easy to control
plants.seasons: Clusters of tiny yellow flowers on bare stems late March–early April; glossy green summer foliage; olive-shaped red 'cherries' ripen mid-summer; orange-red to purple fall color; handsome multi-stem form in winter
plants.wildlife: Among the earliest yellow flowers of spring — a critical early pollen source for native bees and honey bees before most trees leaf out; ripening fruit eaten by birds and squirrels
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant; leaves and twigs may be browsed lightly but not a preferred target
plants.toxic: No known toxicity; ripe fruit flesh is edible, pit is not eaten
plants.origin: Central & southern Europe to western Asia (Turkey, Caucasus)
plants.habitat: Woodland edges & scrub on limestone soils

edible.summary: The ripe red fruit is edible and long-used in European and Middle Eastern cuisine — tart and juicy when fully soft, best cooked into jelly, jam, syrup, or juice; the pit is not eaten.[1][2]
edible.parts: The edible part is the ripe fruit flesh (mesocarp) of the olive-shaped drupe, which turns deep red and softens in mid-summer (August on the Front Range). The flavor is tart and pleasantly acidic — described as having a plum-like quality when fully ripe. Cornelian cherries have a very long history of use in Middle Eastern and European cooking: jams, jellies, syrups, pickles, liqueurs, and dried fruit are all traditional preparations. The large, hard pit (stone) inside each drupe is not eaten.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Pick fully ripe, deeply red, slightly yielding fruit. Cook: simmer with water and sugar, strain through a jelly bag for clear jelly or press for jam; the tart flavor concentrates beautifully. Juice by macerating ripe fruit and straining. For liqueur, steep in vodka or grappa with sugar. Fruit can also be dried whole and reconstituted. Unripe fruit is sharply astringent and should not be eaten raw — ripeness is critical. No toxic compounds are present in ripe fruit; no special detoxification is needed beyond cooking.[1][2]
edible.caution: No significant toxicity is recorded for Cornus mas. The ripe fruit flesh is safe to eat. The pit (stone) inside each drupe should not be chewed or eaten; simply spit it out or strain it out during cooking. Eat only fully ripe (deep red, soft) fruit — underripe cornelian cherries are very astringent and unpleasant. Do not confuse with ornamental dogwoods (Cornus florida, Cornus kousa) whose fruits are mildly toxic; Cornus mas fruits are distinctly olive-shaped and ripened to a solid deep red, quite unlike any toxic look-alike.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Silvery furry catkins on bare stems in late winter — one of the single most important early pollen sources for native and honey bees.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade; best catkins in full sun
plants.water: High — moist/wet soil; a wetland shrub, not for dry zones
plants.spread: Suckering — forms multi-stem clumps; fast-growing, contains well by coppicing
plants.seasons: Silvery-gray catkins on bare stems late Feb–Mar; bright yellow pollen phase in March (male plants); fresh green foliage spring through fall; gold fall color; clean bare stems in winter
plants.wildlife: Premier early bee forage — male catkins deliver one of the highest-sugar pollens of any late-winter plant; also supports Viceroy butterfly larvae and provides winter bud forage for grouse and songbirds
plants.deer: Browsed by deer — not deer-resistant; also browsed by moose and rabbits
plants.toxic: Not a food plant; bark contains salicin (the precursor to salicylic acid/aspirin) — medicinal in traditional use but not for casual ingestion
plants.origin: Northern & eastern North America (boreal Canada, NE US to the Rockies) — N. American native, not native to the Colorado Front Range
plants.habitat: Wet meadows, marsh edges, streambanks & poorly drained thickets

edible.summary: Not a food plant. Willow bark contains salicin and related salicylates — the traditional medicinal compounds behind aspirin — which are pharmacologically active and not for casual consumption.[1][2]
edible.caution: No part of Salix discolor is a recognized food. The bark and twigs contain salicin (a salicylate glycoside) that historically was chewed or brewed as a fever-and-pain remedy by Indigenous peoples across North America — this is the compound that led to the development of aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid). While a single catkin petal touched to the lips for flavor is essentially harmless, ingesting willow bark preparations without medical guidance is not safe: salicylates can cause stomach irritation, anti-platelet effects, and in large doses salicylate toxicity, especially in children or people sensitive to aspirin. The catkins themselves (the silky bracts) are not eaten. Do not confuse with edible willows used in foraging contexts — those refer specifically to the young leaves of certain species used in very small quantities; pussy willow is not a culinary plant.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Colorado's own wild sunflower — branching stems carry a parade of golden, dark-eyed heads that bees mob all summer and goldfinches strip for seed in fall.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Self-sows freely into drifts; not invasive
plants.seasons: Golden dark-centered heads Jul–Oct; seed-packed heads ripen and feed birds into fall; killed by hard frost
plants.wildlife: Pollinator and bird magnet — native bees, honey bees and butterflies on the bloom; seeds are prime forage for goldfinches and other songbirds
plants.deer: Not notably deer-resistant; young plants may be browsed, mature stalks usually left
plants.toxic: Not toxic to people; seeds are eaten — note hulls/foliage can be a mild digestive irritant to pets in quantity

edible.summary: The ripe seeds are edible and nutritious — roasted as a snack or pressed for oil — and the wild sunflower is a long-documented Native American food and oilseed crop.[1][4]
edible.parts: The mature seeds (kernels) are the food part, eaten roasted and salted, ground into meal, or pressed for oil; the wild species is the ancestor of the cultivated oilseed and confection sunflowers. Unopened flower buds and young shoots have also been eaten cooked, though they are minor and bitter compared with the seed.[1][4]
edible.preparation: Let heads ripen fully, then dry, thresh, and clean the seeds. Roast in or out of the hull for snacking, or hull and grind for meal; the kernels are also pressed for cooking oil. Rinse and roast lightly to improve flavor and digestibility.[4]
edible.caution: The seed kernels are the safe food part. Eating large amounts of the fibrous hulls is not recommended for people and can cause digestive upset, and the hulls and foliage may irritate pets' digestion if eaten in quantity. As with any wild plant, be certain of identification before eating, and don't harvest seed from roadsides or sprayed ground where it may carry herbicide or contaminants.[1][4]

plants.blurb: Colorado's wild blue flag — pale lavender-blue, purple-veined irises rise from grassy fans across wet mountain meadows in late spring.
plants.water: High in spring; needs steady moisture through bloom, tolerates dry summer
plants.spread: Slowly clumping/spreading by rhizome into dense patches; not aggressive in dry gardens
plants.seasons: Lavender-blue, purple-veined flowers May–Jun; upright grassy fans summer; foliage dies back, dormant in dry late summer/winter
plants.wildlife: Flowers visited by bees and hummingbirds; forms cover in moist meadows
plants.deer: Avoided by deer and cattle — the tough, slightly toxic foliage is left ungrazed
plants.toxic: Toxic — rhizomes and rootstock contain irritant compounds causing GI upset (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) and skin irritation in people, pets, and livestock

edible.summary: Rocky Mountain iris is toxic and is not eaten — the rhizomes and rootstock contain irritant compounds that cause digestive upset and skin irritation in people, pets, and livestock.[1][2]
edible.caution: Do not eat any part of this plant. The thick rhizomes and rootstock contain irritant resinous/glycoside compounds; ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea in humans and pets, and handling the cut rhizome can cause skin irritation (wear gloves when dividing). The tough foliage is also unpalatable and is avoided by cattle and deer — its toxicity is part of why it is left ungrazed in meadows. Keep rhizomes and foliage away from children, dogs, cats, and livestock. If ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435).[1][2]

plants.blurb: A dainty native onion whose flower stalk crooks at the tip so its loose umbel of pink bells nods toward the ground, alive with bees in midsummer.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumps slowly from offset bulbs and self-sows modestly; well-behaved
plants.seasons: Nodding pink (sometimes white) flower clusters Jun–Aug; grassy onion-scented foliage spring–summer; dies back, dormant in winter
plants.wildlife: Nectar for bees, butterflies and hummingbirds; seeds and bulbs taken by birds and small mammals
plants.deer: Largely deer- and rabbit-resistant — the onion scent deters most browsers, though elk/deer graze early herbage
plants.toxic: Edible in normal food amounts; like all onions, large quantities are toxic to dogs, cats and livestock

edible.summary: The bulbs and leaves are edible and used like a mild onion or chive — but ONLY eat plants that smell strongly of onion, because the deadly death-camas grows in the same meadows and looks similar.[3][4]
edible.parts: The small bulbs and the grassy leaves are the edible parts, with a mild oniony flavor; they are eaten raw in small amounts or cooked as a seasoning, and the plant is a documented Native American food and seasoning. Use it as you would a chive or scallion.[3]
edible.preparation: Snip leaves through the growing season; dig bulbs in summer. Use raw as a mild flavoring or cook into dishes like any onion. Eat in ordinary culinary amounts — like all onions, very large quantities can upset the stomach.[3]
edible.caution: CRITICAL look-alike: deadly death-camas (Toxicoscordion / Zigadenus) grows in the same mountain meadows and grasslands and has similar grass-like leaves and a bulb that can be mistaken for a wild onion — but it contains a poisonous alkaloid in all parts at all growth stages and has caused human and livestock deaths. THE TELL: true Allium smells strongly of onion; death-camas has NO onion odor. NEVER eat any wild 'onion' bulb or leaf unless it smells clearly of onion, and be certain of your identification before harvesting. Also note that, like all onions/garlic, the plant is toxic to dogs, cats, and livestock in quantity. If poisoning is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435).[3][4]

plants.blurb: A woolly silver-leaved mound that throws up long-stemmed, brilliant lemon-yellow daisies for months on end in the hottest, driest, leanest ground.
plants.water: Very low; xeric once established — overwatering kills it
plants.spread: Short-lived; self-sows to perpetuate itself; clumping, non-aggressive
plants.seasons: Lemon-yellow daisies over silver foliage spring through fall; papery seedheads follow; foliage shabby in cold winters
plants.wildlife: Long-season nectar/pollen for native bees and butterflies; special value to native bees
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant — woolly, bitter, toxic foliage is avoided by browsers and livestock
plants.toxic: Toxic to livestock — foliage poisons sheep and goats (and cattle); not a human food plant
plants.origin: Southwestern U.S. deserts (AZ, NM, TX, N. Mexico)
plants.habitat: Desert flats, washes, gravelly slopes & roadsides

edible.summary: Desert marigold is not a food plant and is toxic to grazing livestock — its foliage poisons sheep and goats (and cattle) and it has no edible use for people.[1][2]
edible.caution: Do not eat any part of this plant, and be aware it is a documented livestock-poisoning plant on Southwestern rangeland: the foliage is toxic to sheep and goats (and cattle), causing illness when grazed, especially where other forage is scarce. The woolly, bitter leaves are normally avoided by animals, but hungry stock can be poisoned. It has no traditional or documented human food use. Keep it away from grazing animals; if livestock or pet poisoning is suspected, contact a veterinarian or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435).[1][2]

plants.blurb: The cheerful prairie daisy — golden petals around a dark chocolate cone, blooming for months and seeding itself into easygoing drifts that bees and goldfinches love.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Short-lived but self-sows freely to persist in colonies; not aggressive
plants.seasons: Golden dark-coned daisies Jun–Oct; dark seedheads stand into fall and winter feeding finches; dies back in winter
plants.wildlife: Strong pollinator plant — bees and butterflies on the bloom, a butterfly larval host, and goldfinches strip the seedheads
plants.deer: Moderately to strongly deer-resistant — the bristly, hairy foliage is usually passed over
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic if eaten and a contact skin irritant for some people; not a food plant — handle and site away from grazing pets
plants.origin: Central & eastern North America
plants.habitat: Prairies, meadows, open woods and roadsides

edible.summary: Black-eyed Susan is not a food plant — it is considered mildly toxic if eaten and can cause skin irritation in sensitive people from handling.[1][2]
edible.caution: Do not eat any part of this plant. Rudbeckia hirta is regarded as mildly toxic if ingested and the bristly, hairy foliage and sap can trigger contact dermatitis (skin irritation) in sensitive people, so wear gloves when cutting or handling lots of it. While historically used externally/medicinally by some Native American groups, it is not a culinary plant and should not be treated as edible. Keep it away from pets that graze on plants; if ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435).[1][2]

plants.blurb: The bulletproof bedding annual — ferny aromatic foliage topped all summer by yellow-to-mahogany ruffled blooms that shrug off heat and feed late-season pollinators.
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist, tolerates short dry spells
plants.spread: Clumping; non-running, but self-sows lightly from dropped seed
plants.seasons: Yellow, orange, and red-brown flowers early summer to frost; aromatic ferny foliage spring–fall; killed outright by the first hard frost
plants.wildlife: Open single and semi-double types draw bees, hoverflies, and butterflies; a long-season nectar source into fall; tightly double pompons offer little forage
plants.deer: Deer- and rabbit-resistant — the pungent foliage is generally passed over
plants.toxic: Low toxicity; the foliage can cause skin redness or dermatitis on contact (phototoxic thiophenes), and sap may mildly irritate pets' mouths or stomachs
plants.origin: Mexico & Guatemala
plants.habitat: Open, seasonally-moist disturbed ground and roadsides

edible.summary: French marigold petals are edible in small amounts as a colorful, mildly citrusy-to-bitter garnish; use the ray petals only and in moderation, not as a vegetable.[1][3]
edible.parts: The flower petals (ray florets) are the edible part, used fresh as a confetti-like garnish on salads, rice, soups, and cakes and as a yellow food coloring; the leaves are used sparingly for flavoring. Pull the petals from the bitter green base before use. African/American marigold (Tagetes erecta) petals are edible too but markedly more bitter, and the strongly scented foliage of all marigolds is not a salad green.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Use only the colorful ray petals, removed from the calyx, and use them sparingly — the flavor ranges from mildly citrus to distinctly bitter and pungent depending on type. Eat in small quantities as a garnish, not as a main ingredient. Always use plants you know are pesticide-free, and do not confuse culinary marigold with pot marigold/calendula (Calendula officinalis), a different, milder genus.[3]
edible.caution: Tagetes contains phototoxic thiophene compounds in its foliage, flowers, and roots: handling the plant (especially with broken skin, then sun exposure) can cause skin redness, burning, and blisters in sensitive people, so wear gloves if you react. Eat only the petals and only in small amounts; the pungent leaves and seeds are not a food. For pets, the plant is of low toxicity but the sap may cause mild mouth or gastrointestinal irritation if chewed. Anyone with a known allergy to Asteraceae (daisy-family) plants should avoid eating it.[1]

plants.blurb: Love-in-a-mist — sky-blue flowers nestled in a haze of thread-fine foliage, followed by extraordinary inflated, horned 'balloon' seedpods prized for drying.
plants.water: Moderate; even moisture, well-drained soil
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows freely into drifts where it's grown
plants.seasons: Blue (or white/pink/purple) flowers June–Aug above ferny foliage; inflated horned seedpods follow and persist; dies at frost
plants.wildlife: Open flowers offer pollen and nectar to bees and hoverflies; the dried seedheads feed seed-eating birds and are valued for cut/dried arrangements
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant — the lacy, slightly pungent foliage is usually passed over
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic in quantity — the seeds contain saponins and alkaloids; this is the ornamental love-in-a-mist, NOT the culinary black-cumin spice (N. sativa)
plants.origin: Southern Europe & N. Africa (Mediterranean)
plants.habitat: Disturbed fields, rocky slopes, and cultivated ground

edible.summary: Love-in-a-mist (Nigella damascena) is grown for its flowers and ornamental seedpods, not for food — its seeds are used only sparingly as an aromatic and can be mildly toxic in quantity. It is NOT the culinary black-cumin spice.[1][3]
edible.caution: Do not confuse Nigella damascena (love-in-a-mist / devil-in-the-bush) with Nigella sativa, the true culinary 'black cumin' / 'kalonji' spice — they are different species. The seeds of N. damascena have only limited, traditional use as a mild nutmeg-like aromatic and are not a recognized food or spice; they contain saponins and trace alkaloids (the genus carries the toxic alkaloid damascenine in damascena) and can cause stomach upset if eaten in any quantity. Treat this plant as ornamental: enjoy the flowers and dried pods, but do not eat the seeds or any other part, and keep the seedpods away from children who might mistake them for edible pods. Anyone wanting the culinary black-seed spice should buy Nigella sativa, not save seed from this ornamental.[1][3]

plants.blurb: The cottage-garden cornflower — intense true-blue (or pink/white) fringed buttons on wiry grey-green stems, an easy cool-season annual that reseeds and draws bees by the dozen.
plants.water: Low to moderate; tolerates drought once established
plants.spread: Clumping; reseeds and naturalizes readily where it's grown
plants.seasons: Blue (or pink/white) fringed flowers late spring into summer; grey-green foliage spring–summer; fades and dies at frost
plants.wildlife: A magnet for bees and butterflies; the blooms carry nectaries that draw beneficial insects, and seedheads feed finches and other small birds
plants.deer: Generally deer-resistant — the wiry, grey-felted stems are usually passed over
plants.toxic: Non-toxic — reported safe around horses, dogs, and cats; the petals are a traditional edible garnish
plants.origin: Europe & Western Asia (Mediterranean origin)
plants.habitat: Cornfields, disturbed grassland, roadsides, and waste ground

edible.summary: Cornflower petals are a long-standing edible garnish — mild, faintly sweet-to-spicy, and prized for their vivid true-blue color on salads, cakes, and as a dried tea/confetti.[1][3]
edible.parts: The flower petals (ray and disk florets) are the edible part, pulled from the bitter green base. They are used fresh to scatter brilliant blue color over salads, desserts, and drinks, and dried as a colorful component of herbal-tea blends (a signature ingredient of Earl Grey-style blends). The flavor is mild — faintly clove-like to slightly bitter — so they are valued more for color than taste.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Use only the petals, separated from the green calyx (which is bitter), as a fresh garnish or dried for tea and confetti. Use pesticide-free flowers grown for eating. Treat them as a decorative garnish, not a bulk ingredient.[3]
edible.caution: Cornflower is non-toxic and reported safe around horses, dogs, and cats, but two cautions apply: people allergic to Asteraceae (daisy-family) plants — ragweed, chrysanthemum, marigold — may react to it, and as with any edible flower you should eat only pesticide-free blooms in moderation. Use the petals only; the green base is bitter. Do not confuse the edible annual cornflower (Centaurea cyanus) with the noxious knapweeds in the same genus, which are not grown for food.[1]

plants.blurb: The most fragrant of climbing annuals — ruffled, jewel-toned blooms scrambling up a trellis on cool spring air, perfuming the garden until summer heat shuts them down.
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist, cool roots
plants.spread: Clumping climber; clings by tendrils, needs a trellis or support
plants.seasons: Intensely fragrant spring-to-early-summer flowers in many colors; declines fast in summer heat; killed by frost
plants.wildlife: Open, nectar-rich flowers draw long-tongued bumblebees and butterflies; a good cool-season nectar source before the summer heat ends bloom
plants.deer: Not reliably deer-resistant — tender new growth and flowers may be browsed
plants.toxic: Toxic if eaten — seeds and pods contain a lathyrogen (ODAP) and are poisonous despite resembling edible garden peas; toxic to horses
plants.origin: Sicily & southern Italy / Aegean (Crete)
plants.habitat: Mediterranean scrub, rocky slopes, and disturbed ground

edible.summary: Ornamental sweet pea is grown only for its flowers — it is NOT a food plant. The seeds and pods are toxic despite looking like edible garden peas, and the plant is poisonous to horses.[1][3]
edible.caution: Do not eat any part of ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus). Although the pods look like edible vegetable peas (Pisum sativum), they are inedible and poisonous: the seeds contain a lathyrogen (β-aminopropionitrile / ODAP-type compounds), and chronic or large ingestion of Lathyrus seeds causes lathyrism — a serious neurological poisoning marked by weakness, slow/weak pulse, shallow breathing, paralysis, tremors, seizures, and potentially death. The greatest practical danger is confusion with garden peas: keep sweet pea out of the vegetable garden where someone might mistake the pods, and teach children NOT to eat the pods, however pea-like they look. The plant is listed as toxic to horses (keep it away from pasture and grazing); ASPCA reports the closely related perennial sweet pea (L. latifolius) as non-toxic to dogs and cats, but the seeds/pods of any Lathyrus should be considered unsafe to eat. If ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) for people or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) for pets.[1][3]

plants.blurb: Love-lies-bleeding drapes tall stems in long, drooping ropes of crimson tassels — a dramatic ancient grain crop that doubles as an edible leafy green.
plants.water: Moderate; drought-tolerant once established, deeper water in heat
plants.spread: Single upright annual; self-sows from dropped seed but pulls easily
plants.seasons: Drooping crimson flower tassels mid-summer to first frost; bushy upright foliage spring–summer; killed outright by the first hard freeze
plants.wildlife: Seeds feed finches and other birds; the dense seedheads provide late-season forage
plants.deer: Generally not a deer favorite, but not reliably deer-proof
plants.toxic: No part toxic to people; leaves and seeds are edible cooked. Like all amaranths, foliage can concentrate nitrates when grown on heavily fertilized soil
plants.origin: Andes / South America (ancient cultigen)
plants.habitat: Cultivated fields and disturbed ground; the only amaranth grown above ~2,500 m in the Andes

edible.summary: Both the grain (seed) and the young leaves are edible — Amaranthus caudatus (kiwicha) is an ancient Andean pseudocereal — but the leaves should be cooked, not eaten raw in quantity.[2][4]
edible.parts: The small seeds are a gluten-free pseudocereal high in protein and lysine; they are ground into flour, popped like tiny popcorn, or cooked as a cereal, and in Mexico popped seeds are bound with honey into the sweet alegría.[4] The young, tender leaves are eaten as a cooked leafy green, mild and spinach-like.[2]
edible.preparation: Cook the leaves — boil or sauté them like spinach (they soften quickly and shouldn't be overcooked). Toast or pop the seeds before eating, or grind them to flour; the raw seed is not eaten as-is.[2][4]
edible.caution: Like all amaranths, the leaves can accumulate oxalates and, when the plant is grown on heavily fertilized (nitrogen-rich) soil, nitrates — so eat leaves cooked and in moderation and avoid leaves from plants pushed with synthetic fertilizer; those with kidney/oxalate concerns should limit them.[2] Be sure of your plant: this is the cultivated grain/ornamental amaranth, not a wild pigweed (do not confuse it with the noxious Palmer amaranth, Amaranthus palmeri, a Colorado-listed weed). No part of A. caudatus is reported poisonous to people.[2][4]

plants.blurb: Heat-loving celosia throws up velvety flame-like plumes or brain-folded crested combs in electric reds, oranges, and golds that shrug off the hottest Front Range summer.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought- and heat-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Upright clump; does not run, may self-sow lightly in warm sites
plants.seasons: Plumed or crested flowerheads from early summer to first frost, holding color for weeks; frost-tender foliage; killed by the first hard freeze
plants.wildlife: Flowers draw bees, butterflies, and other pollinators; seedheads feed small birds
plants.deer: Not deer-resistant — deer will browse it
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses; leaves and young shoots are eaten cooked
plants.origin: Tropical Africa & Asia
plants.habitat: Warm disturbed ground, fields, and roadsides in the tropics (weedy in parts of its range)

edible.summary: The young leaves and shoots of Celosia argentea are eaten cooked as a leafy vegetable — 'Lagos spinach' (soko) across West Africa — but garden cultivars are bred for show, not the table, and the greens are eaten cooked, not raw.[2][4]
edible.parts: The young leaves and tender shoots are the food part: an important, nutritious cooked vegetable used in soups and stews in West Africa and Southeast Asia, mild and spinach-like with no bitterness when cooked. An edible oil can be pressed from the seed.[2][4]
edible.preparation: Cook the leaves and shoots — they soften readily and should not be overcooked, holding a pleasant green color; they are added to soups, stews, and boiled-green dishes, not eaten as a raw salad.[2]
edible.caution: Eat only the young leaves/shoots and only cooked; ornamental bedding and cut-flower celosias are selected for flower form and color, not for food quality, so treat homegrown ornamental plants as a leafy green of last resort rather than a deliberate vegetable. Be certain of your identification. The plant is non-toxic to people and to dogs, cats, and horses, and PFAF lists no known culinary hazard, but it carries a medicinal caution against use by people with glaucoma (it can dilate the pupils) — that is a herbal-medicine concern, not an issue for cooked greens.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Strawflower opens stiff, papery 'everlasting' bracts in hot golds, oranges, reds, and pinks that feel crisp to the touch and dry perfectly for winter arrangements.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Upright clump; does not run, may self-sow lightly
plants.seasons: Papery daisy-like flowerheads from early summer to first frost; gray-green foliage spring–summer; frost-tender, killed by the first hard freeze
plants.wildlife: Open flowers attract butterflies and bees; seedheads draw small birds
plants.toxic: No known toxicity and no known edible use — grown ornamentally and for dried flowers
plants.origin: Australia
plants.habitat: Open grassland and open woodland across much of Australia

edible.summary: Strawflower is an ornamental grown for its papery 'everlasting' flowers — there is no documented edible use, and it is not eaten.[1]
edible.caution: Xerochrysum bracteatum is cultivated purely for its dried and cut flowers and has no known culinary use; it is not reported as a food plant, so do not eat any part of it. It is not, however, listed as notably poisonous to people or pets — it simply has no edible use. As with any non-food plant, keep flowers and foliage out of reach of small children and pets, and don't substitute it for an edible look-alike daisy.[1]

plants.blurb: The true edible 'pot marigold' — cheerful yellow-to-orange daisies on aromatic stems that bloom hardest in cool spring and fall, sulking only in midsummer heat.
plants.sun: Full sun to part afternoon shade
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist, dislikes drought and heat
plants.spread: Clumping; reseeds readily into the garden
plants.seasons: Yellow-to-orange daisies in cool spring and again in fall, pausing in peak heat; aromatic foliage spring–fall; killed by hard frost
plants.wildlife: Open daisy form is an easy landing pad for bees, hoverflies, and butterflies; a long-season nectar plant that often blooms into late fall
plants.deer: Deer- and rabbit-resistant — the resinous, scented foliage is usually left alone
plants.toxic: Non-toxic — listed as safe around dogs and cats, and the petals are a traditional edible
plants.origin: Southern Europe (Mediterranean), original wild range uncertain
plants.habitat: Disturbed, cultivated, and waste ground

edible.summary: Calendula is the true edible 'pot marigold': the petals are a long-standing culinary ingredient, used fresh or dried for golden color and a mildly bitter, peppery flavor.[1][3]
edible.parts: The flower petals (ray florets) are the edible part, pulled from the bitter central base. They add color and a slightly bitter, tangy, peppery note to salads, soups, custards, rice, cookies, herb butters, and vinegars, and dried petals are used as an inexpensive saffron-style coloring ('poor man's saffron'). The flowers and young leaves are edible but somewhat bitter; calendula is also a traditional herbal-tea and skin-salve plant.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Use the petals fresh as a garnish or steep/dry them for color; remove them from the green base, which is bitter and resinous. Use plants grown without pesticides. This is the genuinely culinary 'pot marigold' (Calendula officinalis) — distinct from the more bitter, scented Tagetes 'marigolds,' whose petals are also edible but used far more sparingly.[1][3]
edible.caution: Calendula is non-toxic and listed as safe around dogs and cats, but a few cautions apply: people with allergies to Asteraceae (daisy-family) plants — ragweed, chamomile, chrysanthemum — may react to it, and it is traditionally avoided during pregnancy as an internal herb. As with any edible flower, use only pesticide-free blooms and eat in reasonable amounts. Do not confuse the edible petals with the whole flower head, whose green parts are unpalatably bitter.[1]

plants.blurb: The big, bold garden tulip — goblet flowers in every saturated color that headline the mid-spring border, though here they often blaze brightest the first year and fade after.
plants.water: Low; water at planting and in spring growth, then bake dry through summer dormancy
plants.spread: Clumping; large hybrids tend to decline rather than spread and are often replanted
plants.seasons: Showy goblet flowers mid-to-late spring (Apr–May); broad grey-green leaves spring; fully dormant by midsummer; nothing above ground fall–winter
plants.wildlife: Early-spring nectar and pollen for bees when flowers are open; not a major wildlife plant
plants.deer: Not deer-resistant — deer, rabbits, voles, and squirrels all browse or dig the buds and bulbs; protect new plantings
plants.toxic: Toxic — all parts contain tulipalin (tulipalin A and B) and glycosides; ingestion causes vomiting, drooling, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, with the bulb most concentrated; handling bulbs can cause 'tulip fingers' (contact dermatitis)
plants.origin: Garden hybrid; wild ancestors of Central Asia & Turkey
plants.habitat: Steppe & rocky mountain slopes with hot, dry summers

edible.summary: Do not eat any part of a garden tulip — all parts are toxic, the bulb most of all, and handling bulbs can irritate skin.[1][4]
edible.caution: Every part of Tulipa gesneriana contains tulipalin A and B (a lactone), glycosides, and allergenic glycoproteins, with the toxin most concentrated in the bulb.[1][4] Ingestion causes vomiting, hypersalivation (drooling), depression, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, and stomach pain, nausea, and vomiting in people; large amounts can be more serious.[1][4] Tulip bulbs are sometimes mistaken for onions or shallots — never eat an unlabeled bulb. Handling the bulbs (especially peeling many of them) commonly causes an allergic contact dermatitis known as 'tulip fingers' — itching, redness, cracking, and blisters on the hands; wear gloves.[1] Keep bulbs and plants away from children and pets, and if ingestion is suspected call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435).[1][4]

plants.blurb: A low, tough wild tulip whose starry yellow-and-white flowers open flat in the sun — and, unlike the big hybrids, it actually comes back and naturalizes in our dry-summer climate.
plants.water: Low; water in spring growth, then keep dry through summer dormancy
plants.spread: Naturalizing; multiplies into clumps and self-sows where summers are dry, but stays well-behaved
plants.seasons: Star-shaped yellow flowers with white tips, opening flat in sun, early-to-mid spring; narrow strappy leaves spring; fully dormant by midsummer; nothing above ground fall–winter
plants.wildlife: Early-spring nectar and pollen for bees when flowers open flat in sunshine
plants.deer: Not reliably deer-resistant — like other tulips it can be browsed by deer and rabbits and dug by rodents; the small bulbs are less of a target than big hybrids
plants.toxic: Toxic — all parts contain tulipalin (tulipalin A and B) and glycosides, like all tulips; ingestion causes vomiting, drooling, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, with the bulb most concentrated; handling bulbs can cause 'tulip fingers'
plants.origin: Central Asia (Tien Shan)
plants.habitat: Stony alpine slopes & steppe with hot, dry summers

edible.summary: Do not eat any part — like all tulips, every part is toxic (the bulb most of all) and handling bulbs can irritate skin.[1][4]
edible.caution: Tulipa tarda, like all tulips, contains tulipalin A and B and glycosides in every part, most concentrated in the bulb.[1][4] Ingestion causes vomiting, hypersalivation, depression, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, and stomach pain, nausea, and vomiting in people.[1][4] Never eat an unlabeled bulb — tulip bulbs can be mistaken for onions. Handling bulbs can cause an allergic contact dermatitis ('tulip fingers'); wear gloves. Keep bulbs and plants away from children and pets; if ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435).[1][4]

plants.blurb: The bulletproof spring bulb — golden trumpets that come back and multiply for decades, and that deer, rabbits, and voles won't touch because every part is poisonous.
plants.water: Low; water in spring growth, then dry summer dormancy
plants.spread: Naturalizing; multiplies by offsets into ever-larger long-lived clumps
plants.seasons: Yellow-to-white trumpet flowers late winter through late spring (Mar–Apr here); strappy leaves spring; fully dormant by midsummer; nothing above ground fall–winter
plants.wildlife: Early-spring nectar and pollen for bees when in bloom; otherwise low wildlife value
plants.deer: Excellent — deer-, rabbit-, and rodent-proof; all parts are toxic so animals leave the bulbs and flowers alone (often interplanted to protect tulips)
plants.toxic: Toxic — all parts contain phenanthridine alkaloids (chiefly lycorine) plus calcium oxalate crystals, the bulb most poisonous; ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, salivation, and trembling in people and pets, and handling can cause 'lily rash' contact dermatitis; bulbs are dangerously mistaken for onions
plants.origin: Western Europe & the Mediterranean
plants.habitat: Meadows, woods & rocky ground

edible.summary: Do not eat any part of a daffodil — every part is poisonous, the bulb most of all, and bulbs are dangerously mistaken for onions.[1][4]
edible.caution: All parts of Narcissus contain phenanthridine alkaloids — chiefly lycorine — plus calcium oxalate crystals, with the highest concentration in the bulb.[1][4] Ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, hypersalivation, and trembling, and in larger amounts convulsions, cardiac effects, and it may be fatal; it is toxic to people, dogs, cats, and horses.[1][4] The most common serious poisonings happen when daffodil bulbs are mistaken for onions and eaten — never cook or eat an unlabeled bulb. Handling bulbs and stems can cause contact dermatitis ('lily rash' or 'daffodil itch') and the cut-stem sap can irritate skin; wear gloves. Keep bulbs and plants away from children and pets, and if ingestion is suspected call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435).[1][4]

plants.blurb: The big, goblet-flowered spring crocus — larger and a touch later than the snow crocus — that naturalizes through lawns in purple, white, and striped drifts to feed the very first bees.
plants.water: Low; water in growth, dry summer dormancy
plants.spread: Naturalizing; spreads by corm offsets and self-seeding into colonies, naturalizes well in lawns
plants.seasons: Plump purple, white, or striped goblets late winter to early spring (Feb–Apr); grassy white-striped leaves through spring; fully dormant by midsummer; nothing above ground summer–winter
plants.wildlife: Early-spring pollen and nectar for queen bumblebees and emerging bees; flowers open in sun and close at night and in cloud
plants.deer: Deer-resistant and largely rabbit-avoided, but squirrels, voles, and chipmunks dig and eat the corms — protect new plantings
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic — corms contain colchicine-like alkaloids; ingestion may cause stomach upset in pets and people. CRITICAL LOOK-ALIKE: the DEADLY autumn crocus (Colchicum autumnale), which it closely resembles — spring Crocus has 3 stamens and blooms in spring with foliage present, while Colchicum has 6 stamens and blooms leafless in autumn. Never eat unmarked corms.
plants.origin: Alpine & southern Europe (Alps, Pyrenees, Balkans)
plants.habitat: Alpine & subalpine meadows

edible.summary: Dutch crocus is not eaten — the corms contain alkaloids and are mildly toxic, and the critical hazard is confusion with the DEADLY autumn crocus (Colchicum), which looks nearly identical.[1][4]
edible.caution: The corms of Crocus vernus contain colchicine-like alkaloids that can cause stomach upset (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) if ingested; severity is generally low for a small accidental taste, but the plant is not a food crop and should not be eaten. The far greater danger is mistaking it for Colchicum autumnale (autumn crocus / meadow saffron), which is HIGHLY TOXIC and potentially fatal to people, dogs, cats, and horses: Colchicum contains colchicine, which causes cramping, bloody vomiting and diarrhea, respiratory failure, shock, multi-organ damage, and bone marrow suppression. Every part of Colchicum is dangerous — especially the bulb, which is nearly indistinguishable from a Crocus corm without a label.[4] How to tell them apart: true spring Crocus (including Dutch crocus) has 3 stamens and flowers in late winter/spring WITH foliage present; Colchicum has 6 stamens and flowers in autumn with NO foliage (its leaves emerge separately in spring). Never harvest unlabeled corms from the ground, and keep all Crocus/Colchicum corms and foliage away from children, dogs, cats, and livestock. If ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435) immediately.[1][4]

plants.blurb: Sword-leaved fans send up ruffled, fragrant late-spring flags in nearly every color, their fuzzy 'beards' glowing against silky falls — and they revel in our sun, alkaline clay, and dry summers.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established, avoid wet feet
plants.spread: Clumping; rhizomes creep outward into wider clumps over a few years and want dividing
plants.seasons: Ruffled flags in late spring (May–Jun), some cultivars reblooming in fall; upright grey-green sword foliage spring–fall; foliage dies back for winter
plants.wildlife: Visited by bees and the occasional hummingbird for nectar; not a major wildlife plant but its early-season bloom offers some forage
plants.deer: Deer- and rabbit-resistant; the rhizomes and foliage are bitter and toxic, so browsing animals leave them alone
plants.toxic: Toxic — the rhizomes (and to a lesser degree leaves and sap) contain irisin/iridin and pentacyclic terpenoids; ingestion causes drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea in dogs, cats, and horses, and the sap can irritate skin
plants.origin: Garden hybrid; Mediterranean & southern-European ancestry (I. pallida × I. variegata)
plants.habitat: Dry, sunny rocky slopes and hillsides

edible.summary: Bearded iris is toxic — no part is edible. The rhizomes are the most poisonous part and cause vomiting and diarrhea in people and pets; the sap can irritate skin.[1][2]
edible.caution: All parts of Iris × germanica are toxic and it is not a food plant. The rhizomes (underground stems) contain the highest concentration of toxins — irisin/iridin and pentacyclic terpenoids (zeorin, missourin, missouriensin) — with lower amounts in the leaves, sap, and seeds. Ingestion by people, dogs, cats, or horses causes salivation/drooling, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea; the irritant sap can also cause skin irritation (handle with care, wash after dividing rhizomes). Severity is generally low for a small accidental nibble, but keep rhizomes, foliage, and cut flowers away from children and pets, and do not eat any part. Note the rhizome's superficial resemblance to ginger or other edible rhizomes — never confuse them. If significant ingestion is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435).[1][2]

plants.blurb: Perfect violet-purple drumstick globes float on tall bare stems in early summer, humming with bees, then dry to architectural seed heads that hold for weeks.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established, dry summer dormancy
plants.spread: Clumping; multiplies slowly by bulb offsets and can self-seed where happy
plants.seasons: Round purple flower globes late spring to early summer (May–Jun); strappy basal leaves emerge early and often yellow by bloom; tan seed heads persist into summer; dormant underground in winter
plants.wildlife: Excellent pollinator magnet — the globe heads draw bees, butterflies, and other beneficial insects; the dried seed heads add winter structure
plants.deer: Deer- and rodent-resistant — the oniony scent and taste deter deer, rabbits, voles, and squirrels, which is a major advantage over tulips and crocus
plants.toxic: Toxic to pets — like all alliums (onion/garlic relatives), every part is poisonous to dogs, cats, and horses, and toxic to livestock in quantity; mildly irritating to people but not grown or used as food
plants.origin: Central & southwest Asia (Iran, Kyrgyzstan)
plants.habitat: Open mountain slopes and meadows

edible.summary: Although it is a true onion relative and smells oniony, ornamental allium is grown for its flowers, not as food — and every part is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses (and to livestock in quantity).[1][2]
edible.caution: Allium hollandicum is in the onion/garlic genus (Allium) and contains the same organosulfur compounds (e.g. N-propyl disulfide) that make all alliums dangerous to pets and livestock: ingestion by dogs, cats, or horses causes vomiting, drooling, lethargy, diarrhea, and — more seriously — the breakdown of red blood cells (Heinz-body/hemolytic anemia), which can show as weakness, blood in the urine, rapid heart rate, and panting. Keep bulbs, foliage, flowers, and seed heads away from pets and grazing animals. For people: this is an ornamental selection bred for show, not a culinary onion — it is not used as food, the bulbs and foliage are unpalatable and can cause stomach upset, and (as with the whole genus) eating large amounts is harmful. If a pet ingests any part, call ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) or your veterinarian; for people call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222).[1][2]

plants.blurb: Tall sword-leaved spikes unzip from bottom to top in mid-to-late summer, each carrying a column of big silky funnels in nearly every color — but the corms must be dug and stored frost-free each fall to survive a Front Range winter.
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist in summer, do not let dry out
plants.spread: Clumping; each corm forms a new corm plus small cormels, but tender — does not naturalize or spread on the Front Range
plants.seasons: Tall flower spikes mid-to-late summer (Jul–Sep); upright sword foliage through the growing season; nothing above or below ground in winter (corms are lifted and stored)
plants.wildlife: The tubular flowers draw bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds; a useful late-summer nectar source
plants.deer: Not deer-resistant — deer and rabbits will browse the foliage and flower stalks; protect where browsing pressure is high
plants.toxic: Toxic — the corms (bulbs) are the most poisonous part, with leaves and roots also toxic to dogs, cats, and horses (drooling, vomiting, diarrhea); the sap can irritate skin
plants.origin: Garden hybrid; South African (Cape) ancestry
plants.habitat: Summer-rainfall grassland and rocky slopes

edible.summary: Gladiolus is toxic and not a food plant — the corms (bulbs) are the most poisonous part, with leaves and roots also toxic to dogs, cats, and horses; the sap can irritate skin.[2][3]
edible.caution: All parts of Gladiolus × hortulanus are toxic, with the toxin most concentrated in the underground corms (bulbs). Ingestion by dogs, cats, or horses causes salivation/drooling, vomiting, lethargy, and diarrhea; severity is generally low for a small accidental nibble, but the plant is not edible and should be kept away from pets and livestock. Handling the corms and sap can cause skin irritation in sensitive people — wear gloves when dividing and cleaning corms. Note: while the petals of some garden gladiolus are sometimes used as a garnish with the bitter anthers removed, this guide treats the plant as non-food given the toxic corms/foliage and the risk of misidentification — do not eat the corms, leaves, or roots. If a pet ingests any part, call ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) or your veterinarian; for people, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222).[2][3]

plants.blurb: Colorado's most dependable native penstemon — dense wands of deep blue-violet tubular flowers over glossy strap-shaped leaves, a magnet for native bees and hummingbirds that thrives on lean, dry, unimproved soil.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — no supplemental water in average years
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows modestly
plants.seasons: Blue-violet flower spikes late spring–early summer; semi-evergreen basal rosette; dark seed capsules persist into winter
plants.wildlife: Major nectar source for native bees and bumblebees; visited by hummingbirds
plants.deer: Seldom browsed — deer and rabbit resistant

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally as a native pollinator plant — no documented edible use.[3]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[3]

plants.blurb: Tall, airy wands of brilliant scarlet tubular flowers from early summer into fall — a hummingbird favorite that shrugs off heat and drought on the lean Front Range.
plants.size: ~2–3 ft tall × 1–2 ft wide (flower stalks to 4 ft)
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping; reseeds
plants.seasons: Scarlet tubular flowers early summer–fall; narrow blue-green foliage; tan seed stalks in winter
plants.wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant; also native bees

edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[3]

plants.blurb: A low Front Range foothills native forming neat glossy evergreen rosettes that throw up clouds of small blue-violet flowers in late spring; happy in dry shade and lean granitic soil.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade (takes dry shade)
plants.water: Low; xeric
plants.spread: Clumping evergreen rosettes; self-sows
plants.seasons: Clouds of blue-violet flowers late spring–early summer; glossy evergreen basal rosette year-round
plants.wildlife: Native bees, bumblebees, and hummingbirds

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally as a native pollinator plant — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[2]

plants.blurb: A tough shortgrass-prairie native whose stiff stems light up late summer with crowded spikes of rosy-purple bottlebrush flowers — a butterfly magnet anchored by a deep taproot that laughs at drought.
plants.water: Low; xeric — deep taproot, very drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Clumping from a woody taproot; reseeds
plants.seasons: Rosy-purple flower spikes late summer–fall; fine grassy foliage; tan seedheads persist into winter
plants.wildlife: Top butterfly nectar plant; native bees; seeds eaten by birds

edible.caution: Liatris punctata has only documented medicinal/traditional uses, not food uses, so it is treated as inedible here — don't eat any part. It is not notably poisonous, but absence of food documentation is not a green light.[1]

plants.blurb: Cheerful, long-blooming prairie native with drooping yellow (sometimes mahogany-banded) petals around a tall sombrero-like cone — easy, drought-tough, and worked hard by bees all summer.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows readily
plants.seasons: Yellow/red sombrero flowers early summer–fall; ferny gray-green foliage; dark seed cones into winter
plants.wildlife: Bees and butterflies; seeds for birds

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental pollinator plant; documented only for a traditional leaf tea, not as a modern food.[1]
edible.caution: Only a traditional beverage tea from the leaves is documented — that ethnobotanical/medicinal use is not the same as established food safety, so this guide treats it as inedible. It is not notably poisonous, but don't rely on it as food.[1]

plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping; slowly spreads by rhizome
plants.seasons: Nodding pink flowers in spring, then smoky feathered seedheads; semi-evergreen ferny foliage
plants.wildlife: Early native bees

plants.water: Low; xeric
plants.spread: Mat-forming; clumping
plants.seasons: Sulphur-yellow umbels early summer aging to orange-red; evergreen blue-green foliage; russet fall and winter color
plants.wildlife: Major native-bee and butterfly plant; larval host for blues and hairstreaks

plants.blurb: Bright lupine-like spikes of golden pea flowers light up late spring over clean blue-green trifoliate foliage — a vigorous, deer-proof native that spreads into bold drifts.
plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
plants.spread: Spreads by rhizome into colonies
plants.seasons: Golden flower spikes late spring–early summer; blue-green trifoliate foliage; curved seed pods in summer
plants.wildlife: Bumblebees and native bees; larval host for sulphur butterflies
plants.toxic: Foliage and seeds contain quinolizidine alkaloids — toxic if eaten; keep from children, pets, and livestock.

edible.summary: Poisonous — all parts contain lupine-like quinolizidine alkaloids; do not eat any part.[3]
edible.caution: Golden banner contains quinolizidine alkaloids (anagyrine, thermopsine, cytisine, N-methylcytisine), the same alkaloid class that makes lupines toxic, with the highest concentration in the seeds and flowers. The pea-like green pods and 'beans' are the real danger: children have been poisoned after eating the immature seeds mistaken for edible peas, developing headache, vomiting, dizziness, and abdominal pain. It is also hazardous to pets and livestock — these alkaloids deter browsing. Keep seeds and foliage away from children and animals; do not eat any part.[3]

plants.blurb: A silvery-leaved shortgrass-prairie native studding low spreading mats with apricot-scarlet cup flowers all summer — bone-dry tough and a reliable native-bee plant for the harshest, leanest ground.
plants.water: Low; xeric — extremely drought-tolerant
plants.spread: Spreads by rhizome into low colonies
plants.seasons: Apricot-orange cup flowers late spring–summer; gray-green lobed foliage
plants.wildlife: Native bees (specialist globemallow bees); butterflies

edible.summary: No documented culinary use; grown as a xeric ornamental, and the leaf hairs are a skin/eye irritant.[1][3]
edible.caution: No authoritative food use is documented, so this guide treats it as inedible — don't eat it. The whole plant is covered in fine star-shaped (stellate) hairs that can irritate skin and especially eyes (its folk names 'sore-eye poppy' / 'mal de ojo' come from this), and handling or collecting seed has caused dermatitis and eye irritation — wear gloves and don't rub your eyes after touching it.[3]

plants.spread: Spreads slowly by rhizome into low mats

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental groundcover; no culinary use is documented, so it is treated as inedible.[1]
edible.caution: No authoritative food use is documented for prairie zinnia, so don't eat it. It is reported non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses and is not notably poisonous, but lack of documented edibility means it is not a forage plant.[1]

edible.card: Edible fruit, but dry, seedy & bland
edible.summary: The dark-red fruit is edible raw or cooked but — despite the name 'deliciosus' — is usually dry, seedy, and insipid with little flesh.[1][2]
edible.parts: Only the ripe red aggregate fruit is eaten; it can be taken raw or cooked, though most foragers find it disappointing.[1][2]
edible.caution: The fruit is harmless but typically dry and bland with lots of seed and little pulp, so it's not highly valued as food; quality varies plant to plant. Eat only fully ripe fruit.[1][2]

edible.summary: The tart red berries are edible and were traditionally used in foods and beverages — soaked in water to make a tangy lemonade-like drink ('sumac-ade').[1]
edible.parts: The ripe red, sparsely hairy berries (5–7 mm) are the edible part; their tartness comes from a sour surface coating that dissolves in water to flavor a drink.[1]
edible.preparation: Steep or rub the whole berries in cool water (don't boil, which can turn it bitter), then strain out the fruit and hairs; sweeten to taste for a pink lemonade-like beverage. The berries were also used in foods by Native peoples.[1]
edible.caution: This sumac is completely safe — it is NOT poison sumac (Toxicodendron vernix, a white-berried wetland tree) nor poison ivy/oak. Rhus trilobata bears upright clusters of RED berries and is harmless; only the red-fruited sumacs are used for the drink. The crushed foliage has a skunky odor (hence 'skunkbush') but is not toxic.[1][2]

plants.blurb: The largest and smoothest of our native sumacs: a fast, suckering shrub grown for spectacular scarlet fall color and erect cones of fuzzy crimson berries that stand right through winter.

plants.deer: Somewhat deer-resistant; foliage and fruit are browsed when other forage is scarce

edible.summary: The tart red berries are edible — the classic use is a tangy, lemonade-like drink ('sumac-ade') the sour fruit was long steeped in water to make.[3]
edible.parts: The ripe red, hairy drupes borne in the erect terminal cones are the edible part; their sourness comes from a tart coating (rich in malic acid) on the fuzzy hairs that dissolves in water.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Rub or steep the whole berry cones in cool water — do not boil, which draws out bitter tannins — then strain out the fruit and hairs and sweeten to taste for a pink lemonade-like beverage; the sour fruit can also simply be chewed to quench thirst.[3]
edible.caution: This sumac is completely safe and is NOT poison sumac (Toxicodendron vernix), which is a white-berried tree of wet swamps; Rhus glabra bears upright cones of RED berries and is harmless. As a rule only the red-fruited sumacs are used for the drink, never any white-berried look-alike.[1][3]

edible.summary: The fuzzy red berries are edible and tart — traditionally steeped in water to make a tangy pink 'sumac-ade,' like other red-fruited sumacs.[1][3]
edible.parts: The ripe red, hairy drupes are the edible part; a sour coating on the fruit dissolves in water to give the drink its lemonade-like tartness.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Soak or rub the whole berry clusters in cool water (don't boil, which turns it bitter), strain out the fruit and hairs, and sweeten to taste; Native peoples also used the fruit in foods.[1][3]
edible.caution: This is a harmless red-berried sumac, NOT poison sumac (Toxicodendron vernix, a white-berried wetland tree) or poison ivy — only the upright RED-fruited sumacs are used for the drink. The aromatic crushed foliage is citrus-scented and non-toxic.[1][3]

edible.summary: Acorns are a traditional food — but only after the bitter tannins are leached out; raw acorns are astringent and cause stomach upset.[2][4]
edible.parts: The acorn kernels (nutmeats), gathered ripe and brown in fall.[2] They were a staple food of Native American peoples across the oak's range.[2]
edible.preparation: Shell the acorns, then leach out the bitter tannic acid: boil the kernels in repeated changes of water (about 15 min per change) until the water no longer turns brown — roughly two hours total — then roast or grind them.[4]
edible.caution: Do NOT eat raw acorns. All parts of Gambel oak contain gallotannins (juvenile foliage and acorns highest), which are bitter, astringent, and cause GI upset in quantity; the same tannins poison livestock that eat oak as more than ~50% of their diet.[2][3] Only the leached, cooked kernels are safe to eat.[4]

edible.summary: The seeds — piñon / pine nuts — are a prized, richly flavored food eaten raw or roasted.[1]
edible.parts: The large wingless seeds (10–15 mm) held in the cones; ripe in fall. Piñon ranks first among native North American nut trees and was once a staple food of Southwestern peoples.[1]
edible.preparation: Gather cones in late summer/fall before they fully open, dry them to release the seeds, then eat the nuts raw, roasted in the shell, or candied in syrup.[1]
edible.caution: The nuts themselves are wholesome with no special toxicity. The main catch is supply: good seed crops occur only every 4–7 years, trees don't bear heavily until decades old, and birds, woodrats, bears, and deer compete hard for the harvest.[1]

plants.seasons: Blue-green fine turf in summer; horizontal comb seedheads summer into fall; blond and persistent through winter
plants.wildlife: Larval host for skipper butterflies; seeds feed birds; warm-season prairie cover
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; a valuable native forage grass

edible.summary: A valuable native forage grass, not a human food — no part is eaten by people.[1][2]
edible.caution: Blue grama is a highly palatable, nutritious, non-toxic forage grass that feeds livestock year-round (curing well on the stem) and whose seeds feed birds and small mammals, but there is no documented human food use of any part — so it is treated as inedible here. It is not poisonous; the absence of food documentation simply means it is grown as turf, forage, and ornament, not for eating.[1][2]

plants.blurb: The most refined native bunchgrass: an emerald fountain of hair-fine leaves, airy seedheads that famously smell of coriander and popcorn, and glowing apricot-gold fall color.
plants.size: 2–3 ft tall in seed × 2 ft wide (foliage mound ~18 in)
plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
plants.spread: Tight clumping — non-running, well-behaved
plants.seasons: Emerald fine fountain in summer; airy fragrant seedheads late summer; apricot-gold fall fading to light bronze in winter
plants.wildlife: Seeds feed birds; larval host; warm-season prairie grass
plants.toxic: Non-toxic

edible.summary: A minor, historic grain: the seeds were used by some Native peoples to make flour, though it was never a major staple.[3]
edible.parts: The small seeds, borne on the fragrant airy seedheads in late summer. Documented food use is limited — primarily noted as a flour source — so treat it as a minor or emergency grain, not a productive food crop.[3]
edible.preparation: Where used, the ripe seeds were gathered and ground into flour/meal; the seeds are very small and the per-plant yield is low, so it is labor-intensive for little return.[3]
edible.caution: Documented food use for this species is thin (its better-documented Native uses are medicinal), so be conservative — be certain of your identification, harvest only clean ripe seed, and discard any seedheads showing dark, hardened ergot-like fungal bodies that can infect grass florets.[3][2]

plants.blurb: Tall, upright native prairie grass with clouds of airy pink-tinged seed panicles, fiery gold-to-burgundy fall color, and stiff tan stems that hold structure all winter.
plants.water: Low to moderate; remarkably adaptable from wet to dry
plants.spread: Clumping with short rhizomes; can self-sow
plants.seasons: Upright blue-green to green in summer; airy pink-tinged panicles late summer; gold-to-burgundy fall; stiff tan winter structure
plants.wildlife: Larval host for skippers; seeds feed sparrows and other birds; nesting and winter cover
plants.toxic: Can cause photosensitization (saponin hepatotoxicity) in grazing livestock, especially sheep, goats, and horses on young growth; not a concern for people or as an ornamental

edible.summary: Not a human food plant - grown purely as an ornamental and wildlife grass; its seeds feed birds, not people.[1][2]
edible.caution: Switchgrass is not eaten by people, but it carries a real LIVESTOCK hazard: it contains steroidal saponins (diosgenin) and has been implicated in hepatogenous (secondary) photosensitization and crystal-associated liver damage in grazing animals - documented in sheep, goats, lambs and horses, especially on lush growth. Horses cannot neutralize the toxins the way cattle largely can, so switchgrass hay or pasture is never recommended for horses; affected animals show sunburned muzzle/skin, jaundice and liver failure. It is NOT a concern to handle or to grow as a garden ornamental, and no part is used as food - treat it as inedible.[4]

plants.blurb: Tough native prairie grass named for the oat-like seeds dangling along one side of each arching stem; flashes bright orange-red anthers at bloom and warms to bronze in fall.
plants.size: 1.5–2.5 ft tall in seed × 1.5 ft wide
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping to slowly spreading by short rhizomes
plants.seasons: Blue-green in summer with one-sided seed spikes and orange anthers; bronze-purple fall; tan winter
plants.wildlife: Larval host for several skippers; seeds feed birds; warm-season prairie cover
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; excellent native forage grass

edible.summary: A high-quality native forage grass, not a human food — no part is eaten by people.[1][2]
edible.caution: Sideoats grama is a highly palatable, nutritious, non-toxic forage grass relished by all classes of livestock and whose seeds feed birds (including wild turkeys) and small mammals, but there is no documented human food use of any part — so it is treated as inedible here. It is not poisonous; it is simply grown as forage and ornament, not for eating.[1][2]

plants.blurb: An exquisite xeric native of sandy ground: a fine green clump topped by an airy, see-through cloud of tiny seeds on hair-thin branches — and a historically important Indigenous grain.
plants.water: Low; very xeric — thrives on sandy, dry, lean ground
plants.spread: Clumping cool-season grass; self-sows
plants.seasons: Fine green clump in spring; delicate airy seed clouds in early summer; bleaches golden and goes summer-dormant in heat
plants.wildlife: Seeds are important food for birds and small mammals; larval host; cool-season
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; seeds were a staple Indigenous food grain

edible.summary: The nutritious seed was a staple food of many American Indian peoples, ground into meal and flour.[1][4]
edible.parts: The plump, protein-rich seeds — borne on the airy panicle and shattering readily when ripe in early-to-mid summer. They were one of the most important wild grains across the arid West.[1][3][4]
edible.preparation: Traditionally the seeds were parched, then ground into a fine meal or flour and made into mush, porridge, bread, cakes, or dumplings — often mixed with cornmeal; some peoples also kept it as a reserve food.[4][3]
edible.caution: Harvesting is laborious: the tiny seeds shatter from the heads as they ripen and must be gathered quickly and winnowed from the husk. As with any wild grass grain, inspect the seedheads and discard any showing the dark, hardened, ergot-like fungal bodies that can infect grass florets, and be sure of your identification before eating.[1][3]

plants.origin: Sterile garden hybrid of two Eurasian species
plants.habitat: Wild parent species grow in damp meadows and open woodland edges across Europe and Asia
plants.blurb: The classic vertical accent grass: a dependable cool-season clump sending up feathery plumes that turn wheat-gold and stand stiff and upright from June clear through winter. Sterile — never self-sows.
plants.size: 4–5 ft tall in plume × 2 ft wide
plants.water: Moderate; tolerates clay and is not strictly xeric — give occasional deep water
plants.spread: Clumping and sterile — sets no viable seed, so it never spreads
plants.seasons: Fresh green upright clump in spring; pinkish plumes in early summer turning wheat-gold; stiff golden wands standing through winter
plants.wildlife: Sterile, so limited seed value; provides cover and winter structure
plants.toxic: Non-toxic
