edible.summary: Not edible - a sterile ornamental grass grown for its plumes, with no culinary use.[1][2]
edible.caution: No part of feather reed grass is used as food, and there are no documented edible uses; it is grown solely as an ornamental. It is not reported as toxic to people or pets, but absence of any food documentation means it should simply be treated as inedible - don't eat it.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Colorado's native climbing clematis: nodding lavender-blue bells scramble through foothill shrubs in spring, followed by silky seed plumes. A cold-hardy, well-behaved native alternative to the big garden clematis.
plants.sun: Part shade to part sun — a foothills woodland-edge plant
plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
plants.spread: Twining/scrambling climber (clings by its leaf-stalks); clumping root, not running
plants.seasons: Nodding lavender-blue bells in spring; divided foliage through summer; silky seed plumes after bloom; deciduous in winter
plants.wildlife: Early-season nectar; seed plumes and cover for birds and insects
plants.deer: Generally avoided — clematis foliage is acrid
plants.toxic: Like all clematis, contains protoanemonin — foliage and sap are irritant and toxic if eaten raw; wash hands after handling. NOT the CO List B orange-peel clematis (Clematis orientalis)

edible.summary: Like all clematis, Rocky Mountain clematis is toxic — no part is edible, and the bruised sap irritates skin.[3][4]
edible.caution: All parts contain protoanemonin, an acrid irritant glycoside released when the tissue is bruised or chewed. Eating it causes intense burning and pain in the mouth, salivation, vomiting, and diarrhea; the same compound makes the fresh sap a skin and mucous-membrane irritant, so handling cut stems can cause redness and a burning sensation — wear gloves when pruning. It is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses as well as people; keep children and pets from chewing it. (Drying largely destroys protoanemonin, but never rely on that — treat the whole plant as not for consumption.)[3][4]

plants.blurb: A vigorous native climber that smothers fences and foothill thickets in frothy white late-summer flowers, then erupts into silvery 'old man's beard' seed plumes that catch the autumn light. Give it room to roam.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established (often along streams in the wild)
plants.spread: Vigorous twining climber that can spread widely — site it where it has room
plants.seasons: Masses of starry white flowers in late summer; spectacular silvery seed plumes through fall into winter; deciduous
plants.wildlife: Important late-summer nectar for bees; seed plumes and dense cover for birds
plants.toxic: Like all clematis, contains protoanemonin — sap and foliage are irritant and toxic if eaten raw, despite historical topical folk use

edible.summary: Western virgin's bower is toxic — no part is edible, and the bruised sap irritates skin, despite its historical topical folk use.[3][4]
edible.caution: All parts contain protoanemonin, an acrid irritant glycoside released when the tissue is bruised or chewed — eating any part causes severe burning and pain in the mouth, salivation, vomiting, and diarrhea, and the fresh sap blisters and irritates skin and mucous membranes (it was historically used topically as a counter-irritant and a wash, never as a food). Treat it as poisonous to eat; it is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses as well as people. Wear gloves when pruning, since handling cut stems can redden and burn the skin. (Drying largely destroys protoanemonin, but never rely on that — it is not food.)[3][4]

plants.blurb: Colorado's tough native wild grape: a vigorous tendril-climber with handsome lobed leaves, clusters of tart blue-black fruit for jelly and the birds, and clear-yellow fall color. Plant it where it can ramble.
plants.water: Moderate; adaptable but appreciates some moisture (often riparian in the wild)
plants.spread: Vigorous woody climber by forked tendrils; can be aggressive — prune to control
plants.seasons: Fragrant greenish flowers in spring; bold lobed foliage in summer; tart blue-black fruit clusters in late summer; clear-yellow fall color; bare woody vine in winter
plants.wildlife: Fruit feeds many birds and mammals; larval host for sphinx moths; dense nesting cover
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; the fruit and young leaves are edible (see Edible parts)

edible.card: Ripe fruit (jelly, juice, wine) & young leaves
edible.summary: A genuine native wild edible: the ripe blue-black fruit makes excellent jelly, juice, and wine, and the young leaves are used like cultivated grape leaves.[2][3][4]
edible.parts: Two parts are eaten. The ripe fruit — small, blue-black, with a heavy bloom — is sour until the first frosts, after which it sweetens; it may be eaten fresh but is far better cooked. The tender young leaves, picked in spring, are eaten like the grape leaves of commerce. Native peoples ate the fruit fresh and dried it for winter, and riverbank grape was a documented food plant across its range.[2][4]
edible.preparation: Because the fruit is tart and seedy, it is best cooked: simmer and strain it for jelly, juice, syrup, or wine, or wait for fully frost-sweetened berries to eat fresh. Young leaves are blanched and used as wraps (dolma-style) like other grape leaves. No detoxification is needed — only ordinary cooking.[2][3][4]
edible.caution: Identify with certainty before eating. The most dangerous look-alike is Canada moonseed (Menispermum canadense), a poisonous vine whose blue-black grape-like fruit grows in similar clusters; the reliable tell is the seed — true grape has 2–4 rounded, pear-shaped seeds per berry, while moonseed contains a single flattened, crescent- or moon-shaped seed. If a 'grape' has one crescent seed, do not eat it. Also note the raw fruit is very tart, acidic, and seedy and is best cooked; eat only fully ripe, frost-sweetened berries fresh.[3][4]

plants.blurb: The native, well-mannered cousin of Virginia creeper: palmate five-leaflet leaves that blaze scarlet in fall over blue berries on coral-red stalks. It twines and drapes rather than gluing itself to walls — a superb fall-color screen for a fence or arbor.
plants.sun: Full sun to shade — very adaptable
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Vigorous climber by twining tendrils WITHOUT adhesive disks — it drapes over supports rather than cementing to walls (unlike true Virginia creeper)
plants.seasons: Green palmate five-leaflet foliage in summer; inconspicuous flowers; blue-black berries on coral-red stalks and brilliant scarlet foliage in fall; bare in winter
plants.wildlife: Berries are an important fall food for birds; larval host for sphinx moths; cover and nesting
plants.toxic: Berries are toxic to people (calcium-oxalate raphides and oxalic acid) and the sap can irritate skin — though birds eat the fruit safely. Looks grape-like but is NOT edible

edible.summary: Poisonous — the grape-like blue-black berries are toxic to people; do not eat them. Birds eat the fruit safely, but humans must not.[3][4]
edible.caution: The blue-black berries are poisonous to people: they contain calcium-oxalate raphides (needle-like crystals) and oxalic acid, which cause burning and irritation of the mouth, throat, and digestive tract, with nausea, abdominal pain, and vomiting; the fruit is rated highly toxic and reported as potentially fatal in quantity. The sap can also irritate sensitive skin, so wear gloves when pruning. The berries dangerously resemble small wild grapes — never confuse this vine with edible riverbank grape (Vitis riparia). Birds consume the fruit without harm, but it is not human food; keep the berries away from children and pets.[3][4]

plants.blurb: The well-behaved native goldenrod: dense club-shaped plumes of gold light up the fall garden on tidy upright clumps — a keystone late-season nectar source for bees and migrating monarchs, and (despite the myth) not the cause of hay fever.
plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping and well-behaved — a non-running goldenrod that stays put, unlike the weedy Canada goldenrod
plants.seasons: Upright leafy clumps through summer; showy golden pyramidal plumes in late summer and fall; tan seedheads stand into winter
plants.wildlife: A keystone late-season nectar plant — bees, wasps, butterflies and migrating monarchs; seeds feed birds; larval host for many moths
plants.toxic: Non-toxic — and NOT the cause of hay fever (that's wind-pollinated ragweed, which blooms at the same time)

edible.summary: Showy goldenrod is grown as an ornamental and keystone pollinator plant; the documented Native American use of this species is medicinal, not culinary, so it is not treated as a food here.[4]
edible.caution: The authoritative ethnobotanical record for Solidago speciosa lists only medicinal (root infusion/decoction) uses, with no documented food or beverage use, so don't eat it as food.[4] Goldenrods as a genus are sometimes brewed as an herbal tea, but that is not specifically documented for this species and is best left to those who know the plant — be certain of identification first. One thing it is NOT: a cause of hay fever. Goldenrod pollen is heavy and insect-carried, not airborne; the sneezing blamed on it comes from wind-pollinated ragweed blooming at the same time.[1][4]

plants.blurb: A towering native prairie sunflower — 6 to 10 feet of arching stems lined top to bottom with golden, dark-eyed flowers in fall, mobbed by bees and stripped for seed by goldfinches. Give it room at the back of the border.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping but spreads by rhizomes into colonies — give it room or divide to contain
plants.seasons: Tall leafy stems through summer; golden dark-eyed flowers lining the upper stems in late summer and fall; seedheads feed birds into winter
plants.wildlife: Major fall nectar for bees and butterflies; oil-rich seeds heavily used by goldfinches and other birds; larval host
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; the seeds and tubers are edible (see Edible parts)

edible.summary: A genuine native food: the oil-rich seeds were eaten and ground, and the thickened roots/tubers were a documented Great Plains food, prepared like a Jerusalem artichoke.[3][4]
edible.parts: The two food parts are the seeds and the tuberous roots. The small, oil-rich seeds were eaten and ground for meal; they are smaller than a cultivated sunflower's but the same kind of food. The thickened roots/tubers were dug and eaten by Plains tribes, and can be prepared and eaten like those of its close relative the Jerusalem artichoke (Helianthus tuberosus).[3][4]
edible.preparation: Let the heads ripen and dry, then thresh and clean the seeds — roast them, or grind them into meal; the kernels can also be pressed for oil. The tubers are best cooked: dig them in fall or early spring and eat raw, boiled, or roasted, as the Plains tribes did.[3][4]
edible.caution: The seeds are safe but small and fiddly to clean compared with a cultivated sunflower, and the hulls are not for eating. The tubers are best cooked and, like Jerusalem artichoke, can cause digestive gas if eaten in quantity. As with any wild harvest, be certain of identification and don't gather seed or roots from roadsides or sprayed ground.[3][4]

plants.blurb: An airy native daisy that hazes a xeric border with hundreds of small golden suns from midsummer until hard frost — wiry, see-through, and one of the longest-blooming wildflowers for hot, lean ground.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows modestly where happy
plants.seasons: A wiry, airy plant smothered in small yellow daisies from midsummer until hard frost; dies back in winter
plants.wildlife: Long-season nectar for bees and butterflies; small seeds for birds
plants.deer: Not deer-resistant — deer and elk browse the leaves and flower heads
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; no documented edible use

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental and pollinator/wildlife plant, not for eating; no established culinary use and not known to be poisonous to people.[1]
edible.caution: Showy goldeneye is treated as inedible — the only food record is historical, the Goshute using the small seeds, and there is no established modern culinary use, so don't eat any part.[1] One real hazard: when grown in high-nitrogen ground (heavy fertilizer or manure) the plant can accumulate nitrate to levels that poison grazing livestock — a reason to keep it lean and unfertilized.[1] As with any wild daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant, never eat one without certain identification.[1]

plants.blurb: Among the very last wildflowers to bloom: a tidy mound of aromatic foliage that erupts in October into a haze of lavender-blue, gold-eyed daisies — vital late forage for bees and monarchs when almost nothing else is flowering.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping mound, spreading slowly by short rhizomes — well-behaved
plants.seasons: A bushy mound of aromatic (when crushed) foliage through summer; a sheet of lavender-blue gold-eyed daisies in fall — one of the last to bloom; seedheads in winter
plants.wildlife: Critical late-fall nectar for bees and migrating monarchs and other butterflies; larval host for the Pearl Crescent and others; seeds for birds
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; no notable edible use

edible.summary: Aromatic aster is a pollinator and ornamental plant, not a food — it has no documented edible use and is not notably toxic.[1]
edible.caution: There is no recorded use of aromatic aster (Symphyotrichum oblongifolium) as food; it is grown for its very late bloom and aromatic foliage, not for eating, so no part should be consumed. No part is reported as a serious poison to people or pets — deer and rabbits leave it alone for its aromatic, rigid stems rather than toxicity — but absence of food documentation is not a green light. As with any daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant, those with ragweed/aster pollen allergies may react, and a wild plant should never be eaten without certain identification.[1]

plants.origin: Mountains of southern Europe (Alps to the Balkans)
plants.habitat: Rocky woodland and shady limestone slopes of southern Europe
plants.blurb: The premier dry-shade groundcover: a weed-proof carpet of aromatic, semi-evergreen lobed leaves that flush red in fall, studded with magenta flowers in late spring. It thrives in the dry shade where little else will — and deer leave it alone.
plants.sun: Part shade to full shade (takes sun with steady moisture)
plants.water: Low; notably drought-tolerant once established — a top dry-shade groundcover
plants.spread: Spreads steadily by thick surface rhizomes into a weed-smothering mat; easy to pull back
plants.seasons: Aromatic, semi-evergreen lobed foliage that flushes red in fall; magenta-pink flowers in late spring
plants.wildlife: Bees work the spring flowers; the aromatic foliage is deer- and rabbit-resistant
plants.deer: Resistant — the aromatic foliage is left alone
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; not a food (the foliage yields the aromatic 'zdravetz' essential oil)

edible.summary: Bigroot geranium is grown as an ornamental dry-shade groundcover, not as a food — don't eat it.[1][2]
edible.caution: Geranium macrorrhizum is grown for its aromatic foliage and ground-covering habit, not for eating, and no authoritative source documents an edible use for the plant itself. Its leaves are the source of 'zdravetz' essential oil, which is used in perfumery and as a flavouring/aroma ingredient, but that is a distilled oil — the plant is not a food crop and should not be grazed. It is not listed as notably poisonous by the Missouri Botanical Garden or RHS, so treat it as inedible: not a known poison, but not a tested food.[1][2]

plants.origin: Mountains of Arizona, New Mexico & northern Mexico
plants.habitat: Shaded rock crevices and canyon walls of the desert Southwest
plants.blurb: The original coral bells: a tidy evergreen mound of scalloped leaves sending up airy wands of true coral-red bells that hummingbirds work from late spring into summer. A Southwest native that takes dry shade and rocky ground in stride.
plants.size: 12–18 in tall in bloom × 12 in wide
plants.sun: Part shade to full shade (morning sun with moisture)
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant in shade once established
plants.spread: Clumping evergreen mound of foliage with airy flower wands above
plants.seasons: An evergreen mound of scalloped leaves year-round; airy wands of coral-red bell flowers from late spring into summer
plants.wildlife: Hummingbirds and bees work the red bells; semi-evergreen winter foliage
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; the astringent root was used medicinally (alumroot), not as food

edible.summary: Coral bells is a garden ornamental with no food use — not poisonous, but not eaten.[1][2]
edible.caution: Heuchera sanguinea has no culinary use and should not be treated as food. The genus is sometimes called "alumroot" for the astringent, tannin-rich root, which had a folk-medicinal use (as a styptic/astringent), not a food use — that is herbal-medicine history, not an invitation to eat the plant. It is not a recognized garden poison, but don't consume it.[1][2]

plants.origin: Woodlands of Japan, Korea & northeast China
plants.habitat: Shaded forest floors and woodland slopes of temperate East Asia
plants.blurb: Barrenwort, the dry-shade champion: airy sprays of delicate, long-spurred 'bishop's hat' flowers in spring over heart-shaped leaflets that emerge red-bronze. Once established it shrugs off the dry, root-filled shade under trees where almost nothing else will grow.
plants.size: 8–12 in tall × 12 in wide, slowly spreading
plants.water: Low; one of the most drought-tolerant perennials for dry shade once established
plants.spread: Slowly spreading clump (short rhizomes) into a low groundcover; well-behaved
plants.seasons: Heart-shaped leaflets flush red-bronze in spring then green in summer; delicate spurred flowers on wiry stems in spring
plants.wildlife: Minor pollinator value; prized mainly as a tough dry-shade groundcover; deer- and rabbit-resistant
plants.toxic: Non-toxic in the garden; the leaves are a traditional East Asian medicinal herb, not a food

Spring — the long-spurred flowers up close · © Timur Kalininsky (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

Winter — bronzed semi-evergreen leaflets persisting over dormant ground · © hakkahamushi (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

edible.summary: Epimedium is a garden ornamental with no culinary use — not a garden poison, but not a food.[1][2]
edible.caution: Epimedium grandiflorum is grown as a shade ornamental and has no place in cooking. The leaves are a traditional East Asian medicinal herb — "yin yang huo," sold as the supplement "horny goat weed" — but that is herbal-medicine use, not a culinary food, and self-medicating with the raw plant or unregulated extracts is inadvisable (it can affect heart rhythm and blood pressure and interacts with medications). It is not a notable garden poison, but don't eat it.[1][2]

plants.origin: Siberia & Mongolia (the Altai mountains)
plants.habitat: Rocky meadows and open woodland of cold Central Asia
plants.blurb: Pigsqueak: bold, glossy, cabbage-like evergreen leaves that turn burnished bronze-red through winter, topped by stout clusters of pink flowers in earliest spring. Iron-tough and one of the very few bold evergreen leaves for dry shade.
plants.size: 12–18 in tall in bloom × 18–24 in wide
plants.sun: Part shade to full shade (tolerates sun with moisture)
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Spreads slowly by thick rhizomes into evergreen clumps; a good edger and groundcover
plants.seasons: Big leathery evergreen leaves that flush bronze-red in winter cold; stout clusters of pink flowers in earliest spring
plants.wildlife: Early bees work the spring flowers; year-round evergreen cover
plants.deer: Generally resistant — the leathery leaves are left alone
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; the tannin-rich leaves have a history of use as tea, but it is grown as an ornamental, not a food

edible.summary: Grown here strictly as an ornamental evergreen groundcover — treat it as inedible.[1][2]
edible.caution: Bergenia leaves are very high in tannins. The closely related Bergenia crassifolia (badan / 'Mongolian' or 'Siberian' tea) has a documented history of its old, partly-fermented leaves being brewed as a tannin-rich tea in Siberia, Mongolia and northern China, and B. cordifolia is grown ornamentally as 'pigsqueak' — but it is sold and grown here purely as a landscape plant, not as a food, and is not a casual edible. The high tannin content makes fresh leaves astringent and unpalatable, species/cultivar identity matters, and no Front-Range authority recommends eating it, so it is treated as inedible. It is not a notable poison — the Missouri Botanical Garden lists no toxicity — but it is not a tested food crop; don't graze it.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A graceful native of dappled woodland: arching, unbranched leafy stems tipped with sprays of small white stars in spring, then green berries striped like tiny watermelons that ripen wine-red. A spreading native groundcover for the dry shade under trees.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant in shade once established
plants.spread: Spreads by rhizomes into open colonies — a native groundcover for dappled and dry shade
plants.seasons: Arching unbranched leafy stems; sprays of small white star flowers in spring; striped green berries ripening dark red in late summer; gold foliage in fall
plants.wildlife: Spring flowers for small pollinators; berries eaten by birds and small mammals; woodland cover
plants.toxic: Edible in part but with cautions — the ripe berries are mildly laxative in quantity (see Edible parts)

edible.card: Young shoots cooked; a few ripe berries
edible.summary: The young spring shoots are eaten as a cooked green and the ripe red berries were eaten as food, but the berries are mildly laxative in quantity — eat only a few, fully ripe, and be certain of your identification.[1][4]
edible.parts: Two parts have documented food use: the young shoots, as they emerge in spring, eaten cooked as greens or like an asparagus substitute; and the ripe berries, which Northwest peoples (e.g. the Nuxalk) gathered in mid-to-late summer for food. The shoots are the more reliable food; the berries are a nibble, not a staple.[1][4]
edible.preparation: Pick the shoots only while young and tender and cook them — do not eat the mature stems or leaves. Eat the berries only when fully ripe (wine-red) and only a few, because they are laxative raw in quantity; thorough cooking is said to reduce that effect.[4]
edible.caution: Three cautions. (1) The ripe berries are mildly cathartic / laxative (purgative) when eaten raw in any quantity, especially if you are not used to them — eat only a few, fully ripe; large amounts cause stomach upset and diarrhea. (2) Take the shoots only when young and tender and cook them — do not eat the older foliage. (3) BE CERTAIN OF YOUR IDENTIFICATION: this plant superficially resembles other lily relatives (true Solomon's seal, twisted-stalk, and the young shoots of toxic species can look similar), so positively identify it as Maianthemum stellatum — the arching unbranched leafy stem ending in a terminal spray of white star flowers, then striped-green berries ripening dark red — before eating any part. If poisoning is suspected, call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222).[1][4]

plants.blurb: The monarch's lifeline: domes of intricate rose-pink stars over bold blue-green leaves, alive with butterflies and bees all summer, then spiky pods that split to silky floss. The single most important plant you can add for monarchs on the Front Range.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Spreads by deep rhizomes into colonies — can be aggressive; site where it can roam
plants.seasons: Bold blue-green leaves in summer; spheres of rose-pink star flowers early-to-mid summer; spiky pods splitting to silky floss in fall
plants.wildlife: THE monarch host (sole caterpillar food) and a rich nectar source for many butterflies and bees; supports a suite of milkweed-specialist insects
plants.deer: Generally avoided — the foliage is toxic
plants.toxic: All parts contain toxic cardiac glycosides and irritant milky latex; poisonous to livestock, pets and people if eaten raw — see Edible parts

shots.cap: Summer — a monarch on the rose-pink flowers · © David Martin (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — star flowers opening from pink buds · © Suzanne Dingwell (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — flowers over the bold blue-green leaves · © Sequoia Janirella Wrens (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Spring — young shoots emerging · © Justin Mair (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Summer — a flowering stand in a wild meadow · © Nick Moore (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — the spiky green seed pods · © Karro Frost (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — pods splitting to silky floss · © hedgie7 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Winter — split seed pods spilling floss on dried stems above the snow · © reidedo (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

edible.card: Young shoots, buds & young pods — boiled in several changes of water (never raw)
edible.summary: Young spring shoots, flower buds, and young firm pods are edible ONLY after thorough cooking in several changes of water; all parts are toxic raw.[1][2][4]
edible.parts: Various Indigenous groups boiled and ate the young shoots and stems (like asparagus), the immature flower buds (boiled for soup or with meat), and the young firm green pods as a cooked vegetable; this is among the least toxic milkweeds, with documented recipes for preparing it as a potherb.[2][3][4]
edible.preparation: Eat young growth ONLY — never the mature plant. Boil thoroughly in several changes of water to leach out the bitter, toxic latex before eating; the changes of water are not optional flavoring but the safety step that removes the cardenolides. Never eat any part raw.[1][2][3]
edible.caution: All parts contain toxic cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) and an irritant milky latex; raw, the plant is poisonous — cardenolides cause vomiting at low doses and can be lethal at higher ones, and milkweed is poisonous to cattle, sheep, and other livestock and toxic to dogs, cats, and horses (the ASPCA lists Asclepias as toxic, with cardiotoxic cardenolides and signs from vomiting and weakness to seizures, dilated pupils, and death).[1][2][6] Only the YOUNG shoots, buds, and firm immature pods are eaten, and only after thorough boiling in several changes of water; the mature plant, leaves, and sap are not edible. ID is critical: before it flowers, milkweed can be confused with toxic dogbane (Apocynum), which is poisonous and must not be eaten — confirm the milky sap, opposite leaves, and milkweed flower/pod before harvesting.[1][3]

plants.blurb: The most garden-mannered milkweed: blazing flat-topped clusters of pure orange that butterflies and bees swarm, on a tidy non-spreading clump from a deep drought-proof taproot. A monarch host that behaves itself — just site it permanently, as the taproot hates being moved.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established (deep taproot)
plants.spread: Clumping from a deep taproot — well-behaved, does NOT run like showy milkweed; resents transplanting
plants.seasons: Brilliant flat-topped orange flower clusters in mid-to-late summer over narrow leaves; slender pods to silky floss in fall
plants.wildlife: Monarch caterpillar host and a nectar magnet for butterflies and native bees; supports milkweed-specialist insects
plants.toxic: Contains cardiac glycosides like other milkweeds (with less latex); toxic if eaten raw and not for grazing animals — see Edible parts

edible.summary: Not a food plant — grown as a monarch/pollinator plant; all parts are toxic raw (cardiac glycosides) and the root is a folk medicinal ('pleurisy root'), not a casual edible.[1][2][3]
edible.caution: Butterfly milkweed contains cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) plus resinoids and a little alkaloid in all parts, poisonous to humans and to livestock; poisoning signs include weakness, bloating, rapid weak pulse, dilated pupils, difficulty breathing, spasms, and coma, and the ASPCA lists Asclepias as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses.[1][3] The Wildflower Center flags it 'not edible,' with the roots and sap of all parts poisonous.[2] It is grown here strictly as a monarch larval host and pollinator plant, not for eating. Its tuberous root was the historic folk medicinal 'pleurisy root' (listed in the U.S. Pharmacopoeia 1820–1905) — a medicinal preparation, NOT a casual edible; do not eat it. While some sources note young shoots of milkweeds were boiled and eaten by Indigenous peoples, butterfly milkweed is far better documented as toxic and as a medicine than as a food, and we treat it as not-food here; never eat any part raw.[1][5]

plants.blurb: The wetland monarch host: flat-topped domes of small rose-pink stars over slender willow-like leaves, humming with butterflies and bees. Unlike its dryland cousins, it wants consistently moist ground.
plants.water: High — moist to wet soil; not for dry zones
plants.spread: Clumping and well-behaved; grows from a single crown and does not run — far less aggressive than showy milkweed
plants.seasons: Slender willow-like leaves on tall stems; rose-pink flower domes mid-to-late summer; slim seed pods splitting to silky floss in fall
plants.wildlife: Monarch host (caterpillar food) and a major nectar source for butterflies, bees and other pollinators
plants.deer: Generally avoided — the foliage is toxic
plants.toxic: All parts contain toxic cardiac glycosides and irritant milky latex; poisonous raw to livestock, pets and people — see Edible parts

edible.summary: All parts are toxic raw; while some Indigenous peoples historically ate the young cooked shoots, swamp milkweed is more bitter and less palatable than other milkweeds and is best treated as a pollinator plant, not a food.[1][3]
edible.parts: Historically, the young shoots, immature flower buds, and young leaves were sometimes cooked in several changes of water and eaten, as with other milkweeds;[1] but swamp milkweed is noticeably more bitter, its documented internal use is largely medicinal (an emetic), and this guide does not recommend it as food — it is grown here for monarchs and pollinators, not the table.[3]
edible.caution: All parts contain toxic cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) and an irritant milky latex; raw, the plant is poisonous, and the sap causes contact dermatitis on skin.[1][3] Although swamp milkweed's cardenolide content is relatively low, it is still toxic to livestock (it has poisoned sheep) and the ASPCA lists Asclepias as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, with signs from vomiting and weakness to seizures, dilated pupils, and death.[1][6] Identification is critical: before it flowers, milkweed can be confused with the toxic dogbanes (Apocynum), which must not be eaten — confirm the milky sap, opposite leaves, and the milkweed flower or slim seed pod before ever handling it as food.[1][3]

plants.blurb: A fine-textured eastern-US milkweed: threadlike leaves in tidy whorls up wiry stems, topped in late summer by small umbels of greenish-white stars. A monarch host that thrives on the lean, dry ground where showier milkweeds won't.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Spreads by rhizomes into colonies
plants.seasons: Fine whorled needle-like leaves up the stems; greenish-white flower umbels in late summer; slender pods splitting to silky floss in fall
plants.wildlife: Monarch host and a late-season nectar source for many bees, wasps and butterflies
plants.deer: Generally avoided — the foliage is toxic
plants.toxic: All parts contain toxic cardiac glycosides; among the most toxic milkweeds to livestock — poisonous, see Edible parts
plants.origin: Eastern & central US to the Great Plains
plants.habitat: Dry prairies, barrens, glades & roadsides on lean, poor soils

edible.summary: Do not eat — no part of whorled milkweed is food. All parts are poisonous, and this narrow-leaved milkweed ranks among the most toxic to livestock.[1][5][7]
edible.caution: All parts contain toxic cardiac glycosides (cardenolides) and an irritant milky latex, and the narrow-leaved, whorled milkweeds like this one are among the most toxic milkweeds to grazing animals.[1][5][7] Poisoning is not uncommon in sheep and cattle and occurs in horses, goats, and other stock; as little as a fraction of a percent of body weight can sicken or kill an animal, and the plants stay toxic even dried in hay, so contaminated hay is a common cause of loss.[5][7] It is a documented rangeland weed for exactly this reason.[1] The ASPCA lists Asclepias as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, with cardenolides causing vomiting, weakness, dilated pupils, seizures, and — at higher doses — heart and respiratory failure.[6] Before it flowers, milkweed can be confused with poisonous dogbane (Apocynum); neither is edible, so do not gather this plant, and no preparation makes any part of it safe to eat.[1]

plants.origin: W & S Europe to SW Asia
plants.habitat: Woodland floors, hedge banks & shady grassy slopes
plants.blurb: The classic woodland primrose: soft pale-yellow flowers nestled singly in a rosette of crinkled tongue-shaped leaves, among the very first blooms of spring. Wants cool, moist shade — a real challenge in our dry heat and alkaline clay.
plants.water: Moderate to high; needs consistently moist soil — not for dry zones
plants.spread: Clumping rosettes; slowly by self-seeding
plants.seasons: Semi-evergreen leaf rosette; pale-yellow flowers in early spring
plants.wildlife: Early nectar for bees, bee-flies, butterflies and moths
plants.toxic: Foliage/sap contain primin, which can irritate skin in sensitive people; flowers & young leaves are edible in moderation — see Edible parts

edible.card: Flowers (salads, candied) & young leaves (raw or cooked) — in moderation
edible.summary: The flowers and young leaves are edible — flowers raw in salads or candied, young leaves raw or cooked — but eat only in moderation, and skin-sensitive people should take care handling the foliage.[3][5]
edible.parts: The cheerful pale-yellow flowers are a classic edible garnish — scattered fresh over salads and desserts, crystallized/candied, or used in conserves and to flavor drinks. The young leaves have a mild flavor and can be eaten raw in salads or cooked in soups; only the tender young growth is used, as older leaves turn tough and more strongly flavored.[3][5]
edible.preparation: Pick the flowers and only the youngest leaves; use flowers fresh as a garnish or candy them, and eat young leaves raw in small amounts or cooked like a mild green. Wash well and use in moderation rather than as a bulk vegetable.[5]
edible.caution: Eat in moderation only. The leaves and sap of Primula contain primin and related quinones that can cause allergic contact dermatitis (an itchy rash) in sensitive people — wear gloves if you are prone to it — and the foliage contains saponins, so eating large quantities can cause mild stomach upset and vomiting.[1][3][5] Be sure of your identification: do NOT eat the common potted florist primrose Primula obconica (poison primrose), a notorious strong skin irritant, and never eat nursery plants that may have been sprayed. As with all edible flowers, try only a little at first to check for personal sensitivity.[5]

plants.origin: Garden hybrid (Primula vulgaris × P. veris)
plants.habitat: Garden origin; parent species of European woods & meadows
plants.blurb: The bright bedding primrose: bold flat flowers with a yellow eye — in nearly every color — clustered on a stout stalk over a crinkled-leaf rosette. Cheery cool-season color for a shady, moist spot.
plants.water: Moderate to high; keep evenly moist — not for dry zones
plants.spread: Clumping; divide every few years
plants.seasons: Rosette of crinkled leaves; big multicolored flower clusters in spring
plants.wildlife: Early nectar for bees
plants.toxic: Foliage and sap (primin) can irritate skin — see Edible parts

edible.summary: The flowers are generally regarded as edible, like those of its cowslip and primrose parents — but treat with caution: nursery bedding plants are often pesticide-treated, and the foliage and sap can irritate the skin.[5][6]
edible.parts: Only the flowers are used as food. The flowers of its parent cowslip (Primula veris) are eaten raw or cooked — added to green and fruit salads, used as a garnish, made into wine, or used to flavor creams and desserts — and polyanthus flowers are used the same way as an ornamental, edible garnish.[5]
edible.preparation: Use flowers only from plants you know have not been sprayed; pick them fresh, remove the green calyx, and use them raw as a garnish or in salads, or candied/infused as with cowslip.[5] Do not eat the leaves as a vegetable, and never eat commercial nursery bedding plants, which are commonly treated with pesticides.[5][6]
edible.caution: Primula foliage and sap contain primin (a benzoquinone allergen concentrated in the glandular hairs on the leaves and stems) that causes allergic contact dermatitis in sensitive people — wear gloves when handling.[6] The notorious 'poison primrose' Primula obconica is the worst offender, but P. veris and other primulas also carry primin, so skin reactions are possible from polyanthus too.[6] Because these bedding plants are frequently grown with pesticides and are sold as ornamentals, eat only the flowers of plants you are certain are unsprayed, and never the foliage.[5][6]

plants.origin: Europe & W Asia
plants.habitat: Meadows, pastures & grassy slopes, often on lime
plants.blurb: The meadow cowslip: nodding clusters of deep-golden, orange-flecked bells on slender stalks over a leaf rosette, sweetly scented. Tougher and more sun- and drought-tolerant than most primroses — the best bet of the group here.
plants.water: Moderate; more drought-tolerant than other primroses once established
plants.spread: Clumping; self-seeds into grass
plants.seasons: Leaf rosette; golden nodding flower clusters mid-spring; brown seed capsules by summer
plants.wildlife: Early nectar for long-tongued bees and butterflies; larval food plant for the Duke of Burgundy butterfly in its native range
plants.toxic: Foliage and sap (primin) can irritate skin — see Edible parts

edible.summary: Flowers and young leaves are traditionally edible — the flowers famously flavour cowslip wine, and the young leaves go raw into salads — but eat only in moderation.[6][7]
edible.parts: The fragrant yellow flowers are the classic flavouring for English cowslip country wine and are also used in vinegars, syrups and desserts; the young leaves have a mild, slightly citrusy taste and are eaten raw as a salad green (traditional in Spanish cooking) or cooked as a potherb.[6][7]
edible.preparation: Use only young, tender leaves and freshly opened flowers — older leaves toughen and can taste soapy. Flowers can be candied, steeped for wine or tea, or scattered fresh over food; young leaves are used raw or lightly cooked. Treat it as an occasional garnish, not a staple.[6][7]
edible.caution: This is a traditional edible, not a mainstream food, and should be eaten sparingly. All parts contain triterpene saponins that in quantity can cause nausea or diarrhoea, so keep servings small; the roots are much higher in saponins and are a medicinal, not a food.[6][7] The foliage and sap contain primin and related compounds that cause contact dermatitis — a skin rash — in sensitive people, so wear gloves when handling and be cautious if you are prone to allergies.[3][6] Cowslip has declined seriously in the wild and is legally protected in places (e.g. Northern Ireland), so harvest only from your own garden plants, never from wild colonies.[4]

plants.origin: Himalaya to W China
plants.habitat: Moist alpine meadows, streamsides & clearings
plants.blurb: Drumstick primrose: perfect spherical globes of lilac, purple, pink or white flowers on stout stalks over paddle-shaped leaves — among the earliest primulas, opening as the snow leaves. Wants cool, humus-rich, constantly moist ground.
plants.sun: Part shade to full sun (with moisture)
plants.water: High; wants consistently moist, humus-rich soil — not for dry zones
plants.spread: Clumping rosettes; self-seeds
plants.seasons: Big paddle-leaf rosette; spherical flower globes in very early spring
plants.wildlife: Early nectar for bees and butterflies
plants.deer: Usually passed over by deer and rabbits
plants.toxic: Foliage and sap (primin) can irritate skin — see Edible parts

edible.summary: Not a food plant — grown purely as an ornamental; handling the foliage and sap can irritate skin.[4][5]
edible.caution: Primula foliage and sap contain the contact allergen primin and related quinoid compounds, which can trigger allergic contact dermatitis — itching, redness, and rash — in sensitive people, so wear gloves when dividing or handling it. No part is eaten; it has no documented culinary use and is grown as an ornamental only.[4][5]

plants.blurb: Colorado's showiest alpine primrose: vivid magenta flowers with a golden eye over big fleshy paddle leaves, lining icy snowmelt streams at 10,000–12,000 ft. Gorgeous but famously skunky-scented — and a hard high-alpine specialist to grow down in the garden.
plants.water: High — constant cold moisture; grows in and beside snowmelt streams
plants.spread: Clumping from a stout crown
plants.seasons: Big fleshy paddle-shaped leaves; clusters of magenta golden-eyed flowers in mid-summer; dry seed capsules by late summer
plants.wildlife: Nectar for alpine bumblebees (several Bombus spp.) and other high-country pollinators
plants.deer: Rankly skunk-scented foliage is little browsed
plants.toxic: Foliage can cause allergic contact dermatitis (the genus allergen primin) in sensitive people — handle with care; see Edible parts

edible.summary: Not a food plant — Parry's primrose has no documented edible use, is famously skunk-scented, and, like others in its genus, can cause allergic skin reactions on handling.[2][7]
edible.caution: This alpine wildflower is not eaten and has no established edible use, so don't forage it. The whole plant is rankly skunk-scented — even a light touch releases a fetid, skunk-like odor that can persist for years on dried material.[2][4] Handle it with care as well: plants of the genus Primula are a well-known cause of allergic contact dermatitis, driven by the allergen primin concentrated in the glandular hairs on the leaves and stems, so wear gloves if you are sensitive.[7] On principle, high-alpine natives like this should never be dug, picked, or foraged from the wild — they are slow, fragile, and often protected on public land; enjoy them in place and grow only nursery-propagated seed.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A jewel of the shortgrass prairie: wiry stems and fine ferny leaves topped by rose-purple thimbles whose tiny flowers open in a rising, golden-anthered ring. A nitrogen-fixing legume and a keystone for native bees — and tough as nails in hot, lean ground.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established (deep taproot)
plants.spread: Clumping from a deep taproot; a nitrogen-fixing legume that enriches lean soil
plants.seasons: Fine ferny foliage; rose-purple thimble flowers opening in a rising ring early-to-mid summer; seedheads in fall
plants.wildlife: A keystone for native bees (a specialist-bee host) plus many butterflies; a high-protein legume that also feeds wildlife and fixes nitrogen
plants.deer: Palatable — the high-protein foliage is browsed
plants.toxic: Non-toxic

edible.card: Leaves for tea; sweet roots chewed
edible.summary: A minor traditional food, not a staple: the leaves were steeped into a pleasant beverage (tea) and the sweet roots were chewed like candy by Plains peoples.[4]
edible.parts: The dried leaves were steeped to make a pleasant tea-like beverage, and the long, sweet taproot was chewed as a nibble or candy by the Oglala, Lakota, Comanche, and other Plains peoples — a minor traditional food, not a dietary staple.[4]
edible.preparation: Steep the leaves (fresh or dried) in hot water for a mild herbal beverage; the sweet root was simply chewed. Be sure of your identification first — confirm the wiry stems, fine pinnate leaves, and the distinctive rose-purple thimble of flowers opening in a rising golden ring.[1][4]
edible.caution: This is a documented but minor traditional tea/nibble, not a well-known food crop — treat it as an occasional novelty, not a staple, and only after confirming the plant's identity. No part is known to be toxic, but as with any wild plant, sample sparingly the first time.[4]

plants.blurb: A tough little keystone: low silvery-hairy mounds smothered in golden daisies from midsummer to frost, feeding fifty kinds of specialist native bee. Thrives on hot, lean, dry ground where fussier plants fail.
plants.water: Low; very xeric once established
plants.spread: Low, clumping and bushy; self-sows modestly
plants.seasons: Silvery-hairy gray-green foliage; golden daisies from midsummer well into fall — a very long bloom
plants.wildlife: A specialist-bee keystone (some 50 native bee species) plus nectar for many bees and butterflies; seeds for birds
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; no documented edible use

edible.summary: Grown as a native ornamental and keystone pollinator plant, not for eating; no documented culinary use and not known to be poisonous.[1][2]
edible.caution: Hairy golden aster has no documented food use, so it is treated as inedible — don't eat any part. It is not known to be notably poisonous, but absence of food documentation is not a green light, and as with any wild daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant you should never eat one without certain identification.[1][2]

plants.blurb: The long-blooming false sunflower: cheerful golden daisies (with golden, not dark, centers) from early summer clear to frost on a sturdy clump. Easier and longer-blooming than a true sunflower, and a magnet for bees and butterflies.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; may self-sow
plants.seasons: Golden daisies with golden centers from early summer to frost — very long blooming; toothed foliage
plants.wildlife: A specialist-bee host plus nectar for many bees and butterflies; seeds feed birds; a caterpillar host
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; no notable edible use

edible.summary: Grown as a native ornamental and pollinator/wildlife plant, not for eating; no established culinary use and not known to be poisonous to people.[1][2]
edible.caution: False sunflower has no documented food use, so it is treated as inedible — don't eat any part. It is not known to be notably poisonous, but absence of food documentation is not a green light, and as with any daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant you should never eat one without certain identification.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Bigelow's tansyaster throws big lavender-purple, gold-eyed daisies on branching stems from late summer into fall, when pollinators need them most. A short-lived, self-sowing native of dry, sunny ground that drifts politely through a xeric bed.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping and short-lived (biennial to short-lived perennial); self-sows to persist
plants.seasons: Grayish foliage; big lavender-purple, gold-eyed daisies from late summer into fall
plants.wildlife: Important late-season nectar for native bees and butterflies; a specialist-bee (tansyaster) host
plants.toxic: Non-toxic

edible.summary: No documented culinary use; grown as a native ornamental and late-season pollinator plant, not for eating.[1]
edible.caution: Tansyaster has no documented food use, so it is treated as inedible — don't eat any part. As an aster-family (Asteraceae) native it is not known to be notably poisonous, but absence of documented edibility is not a green light to eat it.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A tough native hawthorn of streambanks and foothill draws: clouds of white spring flowers, glossy leaves, and dark wine-black haws that feed the birds, all on a thorny small tree that makes superb wildlife cover. A caterpillar keystone — and it wants a bit more water than the xeric natives.
plants.size: 10–20 ft tall × 8–15 ft wide (small tree / large shrub)
plants.water: Moderate — a riparian native that wants more water than the xeric plants; not for dry zones
plants.spread: Single- or multi-stemmed thorny small tree; can sucker into a thicket
plants.seasons: Clusters of white flowers in spring; glossy toothed leaves in summer; dark purple-black haws in fall that persist; bare thorny structure in winter
plants.wildlife: A caterpillar keystone (host to ~90 butterfly and moth species); spring nectar for bees; haws feed many birds and mammals; dense thorny cover and nesting
plants.deer: Browsed when young; the thorns deter larger browsers
plants.toxic: The ripe haws are edible (see Edible parts); the thorns are sharp and the seeds should not be eaten (cyanogenic, like other Rosaceae)

edible.summary: The ripe dark-purple haws (fruit flesh) are edible — mealy and seedy fresh, so they are best cooked into jelly, jam, or syrup; a traditional Indigenous food.[1][2]
edible.parts: Only the flesh of the fully ripe, dark purple-black haw is eaten. Hawthorn fruit was a traditional food across the Interior West, gathered in autumn and eaten fresh or, more often, dried and cooked into jelly, jam, syrup, and fruit leather. The flesh is rather mealy, dry, and full of hard seeds, so it is far better cooked and strained than eaten out of hand.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Harvest when the haws are fully ripe and dark; cook the flesh down and strain out the large, hard seeds to make jelly, jam, or syrup. Do not chew or eat the seeds (pips). Watch the sharp thorns while picking.[1][2]
edible.caution: Eat only the flesh of ripe (dark) haws. As with other rose-family stone/pip fruits (apple, cherry, chokecherry), the seeds (pips) contain cyanogenic compounds and should not be chewed or eaten — eat the flesh and spit out or strain off the seeds. Be sure the fruit is dark-ripe, and mind the plant's sharp thorns when harvesting.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Unglamorous but a pollinator powerhouse — the region's #2 plant for specialist native bees: bright yellow daisies cupped in sticky, curled-back resinous bracts on a tough, drought-proof native of hot, lean, disturbed ground. A self-sowing pioneer; deadhead if you want to keep it in bounds.
plants.water: Low; very xeric — a tough pioneer of dry, lean ground
plants.spread: Clumping biennial/short-lived perennial that self-sows readily — a pioneer of disturbed ground; deadhead to limit spread
plants.seasons: Resinous gray-green foliage; yellow daisies cupped in distinctive sticky, curled-back green bracts from midsummer into fall
plants.wildlife: A keystone for specialist native bees (about 68 species — the region's #2 specialist-bee plant) plus many bees, butterflies and beetles; seeds for birds
plants.deer: Resistant — the resinous, bitter foliage is left alone
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to people (the sticky resin was a traditional medicinal); can accumulate selenium on seleniferous soils, a minor livestock note

edible.summary: Not a food — bitter and resinous, with only documented medicinal/folk use, not culinary; grow it as a native pollinator plant, not for eating.[1][2]
edible.caution: Curlycup gumweed is not eaten as food: the foliage and flower heads are coated in a sticky resin and are intensely bitter (tannins, volatile oils, resins, bitter alkaloids and glucosides), which is why livestock and deer avoid it. Its only documented human uses are medicinal/folk — the resin was traditionally used for respiratory complaints (asthma, bronchitis, cough) and skin irritations such as poison-ivy/poison-oak rash — not as a vegetable or seasoning, and that historical medicinal use is not a basis for casual consumption. It is also a facultative selenium accumulator on seleniferous soils, so plants from such ground can carry elevated selenium. Treat it as inedible.[1][2]

plants.origin: Turkey and western Asia
plants.habitat: Rocky slopes, roadsides, and disturbed ground in dry temperate regions
plants.blurb: The quintessential cottage-garden spire: hollyhocks send up 5–8 ft stalks packed with crepe-paper flowers in every color from white to near-black, a magnet for painted lady butterflies and bumblebees. Biennial or short-lived perennial, they self-sow reliably into a permanent colony wherever the soil is lean and drainage is sharp.
plants.sun: Full sun (6+ hours); tolerates light afternoon shade
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established — avoid wet feet
plants.spread: Self-sows freely; forms loose colonies over time
plants.seasons: Bold basal rosettes emerge in spring; tall spires of saucer flowers in summer (June–August); dried disk-shaped seedheads on bare stalks persist into fall and winter
plants.wildlife: Bees, hummingbirds, and butterflies work the flowers; larval host for painted lady butterfly (Vanessa cardui); songbirds eat the seeds
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses; flowers and young leaves are edible; sap may cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals

edible.card: Flowers as edible garnish or salad; young leaves cooked
edible.summary: The flowers are edible fresh and commonly used as a showy garnish or salad decoration; young leaves can be cooked as a pot-herb. The whole plant is non-toxic to people and pets.[1][2]
edible.parts: The **flowers** (petals and buds) are the best-known edible part: mild-tasting, slightly mucilaginous, used raw as a colorful garnish in salads and cocktails, or floated in beverages. They can also be stuffed or crystallized. Young **leaves** are edible cooked — the mild flavor is palatable as a pot-herb or finely chopped into cooked dishes, though the texture is somewhat rough and mucilaginous. Young **stems** can be eaten raw. The **root** yields a starchy mucilage historically used as a thickener, and a refreshing flower-petal tea is documented in traditional practice.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Flowers need no preparation — rinse and use raw. For leaves, cook briefly as a pot-herb; the mucilaginous texture softens on cooking and the mild flavor blends well with other greens. Choose only young, fresh leaves before the plant bolts; older leaves become tough. The root is a traditional, minor medicinal-culinary item, not a main food crop.[1][2]
edible.caution: The whole plant is listed as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses by the ASPCA; no toxic compounds are documented in the literature for this species. However, the sap and leaf hairs can cause mild contact dermatitis (skin irritation) in sensitive individuals — wear gloves when handling if you have sensitive skin. The leaves are mucilaginous and rough-textured; large raw quantities may cause mild digestive discomfort. As with all edible flowers, avoid plants that have been treated with pesticides.[1][3]

plants.origin: Europe and western Asia, from Britain east to the Urals and into Siberia
plants.habitat: Moist mixed deciduous woodland, forest margins, and sheltered ravines on fertile, well-drained soils
plants.blurb: The classic European street and shade tree — and one of the finest bee trees in the temperate world. Heart-shaped leaves cast a dense umbrella of shade; in midsummer every hive within range descends on the intensely fragrant pale-yellow flowers. Tolerates Boulder's alkaline clay soils and harsh winter cold, though it wants reliable moisture and shows leaf scorch when stressed by heat and drought.
plants.size: 50–70 ft tall × 35–50 ft wide at maturity; pyramidal when young, broadly oval with age; slow- to medium-growing
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade; at least 6 hours direct sun for best form and flowering
plants.water: Moderate — not xeric; needs regular deep watering in summer, especially in heat or wind; drought stress causes leaf scorch
plants.spread: Non-suckering; upright pyramidal crown rounding with age; casts dense shade under the canopy
plants.seasons: Fragrant pale-yellow flowers midsummer draw bees for days; clear yellow fall color; smooth silvery-gray bark and upright branching create winter presence; persistent fruit bracts hang on bare twigs into winter
plants.wildlife: A premier bee tree — linden nectar and pollen are a major midsummer bee resource for honeybees and native bees alike; aphid colonies on the foliage feed ladybirds, hoverflies, and other beneficial insects; foliage hosts caterpillars of many moths
plants.deer: Browsed when young; larger established trees are largely resistant to deer browse
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to people, dogs, and cats; linden flowers and young leaves are edible

edible.summary: Linden flowers are the classical tilleul herbal tea of European tradition — fragrant, sweet, and mild. Young spring leaves are also edible as a salad green. Both are used with ordinary care; very old or improperly dried flowers should be avoided.[3][4]
edible.parts: The fragrant pale-yellow flowers (collected with their bract) are dried and brewed as tilleul (linden blossom) tea — one of Europe's best-known herbal teas, with a sweet, honey-like fragrance. They can also be candied, added to cordials, or used to infuse honey or cream. Young leaves gathered in spring, before they fully harden, are mild, slightly mucilaginous, and edible raw in salads or added to sandwiches. The sweet sap can be tapped in spring. The ground flowers and immature fruit have been used as a sort of chocolate substitute, though this preparation is prone to decomposition and is rarely used today.[3][4]
edible.preparation: For tea: harvest freshly opened flower clusters with the bract, dry in a single layer at room temperature out of direct light for one to two weeks, then steep two to three teaspoons of dried flowers in hot (not boiling) water for 5–10 minutes. Use within one year of drying for best flavor and fragrance. For salad leaves: harvest only the freshest, most tender spring leaves (before the leaf hardens) — they are mild and pleasant raw and need no cooking. Do not eat the hard mature leaf or seeds.[3]
edible.caution: Only harvest fresh, properly dried flowers — very old or improperly stored dried flowers have been reported in some historical sources to cause headache or mild narcotic-like symptoms if brewed very strong; the risk is low with properly cured stock, but it is reason to use freshly dried flowers and avoid flowers that have been stored for more than a year. Do not confuse linden (Tilia) with the unrelated lime tree (Citrus aurantifolia) or with any species of Toxicodendron. Linden is non-toxic to people, dogs, and cats. The seeds and mature foliage are not culinary.[3][4]

plants.origin: Mediterranean basin to western Asia
plants.habitat: Disturbed ground, roadsides, and dry stony hillsides of the Mediterranean region
plants.blurb: The peppery salad rocket — sow early, harvest baby leaves cut-and-come-again, and pull it before summer heat turns it bitter and sends it bolting into its lacy cream flowers. Two short seasons (spring and fall) are better than one long one here.
plants.size: 6–18 in tall × 6–12 in wide (rosette stage); to 3 ft when bolted
plants.sun: Full sun in spring and fall; part shade or afternoon shade helpful in summer to slow bolting
plants.water: Moderate; keep soil evenly moist — drought stresses plants and hastens bolting
plants.spread: Self-sows readily once it bolts; thin seedlings and pull unwanted volunteers
plants.seasons: A leafy rosette in cool weather (spring/fall); bolts to knee-high branching stems covered in cream 4-petaled flowers with distinctive dark veining in the heat; frost-killed, no winter presence
plants.wildlife: Flowers draw bees, hoverflies, and small butterflies once the plant bolts
plants.deer: Seldom browsed — the pungent leaves are unappealing to deer
plants.toxic: Not toxic; a brassica — contains glucosinolates (the source of its peppery bite), which are benign in food quantities

edible.card: Peppery leaves (salad, raw or wilted); edible flowers; young seed pods and seeds
edible.summary: Arugula is a long-cultivated salad and potherb: the young leaves are the primary food, eaten raw or wilted, with a peppery-nutty bite; the flowers, young seed pods, and seeds are also edible.[1][2]
edible.parts: The **young leaves** are the main crop, harvested cut-and-come-again for salads, sandwiches, pizza, or lightly sautéed as a wilted green. The **flowers** (cream, 4-petaled, purple-veined) are edible raw as a garnish or salad ingredient. **Immature seed pods** can be eaten raw or cooked when still green and tender. The **seeds** have a history of use as a condiment and oil source (taramira oil in South Asian cooking).[1][2]
edible.preparation: Young leaves need no preparation — rinse and eat raw. Larger, more mature leaves are pungent; blanching or sautéing briefly in olive oil mellows the bitterness. Flowers are eaten as-is. Immature pods can be pickled or stir-fried. Seeds are pressed for taramira oil or ground as a condiment in traditional use.[1][2]
edible.caution: Arugula is a brassica and contains **glucosinolates** — the compounds responsible for its peppery flavor. In ordinary salad quantities these are harmless and have no adverse effect on healthy adults. In very large raw quantities (unusual diet amounts), the goitrogenic breakdown products of glucosinolates could theoretically affect thyroid function in sensitive individuals, though arugula is considered low-risk in this regard compared to raw kale or broccoli. **ID caution:** Arugula is sometimes confused with other wild Brassicaceae, particularly *Diplotaxis* species ('wild rocket') — *Diplotaxis tenuifolia* has yellow flowers, not the cream-and-purple-veined flowers of true arugula (*Eruca vesicaria*); both are edible, but verify your ID before eating any unconfirmed wild mustard. The seeds, leaves, and oil are safe; no parts are toxic.[1][2]

Summer — arugula growing wild in rocky ground, the whole plant spreading low · © Tony KM (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

plants.origin: Mediterranean & Near East (Fertile Crescent)
plants.habitat: Disturbed ground, field margins, and rocky Mediterranean scrub
plants.blurb: The classic cool-season edible climber — delicate compound leaves, white or purple pea flowers, and fat green pods ready to pick from spring through early summer. Direct-sow as early as March on the Front Range; it thrives in our cool spring air and needs a trellis or twiggy sticks to scramble up.
plants.size: Dwarf types 1–2 ft; tall types 4–6 ft tall × 1–2 ft wide
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist throughout the season — not xeric; mulch to keep roots cool in our warm spring
plants.spread: Climbs by branched tendrils; needs a trellis, netting, or twiggy sticks; does not spread or self-sow aggressively on the Front Range
plants.seasons: Cool-spring foliage, flowers, and pods (March–June on the Front Range); declines and dies down as summer heat builds; no winter presence
plants.wildlife: Self-pollinating but bumblebees visit; a nitrogen-fixing legume that enriches the soil for follow-on crops
plants.deer: Not reliably deer-resistant — tender new growth and pods may be browsed; bird netting also protects seedlings from pigeons
plants.toxic: Edible and non-toxic; do not confuse with ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus), a separate plant in this guide that is toxic

edible.card: Seeds (shelled peas), edible pods (snap/snow), and pea shoots
edible.summary: One of the most important food plants in the world — the ripe or immature seeds (shelled peas), the whole tender pod (snap and snow peas), and the young shoot tips and leaves are all edible raw or cooked.[1][2]
edible.parts: Three distinct edible products: (1) **Shelled peas** — the seeds inside mature pods; sweet and eaten raw or cooked, fresh or frozen. (2) **Edible pods** — in snap-pea and snow-pea varieties the whole pod is eaten; snap pods are harvested rounded and full, snow peas when flat. (3) **Pea shoots** — the young tendrils and growing tips of any variety; delicate and sweet, eaten raw in salads or briefly stir-fried. The pea flower is also edible as a garnish.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Shelled garden peas: eat raw when young and sweet, or boil/steam briefly (2–4 minutes) and season simply. Frozen peas are blanch-frozen and need only a brief heat through. Snap and snow peas: eat raw, stir-fry for 1–2 minutes, or steam briefly to retain crunch. Pea shoots: eat raw in salads or wilted for 30 seconds in a hot pan. Do not confuse garden pea pods with ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus) pods, which are TOXIC — Lathyrus grows alongside Pisum in some gardens and the pods are visually similar.[1][2]
edible.caution: Garden pea (Pisum sativum) is a safe, mainstream food with no significant toxicity. The main practical danger is **confusion with ornamental sweet pea (Lathyrus odoratus)**, which is a separate vine plant in this guide (listed as Toxic). Lathyrus pods look similar to pea pods but are poisonous — they cause lathyrism, a serious neurological syndrome. Always confirm identification by smell (garden peas smell faintly sweet/green; sweet pea flowers are intensely fragrant) and plant source before eating any pod you did not grow yourself. Large quantities of raw mature peas are mildly hard to digest (raw starch + lectins), though normal garden portions are fine.[1][2]

plants.origin: Southwestern & Eastern Cape Province, South Africa
plants.habitat: Grassy mountain slopes and marshy stream margins of the Cape
plants.blurb: The original torch lily: a bold clump of arching strap foliage that sends up tall spikes graduating from blazing red-orange at the top to lemon-yellow at the base — a hummingbird and bee magnet from midsummer into fall. Tough and xeric-once-established, but sharply drained soil is non-negotiable.
plants.size: 3–5 ft tall in bloom × 2–3 ft wide (foliage clump 18–24 in)
plants.water: Moderate; sharp drainage critical — wet winter crowns rot
plants.spread: Slowly clumping via rhizomes; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Bold evergreen strap foliage year-round (may bronze at tips in hard winters); red-to-yellow torch spikes midsummer into fall; no ornamental winter structure once leaves lie flat
plants.wildlife: Hummingbirds and bees work the nectar-rich tubular flowers
plants.toxic: Not significantly toxic; no part is eaten — grown as an ornamental

Summer — a single orange-red poker spike face-on in the garden · © rbooker (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

edible.summary: Red hot poker is a garden ornamental with no food use and no significant toxicity — not eaten, not poisonous.[1][2]
edible.caution: Kniphofia uvaria has no documented edible use; no part of the plant is eaten as food. Plants for a Future rates its edibility as 0 with 'None known' under edible uses.[3] No significant mammalian toxicity is attributed to it in the horticultural literature, and it is not a recognized poisonous plant.[1] The milky sap of the stems can mildly irritate skin or eyes — avoid rubbing your face after handling and wash hands after working with the cut stems.[2] Treat it as an ornamental only and do not consume any part.

plants.origin: Rocky hills of Turkey, the Caucasus, and northern Iran
plants.habitat: Dry rocky hillsides and stony slopes in full sun
plants.blurb: The ultimate silver-foliage groundcover: a spreading mat of impossibly soft, thick, woolly leaves that beg to be touched. Front Range sun and lean, dry soil are exactly what it wants — it thrives where many plants struggle, smothering weeds in a river of silver and sending up tall woolly flower spikes studded with pink-purple whorls in summer. The catch: overhead irrigation and high humidity rot the wool, so it fits xeric and well-drained plantings, not thirsty borders.
plants.size: 6–12 in foliage mat × 18–24 in flowering spikes; spreads to 1–2+ ft wide
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — overhead watering and wet conditions rot the woolly leaves
plants.spread: Spreads by rooting at stem nodes to form a dense mat; moderate self-sowing
plants.seasons: Silver-woolly mat year-round (semi-evergreen); tall woolly flower spikes with pink-purple whorls in summer; foliage may brown and tatter in wet winters but persists
plants.wildlife: Bees and butterflies visit the flowers; wool carder bees (Anthidium spp.) harvest the leaf fibers to line their nests — this plant is their primary fiber source
plants.deer: Deer and rabbit resistant — both avoid the fuzzy, aromatic foliage
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to people and pets; not a culinary food — leaves have folk-medicinal wound-dressing history but are not eaten

shots.cap: Summer — the dense silver-woolly mat; tongue-shaped leaves up close · © clarissakelly (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — face-on: whorled purple-pink flowers on the woolly spike · © Jordi Bassols Garcia (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — whole plant in full bloom: multiple woolly flower spikes over the silver mat · © hulene (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — the silver-woolly foliage mat with a rising flower spike · © ingridmeandre (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

edible.summary: Lamb's ear is a garden ornamental with a folk-medicinal wound-dressing history but no culinary use — not a food plant.[1][2]
edible.caution: Stachys byzantina is not eaten as food. Its leaves have a long folk-medicinal history as an emergency wound dressing — the soft, absorbent, naturally antiseptic wool was pressed against cuts and scrapes in the field, giving rise to the old common name 'woundwort' (shared with other Stachys species). This is a wound-dressing tradition, not a culinary one. Neither the NC State Plant Toolbox nor other authoritative horticultural references lists any edible use or records it as toxic — it is simply a non-food ornamental plant. Do not eat it; there is no preparation that makes the leaves palatable or useful as a food, and the aromatic foliage has an unpleasant taste when chewed.[1][2]

plants.origin: Eastern & central North American prairies and open woodlands
plants.habitat: Open prairies, meadows, roadsides and woodland edges on moist to average soils
plants.blurb: The eastern American prairie beardtongue that tolerates clay and moister ground — tall white foxglove-like wands in early summer, glossy foliage (maroon-burgundy on 'Husker Red' and 'Dark Towers'), and persistent seed capsules through winter. More adaptable to Colorado's denser soils than the xeric Western penstemons, and just as attractive to bees and hummingbirds.
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade (at least 4–6 hours)
plants.water: Low to moderate; more moisture- and clay-tolerant than xeric Western penstemons — needs supplemental water at lower Front Range elevations
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows modestly
plants.seasons: White foxglove-like wands in late spring–early summer; glossy green (or maroon on cultivars) foliage through growing season; dark oval seed capsules persist on stems into winter
plants.wildlife: Excellent native-bee and bumblebee plant; specialist bee Osmia distincta uses it; visited by hummingbirds and butterflies

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally as a native-bee and hummingbird pollinator plant — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: Despite being called 'foxglove beardtongue,' Penstemon digitalis is NOT related to foxglove (Digitalis) and does not carry foxglove's cardiac-glycoside toxicity.[2] It has no known culinary use and is not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it. Don't let the common name suggest otherwise.

plants.origin: SE Europe to central Asia (Balkans, Ukraine, steppe belt to Iran)
plants.habitat: Dry stony grasslands, open steppe, and rocky hillsides on lean alkaline soils
plants.blurb: The elegant mullein: slender wispy spires of flat saucer-shaped flowers in shades of purple, rose, and white rise from a bold basal rosette of crinkled leaves — far more refined than the roadside yellow club of common mullein. Short-lived but self-sows reliably on lean, sharply drained ground; a xeric Front Range perennial that bees and pollinators work steadily through early summer.
plants.size: 2–3 ft tall in bloom × 12–18 in wide
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — sharp drainage is essential, wet soil kills
plants.spread: Clumping rosette; self-sows modestly on bare or lean ground
plants.seasons: Basal rosette of crinkled, often purple-flushed leaves through spring; slender spires of purple, rose, or white saucer flowers from late spring into summer; spent seed spikes persist into fall
plants.wildlife: Bees, hoverflies, and butterflies work the open saucer flowers; an RHS Plants for Pollinators selection
plants.deer: Resistant — fuzzy, slightly bitter leaves deter browsing
plants.toxic: Not toxic to people or pets; leaf hairs can mildly irritate skin on contact

edible.summary: Purple mullein is a garden ornamental with no documented culinary use — grown for its flowers, not as food.[1][2]
edible.caution: Verbascum phoeniceum has no recognized food use and should not be eaten. The genus Verbascum has a long folk-medicinal history (the common mullein V. thapsus was used as an expectorant herb), but that is V. thapsus, not this species, and it was a medicinal application under specific preparation, never a food. The leaf and stem hairs of V. phoeniceum can cause mild skin irritation on contact in sensitive individuals. It is not classified as a significant poison, but it is not food — treat it as an ornamental only.[1][2]

plants.origin: Central and southern Europe (Italy to the Balkans)
plants.habitat: Open rocky slopes, dry meadows, roadsides, and disturbed ground on well-drained calcareous soils
plants.blurb: A refined, long-lived perennial mullein bearing slender spires of small yellow (or white in 'Album') flowers, each with a tuft of distinctive purple-woolly stamens. More floriferous and persistent than the biennial common mullein, it forms a basal rosette of dark, nettle-textured leaves and self-sows gently without becoming weedy — a polished mid-summer vertical for dry, lean ground.
plants.size: 2.5–3.5 ft tall in bloom × 1.5–2 ft wide (rosette)
plants.sun: Full sun to part shade (best in full sun)
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established — sharp drainage essential
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows modestly in open ground
plants.seasons: Semi-evergreen dark-green basal rosette year-round; slender yellow flower spires midsummer; persistent dry seed spikes through fall and winter
plants.wildlife: Bees work the flowers; the purple woolly stamens are a tactile draw for pollen-foraging bees
plants.toxic: Not significantly toxic; contact with the foliage may cause mild skin irritation in sensitive individuals

edible.summary: Nettle-leaved mullein is a garden ornamental with no documented culinary use.[1][2]
edible.caution: Verbascum chaixii has no recognized food use and should not be eaten. Like all mulleins, the foliage bears fine hairs that can cause mechanical skin and mucous-membrane irritation on contact. Some Verbascum species have historical folk-medicinal applications (dried flowers for herbal teas in closely related species), but V. chaixii is not a documented culinary or tea plant, and the hairy foliage and seeds should not be consumed.[1][2]

plants.origin: Western Turkey (Anatolia)
plants.habitat: Dry rocky hillsides and disturbed ground at low to mid elevations
plants.blurb: The most dramatic of the ornamental mulleins: a first-year silver-white woolly rosette the size of a dinner platter, then a towering 5–6 ft candelabra of sulphur-yellow flowers on silver-felted stems. The silver wool that gives 'Arctic Summer' its name glows in winter light. Self-sows to persist; the whole biennial cycle is the show.
plants.size: 5–6 ft tall in bloom × rosette 2–3 ft wide
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — sharp drainage essential, tolerates lean dry soil
plants.spread: Self-sows freely; deadhead spent spikes before seed set to control; thin seedlings to 18–24 in
plants.seasons: Year 1: a spectacular flat silver-woolly rosette, striking through summer and winter; Year 2: towering candelabra spire of yellow flowers all summer, then silvery dried stems persist through fall
plants.wildlife: Bees (especially bumble bees) work the yellow flowers; hummingbirds occasionally visit
plants.deer: Resistant — the dense white wool deters browsing
plants.toxic: Leaf hairs irritate skin and mucous membranes on contact; no documented culinary or poisoning risk

edible.summary: Giant silver mullein is a garden ornamental with no documented food use; not a culinary plant.[1][2]
edible.caution: Verbascum bombyciferum has no established edible use and should be treated as an ornamental only — do not eat any part. Like other mulleins it is grown for its foliage and flower spires, not as food, and the dense white woolly hairs that cover the leaves are a mechanical irritant to skin and mucous membranes, so handle the foliage with gloves. (The unrelated common mullein, V. thapsus, has a folk-medicinal cough-tea history, but that is a different, weedy species and is not an endorsement to consume this ornamental.)[1][2]

plants.origin: Garden hybrids of European and Eurasian Verbascum species
plants.habitat: Ancestors native to dry rocky slopes, disturbed ground, and thin soils of Europe and W. Asia
plants.blurb: Pastel-flowered hybrid mulleins — soft saucer blooms in peach, apricot, pink, lilac, and cream on branching spikes over woolly grey-green rosettes. Short-lived but stunning, and a magnet for bees.
plants.water: Low; xeric — sharp drainage essential, resents wet soil
plants.spread: Clumping; hybrids self-sow little to none
plants.seasons: Woolly grey-green basal rosette spring–fall; branching flower spikes midsummer; plants often short-lived (1–3 years); may not persist through winter in clay/wet soil
plants.wildlife: Bees, including native bees and bumblebees

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally — no documented culinary use for these garden hybrid mulleins.[1]
edible.caution: Garden hybrid Verbascum are not documented as food plants. The foliage and stems have fine hairs that are a mechanical irritant to skin and mucous membranes — handle with gloves if sensitive, and keep children from rubbing their eyes after touching the woolly leaves. No part is eaten; not documented as systemically poisonous, but do not consume.[1][2]
