plants.blurb: Colorado's iconic Great Plains native — a clump of stiff, blue-green sword leaves year-round and a towering spike of creamy bell flowers in early summer, pollinated exclusively by the yucca moth in one of nature's most celebrated obligate mutualisms.
plants.size: 2–3 ft clump × 3–4 ft wide; flower stalk to 2–5 ft tall
plants.water: Very low; xeric once established — no supplemental water needed on the Front Range
plants.spread: Spreads slowly by rhizomatous offsets into a colony; non-invasive in the garden
plants.seasons: Evergreen blue-green sword-leaf clump year-round; creamy white bell flowers June–July on tall stalks; brown oval seed capsules persist through fall and winter
plants.wildlife: Obligate mutualist with the yucca moth (Tegeticula yuccasella) — its sole pollinator; provides food and nesting for small mammals, birds, and reptiles; aphids on flowers attract ladybugs
plants.deer: Highly deer-resistant — sharp spines tip every leaf
plants.toxic: Roots contain saponins — toxic to dogs, cats, and horses (vomiting, liver effects); mildly irritating to people; flowers and fruit are edible when prepared

edible.card: Flowers, young fruit, and seeds — cooked
edible.summary: Flowers, young fruit, and seeds of Yucca glauca are edible when cooked — a genuine traditional food of Great Plains peoples. Raw fruit is bitter; roots are NOT edible (saponins).[1][4]
edible.parts: The **flowers and buds** can be eaten raw in small amounts but are better lightly cooked (stir-fried, blanched, or added to soups); the petals are mildly sweet. The **immature green fruit** (before the capsule hardens) can be peeled and boiled or roasted as a vegetable. Mature fruit has been dried and formed into cakes for storage. The **seeds** are edible roasted. The young **flower stalk** (before the buds open) can be harvested and cooked like asparagus — peel and boil or roast the white inner core.[1][4]
edible.preparation: Flowers and buds: blanch or lightly sauté to remove bitterness. Young fruit: peel the green capsule and boil with a change of water to reduce bitterness, then season; or roast directly in coals. Flower stalk: harvest the emerging stalk before buds break, peel to the white interior, and boil or roast. Seeds: harvest from ripe capsules, dry, and roast. Do not eat roots in any form.[1][4]
edible.caution: The **roots are NOT edible** — they contain high concentrations of steroidal saponins, which are toxic to people (gastrointestinal irritation), highly toxic to dogs, cats, and horses (vomiting, potential liver effects), and acutely toxic to fish. Do not prepare or ingest roots. Saponins in roots are not destroyed by brief cooking. The edible parts (flowers, young fruit, seeds, flower stalk) also contain milder saponins and should not be eaten raw in large quantities — cook them to reduce bitterness and irritants. Note: the yucca moth larvae consume the developing seeds; heavily infested capsules should not be collected for food. Harvest the flower stalk only from your own plants — in wild populations removal of the stalk destroys that year's seed set and disrupts the yucca-moth mutualism.[1][2][4]

plants.blurb: Colorado's toughest native cactus — a low, mat-forming clump of flat jointed pads armored with spines and tiny barbed glochids, erupting in silky 3-inch blooms of yellow, pink, or magenta in late spring, then lying dormant and shriveled-purple in the cold before plumping back up in spring.
plants.water: Very low; xeric once established — no supplemental water needed
plants.spread: Slow-spreading mat by pad rooting; non-invasive in garden settings with sharp drainage
plants.seasons: Evergreen pad mat year-round; silky yellow or pink flowers May–Jun; dry spiny fruit fall; pads shrivel and purple-tinge in cold, re-plump in spring
plants.wildlife: Flowers attract native bees, especially specialist cactus bees (Diadasia); pads and fruit provide shelter and food for birds, small mammals, and lizards
plants.deer: Highly deer- and rabbit-resistant — the spines and glochids deter all browsers
plants.toxic: Not chemically toxic; the real hazard is glochids — tiny, barbed, easily-detached bristles that lodge painfully in skin, eyes, and mouth; handle only with thick gloves or tongs

edible.card: Fruit (tunas) peeled; young pads (nopales) despined and cooked
edible.summary: Edible with careful preparation: ripe fruit (tunas) and young pads (nopales) are traditional foods once spines and glochids are thoroughly removed — glochids are a serious hazard if any remain.[1][2]
edible.parts: The ripe fruit (tunas) is edible raw or cooked into jelly, syrup, or candy once peeled. Young pads harvested in spring are edible as nopales — a traditional food of Great Plains Indigenous peoples and a staple vegetable across the American Southwest. Seeds can be dried, parched, and ground into meal. The flowers are edible as well, though seldom used.[1][2]
edible.preparation: **Fruit:** harvest ripe tunas with tongs when brownish-tan and soft (late summer–fall). Hold with tongs over an open flame (camp stove, torch) to burn off glochids, or roll vigorously in coarse sand; then slice off both ends and peel back the skin. Do not rinse until glochids are fully removed — wet glochids adhere to skin even more stubbornly. **Pads (nopales):** use tongs to cut a young spring pad where it joins the parent; carry with tongs to a work surface. Burn or scrape off glochid clusters (areoles) with a stiff brush, then peel the outer skin or cube the pad and boil until tender (10–15 min), discarding the cooking water to remove some mucilaginous texture. Grill, sauté, or add to eggs, tacos, or salads. **Note on O. polyacantha:** the fruit is drier and spinier than the commercial Mexican prickly pear (*O. ficus-indica*) and requires more glochid-removal effort.[1][2]
edible.caution: The primary hazard is glochids — the tiny, barbed, hair-like bristles clustered at each areole. They detach on the lightest touch, lodge deep in skin and soft tissue (including lips and tongue), are almost invisible, and cause persistent stinging pain. Even on fully ripe, peeled fruit, embedded glochids can remain — inspect carefully and use the flame or tape methods above. Never bite raw fruit directly from the plant. Raw young pads also contain oxalic acid; cook and discard the cooking water to reduce this. The plant itself is not chemically toxic to people or domestic animals (the ASPCA and NC State Plant Toolbox list no systemic toxicity for prickly pear), but glochid contact with eyes requires medical attention. Keep children away from unprocessed pads and fruit.[1][2]

plants.blurb: Colorado's own state cactus — a mounding hedgehog cactus that erupts in brilliant scarlet cups mid-spring through early summer, drawing hummingbirds to its nectar-rich flowers while thriving in the leanest, rockiest, driest ground the Front Range offers.
plants.water: Very low; xeric once established — no supplemental water needed in most Front Range settings
plants.spread: Slowly widens by offsets into a dense multi-stemmed mound; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Evergreen ribbed cylindrical stems year-round; scarlet funnel flowers Apr–Jun; small reddish fruit Jul–Aug; ribbed green mound persists through winter (stems shrivel in cold, plump back up in spring)
plants.wildlife: Premier early hummingbird plant — broad-tailed and rufous hummingbirds are the primary pollinators; bees also work the flowers

edible.card: Ripe fruit (despine first — burn or rub off spines before eating)
edible.summary: The ripe berry-like fruit is edible and reportedly sweet-tasting; Native peoples of the Southwest ate it fresh and prepared it in various ways.[1][3]
edible.parts: The fruit (a fleshy berry, 15–35 mm, ripening reddish) is the edible part, available roughly 2 months after the spring bloom (Jul–Aug). At full ripeness the spines loosen and can be rubbed or burned off. The flesh is white to pale, sweet, and juicy — regarded as among the most flavorful of all Southwestern cactus fruits. Stems have been used ethnobotanically but require elaborate processing and are not a practical food for gardeners.[1][3]
edible.preparation: Wear thick gloves and use tongs to harvest ripe fruit. Remove spines completely before eating — the traditional method is to burn them off over a flame or rub them off on a rough surface; handle with care as residual spines and glochid-like clusters at the fruit tip can embed in lips and throat. Eat fresh out of hand once despined, or cook down into jam or syrup. The flesh is sweet enough to eat raw when fully ripe. Do not eat unripe fruit (spines firmly embedded, flesh unpalatable).[1][2][3]
edible.caution: The spines and any small spine clusters (at the fruit's areoles) are the primary hazard — they are stiff and can embed in skin, lips, and throat if the fruit is eaten without thorough spine removal first. This is different from Opuntia's detachable glochids but still requires careful preparation. No known toxicity in the fruit flesh itself; this is a well-documented traditional food of multiple Southwestern Indigenous nations. Children should not handle or eat the fruit without adult supervision due to spine risk. The stems and roots are not edible and contain compounds that make them unpalatable raw.[1][2][3]

Winter — dormant mound shriveled by cold and frost; stems plump back up with spring moisture · © Trevor Harding (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: The indestructible alpine succulent — tight geometric rosettes (the 'hens') surrounded by a ring of miniature offsets ('chicks') that spread into a dense, weed-proof mat. Thrives in blazing sun, bone-dry rocky soil, and hard cold, with a monocarpic flowering show each summer and foliage that bronzes beautifully in winter.
plants.size: Rosettes 1–4 in across; mat spreads to 12–24 in; flower stalks 8–12 in tall
plants.water: Very low; xeric once established — excess moisture causes rot
plants.spread: Spreads steadily by offsets to form a dense, weed-smothering mat; non-invasive
plants.seasons: Evergreen succulent mat year-round; rosettes bronze and redden in winter cold; thick flower stalks with star-shaped pink-red flowers Jun–Aug; spent monocarpic rosette dies after bloom and is replaced by chicks
plants.wildlife: Summer flowers attract native bees and butterflies
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to pets (ASPCA); not established as a culinary food
plants.origin: Mountains of southern & central Europe (Alps, Pyrenees, Apennines, Dinarides)
plants.habitat: Rocky alpine slopes, screes, grassland crevices, and stony siliceous soils

edible.summary: Hens and chicks has folk topical uses but is not an established culinary food — the ASPCA lists it as non-toxic to pets, and no authoritative source documents it as safe or tested to eat.[1][3]
edible.caution: Sempervivum tectorum is not a culinary food. Historically, the leaf juice was applied externally — by Galen and Dioscorides — as an astringent for skin ulcerations, burns, and insect stings, much like aloe gel. This is a topical folk-medicinal use, not documented edibility: no authoritative food or horticultural source (USDA, NC State, MBG, RHS) lists it as a food plant, and no preparation has been established as culinary-safe for internal use.[1][2] The ASPCA classifies the closely related Sempervivum/Echeveria group as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, and Sempervivum tectorum is not known to cause acute poisoning.[3] However, 'non-toxic' in the pet-safety sense does not mean 'food' — the plant contains organic acids and astringent tannins, and no safety data exist for regular human consumption. Treat it as non-toxic but inedible: it is a garden ornamental, not a herb or vegetable. Keep out of reach of small children who might graze on the rosettes.

plants.blurb: Colorado's own native succulent groundcover — a tight mat of fleshy, lance-shaped leaves that turns fire-red in drought and cold, then explodes into bright yellow star-flowers in early summer, thriving in the very hottest, rockiest, leanest spots where almost nothing else will grow.
plants.water: Very low; xeric once established — thrives on neglect in dry, rocky sites
plants.spread: Slowly creeps and self-layers into a tidy low mat; non-invasive, never weedy
plants.seasons: Evergreen succulent mat year-round; foliage flushes red-bronze in heat, drought, and winter cold; star-shaped bright yellow flowers Jun–Jul; wispy seed capsules late summer; red-flushed mat persists through winter
plants.wildlife: Flowers attract native bees, bumblebees, and butterflies; primary larval host of the Rocky Mountain apollo butterfly (Parnassius smintheus)

edible.summary: Leaves have been eaten raw or cooked by Indigenous peoples, but the plant contains alkaloids and a cyanogenic compound (sarmentosin) that can cause GI upset or act as a laxative — eat only small amounts if at all, and never substitute it for confirmed edibles without expert ID.[2][3]
edible.caution: Sedum lanceolatum leaves have documented traditional food use — the Okanagan-Colville used the plant and foragers describe the leaves as mildly mucilaginous with a crisp texture, edible raw or pickled in small quantities. However, several important cautions apply: (1) The plant contains alkaloids (sedine, sedamine) and the cyanogenic compound sarmentosin, the same toxin that makes it a defended host plant for Parnassius smintheus butterfly larvae; eating large quantities can cause gastric upset, and the plant has been used specifically as a laxative by Indigenous groups. (2) ID risk is significant — Sedum lanceolatum closely resembles related yellow-flowered stonecrops (S. stenopetalum, S. debile) in the Rocky Mountains; a misidentification is easily made by non-botanists. (3) No authority we have reviewed comprehensively documents it as a reliably safe culinary plant for regular use. Treat as a curiosity edible only: in a very small tasting quantity by a confident identifier, not as a food crop. Keep children and pets from grazing. The PFAF database rates edibility 1/5 and flags the stomach-upset risk explicitly.[2][3]

plants.blurb: A Plant Select garden strain raised from Bruce Meyers' big-flowered penstemon crosses — sturdy spires of giant goblet flowers in a jewel-box mix of white, rose-pink, lavender and deep purple-violet over thick waxy gray-green leaves in early summer.
plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established (overwatering shortens its life)
plants.spread: Clumping; short-lived but self-sows
plants.seasons: Giant white-to-purple goblet flowers late spring–early summer; thick waxy gray-green basal leaves; tan seed stalks into fall
plants.wildlife: Bees and other pollinators; large flowers draw bumblebees
plants.origin: Garden hybrid (Penstemon ×mexicali / grandiflorus parentage)
plants.habitat: n/a — garden hybrid (wild parents of dry rocky plains & slopes)
plants.gaps: Photos are species-representative of the parent species Penstemon grandiflorus (large lavender goblet flowers, waxy gray-green clasping leaves), honestly captioned: 'Prairie Jewel' is a garden strain with no clean iNaturalist taxon, and no open-licensed cultivar-specific photo set exists. The cultivar's flowers range across the same white/pink/lavender/purple jewel mix.

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally for its big jewel-toned flowers — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[2]

plants.blurb: A Plant Select workhorse — one of Bruce Meyers' Mexicali crosses of showy Mexican penstemons with hardy American kinds, throwing dense wands of violet-purple flowers all summer over a tidy mound of narrow dark-green leaves.
plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping; reseeds modestly
plants.seasons: Violet-purple flower wands June–August; tidy mound of narrow dark-green leaves; tan seed stalks into fall
plants.wildlife: Bees and other pollinators; long summer bloom feeds native bees
plants.origin: Garden hybrid (Penstemon ×mexicali — Mexican-species parentage)
plants.habitat: n/a — garden hybrid
plants.gaps: Photos are species-representative of the Mexicali parent species Penstemon campanulatus (violet-purple tubular flowers, narrow dark-green leaves — the same look as 'Pikes Peak Purple'), honestly captioned: this named ×mexicali hybrid has no clean iNaturalist taxon and no open-licensed cultivar-specific photo set exists. No clean (non-hand-held) open-licensed seed-capsule shot of the parent species was available, so the reel covers flower/bloom-mass/habit/foliage/context instead of a dedicated seedhead.

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally for its long violet-purple bloom — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[2]

plants.blurb: A bushy Sonoran-Desert penstemon hung with airy whorls of rose-pink to magenta trumpets over blue-green clasping leaves — a hummingbird magnet that blooms early and shrugs off heat, drought and lean ground.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — water monthly at most
plants.spread: Clumping; reseeds
plants.seasons: Rose-pink trumpets spring into early summer; blue-green clasping foliage; tan seed stalks afterward
plants.wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant; special value to native bees (Xerces)
plants.origin: Sonoran Desert of the SW US (AZ, NM, CA) & NW Mexico
plants.habitat: Desert washes, gravelly slopes & roadsides, 2,000–7,000 ft

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally for its hummingbird flowers — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[2]

plants.blurb: The gray-green, mountain form of wild bergamot — the more drought-tolerant Rocky Mountain native. Rounded whorls of ragged lavender-pink mint flowers crown aromatic, felted foliage all summer, mobbed by bees, butterflies and hummingbirds.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; slowly spreads by rhizome, not aggressive here
plants.seasons: Aromatic gray-green foliage spring–fall; lavender-pink whorls midsummer; round dark nutlet seedheads stand into winter
plants.wildlife: Outstanding pollinator plant — bumble bees, native bees, butterflies and hummingbirds; songbirds take the seed

edible.summary: An aromatic mint-family herb — the fragrant leaves and flowers make a pleasant minty-oregano tea and seasoning, like wild bergamot.[1][2]
edible.parts: The aromatic leaves and flowers are the edible parts. Use them fresh or dried to brew a fragrant tea and to season cooked dishes; the flavor is a thyme/oregano-mint blend with the characteristic bergamot scent.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Steep fresh or dried leaves and flower whorls as a hot tea, or chop the leaves as a culinary herb. Harvest just before or at full bloom when the aromatic oils peak. The flowers also make an edible garnish.[1][2]
edible.caution: A recognized aromatic herb with no notable toxicity, but it is strongly flavored — use in moderation as a seasoning/tea, not a bulk food. As with any wild plant, positively confirm the gray-leaved mint-scented identity before eating, and people sensitive to mint-family herbs should sample cautiously.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A lemon-scented annual bee balm whose stems stack tier upon tier of tubular pink-to-lavender flowers between showy bracts, like a pagoda. Easy from seed, it self-sows into colonies and draws clouds of bees, butterflies and hummingbirds.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Upright annual; self-sows freely into colonies
plants.seasons: Lemon-scented foliage rosette through spring; tiered pink-lavender-white flower whorls early–midsummer; tan tiered seed spikes after
plants.wildlife: Magnet for bees, butterflies and hummingbirds; a top nectar annual
plants.origin: South-central US (TX, OK, KS, NM) & N Mexico
plants.habitat: Prairies, glades & open ground on limestone

edible.summary: A lemon-scented mint-family herb — the citrusy leaves and flowers make a refreshing tea and a flavoring for salads and cooked dishes.[1][2]
edible.parts: The aromatic leaves and flowers are the edible parts, prized for a distinct lemon-mint scent and flavor.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Use leaves and flowers raw or cooked — steep them fresh or dried for a lemony tea, scatter the flowers over salads as an edible garnish, or chop the leaves to season cooked foods. Harvest at peak bloom when the oils are strongest.[1][2]
edible.caution: A recognized culinary aromatic with no notable toxicity, but it is strongly flavored — use as a seasoning/tea rather than a bulk food, and confirm the lemon-scented identity before eating. Those sensitive to mint-family herbs should sample cautiously.[1][2]

plants.blurb: An upright, aromatic mint topped from late summer into fall with spikes of tubular raspberry-pink flowers that hummingbirds and bees mob; crush a leaf and it smells of bubblegum. Xeric, deer-proof, and built for lean, sun-baked Front Range ground.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — avoid wet feet
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows modestly
plants.seasons: Aromatic gray-green foliage spring–fall; raspberry-pink flower spikes late summer into fall; tan seed spikes stand into winter
plants.wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant; also bumblebees and other native bees
plants.origin: S New Mexico, W Texas & N Mexico (Chihuahua)
plants.habitat: Rocky desert-mountain slopes, granite-cliff crevices & canyons, ~5,000–6,000 ft

edible.summary: Grown mainly as an ornamental hummingbird plant; the aromatic leaves (and dried flowers) are used to brew a minty herbal tea and flavor dishes.[3]
edible.parts: The aromatic leaves and dried flower petals are the parts used — steeped fresh or dried for a mint-and-bubblegum-scented herbal tea, or used in small amounts to add a minty note to cooking.[3]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves and dried flowers as a flavoring or tea, not as a vegetable — steep a few clean leaves in hot water. This is a culinary/herbal-tea use of a fragrant mint, not a bulk food.[3]
edible.caution: Agastache cana is grown chiefly as an ornamental, and its food use is limited to leaves and dried flowers as a mint-like tea/flavoring — there is no documented edible use for any other part, so don't eat the roots, stems, or seeds. As with any aromatic mint-family herb, use it in modest culinary amounts and be confident of the identification before brewing.[3]

plants.blurb: A rare penstemon cousin whose airy sprays of crimson tubular flowers look like tiny red birds perched in the branches — and pull in hummingbirds from June to frost. A long-lived, drought-tough New Mexico native for sun to part shade.
plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping; long-lived, not aggressive
plants.seasons: Mound of green foliage spring–fall; crimson flower sprays June into fall; tan seed stalks after bloom
plants.wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant; also bees and other pollinators
plants.origin: Mountains of west-central New Mexico
plants.habitat: Rocky canyon slopes & cliffs, ~5,000–8,000 ft

edible.summary: A figwort grown strictly as an ornamental hummingbird plant — no documented edible use, so don't eat it.[1]
edible.caution: Scrophularia macrantha is grown only as an ornamental and pollinator plant; authoritative sources record no culinary use and no safe edible preparation for any part. Figworts (genus Scrophularia) have a history of external/topical folk-medicine use, not food use — treat this as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots.[1]

plants.blurb: A sprawling, fleshy-leaved native mound that erupts each afternoon with dozens of large magenta trumpets — opening at dusk for hawkmoths and closing by midday. Bone-dry tough, fed by a massive woody taproot, it returns bigger every year.
plants.water: Low; xeric — needs no water once established
plants.spread: Clumping mound from a deep taproot; not aggressive
plants.seasons: Blue-green fleshy foliage emerging purple in spring; magenta evening flowers summer into fall; dies back to the ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Night-blooming for hawk/sphinx moths; also dusk-and-dawn bees and hummingbirds
plants.toxic: Roots contain bioactive compounds historically used medicinally; not a food

edible.summary: Not a food plant — its large taproot carries bioactive compounds that Native peoples used medicinally and as a vision-inducing/appetite-suppressing preparation, not as everyday food.[1][3]
edible.caution: Desert four o'clock is grown as an ornamental and is not a recognized edible. Its big woody taproot contains bioactive compounds: Indigenous peoples used the root as medicine — Navajo as a tea/poultice for swelling and rheumatism, Hopi to induce visions during diagnosis, and Zuni powdered into bread as an appetite suppressant — i.e. it was used for its physiological (narcotic/medicinal) effects, not as ordinary food. Do not eat the roots or any part of the plant; these are documented medicinal/ritual uses by knowledgeable practitioners, not a safe culinary preparation.[1][3]

plants.blurb: The classic cut-flower pincushion — domed, honey-scented blooms in purple, wine-red, pink, and white float on tall wiry stems from summer to frost, drawing bees and butterflies and rebounding harder the more you cut.
plants.water: Moderate; even moisture, never waterlogged
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows lightly
plants.seasons: Basal foliage rosette spring; domed pincushion flowers summer into fall; spiny green-to-tan seedheads late season; killed by hard frost
plants.wildlife: Excellent bee, butterfly, and hoverfly nectar plant; goldfinches take the seed
plants.origin: Mediterranean S Europe (naturalized widely)
plants.habitat: Dry grassy slopes & disturbed ground

edible.summary: Sweet scabious is an ornamental cut flower with no established food use — not notably poisonous, but there is no documented edible part, so don't eat it.[1]
edible.caution: Scabiosa atropurpurea is grown strictly as a cut flower and pollinator plant. Authoritative horticultural sources record no culinary use and no edible part — it is not a recognized edible herb and has no documented safe edible preparation. With no positive food record, treat it as a non-food ornamental and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots. (The old common names "mournful widow"/"mourning bride" and the genus name "scabious" come from historic external folk use against skin complaints, not from any food use.)[1]

plants.blurb: The deep-crimson selection of sweet scabious — glowing wine-red pincushion domes on tall wiry stems from summer to frost, a cutting-garden showstopper that draws bees and rebounds the harder you cut it.
plants.water: Moderate; even moisture, never waterlogged
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows lightly
plants.seasons: Basal foliage rosette spring; deep-red pincushion flowers summer into fall; spiny green-to-tan seedheads late season; killed by hard frost
plants.wildlife: Excellent bee, butterfly, and hoverfly nectar plant; goldfinches take the seed
plants.origin: Garden cultivar of Mediterranean Scabiosa atropurpurea
plants.habitat: n/a — garden cultivar

Per the guide's cultivar-photo convention.

edible.summary: Like all sweet scabious, 'Firecracker' is an ornamental cut flower with no established food use — not notably poisonous, but there is no documented edible part, so don't eat it.[1]
edible.caution: Scabiosa atropurpurea is grown strictly as a cut flower and pollinator plant. Authoritative horticultural sources record no culinary use and no edible part — it is not a recognized edible herb and has no documented safe edible preparation. With no positive food record, treat it as a non-food ornamental and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots.[1]

plants.blurb: Dense upright spikes of dark violet-blue flower beads rise over crinkled deep-green foliage from late spring to frost — a tidy, long-blooming clump (1993 Perennial Plant of the Year) that bees and butterflies work all summer on the Front Range.
plants.water: Moderate; even moisture with sharp drainage, especially in winter
plants.spread: Clumping; slowly expands, not aggressive
plants.seasons: Crinkled dark-green basal foliage spring–fall; violet-blue spikes late May–Oct; cut to the ground for winter
plants.wildlife: Strong bee and butterfly nectar plant through the long bloom season
plants.toxic: No significant toxicity reported
plants.origin: Garden hybrid (Eurasian Veronica longifolia/spicata parentage)
plants.habitat: n/a — garden hybrid (wild parents of damp meadows & grassland)

edible.summary: An ornamental speedwell with no documented edible use — not notably poisonous, but not a food plant, so don't eat it.[2]
edible.caution: Veronica 'Sunny Border Blue' is grown strictly as an ornamental and pollinator plant. Authoritative horticultural sources record no culinary use and no established edible part for this hybrid; it is not a recognized food. With no positive food record, treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots.[2]

plants.gaps: Photos are species-representative spike speedwell (Veronica spicata, an upright violet-blue spike type and a parent of the 'Sunny Border Blue' hybrid) — no clean open-licensed photo of the named cultivar exists. The hybrid's denser, darker-blue spikes read essentially the same; foliage, habit, and winter seed-spikes are equivalent.

plants.blurb: An intensely clove-and-jasmine-scented garden pink: fringed white flowers each centered on a raspberry-pink eye, hovering over a tight blue-green grassy mat. Cool-spring bloom on a tough, deer-proof, drought-tolerant edging plant for the Front Range.
plants.sun: Full sun to light afternoon shade
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established, needs sharp drainage
plants.spread: Clumping; forms a slowly spreading evergreen mat
plants.seasons: Evergreen blue-green grassy foliage year-round; fragrant white-and-pink flowers late spring into summer; semi-evergreen mat in winter
plants.wildlife: Fragrant flowers draw butterflies, bees, and other pollinators
plants.toxic: Mildly toxic to cats, dogs, and horses (foliage can cause mild stomach upset; sap may irritate skin)
plants.origin: Garden hybrid (Eurasian Dianthus parentage)
plants.habitat: n/a — garden hybrid

edible.summary: The flower petals are edible with a sweet, spicy clove flavor — but only the colored petal, with the bitter white heel removed, and only from unsprayed plants.[2][3]
edible.parts: Only the flower petals are used. Garden pinks (Dianthus) have long been a culinary edible flower — the petals carry a sweet, clove-to-jasmine spice that flavors sugars, syrups, vinegars, cordials, and desserts, or garnishes salads, cakes, and drinks. The rest of the plant (leaves, stems, roots) is not eaten.[2][3]
edible.preparation: Pull the colored petals from the calyx and snip or pinch off the narrow pale 'heel' at the base of each petal — it is bitter and should be discarded. Use the trimmed petals fresh: scatter on dishes, crystallize with sugar, or steep to flavor syrups and drinks. Only ever eat petals from plants you know are unsprayed (no pesticide/herbicide residues); never use florist's carnations.[2][3]
edible.caution: Eat only the petal, with the bitter white base removed, and only from your own unsprayed garden plants — commercially grown Dianthus may carry pesticide residues and is not for eating. The foliage and sap are a separate matter: the leaves and stems are rated mildly toxic to cats, dogs, and horses (ingestion can cause mild gastrointestinal upset) and the sap can irritate sensitive skin, so the leaves/stems are not a food. Keep the edible use to clearly identified, unsprayed flower petals.[2]

plants.gaps: Photos are species-representative garden/cottage pinks (Dianthus plumarius and kin — fringed, fragrant, blue-green grassy mats) — no open-licensed photo of the named 'Scent First Jasmine' cultivar exists. That cultivar is a fragrant white flower with a raspberry-pink eye; the shown pinks match its mat, fringed petals, and habit, with some variation in petal color.

plants.blurb: A soft, airy mound of fine threadlike foliage smothered for months in pale butter-yellow daisies — the 1992 Perennial Plant of the Year, drought-proof, deer-resistant, and a butterfly favorite that blooms summer to frost on the lean Front Range.
plants.water: Low; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; slowly spreads by rhizome, sterile so it doesn't self-seed
plants.seasons: Ferny threadleaf foliage spring–fall; pale-yellow daisies June–Oct; dies back to ground in winter
plants.wildlife: Butterfly and native-bee nectar plant; songbirds eat coreopsis seed
plants.toxic: Non-toxic to people and pets
plants.origin: Southeastern US
plants.habitat: Dry open woods, clearings & rocky barrens

edible.summary: An ornamental garden perennial with no documented edible use — not notably poisonous, but not a food plant, so don't eat it.[2]
edible.caution: Coreopsis verticillata 'Moonbeam' is grown strictly as an ornamental and pollinator plant. It is rated non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, but authoritative sources document no established culinary use or edible part for it — there is no positive food record, so treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots.[2]

plants.gaps: Photos are species-representative Coreopsis verticillata (threadleaf foliage, flowers, seedheads) — no open-licensed photo of the named 'Moonbeam' cultivar exists. 'Moonbeam' is a softer pale butter-yellow than the deeper gold of the species shown; the foliage, habit, and seedheads are identical.

plants.blurb: A tough, glaucous-leaved prairie native that holds its smooth blue-green foliage clean all season, then erupts in clouds of lavender-blue, yellow-eyed daisies in fall — one of the last great pollinator plants of the year.
plants.water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping; short rhizomes spread it slowly, also reseeds
plants.seasons: Smooth blue-green clasping foliage spring–summer; lavender-blue daisies Aug–Oct; tan seed plumes into winter
plants.wildlife: Major late-season nectar for native bees and butterflies; seeds for birds
plants.toxic: No documented toxicity; grown as an ornamental native, not a food plant

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental native pollinator plant; no documented use as a food.[1]
edible.caution: Smooth aster is not documented as a food plant and is not eaten. It is not notably poisonous to people or pets, but there is no established edible use — treat it as ornamental and don't eat it.[1]

plants.blurb: The mahogany-red form of our native Mexican hat — drooping velvety crimson rays, often edged or tipped in gold, hung beneath a tall sombrero cone. Just as drought-tough and bee-worked as the yellow species, with deeper color all summer.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Clumping; self-sows readily
plants.seasons: Mahogany-red (gold-tipped) sombrero flowers early summer–fall; ferny gray-green foliage; dark seed cones into winter
plants.wildlife: Bees and butterflies; seeds for birds
plants.toxic: No documented toxicity to people or pets; a traditional leaf/flower-head tea is recorded but is a folk medicinal use, not an established food

edible.summary: Not a recommended food. A traditional tea was brewed from the leaves and flower heads, but that is a documented folk-medicinal use, not established food safety.[3]
edible.caution: Several Native American peoples brewed a beverage/medicinal tea from the leaves and flower heads of prairie coneflower, and applied leaf-and-stem washes externally. That ethnobotanical use is not the same as proven food safety, so this guide treats the plant as caution rather than a casual edible — don't rely on it as food. It is not documented as notably poisonous to people or pets, but there is no reason to eat it.[3]

plants.blurb: A Plant Select selection of the Southwest's golden columbine: big, fragrant, clear-yellow flowers with long elegant spurs that nod and sway from late spring to frost — larger, longer-blooming, and far more heat- and drought-tolerant than the blue.
plants.water: Moderate; tolerates dry shade once established
plants.spread: Clumping & short-lived; reseeds generously to persist
plants.seasons: Golden long-spurred flowers late spring–frost; ferny blue-green foliage; cut back to a fresh rosette in midsummer heat
plants.wildlife: Hummingbird, bee & butterfly nectar; long bloom feeds them for months
plants.toxic: Flowers were historically nibbled in tiny amounts for their sweet nectar, but the mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are poisonous if eaten in quantity — not a safe food
plants.origin: Southwestern US mountains (AZ, NM, S Colorado, W Texas)
plants.habitat: moist canyon seeps, streamsides & rocky limestone slopes

edible.card: Flowers in tiny amounts (seeds & roots toxic)
edible.summary: Like other columbines, the flowers were historically eaten in tiny amounts as a sweet, nectar-flavored garnish, but the mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are genuinely poisonous — this is not a casual edible.[4]
edible.parts: Only the flowers have any food history: several Indigenous North American peoples sucked nectar from columbine blooms or ate small numbers of flowers, and the petals have been used in tiny quantities as a sweet, colorful salad garnish — the sweetness is the nectar held in the long spurs. The seeds, roots, and foliage are not food.[4]
edible.preparation: If used at all, only a few fresh flowers as a garnish — never the seeds, roots, or leaves, and never in quantity. There is no safe way to prepare the seeds or roots for eating. Given documented columbine poisonings, the conservative approach is to treat this plant as ornamental and not eat it.[4]
edible.caution: The mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides that are dangerous to the heart if eaten: documented columbine cases include convulsions, respiratory distress, and heart failure after eating fresh foliage, and a child who ate a dozen flowers developed limb weakness, cyanosis, drowsiness, and pinpoint pupils. Keep seeds, roots, and foliage away from children and pets, and do not rely on the old 'flowers are safe' assumption — treat the whole plant with caution.[4][5]

plants.gaps: Photos are species-representative Aquilegia chrysantha (the wild golden columbine), not the named 'Denver Gold' seed selection specifically — no open-licensed cultivar-labeled photo exists. 'Denver Gold' is a Plant Select selection of this species with the same clear-yellow long-spurred flowers, so the species shots match its described look.

plants.blurb: An upright grain amaranth crowned with bold copper-red flower plumes over dark-flushed leaves — a dramatic, drought-shrugging dual-purpose annual grown for both its edible grain and cooked young greens.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Single upright clump; self-sows if seedheads are left
plants.seasons: Dark-flushed leafy stems through summer; deep copper-red plumes midsummer to frost; dry russet seedheads in fall
plants.wildlife: Pollen and abundant seed draw bees and seed-eating birds (goldfinches, juncos)
plants.deer: Not deer-proof; foliage can be browsed
plants.toxic: Leaves accumulate oxalates and (in rich soil) nitrates — eat young leaves cooked and in moderation
plants.origin: Cultivated grain amaranth (Mesoamerican origin)
plants.habitat: n/a — cultigen (warm tropical America)

edible.summary: A dual-purpose grain amaranth: the tiny seeds are a nutritious gluten-free grain and the young leaves are eaten as a cooked green.[1][3]
edible.parts: The ripe seed (grain) is the main food — ground to flour, popped like tiny popcorn, or cooked as porridge. The tender young leaves and growing tips are eaten as a potherb (cooked like spinach).[1][3]
edible.preparation: Harvest, dry, thresh and winnow the grain, then cook it as porridge, pop it dry, or mill it to flour. Eat the young leaves cooked (boiled or sautéed), not raw — cooking and discarding the water cuts their oxalate and nitrate load.[1][4]
edible.caution: The grain itself is a safe staple. The leaves, like spinach and chard, accumulate oxalates and — especially from heavily fertilized soil — nitrates, which is why they are eaten young, cooked (boiling reduces both), and in moderation; people with kidney-stone history or kidney disease should limit oxalate-rich greens. This is the cultivated grain species Amaranthus cruentus, not the herbicide-resistant weed Palmer amaranth (A. palmeri); buy named seed so you know what you are growing.[1][4]

plants.blurb: Fluffy, thread-like flower clusters in soft powder-blue, lavender, and white smother neat mounding plants from early summer to frost — a long-blooming bedding annual butterflies and bees work all season.
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist — wilts quickly if it dries out
plants.spread: Clumping mound; does not run
plants.seasons: Mounded green foliage spring; fuzzy blue-purple-white flower clusters early summer to frost; killed to the ground by first hard frost
plants.wildlife: Long-season nectar for butterflies, bees, and other pollinators
plants.toxic: Poisonous — contains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids; not for eating, dangerous to grazing livestock
plants.origin: Mexico, Central America & the Caribbean
plants.habitat: Moist disturbed ground, fields & forest edges

edible.summary: Poisonous — Ageratum houstonianum is an ornamental, not a food; it contains hepatotoxic pyrrolizidine alkaloids. Do not eat any part.[3][4]
edible.caution: All parts contain pyrrolizidine alkaloids (and precocenes), liver toxins that cause cumulative, irreversible hepatotoxicity — and the plant is a documented livestock poison, with cattle, horses, and pigs most susceptible to liver damage from grazing it. It has also been linked to photosensitization. There is no safe edible preparation: never eat it, keep it out of pasture and away from grazing animals, and wash hands after handling. Grow it strictly as an ornamental pollinator plant.[3][4]

shots.cap: Summer — fuzzy lavender-blue floss-flower clusters face-on · © Hsiao Ching-heng (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — powder-blue thread-like flower cluster in detail · © Benoit Segerer (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — mounding plant in full blue bloom · © Dan Fitzgerald (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — a drift of blue floss flower in the garden · © Luis Mario Calderon (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — ovate, toothed, soft-green leaves · © suchihfen (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — the white-flowered form, fluffy clusters · © Smile Taiwan (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — mounded plants flowering in context · © MP Zhou (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: An airy, subshrubby xeric mint that fizzes with a cloud of tiny pale lavender-to-white flowers from midsummer to frost over small aromatic leaves — one of the longest-blooming, most bee-beloved plants for hot, lean ground.
plants.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping mound; self-sows modestly
plants.seasons: Fine mounded aromatic foliage spring; haze of tiny lavender-white flowers midsummer to frost; dies back over winter
plants.wildlife: Outstanding late-season bee and butterfly nectar plant; alive with pollinators in bloom
plants.origin: Mediterranean S Europe, N Africa & SW Asia
plants.habitat: Dry sunny banks, scrub & rocky ground

edible.summary: An aromatic culinary mint — the leaves (and edible flowers) make a pleasant minty-oregano herbal tea and seasoning.[1]
edible.parts: The small aromatic leaves are the part used; the tiny flowers are also edible and can garnish or flavor dishes.[1]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves fresh or dried, steeped as a mint-lemon/oregano-scented herbal tea or chopped as a culinary seasoning much like a milder oregano/savory; harvest just before flowering for the strongest scent.[1]
edible.caution: A long-used culinary herb of the mint family with no notable toxicity, but as with any aromatic mint use it as a flavoring herb in ordinary culinary amounts, not as a bulk food. Be sure of your identification — confirm the strong minty-oregano aroma — before using any wild-foraged mint, and as always introduce a new herbal tea in moderation.[1]

shots.cap: Summer — airy spray of tiny pale lavender calamint flowers face-on · © Thomas Dreux (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Summer — mounding plant frothing with tiny flowers · © chimonanthus (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — flowering wands of lavender-white tubular flowers · © Sergey Eliseev (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Summer — small aromatic ovate leaves with flower buds · © joaquinguinea (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — flowering stems on dry rocky ground · © biokovo78 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: A statuesque 5–9 ft tallgrass-prairie forb: enormous deeply-cut basal leaves that famously orient north–south (the "compass"), tall stems hung with yellow sunflower-like heads in summer, and a taproot that sinks many feet down — bone-tough and drought-proof once established, but slow to settle in.
plants.water: Low; deep taproot makes it very drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Clumping; non-spreading deep-taproot clump that does not run
plants.seasons: Big deeply-lobed basal leaves spring–fall; yellow daisy heads on tall stems mid-to-late summer; dried seed stalks and seedheads standing through winter
plants.wildlife: Strong pollinator plant — bumble bees and native bees on the flowers; goldfinches and other birds strip the large seeds; a larval host and nectar source
plants.deer: Browsed by deer and livestock — not deer-resistant
plants.origin: Tallgrass prairie of the central US (east of the Rockies)
plants.habitat: Mesic-to-moist tallgrass prairies & roadside remnants

edible.summary: Not a food plant. Historically the dried resin from the upper stems was chewed as a gum, but compass plant has no real edible part — don't treat it as food.[1][4]
edible.caution: Compass plant is grown as an ornamental and prairie pollinator plant, not as food. There is no established edible part. Plains tribes (Pawnee, Omaha, Ponca, Dakota) did chew the hardened amber resin that beads from the upper stalks as a natural chewing gum to clean the teeth and freshen the breath — the source of its nickname "gum weed" — and made the root into a medicinal tea, but those are traditional non-food uses, not a recommendation to eat the plant. The chewing resin is not a documented food, and no part of the plant has a safe, established culinary preparation. The foliage is palatable to grazing livestock but is not a human food.[1][4]

shots.cap: Summer — crisp yellow daisy head with strappy ray florets · © Jeanette Smith (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — flower head face-on with the plant standing in tallgrass prairie · © Thomas Koffel (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

Summer — the big, deeply-cut basal leaves that give the plant its form · © stanmild (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Fall — dried seedheads and large flat seeds at the stem tips · © moontree (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: The legendary sky-blue Himalayan poppy — huge, satiny azure chalices with a boss of orange stamens over bristly foliage. Famously cool-climate and a real challenge on the hot, dry, alkaline Front Range: grow it potted in an acidic mix, in cool shade, kept constantly moist.
plants.sun: Part to full shade; shelter from hot afternoon sun & drying wind
plants.water: High — keep constantly moist all summer, never dry; not for hot, dry sites
plants.spread: Clumping; established clumps slowly spread by offsets (often short-lived here)
plants.seasons: Bristly basal leaf rosette emerges in spring; satiny sky-blue flowers in early summer (June–July); dies back by fall — no winter presence
plants.wildlife: Sky-blue flowers offer nectar & pollen to bumblebees
plants.deer: Little local browse data; the bristly, alkaloid-bearing poppy foliage is of low interest
plants.toxic: Not for eating — a poppy-family plant carrying alkaloids; the bristly sap can irritate skin. No edible use.
plants.origin: Himalayas — SE Tibet, NW Yunnan & N Myanmar
plants.habitat: Cool, moist alpine meadows & open conifer woodland, ~3,000–4,000 m

edible.summary: Ornamental only — the Himalayan blue poppy has no edible use.
edible.caution: Grown purely as an ornamental. Like other members of the poppy family (Papaveraceae), *Meconopsis* contains isoquinoline alkaloids, so no part should be eaten, and the bristly sap can irritate skin.[3] If a child or pet eats a quantity of the plant, contact poison control (1-800-222-1222) or a veterinarian as a precaution.

plants.gaps: One season of interest: spring rosette + early-summer (June–July) bloom — the plant is herbaceous and fully dormant by fall, so there are no honest fall/winter shots (NA on the seasonal axis). Reel skews flower-heavy, as is typical for this taxon; foliage, bristly-stem detail, seed capsules and near + far habit are all covered. Photos are of the species/garden blue poppy.

plants.blurb: The Front Range's classic tall privacy hedge: a dense, upright Asian shrub with glossy leaves, small black berries, and fiery scarlet fall color.
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Upright, multi-stemmed clump; shears into a dense hedge; occasional bird-sown seedlings nearby
plants.seasons: Pinkish-white flowers late spring; glossy black berries late summer into winter; scarlet-red fall foliage
plants.wildlife: Flowers draw bees; berries feed birds through winter; dense twiggy cover for nesting
plants.deer: Not a deer favorite, but not reliably deer-proof
plants.toxic: Berries and foliage are mildly poisonous if eaten in quantity (people & pets); birds eat the fruit unharmed
plants.origin: Northern Asia — Altai Mts. (Russia, Kazakhstan, Mongolia, China)
plants.habitat: Montane forest edges, thickets and rocky slopes

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental hedge, not a food — the berries and leaves are mildly poisonous.[2]
edible.caution: Like all cotoneasters, the berries and foliage contain cyanogenic glycosides (which release small amounts of cyanide as they break down); the toxicity is generally low, but eating a quantity can cause nausea, vomiting, and stomach upset in people and pets, and grazing animals such as horses are more susceptible. Keep children and pets from gorging on the fruit. Birds, by contrast, eat the berries safely and are the plant's main seed-dispersers — do not take that as a sign the fruit is edible for humans.[2][5]

plants.blurb: A low woody-based evergreen cushion that pumps out hot-pink, snapdragon-like flowers from late spring to frost; a xeric, bee-and-hummingbird magnet that's borderline-hardy here.
plants.sun: Full sun; afternoon shade in the hottest spots
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — needs sharp drainage, not for wet ground
plants.spread: Low woody-based mound spreading into a dense cushion; may self-sow
plants.seasons: Nonstop pink flowers late spring to hard frost; small gray-green leaves evergreen in mild winters but killed back — or out — by hard Front-Range cold
plants.wildlife: Bees, butterflies and hummingbirds work the tubular flowers all season
plants.toxic: No documented toxicity to people or pets; also not a food plant
plants.origin: Mexico — Sierra Madre Oriental (Nuevo León)
plants.habitat: Rocky limestone slopes and dry scrub

edible.summary: Grown purely as an ornamental pollinator plant — no part is used as food.[1]
edible.caution: Pink skullcap has no documented culinary use and is not a food plant. Don't confuse it with the medicinal skullcaps of herbal traditions (Scutellaria lateriflora, S. baicalensis) — those are different species and this one is not a substitute for them. It is not listed as notably poisonous to people or pets, but an absence of a toxicity record is not the same as proof of safety, so enjoy it in the garden and don't eat it.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A compact aromatic mound that hazes over with fuzzy blue flower clusters in late summer and fall — one of the best late-season bee and butterfly plants for a hot, dry, well-drained spot.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established
plants.spread: Compact mounded subshrub; stems often die to the ground in a cold Front-Range winter and regrow in spring; may self-sow lightly
plants.seasons: Aromatic gray-green foliage spring–summer; a haze of powder-blue flower clusters in the leaf axils from late summer into fall; foliage yellows and stems are usually cut back or killed to the ground for winter
plants.wildlife: A major late-season nectar source — bees, honeybees and butterflies mob it when little else is still blooming
plants.deer: Seldom browsed; the aromatic foliage makes it reliably deer-resistant
plants.toxic: No documented toxicity to people or pets; the pungent foliage is not eaten
plants.origin: Garden hybrid (Caryopteris incana × C. mongholica); parent species native to East Asia (China, Japan, Mongolia)
plants.habitat: Parent species grow on dry rocky slopes and open scrub in East Asia

edible.summary: An aromatic ornamental with no documented food use — grown for its late flowers, not eaten.[1]
edible.caution: Bluebeard has no history of culinary or medicinal use; the pungent, sagey foliage is not something people eat. It is not listed as notably poisonous to people or pets, but 'not a documented food' is not the same as 'proven safe to eat' — treat it as a strictly ornamental, non-food plant and don't nibble the foliage or flowers.[1][2]

plants.blurb: A tidy clumping ornamental grass topped by long, soft purplish-pink 'bottlebrush' plumes that arch like a fountain from midsummer into fall — hardier and far better-behaved than the tender invasive fountain grasses.
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established, needs sharp drainage
plants.spread: Tidy clumping bunchgrass; can self-sow in the garden — deadhead to manage; not the tender invasive P. setaceum
plants.seasons: Gray-green fountain of fine foliage spring–summer; pinkish-white to rose-purple bottlebrush plumes late summer into fall; fades to tan and stands into early winter
plants.wildlife: Wind-pollinated grass — minor pollinator value; plumes offer cover, finch seed forage, and winter structure
plants.toxic: No reported toxicity to people or pets; grown as an ornamental
plants.origin: Central & Southwest Asia (Caucasus & Anatolia to Central Asia and India)
plants.habitat: Dry rocky slopes, steppe and open stony ground

edible.summary: Not edible — an ornamental grass grown for its foliage and plumes, with no culinary use.[2]
edible.caution: No part of Oriental fountain grass is used as food and there are no documented edible uses; it is grown purely as an ornamental. It is not reported as toxic to people or pets, but the barbed, awned seedheads can be a mechanical irritant to grazing animals, and the absence of any food documentation means it should simply be treated as inedible — don't eat it.[2][3]

plants.blurb: Bushy old-fashioned annual whose fragrant funnel flowers pop open in the late afternoon — hence the name — and often carry several colors on one plant.
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist through summer heat, tolerates short dry spells once established
plants.spread: Bushy mound from a fleshy tuber; self-sows readily and builds a large storage root — deadhead to contain
plants.seasons: Fragrant funnel flowers open each afternoon, summer into fall; the top is killed by the first hard frost, leaving no winter presence
plants.wildlife: Evening-opening flowers draw long-tongued sphinx/hawk moths and hummingbirds
plants.deer: The poisonous foliage is usually passed over by deer, but it is not reliably deer-proof
plants.toxic: Seeds and the tuberous root are poisonous if eaten (alkaloids including trigonelline — stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea); handling the roots can cause skin dermatitis
plants.origin: Tropical South America — Andes (Peru region)
plants.habitat: Disturbed slopes, thickets and roadsides in warm-temperate to tropical America

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental only — the seeds and roots are poisonous; do not eat any part.[1][4]
edible.caution: Both the large black seeds and the tuberous storage root are poisonous, and children have been poisoned after eating the seeds or roots. They contain toxic alkaloids — trigonelline is reported from the root — and ingestion causes stomach pain, nausea, vomiting and diarrhea. Handling the roots (and contact with the plant's juice) can also cause skin dermatitis, so wear gloves when digging or dividing tubers and keep the seeds away from curious children and pets. No preparation makes any part of this plant safe to eat.[1][2][4]

plants.blurb: A vigorous woody climber that grips by aerial rootlets and drips clusters of big orange-red trumpets all summer — a hummingbird magnet, but aggressive enough to need a firm hand.
plants.sun: Full sun (part shade tolerated, fewer flowers)
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established, lean is better
plants.spread: Aggressive — suckers profusely from underground runners and self-seeds, popping up feet from the plant; clinging aerial rootlets grip anything. Not a CO-listed weed, but behaves invasively — contain it.
plants.seasons: Orange-red trumpets July–September; long bean-like seed pods in fall that dry to split brown husks; bare woody stems in winter
plants.wildlife: A premier hummingbird plant (its principal pollinator); bees and butterflies also work the flowers; dense twiggy cover for birds
plants.deer: Usually passed over by deer, but not reliably deer-proof
plants.toxic: Handling the foliage, flowers, or sap can cause skin redness and itching (contact dermatitis) — hence 'cow-itch'; grown ornamentally, not eaten
plants.origin: Southeastern United States
plants.habitat: Woodland edges, fencerows, riverbanks and thickets of the E/SE US

edible.summary: Not a food — trumpet vine is grown strictly as an ornamental, and handling it can irritate the skin.[1]
edible.caution: Its common name 'cow-itch vine' says it plainly: contact with the leaves, flowers, or sap causes redness, itching, and minor skin irritation (contact dermatitis) in many people — wear gloves to prune it or pull its suckers. No part is grown or documented as food; it is planted for its flowers and its value to hummingbirds, not to eat. Keep children from handling or mouthing the foliage and seed pods.[1][2]

Summer — a flowering vine smothering a tree · CC0 / iNaturalist · © no rights reserved (CC0) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: A thorny small tree in flat clusters of white spring flowers, then round red 'haws' — each capped by a persistent five-pointed calyx star — that hang on into winter to feed the birds; glossy, toothed, mostly unlobed leaves color orange-red in fall. This is a planted ornamental hawthorn identified from photos only to genus: the exact species is uncertain, so it is written honestly at genus level (its unlobed leaves and round red haws best match a cockspur-type). Like all its rose-family kin (apple, pear), it is fire-blight-prone.
plants.size: ~15–30 ft tall × 20–30 ft wide (small, broad, often flat-topped tree)
plants.water: Moderate; drought-tolerant once established
plants.spread: Single- or multi-stemmed thorny small tree with a broad, often flat-topped crown; long, sharp branch thorns
plants.seasons: Flat clusters of white flowers in spring; glossy toothed leaves in summer; round red haws in fall persisting into winter; orange-red fall foliage and bare thorny structure
plants.wildlife: Excellent bird food (haws) and dense thorny nesting cover; nectar and pollen for bees and other insects; a caterpillar/Lepidoptera host
plants.deer: Browsed when young, but the sharp thorns deter larger browsers
plants.toxic: The ripe haw flesh is edible cooked (see Edible parts), but the hard seeds should be spat out, not eaten; the long branch thorns are sharp
plants.origin: Cultivated ornamental hawthorn; exact species and native range uncertain (genus Crataegus is North-temperate)
plants.habitat: (genus) open woodland, hedgerows, forest edges and streamsides

edible.card: Ripe red haws, cooked (jelly/syrup) — spit the seeds
edible.summary: The flesh of the ripe red haws is edible, best cooked into jelly, jam, or syrup — but spit out the hard seeds, and note the exact species here is uncertain, so eat conservatively.[1][5]
edible.parts: Only the flesh of the fully ripe, deep-red haw is eaten. Hawthorn haws are a traditional foraged fruit, gathered in fall and most often cooked into jellies, jams, syrups, wines, and 'ketchups.' The flesh is rather mealy, dry, and seedy, so it is far better cooked and strained than eaten fresh; each haw holds up to about five hard seeds that cling together and are eaten around, not through.[1][5]
edible.preparation: Harvest fully ripe red haws in fall, cook the flesh down, and strain out the hard seeds to make jelly, jam, or syrup. Do not chew or crush the seeds. Mind the sharp thorns while picking.[1][5]
edible.caution: Eat only the flesh of clearly ripe (red) haws, and ideally cooked — eaten raw in quantity haws can cause mild stomach upset.[5] The large, hard seeds should be spat out, never chewed or crushed: hawthorns carry cyanogenic glycosides, and like other rose-family pip and stone fruits (apple, cherry, chokecherry) the seeds are best not eaten.[7] Because this tree is identified only to genus — the exact species is uncertain — treat that as one more reason for caution: confirm the plant and eat only well-cooked flesh of ripe red haws, and never the seeds. Also mind the plant's long, sharp thorns.[5][7]

plants.blurb: A tough, adaptable little mound of a native shrub smothered in bright golden, powder-puff flowers all summer, followed by rusty seed capsules that hold through winter.
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant and adaptable once established
plants.spread: Compact, rounded, multi-stemmed mound; spreads slowly by rhizome and occasional self-sown seedlings, but well-behaved
plants.seasons: Bright yellow flowers early-to-mid summer; cone-shaped reddish-brown seed capsules ripen late summer and persist into winter; older stems show flaky, pale-orange exfoliating bark
plants.wildlife: Pollen-rich flowers are worked heavily by native bees and other pollinators; seeds and cover draw birds
plants.deer: Usually left alone by deer and rabbits — the hypericin-laced foliage is unpalatable, though not guaranteed deer-proof
plants.toxic: Foliage and sap contain hypericin; skin contact or ingestion can cause photosensitivity (a sunburn-like reaction) in people, pets, and grazing livestock
plants.origin: Central & eastern US — New York to Iowa, south to Georgia & Oklahoma
plants.habitat: Rocky slopes, dry open woods, meadows and stream gravel bars

edible.summary: Grown as an ornamental — not a food; the foliage and sap are photosensitizing.[2]
edible.caution: Like other St. John's worts, the plant contains hypericin: skin contact with the sap, or ingestion of the foliage, can cause photosensitivity — an exaggerated, sunburn-like reaction on sun-exposed skin — in some people, pets, and grazing livestock. It is not a foraging plant; do not confuse it with the herbal-supplement common St. John's wort (Hypericum perforatum, a separate species and a Colorado noxious weed) and do not eat any part.[2]

plants.blurb: Grown for show, not the spice rack: a low aromatic mound that erupts in a haze of dark purple bracts and rose-pink flowers from midsummer into fall, swarming with bees and butterflies when little else is blooming.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — needs sharp drainage
plants.spread: Clumping woody-based mound; spreads slowly at the base, not aggressive
plants.seasons: Aromatic green foliage on red-purple stems in summer; dark purple bracts and rose-pink flowers midsummer into fall; leaves flush red in autumn; dies back to a low woody base in winter
plants.wildlife: A top late-season nectar plant — bees, native bees, honeybees and butterflies mob the long-blooming flowers into fall
plants.deer: Aromatic mint-family foliage; reliably deer- and rabbit-resistant, rarely browsed
plants.toxic: Non-toxic — an edible culinary-family herb, safe for people and pets
plants.origin: Eastern Mediterranean — Turkey, Cyprus, and the Levant (Syria, Lebanon)
plants.habitat: Dry rocky hillsides and scrubland on lean, well-drained ground

edible.card: Aromatic leaves & flowers — a mild oregano seasoning or tea
edible.summary: A genuine culinary-family herb: the aromatic leaves and flowers are edible and safe, just milder and more bitter than the oregano you'd cook with.[2][3]
edible.parts: The aromatic leaves and the tiny flowers are both usable in the kitchen — chopped fresh or dried as an oregano-type seasoning, steeped into a mild herbal tea, or scattered as an edible garnish. This is an ornamental selection grown chiefly for its purple bracts and long bloom, so the flavor is noticeably milder and a bit more bitter than the true culinary oregano (*Origanum vulgare* / Greek oregano), which stays the better choice for strong seasoning.[2][3]
edible.preparation: Harvest sprigs anytime through the season, best just before flowering when the leaves are most aromatic; strip leaves from the wiry stems and use fresh or air-dry them. Use with a light hand as you would any oregano — the flavor, while gentle, still reads as oregano — and rinse flowers before garnishing.[3]
edible.caution: No part of the plant is poisonous — this is a safe mint-family culinary herb, not a toxic ornamental. The only real caveats are culinary rather than safety: the flavor is milder and can be faintly bitter, so it underperforms true cooking oregano; and, as with all herbs, concentrated oregano essential oil is a different thing from the leaf and should not be swallowed. Harvest only from plants you haven't sprayed.[2][3]

plants.gaps: Reel shows the species Origanum laevigatum (open-licensed photos), representative of the near-identical 'Herrenhausen' garden selection.

plants.blurb: A jewel-box rock-garden oregano grown for show, not the kitchen: wiry trailing stems drip with cone-like, overlapping bracts that shade from soft green to glowing rose-pink, with tiny pink flowers peeking through — like a cascade of ornamental hops.
plants.water: Very low; xeric — sharp drainage essential, rots in wet soil
plants.spread: Low trailing mat / spiller cascading over rocks, walls and container edges
plants.seasons: Cone-like rose-pink hop bracts with pink flowers summer into fall; dries well for arrangements; foliage semi-evergreen but often dies back over a hard winter here
plants.wildlife: An RHS 'plants for pollinators' selection — the small flowers draw bees and other pollinating insects
plants.deer: Aromatic herbal foliage — deer and rabbits usually leave it alone
plants.toxic: Non-toxic; in the culinary oregano genus, but grown as an ornamental — its foliage is milder and less pungent than kitchen oregano
plants.origin: Garden hybrid of eastern-Mediterranean species (Turkey, Caucasus, Greece)
plants.habitat: Wild parents grow on rocky limestone slopes and screes

edible.summary: Non-toxic and in the culinary oregano genus, but grown as an ornamental — the leaves are only mildly aromatic and it is not really used in cooking.[1]
edible.parts: The small aromatic leaves are edible like other Origanum, but this hybrid was bred for its bracts, not flavor: its foliage lacks the pungency and quality expected of a culinary oregano and is usually not used in the kitchen.[1]
edible.preparation: If you did use it, treat it like any oregano — a few fresh leaves as a mild seasoning or steeped for tea — but for actual cooking reach for true oregano (Origanum vulgare), which carries far more flavor.[1]
edible.caution: This is a garden hybrid grown for looks and is not documented as a culinary herb; there is no serious toxicity concern for people, but it is an ornamental, not a food crop, so enjoy it for the show rather than the plate.[1]

Tiny pink flowers peeking from the pink bracts · © David J. Stang (CC-BY-SA) / Wikimedia Commons

Mass of green-and-pink hop-like bracts — shown on the near-identical parent species, Origanum rotundifolium · © mknesal (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.gaps: 'Kent Beauty' is a hybrid with limited open-licensed imagery: four shots are the true cultivar (Wikimedia Commons); the bract-mass and whole-plant habit shots are the near-identical parent species Origanum rotundifolium, captioned as such.

plants.blurb: The tall, big-pompom marigold — dense double globes of gold, orange, and creamy-white over aromatic ferny foliage all summer; this is cempasúchil, the Day-of-the-Dead flower, and the garden source of lutein.
plants.water: Moderate; keep evenly moist, water at the base
plants.spread: Clumping and upright; self-sows lightly, and the tall, heavy-headed stems can flop or snap in wind and rain, so bigger types may need staking
plants.seasons: Yellow, orange, and creamy-white pompom flowers from early summer to frost; aromatic ferny foliage spring–fall; killed outright by the first hard frost
plants.wildlife: Nectar and pollen for bees, butterflies, and other pollinators; the classic Día de los Muertos (cempasúchil) flower whose petals are strung into Day-of-the-Dead garlands
plants.deer: Seldom damaged by deer — the pungent aromatic foliage is generally passed over
plants.toxic: Petals are edible and the plant is only low-severity toxic, but the cell sap carries phototoxic thiophenes, so handling the crushed foliage or flowers then going into sun can redden or blister sensitive skin
plants.origin: Mexico & Central America
plants.habitat: Open, seasonally dry disturbed ground and roadsides

edible.summary: African marigold ray petals are edible in moderation — used mainly as a golden food coloring and mild flavoring, and as a saffron substitute; snip off the bitter white petal base before use.[2][3]
edible.parts: The colorful ray petals are the edible part. They are used to color and flavor foods, brewed as a tea, and used as a substitute for tarragon and (as a yellow dye) for saffron; the flower is also the garden source of the pigment lutein. Pull the petals from the head and remove their bitter white base before use — the flavor is spicy-herbal and can be quite bitter. The pungent leaves and roots are not a food.[2][3]
edible.preparation: Use only the ray petals, with the bitter white base snipped off, and use them in small amounts as a garnish or natural coloring rather than as a vegetable. Use only pesticide-free plants you have grown or know the source of — marigolds sold by a florist may be treated and are not for eating — and don't confuse culinary marigold (Tagetes) with pot marigold/calendula (Calendula officinalis), a different, milder genus.[3]
edible.caution: All Tagetes carry phototoxic thiophene derivatives in the sap, flowers, foliage, and roots: contact with the cell sap (especially through broken skin) followed by sun can cause skin redness, burning, and blisters in sensitive people, and the flowers' aroma can irritate nose and eyes — wear gloves if you react. The petals are edible only in small amounts and can be harmful eaten in quantity; commercially the flower is grown mainly as a lutein colorant for poultry feed and egg yolks, not as a human vegetable. Anyone with a known Asteraceae (daisy-family) allergy should avoid eating it.[1][3]

plants.blurb: The best-tasting culinary marigold: a compact bushy mound of ferny, lemon-scented foliage smothered all summer in small, single citrus-daisy flowers — dainty where the big French and African types are bold, and far tastier.
plants.water: Low–moderate; fairly drought-tolerant once established, water in dry spells
plants.spread: Mounding, tidy clump; self-sows lightly from dropped seed
plants.seasons: Masses of small single yellow-to-orange daisies from early summer to frost over lacy, aromatic lemon-scented foliage; killed outright by the first hard frost
plants.wildlife: The small, open single flowers are easy, accessible forage for small native bees, hoverflies, and butterflies through a long season into fall
plants.deer: Seldom browsed by deer — the pungent aromatic foliage is generally passed over
plants.toxic: Safe and edible in moderation (flowers and young leaves); the foliage's phototoxic thiophenes can cause minor, usually brief skin irritation in sensitive people
plants.origin: Mexico to Colombia & Peru
plants.habitat: Open, sunny disturbed ground in warm uplands

edible.summary: The premier culinary marigold — the small single flowers carry a genuine citrus-to-tarragon flavor (milder and better than African or French types) and the lacy young leaves are used too; a favorite edible flower.[1][3]
edible.parts: The colorful ray petals are the prize: pulled from the bitter green base and scattered fresh over salads, rice, fruit, and drinks, worked into baked goods, or dried for tea; the flavor runs citrusy-lemon to spicy tarragon and is notably milder than African marigold (Tagetes erecta). The finely divided, lemon-scented young leaves are used sparingly as a herb/garnish as well. Remove the stems, anthers, and pistils, which can be bitter.[1][3][4]
edible.preparation: Use only unsprayed, pesticide-free flowers you have grown yourself — never blooms from florists, garden centers, or nurseries, which are routinely treated. Pull the petals from the calyx, rinse, and use them fresh or dried, sparingly, as a garnish or flavoring rather than as a bulk vegetable. Introduce any new edible flower into your diet slowly and in small amounts.[3]
edible.caution: Eat in moderation — the petals can be harmful in large quantities, so treat them as a garnish, not a staple. Tagetes foliage, flowers, and roots contain phototoxic thiophene compounds that can cause minor, usually brief skin irritation in sensitive people (wear gloves if you react, especially on broken skin followed by sun exposure). Anyone with a known allergy to Asteraceae (daisy-family) plants should avoid eating it. Always be certain of your identification and that the plants were never sprayed.[1][3]

shots.cap: Single orange flower with a red eye · © Bodo Nuñez Oberg (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
A low mound smothered in tiny yellow flowers · © Neptalí Ramírez Marcial (CC-BY) / iNaturalist
Yellow flower over lacy, ferny foliage · © rsalgadogarciglia (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

Rounded, free-flowering mound · © Neptalí Ramírez Marcial (CC-BY) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: The heat-proof tarragon: smooth, un-lacy anise-scented leaves that stand in for finicky French tarragon, topped in late summer and fall by single golden daisies that feed migrating pollinators.
plants.sun: Full sun; tolerates light afternoon shade
plants.water: Low–moderate; drought-tolerant once established, but blooms fuller with occasional summer water
plants.spread: Clumping and upright; lift and pot it up before frost to overwinter indoors and keep the plant
plants.seasons: Golden-yellow single daisies late summer into fall (August–September, later as the days shorten); smooth, glossy anise-scented green foliage spring to frost; killed to the ground by the first hard frost
plants.wildlife: Late-season nectar for bees and butterflies — a fall forage plant that draws migrating monarchs; the pungent foliage is also said to repel some insect pests
plants.deer: Deer- and rabbit-resistant — the strongly aromatic foliage is generally passed over
plants.toxic: Safe and edible — a culinary herb; the only note is that Tagetes foliage carries phototoxic thiophenes that can cause skin redness or dermatitis in sensitive people who handle it, then go into the sun
plants.origin: Mexico & Guatemala
plants.habitat: Sunny hillsides and open woodland clearings

edible.summary: A genuine culinary herb — the smooth green leaves are a well-known substitute for French tarragon, with a sweet anise/licorice flavor, and the golden flowers are edible too.[1][2]
edible.parts: The leaves are the main edible part: used fresh or dried in place of tarragon to flavor vinegars, sauces, salad dressings, egg, fish, and chicken dishes, and steeped into a mild anise tea. The single golden flowers are also edible, brightening salads as a garnish and brewed for tea. The flavor carries notes of anise with hints of mint and a touch of sweetness.[1][2][3]
edible.preparation: Use the leaves fresh or freeze them — drying and excess heat fade the anise flavor, so fresh or frozen keeps it best. Add leaves toward the end of cooking, and use flowers raw as a garnish or for tea. As with any herb, use plants you know are pesticide-free, and use it in ordinary culinary amounts.[2]
edible.caution: This is a mild culinary herb in kitchen quantities, but a few honest notes: like all Tagetes it carries phototoxic thiophene compounds in the foliage, so handling the plant (especially with broken skin, then sun exposure) can cause skin redness or dermatitis in sensitive people, and anyone allergic to Asteraceae (daisy-family) plants should avoid eating it. Use culinary amounts, not medicinal doses — it has a long history of ceremonial and medicinal use in Mexico that is distinct from casual cooking. Don't confuse it with the strongly-scented weedy stinking-roger, Tagetes minuta, which is a different species, nor with the far more bitter African/American marigold (T. erecta).[1][2][3]

Golden flower clusters at the stem tips · © Hugo Basurto (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

plants.blurb: A non-vining SHRUB clematis from the Mongolian steppe: nodding, bright-yellow lantern flowers in late summer over a tidy woody clump, then silvery feathered seed plumes. Bone-dry xeric and iron cold-hardy — and, being a true shrub, NOT the weedy vining Clematis orientalis.
plants.water: Low; xeric once established — little to no supplemental water
plants.spread: Shrubby, upright, well-behaved clump; does not climb or twine
plants.seasons: Nodding yellow lantern flowers late summer (Aug–Sept); silvery feathery seed plumes fall into winter; bare woody clump in deep winter
plants.wildlife: Late-summer flowers draw bees and butterflies; silky seed plumes
plants.toxic: All parts toxic if eaten (protoanemonin); fresh sap irritates skin — wear gloves. A true shrub, NOT the noxious vining Clematis orientalis (CO List B weed)
plants.origin: Mongolia & northern China
plants.habitat: Dry continental steppe and rocky slopes

edible.summary: Poisonous — like all Clematis, every part is toxic if eaten and the sap irritates skin. No part is edible.[2][6]
edible.caution: Clematis tissue contains protoanemonin, an acrid irritant released when leaves or stems are bruised or chewed. Eating any part can cause intense burning of the mouth, mouth ulcers, drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea; the bitter, burning taste usually stops anyone from eating enough to be badly poisoned, but treat the whole plant as not for consumption. The same compound makes the fresh sap a skin and mucous-membrane irritant, so wear gloves when pruning — the RHS flags it as a skin irritant and as harmful to rabbits if eaten. It is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses as well as people; keep children and pets from chewing it. Drying largely destroys protoanemonin, but never rely on that.[2][6][7]

Nodding yellow lantern flowers · © vandandorj (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

mulleins.name: Mulleins
mulleins.blurb: Verbascum — architectural flower-spire mulleins, the garden ornamentals (NOT the weedy common/moth mulleins).

apples.name: Apples
apples.blurb: Cold-hardy apple cultivars (Malus domestica) for the Front Range.

plums.name: Plums
plums.blurb: Hardy European & hybrid plums (Prunus) for our climate.

currants.name: Currants & gooseberries
currants.blurb: Ribes and their kin — currants, gooseberry, and the jostaberry hybrid.

pelargoniums.name: Pelargoniums
pelargoniums.blurb: Tender 'geraniums' (Pelargonium) — bedding, scented, ivy & regal types.

wallflowers.name: Wallflowers
wallflowers.blurb: Erysimum — fragrant cottage-garden wallflowers, perennial & biennial.

tulips.name: Tulips
tulips.blurb: Garden hybrid and perennializing species tulips.

crocuses.name: Crocuses
crocuses.blurb: Early snow crocus and big Dutch crocus for the first bees.

penstemons.name: Penstemons
penstemons.blurb: Beardtongues — Western native and garden penstemons.

maples.name: Maples
maples.blurb: Small Western maples (Acer) grown for fiery fall colour.

milkweeds.name: Milkweeds
milkweeds.blurb: Asclepias — the essential monarch host plants.

alliums.name: Alliums
alliums.blurb: Ornamental and wild onions (Allium) — globe and nodding flowerheads bees mob.

sunflowers.name: Sunflowers
sunflowers.blurb: True sunflowers (Helianthus) — golden heads bees and goldfinches mob.

bee-balms.name: Bee balms
bee-balms.blurb: Monarda — fragrant bergamots that draw bees & hummingbirds.

lupines.name: Lupines
lupines.blurb: Lupinus — native and garden pea-flower spires (toxic alkaloids).

dogwoods.name: Dogwoods
dogwoods.blurb: Cornus — winter stems, early flowers, and bird berries.

clematis.name: Clematis
clematis.blurb: Large-flowered garden climbers and native virgin's-bowers (Clematis).

columbines.name: Columbines
columbines.blurb: Aquilegia — spurred woodland-edge flowers, the wild blue and golden garden selections.

sweet-scabious.name: Scabious
sweet-scabious.blurb: Scabiosa — pincushion flowers: the compact perennial and the tall annual cut-flower forms.

prairie-coneflowers.name: Prairie coneflowers
prairie-coneflowers.blurb: Ratibida columnifera — the yellow Mexican hat and its mahogany-red form.

pinks.name: Pinks
pinks.blurb: Dianthus — fragrant, fringed, mat-forming garden pinks.

coreopsis.name: Coreopsis
coreopsis.blurb: Coreopsis — easy golden tickseed daisies, large-flowered and threadleaf.

sumacs.name: Sumacs
sumacs.blurb: Rhus — tough native shrubs for fiery fall color and tart red 'lemonade' berries.

primulas.name: Primulas
primulas.blurb: Primula — cool-season primroses: leaf rosettes topped with bright early flowers, from woodland classics to a Colorado alpine native.

ornamental-oregano.name: Ornamental oreganos
ornamental-oregano.blurb: Origanum — ornamental oreganos grown for their rosy flowers and cascading hop-like bracts (not the culinary herb).

marigolds.name: Marigolds
marigolds.blurb: Tagetes — cheerful, aromatic, deer-resistant marigolds, from tiny citrus-edible signets to big African pompoms and the tarragon-scented Mexican mint marigold.
